**(a)** *Compare and contrast semantic networks with frames as knowledge representation schemes. Which is more suitable for representing CPS contexts, and why?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Semantic Network | Frame |
|-----------|------------------|-------|
| **Structure** | Graph with labeled nodes/edges | Object with slots and fillers |
| **Inheritance** | Through IS-A graph traversal | Built into frame hierarchy |
| **Default Values** | Not inherently supported | Native support (default slots) |
| **Procedural Attachment** | Not native | Supports *daemons* and *attached procedures* |
| **Visualization** | Excellent (graph) | Good (slot table) |
| **Expressiveness** | Moderate | High (facets, constraints) |
| **Best for CPS** | Topology/dependency modeling | Object-oriented CPS component modeling |

**Suitability for CPS (4 marks):**

- **Semantic nets** excel at modeling **inter-component relationships** (e.g., sensor-to-actuator data flow, dependency graphs in a power grid), making them ideal for **system-level ontology**.
- **Frames** are better for modeling **individual CPS components** with rich attributes, defaults, and procedural behaviors (e.g., a *Robot_Arm* frame with slots for *position*, *velocity*, *gripper_state*).

**Conclusion:** A **hybrid approach** is most effective — semantic nets model the **macro-level inter-component topology**, while frames represent the **micro-level structure of each CPS entity**. This layered representation is the KTU-endorsed best practice.

---

**(b)** *Explain the four major types of semantic relations used in semantic nets. Construct a semantic net for a small hospital CPS involving doctors, patients, ICU, ventilators, and oxygen sensors, and demonstrate at least two multi-hop reasoning paths.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Four Major Relation Types (3 marks):**

1. **IS-A (Taxonomic):** Class-subclass hierarchy (e.g., `Patient IS-A Person`).
2. **HAS-A (Compositional):** Part-whole (e.g., `ICU HAS-A Ventilator`).
3. **Semantic/Associative:** Domain links (e.g., `Doctor TREATS Patient`).
4. **Causal/Temporal:** Cause-effect or time-order (e.g., `Low_SpO2 CAUSES Ventilator_Activation`).

**Hospital CPS Semantic Net (2 marks):**

```
Person → IS-A → Doctor
Person → IS-A → Patient
Patient → IS-A → ICU_Patient
Hospital → HAS-A → ICU
ICU → HAS-A → Ventilator_V1
ICU → HAS-A → Oxygen_Sensor_O2
Doctor → TREATS → ICU_Patient
Ventilator_V1 → MONITORS → Oxygen_Sensor_O2
Oxygen_Sensor_O2 → ALERTS → ICU_Staff
Low_SpO2 (<90%) → TRIGGERS → Ventilator_V1 (Increase O2)
ICU → LOCATED-IN → Hospital_Wing_A
```

**Multi-Hop Reasoning Paths (2 marks):**

**Path 1 — Patient Care Query:**
*"Which doctor is responsible for ICU_Patient_42?"*
`ICU_Patient_42` $\xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}}$ `Patient` $\xrightarrow{\text{TREATS}^{-1}}$ `Doctor` $\rightarrow$ *Answer: Dr_Smith (assuming Dr_Smith is the assigned doctor).*

**Path 2 — Sensor-Driven Actuation:**
*"What happens when Oxygen_Sensor_O2 reads SpO2 < 90%?"*
`Oxygen_Sensor_O2` $\xrightarrow{\text{TRIGGERS}}$ `Ventilator_V1` $\xrightarrow{\text{ALERTS}}$ `ICU_Staff` $\rightarrow$ *Action: Increase O2 flow rate; notify on-call doctor; log event.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Omitting the formal graph definition** $G = (V, E, L)$ — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing IS-A (taxonomic) with INSTANCE-OF (assertional)** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks. Remember: *IS-A* is for classes (`Dog IS-A Mammal`), *INSTANCE* is for individuals (`Rex INSTANCE Dog`).
> 3. **Forgetting the inheritance conflict resolution rule** (closest-ancestor-wins) in multiple-inheritance questions — losing 2 marks.
> 4. **Drawing a net without edge labels** — examiners insist that *every* edge must have a semantic label; unlabeled arrows fetch partial credit at best.
> 5. **Not tracing the actual graph path** during reasoning — stating the answer without showing the traversal sequence loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Using single-arrow notation for multi-hop paths** — always enumerate intermediate nodes explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Semantic Nets (Module 1)**

- ✅ A **semantic net** is a **directed labeled graph** $G = (V, E, L)$ used for **knowledge representation** in cognitive AI systems.
- ✅ **Nodes** represent concepts/objects/events; **edges** represent semantic relations; **labels** name the relation.
- ✅ **Ross Quillian (1968)** is credited with introducing semantic nets as a model of human associative memory.
- ✅ Four primary relation types: **IS-A (taxonomic)**, **HAS-A (compositional)**, **Semantic/associative**, and **Causal/temporal**.
- ✅ **Definitional nets** encode concept definitions; **assertional nets** encode specific beliefs.
- ✅ **Inheritance** is the core inference mechanism: properties propagate up/down the IS-A chain.
- ✅ The **inheritance rule** is: $\text{IS-A}(x,y) \land \text{HAS}(y,z) \Rightarrow \text{HAS}(x,z)$.
- ✅ **Transitivity of IS-A:** $\text{IS-A}(x,y) \land \text{IS-A}(y,z) \Rightarrow \text{IS-A}(x,z)$.
- ✅ **Multiple inheritance conflicts** are resolved by the **closest-ancestor-wins** heuristic (shortest IS-A path).
- ✅ Semantic nets enable **perceive → interpret → reason → act** loops in **Cognitive Cyber-Physical Systems (CCPS)**.
- ✅ They are best suited for **topology, ontology, and dependency modeling** in CPS, while **frames** are better for individual component modeling.
- ✅ **Limitations** (for viva preparation): poor handling of **negation**, **disjunction**, and **quantification** — these limitations motivated the development of **Description Logics** and **Ontology Web Language (OWL)**.
- ✅ **Always label every edge** in your diagrams during the exam.
- ✅ **Always show the graph traversal path** when justifying reasoning answers.

> **End of Module 1 — Topic: Semantic Nets | PECPT754 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: An Abstract View of Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** An Abstract View of Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, *modelling* is the disciplined, mathematical, and computational process of constructing an **abstract representation** of a real-world system that captures its essential structural, functional, and behavioural properties while deliberately suppressing irrelevant implementation details. A *model* therefore serves as a **substitute system** upon which analysis, simulation, verification, and prediction can be performed safely and economically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner phrasing):**
> *"A model is a precise description of a system, captured in a well-defined formal language, whose analysis yields valid and reproducible insights about the behaviour of the original system."*

The phrase **"Abstract View"** specifically refers to the deliberate act of viewing the system **at a chosen level of granularity** — selecting *what* to keep and *what* to discard — so that the resulting representation is **tractable**, **verifiable**, and **computable**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The City Map:**
> Imagine you are giving directions to a friend in **Kochi**. You do not describe every pothole, every autorickshaw, or every streetlight — you provide a **map**. A map is a *model* of the city.
> - A **road-level map** (1:5000 scale) shows every lane — a *fine-grained model*.
> - A **state-level map** (1:5,000,000 scale) shows only inter-state highways — an *abstracted model*.
> Both are "correct" maps, but they serve different purposes. **Abstraction in CPS modelling works the same way**: we choose the level of detail based on the engineering question being asked.

In a CPS (e.g., an **autonomous vehicle**, a **smart grid**, or a **pacemaker**), we cannot analyse the physical hardware directly under every condition. We build mathematical or computational models (ODEs, state machines, hybrid automata) that *imitate* the system's behaviour with provable fidelity.

### 1.3 Why "Abstract"?

The word **abstract** carries three engineering meanings within this KTU module:

1. **Conceptual abstraction** – dealing with ideas (e.g., "a vehicle") rather than concrete instances ("my car KL-07-AB-1234").
2. **Abstraction by simplification** – removing non-essential dynamics (e.g., ignoring air resistance in a low-speed robot).
3. **Hierarchical abstraction** – composing the system from sub-models, each independently verifiable (the *compositional* view).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme stresses that *every* CPS model is a **multiview** construct — it must simultaneously address the **physical view** (continuous dynamics), the **computational view** (discrete algorithms), and the **interaction view** (network/communication). The "abstract view" is the unifying lens that lets us reason across all three.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants Referenced

- **Model fidelity (F)**: a dimensionless ratio ∈ **[0, 1]** indicating how closely model output matches real-system output.
- **Level of Abstraction (LoA)**: an ordinal index; **higher LoA = coarser, less detailed** model.
- **State dimension (n)**: number of independent variables required to fully describe the system at any instant.
- **Time quantisation Δt**: the discrete time-step used in computational models (units: **seconds**).

> [!WARNING]
> Students frequently lose marks by confusing **abstraction** with **approximation**. *Abstraction* is intentional omission of detail; *approximation* is the deliberate introduction of controlled error in retained detail. Both are modelling tools, but they are not synonymous.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Modelling Pipeline (Structured Logic)

The construction of a CPS model follows a **five-stage disciplined process** that examiners routinely test:

1. **System Boundary Definition** – Decide *what is inside* the system and *what is environment*. Boundary conditions are declared explicitly.
2. **Identification of Variables** – Classify variables as **input (u)**, **output (y)**, **state (x)**, **disturbance (d)**, or **parameter (θ)**.
3. **Selection of Modelling Paradigm** – Choose between:
   - *Continuous-time ODE/PDE models* (for physical processes)
   - *Discrete-event / state-machine models* (for logical control)
   - *Hybrid models* (combining both — *the defining feature of CPS*)
   - *Data-driven / ML models* (for unknown dynamics)
4. **Formalisation** – Write the model in a well-defined mathematical syntax.
5. **Validation & Verification (V&V)** – Confirm that the model behaves *as intended* (verification) and *as required* (validation).

### 2.2 The Four Pillars of an Abstract View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every CPS abstract model must answer four orthogonal questions:

| # | Question | View Name | Mathematical Form |
|---|----------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | *What is the system?* | **Structural View** | Graphs, block diagrams, components |
| 2 | *What does it do over time?* | **Behavioural View** | $\dot{x}=f(x,u), \quad y=g(x,u)$ |
| 3 | *What is it built from?* | **Compositional View** | $S = S_1 \parallel S_2 \parallel \ldots \parallel S_n$ |
| 4 | *What guarantees hold?* | **Property View** | Temporal logic: $\Box P$, $\Diamond Q$ |

### 2.3 The Core 'Why' and 'How'

- **Why model?** Because direct experimentation on physical CPS is *expensive* (a Boeing 737 cannot be crash-tested repeatedly), *dangerous* (a nuclear reactor), or *impossible* (a planet's climate). Models enable *prediction*, *analysis*, and *certification*.
- **How do we abstract?** By applying three operations:
  1. **Discretisation** — convert continuous to discrete.
  2. **Aggregation** — merge fine states into coarse states.
  3. **Projection** — eliminate variables that do not affect the output of interest.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is HIGH-YIELD for KTU 2024 ESE — memorise the canonical forms.**

| # | Concept | Canonical Equation | Units / Domain |
|---|---------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Continuous-time state model | $\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t), u(t)), \quad x(0)=x_0$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}, u \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ |
| 2 | Output equation | $y(t) = g(x(t), u(t))$ | $y \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ |
| 3 | Discrete-time state model | $x[k+1] = f_d(x[k], u[k])$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 4 | Hybrid automaton (CPS core) | $(Q, X, f, \text{Init}, E, G, R)$ | $Q$: discrete states, $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| 5 | Euler discretisation | $x[k+1] \approx x[k] + \Delta t \cdot f(x[k], u[k])$ | $\Delta t$ in **seconds** |
| 6 | Linearisation about $x^{\ast}$ | $\dot{\delta x} = A\delta x + B\delta u$, $\; A=\left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right\vert_{x^{\ast}}$ | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| 7 | Lyapunov stability | $\dot{V}(x) < 0,\; V(0)=0,\; V(x)>0$ for $x \neq 0$ | $V: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 8 | Fidelity metric (RMS) | $F = 1 - \frac{\Vert y_{\text{model}}-y_{\text{real}}\Vert_{2}}{\Vert y_{\text{real}}\Vert_{2}}$ | Dimensionless ∈ [0, 1] |
| 9 | Model order $n$ | Order = dim(state vector) | Integer $\geq 1$ |
| 10 | Sampling relation (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | $f_s$ in **Hz** |

> **Critical typographic note:** The notation $\vert x \vert$ used above employs the `\vert` LaTeX command (not the bare pipe `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | CPS Model Used | Purpose |
|----------|----------------|---------|
| **Automotive** | Engine + ECU hybrid model | Fuel-efficiency optimisation, ADAS verification |
| **Healthcare** | Insulin-pump hybrid model | Closed-loop glucose control, FDA certification |
| **Aviation** | Flight dynamics + autopilot model | Fly-by-wire certification (DO-178C) |
| **Smart Grid** | Power-flow + demand-response model | Load balancing, blackout prevention |
| **Robotics** | Kinematic + control FSM model | Motion planning, safety guarantees |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Disclosure:** Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly. No step is abbreviated.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Newton's Second Law to a Canonical CPS State-Space Model

**Problem Setup:** Consider a DC motor driving an inertial load — a fundamental CPS sub-system. Derive its continuous-time abstract model.

**Step 1 — Identify physical variables.**
Let:
- $\theta(t)$ = angular position of the motor shaft (output) — units: **radians (rad)**
- $\omega(t) = \dot{\theta}(t)$ = angular velocity — units: **rad/s**
- $V(t)$ = applied voltage — units: **volts (V)**
- $J$ = moment of inertia — units: **kg·m²**
- $b$ = viscous friction coefficient — units: **N·m·s**
- $K_t$ = motor torque constant — units: **N·m/A**
- $K_e$ = back-EMF constant — units: **V·s/rad**

**Step 2 — Write the electrical equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law).**

The armature circuit yields:
$$V(t) = L \frac{di(t)}{dt} + R i(t) + K_e \omega(t)$$

where $i(t)$ is the armature current (A), $L$ the inductance (H), and $R$ the resistance ($\Omega$).

**Step 3 — Write the mechanical equation (Newton's 2nd law for rotation).**

$$J \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} = K_t i(t) - b\, \omega(t)$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the abstraction: state vector $x(t)$.**

Choose the *minimum complete* set of independent variables:
$$x(t) = \begin{bmatrix} \theta(t) \\ \omega(t) \\ i(t) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$$

The order of the model is therefore $n = 3$.

**Step 5 — Rewrite the equations in canonical form $\dot{x} = f(x,u), \; y = g(x,u)$.**

Computing the derivatives:
- $\dot{\theta} = \omega$
- $\dot{\omega} = \dfrac{K_t}{J} i - \dfrac{b}{J} \omega$
- $\dot{i} = -\dfrac{K_e}{L} \omega - \dfrac{R}{L} i + \dfrac{1}{L} V$

Stacking them:
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(t) &= \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -\frac{b}{J} & \frac{K_t}{J} \\ 0 & -\frac{K_e}{L} & -\frac{R}{L} \end{bmatrix}}_{A} x(t) \;+\; \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \frac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix}}_{B} V(t) \\
y(t) &= \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{C} x(t)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **linear continuous-time state-space model** of the DC motor — a textbook CPS sub-model.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Euler Discretisation of an ODE Model

**Why discretise?** Digital controllers (the *cyber* part of CPS) cannot integrate ODEs continuously — they must sample at discrete time-steps $\Delta t$.

**Given:** $\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t), u(t))$

**Step 1 — Apply the definition of derivative (forward difference).**
$$\dot{x}(t) \approx \frac{x(t+\Delta t) - x(t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the ODE.**
$$\frac{x(t+\Delta t) - x(t)}{\Delta t} = f(x(t), u(t))$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the next state.**
$$x(t+\Delta t) = x(t) + \Delta t \cdot f(x(t), u(t))$$

**Step 4 — Replace continuous time with discrete index $k$.**
$$x[k+1] = x[k] + \Delta t \cdot f(x[k], u[k])$$

**Step 5 — Specialise to the DC motor.**
Substituting $f$ from §3.1:
$$\begin{aligned}
\theta[k+1] &= \theta[k] + \Delta t \cdot \omega[k] \\
\omega[k+1] &= \omega[k] + \Delta t \cdot \left(\frac{K_t}{J}i[k] - \frac{b}{J}\omega[k]\right) \\
i[k+1]    &= i[k] + \Delta t \cdot \left(-\frac{K_e}{L}\omega[k] - \frac{R}{L}i[k] + \frac{V[k]}{L}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Stability constraint (Nyquist):** For the discretisation to be numerically stable,
$$\Delta t < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}(A)}$$
where $\lambda_{\max}(A)$ is the largest eigenvalue of the system matrix $A$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Linearisation of a Non-Linear CPS Model

**Given:** Non-linear system $\dot{x} = f(x,u)$ with equilibrium $(x^{\ast}, u^{\ast})$ such that $f(x^{\ast}, u^{\ast}) = 0$.

**Step 1 — Define perturbation variables.**
$$\delta x \triangleq x - x^{\ast}, \qquad \delta u \triangleq u - u^{\ast}$$

**Step 2 — Taylor-expand $f$ about $(x^{\ast}, u^{\ast})$, retain first-order terms.**
$$f(x^{\ast}+\delta x, \, u^{\ast}+\delta u) \approx \underbrace{f(x^{\ast}, u^{\ast})}_{=0} + \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right\vert_{(x^{\ast},u^{\ast})}\delta x + \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial u}\right\vert_{(x^{\ast},u^{\ast})}\delta u$$

**Step 3 — Define Jacobians $A$ and $B$.**
$$A \triangleq \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right\vert_{(x^{\ast},u^{\ast})} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}, \qquad B \triangleq \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial u}\right\vert_{(x^{\ast},u^{\ast})} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$$

**Step 4 — Write the linearised model.**
$$\dot{\delta x}(t) = A\,\delta x(t) + B\,\delta u(t)$$

This linearised model is what enables classical control techniques (PID, LQR, $H_{\infty}$) to be applied to CPS.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Model Equivalence Table

| Abstract Property | Continuous Form | Discrete Form | Hybrid Form |
|-------------------|-----------------|---------------|-------------|
| State evolution | $\dot{x} = f(x,u)$ | $x[k+1] = f_d(x[k], u[k])$ | Mode-dependent: $f_q(x,u)$ per mode $q$ |
| Output | $y = g(x,u)$ | $y[k] = g_d(x[k], u[k])$ | $y = g_q(x,u)$ |
| Trigger | Continuous time | Clock tick $k\Delta t$ | Event / guard condition $G(q, q', x)$ |
| Use case | Physical dynamics | Digital controller | Full CPS (cyber + physical) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Modelling Process (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the *flow of activities* when constructing an abstract view of a CPS model. It maps how real-world phenomena are progressively transformed into a deployable computational artefact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RWS[Real World System] --> BD[Define System Boundary]
    BD --> VAR[Identify Variables u x y d theta]
    VAR --> PARA[Select Modelling Paradigm]
    PARA --> CT[Continuous ODE Model]
    PARA --> DT[Discrete State Machine]
    PARA --> HY[Hybrid Automaton]
    PARA --> ML[Data Driven ML Model]
    CT --> FORM[Formalise in Math Syntax]
    DT --> FORM
    HY --> FORM
    ML --> FORM
    FORM --> VANDV[Validation and Verification]
    VANDV --> DEPLOY[Deploy for Simulation]
    DEPLOY --> ANA[Analysis Prediction Control]
    VANDV --> FEED[Feedback Refinement]
    FEED --> PARA
```

**Reading guide for the examiner-style answer:**
- The cycle is **iterative** — feedback from V&V can force a re-selection of the paradigm.
- The four paradigm branches are *alternative*, not sequential.

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Abstraction Hierarchy (Lattice of Models)

This diagram shows how successive abstraction layers reduce model complexity while preserving correctness guarantees.

```mermaid
graph LR
    L0[Level 0 Physical Reality] --> L1[Level 1 First Principles ODE]
    L1 --> L2[Level 2 Linearised State Space]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Discretised Sampled Model]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Finite State Machine Abstraction]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 High Level Requirement Spec]
    L0 -.-> L1
    L1 -.-> L2
    L2 -.-> L3
    L3 -.-> L4
    L4 -.-> L5
```

**Key insight:** As we ascend the lattice, *computational tractability increases* but *predictive fidelity decreases*. The art of CPS modelling is choosing the **right level for the right question**.

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Multi-View of a CPS Model

The single CPS artefact can be viewed from four complementary perspectives — this is the heart of the "abstract view" concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPS[Cyber Physical System Model] --> SV[Structural View]
    CPS --> BV[Behavioural View]
    CPS --> CV[Compositional View]
    CPS --> PV[Property View]
    SV --> SG[System Graph and Topology]
    BV --> SD[State Dynamics ODE FSM]
    CV --> CP[Sub system Composition Operators]
    PV --> TL[Temporal Logic Properties Box Diamond]
```

**Key for the student:** In your ESE answer, whenever you describe a CPS model, you should be able to articulate *at least* the structural and behavioural views explicitly. Examiners reward multi-view answers.

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Continuous-Discrete Interplay in a Hybrid Model

Hybrid models are the *defining abstraction* of CPS — they seamlessly couple continuous and discrete dynamics.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state "Mode 1 Continuous Dynamics" as M1
    state "Mode 2 Continuous Dynamics" as M2
    state "Mode 3 Continuous Dynamics" as M3
    [*] --> M1
    M1 --> M2 : Guard x greater than threshold
    M2 --> M3 : Guard u crosses zero
    M3 --> M1 : Reset discrete event timer
    M2 --> [*]
```

**Exam tip:** When asked "what is a hybrid model?", draw a state diagram with *continuous dynamics inside each node* and *guards on the transitions* — this single image carries 3–4 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are framed in the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**: short, application-oriented, and tagged with the relevant Course Outcome (CO) and Bloom's level.

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, CO1, Bloom: Remember]**
*Define a "model" in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. Why is the "abstract view" essential during modelling?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **model** in CPS is a *formal, simplified representation* of a real physical-cyber system, expressed in mathematical or computational form, which captures the system's essential behaviour while omitting non-essential details. The model must be **analysable, simulatable, and verifiable**.

The **abstract view** is essential because real CPS (e.g., a smart factory, an autonomous vehicle) is too complex to analyse in its full detail. Abstraction allows the engineer to:
1. **Suppress irrelevant detail** — e.g., ignoring quantum effects in a motor.
2. **Select the appropriate paradigm** — continuous, discrete, or hybrid.
3. **Enable formal verification** — abstraction is the *prerequisite* for applying tools like model checkers (SPIN, UPPAAL) and theorem provers.

> [!TIP]
> A perfect 3-mark answer includes: (a) one formal sentence, (b) one CPS example, (c) one bullet on why abstraction is necessary.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, CO1, Bloom: Understand]**
*Differentiate between **abstraction** and **approximation** in CPS modelling with a suitable example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Abstraction | Approximation |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| **Definition** | Deliberate *omission* of system details | Deliberate *introduction* of controlled error |
| **Purpose** | Reduce model complexity to a tractable form | Make an intractable model solvable analytically |
| **Example** | Modelling a motor as a *single lumped inertia*, ignoring shaft elasticity | Replacing $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$ for small angles in a pendulum model |

> **Key distinction:** Abstraction changes *what* is in the model; approximation changes *how accurately* the retained elements are described. Both are valid, but they are not interchangeable.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE rules: **Module Internal Choice is mandatory**. Two fully independent alternatives are provided below. Students answer **ONE**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, CO1 + CO2, Bloom: Apply + Analyse]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **five-stage modelling pipeline** for a Cyber-Physical System. For each stage, state one deliverable artefact. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the **inverted pendulum on a cart** (a classic CPS benchmark). Derive its non-linear state-space model using Newton's second law, then perform **first-order linearisation** about the upright equilibrium $\theta^{\ast}=0$. Express the final linear model in the matrix form $\dot{\delta x} = A\,\delta x + B\,\delta u$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

The five-stage modelling pipeline (as per §2.1 of these notes):

1. **System Boundary Definition** — *Artefact:* a context diagram marking system-vs-environment.
2. **Variable Identification** — *Artefact:* a variable dictionary listing $u, y, x, d, \theta$ with physical units.
3. **Paradigm Selection** — *Artefact:* a written rationale justifying the chosen formalism (ODE / FSM / Hybrid / ML).
4. **Formalisation** — *Artefact:* a fully written set of equations or automata definitions.
5. **Validation & Verification (V&V)** — *Artefact:* a V&V report with fidelity metrics and test cases.

Marks distribution: 5 stages × 1 mark for description + 2 marks for the artefact set and overall coherence.

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define the physical setup.** *(0.5 mark)*
Cart of mass $M$ (kg) slides horizontally on a frictionless track. A pendulum of mass $m$ (kg) and length $\ell$ (m) is attached via a frictionless pivot. The cart is driven by horizontal force $u$ (N). Pendulum angle from vertical is $\theta$ (rad).

**Step 2 — Write Newton's equations for the cart and pendulum.** *(2 marks)*

Cart (horizontal):
$$(M+m)\ddot{x} - m\ell \ddot{\theta}\cos\theta + m\ell \dot{\theta}^{2}\sin\theta = u$$

Pendulum (rotational):
$$m\ell^{2}\ddot{\theta} - m\ell\ddot{x}\cos\theta - mg\ell\sin\theta = 0$$

**Step 3 — Choose the state vector.** *(0.5 mark)*
$$x = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ \theta \\ \dot{x} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{4}, \quad \text{input } u \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the algebraic system for the accelerations.** *(2 marks)*

Rearranging and applying Cramer's rule to eliminate $\ddot{x}$ and $\ddot{\theta}$ yields two explicit second-order ODEs. The detailed algebra (omitted here for length, but expected in a full KTU answer) gives:

$$\ddot{x} = \frac{-m\ell \dot{\theta}^{2}\sin\theta + u + m g \cos\theta \sin\theta}{M + m\sin^{2}\theta}$$

$$\ddot{\theta} = \frac{(M+m)g\sin\theta - m\ell \dot{\theta}^{2}\sin\theta\cos\theta - u\cos\theta}{\ell(M + m\sin^{2}\theta)}$$

**Step 5 — Linearise about $\theta^{\ast}=0, \dot{\theta}^{\ast}=0, x^{\ast}=0, \dot{x}^{\ast}=0, u^{\ast}=0$.** *(1.5 marks)*

At the upright equilibrium, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$, and $\dot{\theta}^{2}\theta$ terms vanish (second-order small). The linearised equations become:

$$\ddot{x} = \frac{m g \theta + u}{M}, \qquad \ddot{\theta} = \frac{(M+m)g\theta - u}{M\ell}$$

**Step 6 — Write the final matrix form $\dot{\delta x} = A\,\delta x + B\,\delta u$.** *(0.5 mark)*

$$\dot{\delta x} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & \frac{mg}{M} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{(M+m)g}{M\ell} & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{A} \delta x \;+\; \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \frac{1}{M} \\ -\frac{1}{M\ell} \end{bmatrix}}_{B} \delta u$$

This linearised model is the standard benchmark used in **LQR, MPC, and reinforcement-learning** studies of CPS.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, CO1 + CO2, Bloom: Apply + Analyse]**

**(a)** Explain the **four orthogonal views** of a CPS abstract model (Structural, Behavioural, Compositional, Property). Give one mathematical formalism for each view. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A first-order thermal system obeys the ODE
$$ \dot{T}(t) = -\frac{1}{\tau} T(t) + \frac{K}{\tau} u(t), \quad T(0) = T_0 $$
where $T$ is temperature (°C), $u$ is heater power (W), $\tau$ is the time-constant (s), and $K$ is the steady-state gain (°C/W).
**(i)** Discretise using **Euler's forward method** with step $\Delta t$. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Determine the **steady-state temperature** $T_{ss}$ for a constant input $u = \bar{u}$. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** Compute the **discrete-time steady-state** $T_{ss}^{d}$ and show that it equals $T_{ss}$ as $\Delta t \to 0$. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

| # | View | What it answers | Mathematical Formalism |
|---|------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Structural** | *What is in the system?* | Directed graph $G = (V, E)$; adjacency matrix $A$ |
| 2 | **Behavioural** | *What does it do over time?* | ODE/FSM/Hybrid: $\dot{x}=f(x,u), y=g(x,u)$ |
| 3 | **Compositional** | *How do parts combine?* | Algebra of composition: $S = S_1 \Vert S_2$ (process-algebra operators) |
| 4 | **Property** | *What guarantees hold?* | Temporal logic: $\Box P$ (always), $\Diamond Q$ (eventually), $P \,\mathcal{U}\, Q$ (until) |

Marks distribution: 4 views × 1 mark for description + 1 mark for formalism + 3 marks for a worked CPS example (e.g., autonomous vehicle) showing all four views.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Euler discretisation — 3 marks:**

Substituting the forward-difference approximation:
$$\frac{T[k+1] - T[k]}{\Delta t} = -\frac{1}{\tau}T[k] + \frac{K}{\tau}u[k]$$

Solving for $T[k+1]$:
$$\boxed{\,T[k+1] = \left(1 - \frac{\Delta t}{\tau}\right) T[k] + \frac{K \Delta t}{\tau} u[k]\,}$$

This is the **discrete-time ARX-type model** of the thermal CPS.

**(ii) Continuous steady-state — 2 marks:**

At steady state, $\dot{T}(t) = 0$ and $T(t) = T_{ss}$. Hence:
$$0 = -\frac{1}{\tau}T_{ss} + \frac{K}{\tau}\bar{u} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,T_{ss} = K\bar{u}\,}$$

**(iii) Discrete steady-state and limiting behaviour — 2 marks:**

At discrete steady state, $T[k+1] = T[k] = T_{ss}^{d}$. Substituting into the discretised model:
$$T_{ss}^{d} = \left(1 - \frac{\Delta t}{\tau}\right)T_{ss}^{d} + \frac{K\Delta t}{\tau}\bar{u}$$

Solving:
$$T_{ss}^{d} \cdot \frac{\Delta t}{\tau} = \frac{K\Delta t}{\tau}\bar{u} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_{ss}^{d} = K\bar{u}$$

This is identical to the continuous $T_{ss}$ for *any* $\Delta t > 0$. Hence:
$$\lim_{\Delta t \to 0} T_{ss}^{d} = T_{ss} = K\bar{u} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's insight:** The steady-state is preserved exactly by the Euler scheme for this *first-order* system. This is **not** true in general (e.g., second-order oscillators lose amplitude). Students who note this earn bonus marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks on "Abstract View of Modelling" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing model with simulation.** A model is the *mathematical* artefact; a simulation is the *execution* of a model on a computer. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for using these terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting units in state variables.** A state vector without units is incomplete. Always write, e.g., $x_1 = \theta$ (rad), $x_2 = \dot{\theta}$ (rad/s).
> 3. **Skipping the linearisation step.** Non-linear ODEs *cannot* be plugged directly into a state-space matrix form. You must first linearise (Taylor expand to first order) about an equilibrium.
> 4. **Writing $\dot{x} = Ax$ without defining $A$.** Always *define* the matrix elements using the system parameters — partial credit depends on this.
> 5. **Ignoring the hybrid view.** A 14-mark question that mentions only ODEs is *incomplete* for a CPS course. Always include the discrete-event / hybrid perspective.
> 6. **Not specifying the time-domain.** State explicitly whether the model is continuous-time ($t \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$) or discrete-time ($k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$) — the notation $x(t)$ vs $x[k]$ matters.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and keep it beside you during revision.**

- [ ] **A model is a formal, simplified, tractable representation of a system** — not a copy, not a simulation.
- [ ] **The "abstract view"** means *choosing* the granularity, the paradigm, and the boundary — it is an act of engineering judgement, not a mechanical step.
- [ ] **Three modelling operations**: *discretisation*, *aggregation*, *projection* — know what each removes.
- [ ] **Four orthogonal views of a CPS model**: Structural, Behavioural, Compositional, Property — always mention more than one in your ESE answer.
- [ ] **Canonical state-space form** (continuous): $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu, \; y = Cx + Du$ — memorise the matrix dimensions.
- [ ] **Canonical state-space form** (discrete): $x[k+1] = A_d x[k] + B_d u[k]$.
- [ ] **Euler forward discretisation**: $x[k+1] = x[k] + \Delta t \cdot f(x[k], u[k])$.
- [ ] **Linearisation** requires (a) identifying the equilibrium, (b) Taylor expansion to first order, (c) defining Jacobians $A, B$.
- [ ] **Hybrid model** = continuous dynamics inside modes + guards on transitions; this is the *defining formalism of CPS*.
- [ ] **Validation answers "is it the right model?"**; **Verification answers "is it built correctly?"** — never swap them.
- [ ] **Sampling constraint (Nyquist)**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$.
- [ ] **Fidelity metric** is a *scalar* in $[0, 1]$; higher is better.
- [ ] **Abstraction ≠ Approximation**: omission vs controlled error.
- [ ] **State dimension = order of the model**.
- [ ] Always carry **units** in your equations — Kelvin, rad/s, V, A, N·m — examiners reward unit-conscious answers.
- [ ] The five-stage **modelling pipeline** (Boundary → Variables → Paradigm → Formalisation → V&V) is high-yield and frequently tested.
- [ ] **Lagrangian / Hamiltonian** formulations are *alternative* first-principles routes to the same ODE — know at least one.
- [ ] In a CPS, the **cyber** part is discrete-event; the **physical** part is continuous-time; *their interaction* is what makes the system "cyber-physical".
- [ ] **Compositionality** (building a system model from sub-models) is the engineering trick that makes large-scale CPS tractable.
- [ ] **Temporal logic operators**: $\Box$ (always), $\Diamond$ (eventually), $\mathcal{U}$ (until) — used in property views.
- [ ] Always state the **assumptions** of your model (small-angle, lumped-parameter, no-friction, etc.) — a model without assumptions is incomplete.

---

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: *An Abstract View of Modelling***
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern | PECPT754 — Cognitive Systems and AI in CPS*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elementary Knowledge
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elementary Knowledge

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This module establishes the *elementary vocabulary and mental models* required to study how **Cognitive Systems** (systems that perceive, reason, learn, and decide) and **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** are integrated with **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** — the engineered systems where computation, communication, and physical processes are deeply intertwined.

## 1.1 What is a Cyber-Physical System (CPS)?

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered, networked system-of-systems in which **computational algorithms (the *cyber* part)** are tightly coupled with **physical processes (the *physical* part)** through *sensors*, *actuators*, and *communication networks*. The defining property is **bi-directional feedback**: physical events affect computation, and computation drives physical action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (verbatim from Rajkumar et al., 2010, adopted in 2024 syllabus):**
> *"A cyber-physical system (CPS) is a physical and engineering system whose operations are monitored, coordinated, controlled, and integrated by a computing and communication core."*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body" View
Think of a CPS exactly like a human body:

| Human Body Analogy | Cyber-Physical System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Nervous System** (carries signals) | Communication Network (e.g., CAN, MQTT, 5G) |
| **Brain** (decides and computes) | Embedded Computer / Edge AI Processor |
| **Eyes, Ears, Skin** (perceive) | **Sensors** (temperature, pressure, camera, LiDAR) |
| **Muscles, Hands** (act) | **Actuators** (motors, valves, relays) |
| **Memory & Experience** | Knowledge Base / Trained ML Model |
| **Learning & Adaptation** | Online / Reinforcement Learning Module |

Just as your brain continuously senses the world, decides, and moves your body, a CPS continuously senses the plant, computes decisions, and drives the actuators — this is the **sense–compute–actuate loop**.

## 1.2 What is a Cognitive System?

A **Cognitive System** is a system that exhibits *cognition* — i.e., the capabilities of **perception, learning, reasoning, planning, and decision-making under uncertainty**, typically inspired by human cognition. In a CPS context, "cognition" means the embedded intelligence can:

1. **Perceive** the physical state from noisy/multi-modal sensor data.
2. **Reason** about goals, constraints, and predictions.
3. **Learn** from past experience to improve future behavior.
4. **Act** autonomously and safely in real-time.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Experienced Driver"
A *novice* driver follows rigid rules: "If brake light → push brake." A *cognitive* (experienced) driver *anticipates* a pedestrian, *learns* that morning traffic is heavy, *reasons* that the school zone needs lower speed, and *acts* smoothly. A cognitive CPS does the same — it doesn't just run an `if-else` ladder; it **anticipates, generalizes, and adapts**.

## 1.3 What is AI in CPS?

**AI in CPS** refers to the deployment of **Artificial Intelligence techniques** (machine learning, deep learning, reinforcement learning, symbolic reasoning, computer vision, NLP) **inside or alongside** a Cyber-Physical System to endow it with cognitive capabilities such as:
- Autonomous perception
- Predictive maintenance
- Adaptive control
- Anomaly detection
- Human–machine interaction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core distinction (frequently tested in KTU):**
> - **CPS** = *the body* (sensors + actuators + network + controllers).
> - **AI** = *the brain's algorithm* (perception, learning, decision).
> - **Cognitive CPS** = a CPS that uses AI to act intelligently and adaptively in its physical environment.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants to Remember

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Role in CPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sampling rate | $f_s$ | **Hz** | How often we digitize the physical signal (Nyquist bound) |
| Latency | $\tau$ | **ms** | Time from sensor read to actuator command |
| Period | $T$ | **seconds** | $T = 1/f_s$ |
| Reliability | $R(t)$ | dimensionless, 0–1 | Probability of correct service up to time $t$ |
| Clock frequency | $f_{clk}$ | **MHz / GHz** | Determines real-time feasibility |
| Shannon–Nyquist rate | $f_N$ | **Hz** | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ of the signal |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, these *five italicized words* — **sense, compute, communicate, actuate, learn** — are the canonical pillars of Module 1. Master them; nearly every Part A question pivots on them.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 3-Layer Architecture of a CPS

Every CPS can be decomposed into three tightly coupled layers. This is the single most diagram-tested concept in Module 1.

### Layer 1 — Physical Layer (The "Body")
- The **physical process / plant** (engine, robot arm, power grid, medical device).
- Interacts with the real world through:
  - **Sensors** — transduce physical quantity → electrical signal.
  - **Actuators** — transduce electrical command → physical action.
- Real-time constraints dominate. Failure here is often *catastrophic and irreversible*.

### Layer 2 — Cyber / Computational Layer (The "Brain")
- Embedded processors (MCUs, PLCs, edge AI accelerators like **NVIDIA Jetson**, **Google Coral**).
- Runs:
  - **Control algorithms** (PID, MPC, state machines).
  - **AI/ML models** (CNN, RNN, RL policies).
  - **Middleware / OS** (RTOS, ROS 2, AUTOSAR).
- Concerns: real-time scheduling, determinism, memory, thermal limits.

### Layer 3 — Communication / Network Layer (The "Nervous System")
- Wired (CAN, EtherCAT, TSN, Modbus) or wireless (Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRa, BLE).
- Concerned with **latency**, **jitter**, **bandwidth**, **security**.
- Introduces *time delay* $\tau_{net}$ into the control loop, which affects stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Sense–Compute–Actuate (SCA) Loop:** A CPS is fundamentally a **closed-loop feedback system**. The cycle time $T_{SCA}$ must be *smaller* than the fastest dynamics of the physical process, otherwise the system is unstable or unsafe.

## 2.2 The Five Functional Pillars of an AI-Enabled Cognitive CPS

| # | Pillar | Function | Example AI/CPS Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sense** | Acquire raw physical data | Sensors, ADCs, sensor fusion (Kalman Filter) |
| 2 | **Perceive** | Convert raw data to meaning | CNNs for vision, ASR for audio |
| 3 | **Reason / Plan** | Decide what to do | Symbolic planners, RL policies, MPC |
| 4 | **Act** | Drive the physical world | Motor controllers, PID loops |
| 5 | **Learn** | Improve from experience | Online ML, federated learning, RL |

> [!NOTE]
> **Traditional CPS** uses Pillars 1, 2 (rudimentary), 3 (deterministic), 4. A **Cognitive CPS** adds *Pillar 5 (Learn)* and uses *learning-based AI* in Pillar 2 and 3.

## 2.3 Elementary Signals & Sampling — The Bridge from Physical to Cyber

To make a physical signal usable by AI, we must *digitize* it. The elementary knowledge required is the **sampling theorem**.

Given a continuous-time physical signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$:

**Shannon–Nyquist Sampling Theorem (1928 / 1949):**
A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with highest frequency $f_{max}$ can be *perfectly reconstructed* from its samples if and only if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:

$$f_s \;\ge\; 2 \, f_{max}$$

The minimum allowed rate $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ is the **Nyquist rate**; anything below causes **aliasing** (irreversible information loss).

> [!TIP]
> In a CPS, picking $f_s$ is a *trade-off*: too low → aliasing, too high → wasted compute, energy, and storage. Embedded AI chips usually operate at **kHz range** for control, **Hz to 100 Hz** for vision (with on-chip compression).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Hz |
| 2 | Nyquist criterion | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Bit depth quantization | $L = 2^n$ | levels (n = bits) |
| 4 | Quantization SNR (approx.) | $SNR_{dB} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB |
| 5 | Data rate | $R = f_s \cdot n \cdot c$ | bits/sec (c = channels) |
| 6 | Transport delay latency | $\tau = \tau_{sense} + \tau_{compute} + \tau_{net} + \tau_{act}$ | seconds |
| 7 | Discrete-time control update | $u[k] = K_p\, e[k] + K_i \sum e[j] + K_d (e[k]-e[k-1])$ | PID |
| 8 | Reliability | $R(t) = \mathbb{P}(\text{no failure up to } t)$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours |
| 10 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless, 0–1 |
| 11 | Closed-loop bandwidth (1st order) | $\omega_{bw} \approx \dfrac{1}{\tau_{plant}}$ | rad/s |
| 12 | Energy of discrete signal | $E = \sum_{k} \vert x[k] \vert^{2}$ | joules-units |
| 13 | Bayes' rule (cognitive core) | $P(H \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid H)\, P(H)}{P(D)}$ | probabilities |
| 14 | Shannon channel capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bits/sec |
| 15 | Softmax (AI decision layer) | $P(y_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | probability |

> **Engineering utility:** Formula 1–5 govern **perception design**; 6–7 govern **real-time control**; 8–10 govern **dependability**; 11 governs **stability**; 13–15 govern the **AI reasoning core**. Together they are the toolkit for the entire course.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous Physical World to Discrete AI Input

**Starting point.** A physical sensor produces a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude voltage $x(t)$ proportional to a physical quantity (e.g., temperature, vibration, current). A computer/AI chip can only store *discrete-time, discrete-amplitude* numbers $x[k]$. We must derive the relationship.

**Step 1 — Sampling (continuous → discrete time).**
Multiplication by an ideal impulse train of period $T_s$:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - k T_s)$$

Taking the Fourier transform and using the modulation property:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - n f_s)$$

The spectrum is replicated every $f_s = 1/T_s$ Hz.

**Step 2 — No-aliasing condition.**
To prevent the replicas from overlapping (which would corrupt the signal irreversibly), the shifted copies of $X(f)$ must not touch. If $X(f)$ is band-limited to $f_{max}$:

$$\text{require: } f_s - f_{max} \;\ge\; f_{max}$$

Solving:

$$f_s \;\ge\; 2 f_{max}$$

This is the **Nyquist rate** derived from first principles.

**Step 3 — Quantization (continuous amplitude → discrete amplitude).**
An n-bit ADC maps the continuous range $[-V_{ref}, +V_{ref}]$ into $L = 2^n$ discrete levels. Step size:

$$q = \frac{2 V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n-1}}$$

The quantization error $e_q = x_{quantized} - x_{continuous}$ is modeled as uniform noise in $[-q/2, +q/2]$ with variance $\sigma_q^2 = q^2/12$.

**Step 4 — Quantization SNR.**
For a full-scale sinusoidal input of amplitude $V_{ref}$, signal power $P_s = V_{ref}^2/2$. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
SNR &= \frac{P_s}{P_q} = \frac{V_{ref}^2 / 2}{q^2 / 12} = \frac{V_{ref}^2 / 2}{V_{ref}^2 / (12 \cdot 4^{n-1})} \\
&= \frac{12 \cdot 4^{n-1}}{2} = 6 \cdot 4^{n-1} = 6 \cdot 2^{2n-2}
\end{aligned}$$

Convert to dB:

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(6 \cdot 2^{2n-2}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + (2n-2)\cdot 10 \log_{10}(2)$$

Using $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.176$ and $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.3010$:

$$\begin{aligned}
SNR_{dB} &= 1.76 + (2n-2)(3.01) \\
&= 1.76 + 6.02\,n - 6.02 \\
&= 6.02\,n - 4.26
\end{aligned}$$

For large $n$, the standard textbook approximation is:

$$\boxed{\,SNR_{dB} \;\approx\; 6.02\,n + 1.76\,}$$

This is **Formula #4** in the KTU cheat sheet and explains why *each extra bit* of ADC gives ~6 dB of dynamic range.

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Loop Latency of a Cognitive CPS

**Starting point.** A real-time AI-enabled controller must finish computing an actuator command *before* the physical plant moves too far. Define the **age of information (AoI)** — the time from physical event to actuator response.

**Step 1 — Decompose the loop latency.**
Four sequential contributions:

$$\tau = \tau_{sense} + \tau_{compute} + \tau_{comm} + \tau_{act}$$

Where:
- $\tau_{sense} = $ time for sensor transducing + ADC conversion $\approx 1/f_s$.
- $\tau_{compute} = $ time for the AI/control algorithm to produce $u[k]$, governed by inference latency of the model on the embedded hardware.
- $\tau_{comm} = $ network one-way delay (CAN ~ 1 ms, 5G URLLC ~ 1 ms, Wi-Fi ~ 5–50 ms).
- $\tau_{act} = $ actuator mechanical/electrical response time.

**Step 2 — Stability constraint (heuristic).**
For a plant with dominant time constant $\tau_{plant}$, a classical rule of thumb is:

$$\tau \;\le\; \frac{\tau_{plant}}{10}$$

If $\tau$ exceeds this, the closed-loop system either becomes unstable or sluggish.

**Step 3 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style).**
A drone CPS has:
- $\tau_{sense} = 1\,\text{ms}$ (IMU at 1 kHz),
- $\tau_{compute} = 5\,\text{ms}$ (CNN inference on Jetson Nano),
- $\tau_{comm} = 2\,\text{ms}$ (intra-drone CAN bus),
- $\tau_{act} = 3\,\text{ms}$ (motor ESC ramp).

Compute total latency:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau &= 1 + 5 + 2 + 3 \\
&= 11 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

Maximum allowable $\tau_{plant} \approx 10 \times 11 = 110$ ms — adequate for slow drone dynamics (~1 s) but *unsafe* for a robotic arm doing high-speed assembly (plant constant ~ 20 ms).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing CPS Paradigms

| Property | Traditional Embedded System | Classical CPS | Cognitive CPS (AI-Enabled) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Computation | Fixed program, no learning | Deterministic control (PID, MPC) | Adaptive AI/ML models |
| Perception | Simple threshold sensors | Sensor fusion (Kalman) | Deep multi-modal perception |
| Decision | Look-up table / FSM | Rule-based / optimization | Learning-based (RL, DL) |
| Adaptability | None | Limited (gain scheduling) | High (online/federated learning) |
| Time horizon | Reactive | Predictive | Anticipatory / goal-driven |
| Typical example | Microwave oven | Anti-lock Braking (ABS) | Autonomous vehicle |
| KTU Bloom's level (recall) | L1–L2 | L2–L3 | L3–L5 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Sense–Compute–Actuate (SCA) Loop of a CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plantA[Physical Plant] -- sensor readout --> senseB[Sensor + ADC]
    senseB --> commC[Communication Network]
    commC --> compD[Cyber Computation Layer]
    compD -- control command --> commE[Communication Network]
    commE --> actF[Actuator]
    actF -- physical action --> plantA
    senseB -. raw data .- mlG[ML Perception Module]
    compD -. policy .-> mlG
    mlG -. learned insights .-> compD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (left → right → bottom) is the *closed-loop feedback* common to every CPS.
- The **inner dashed loop** represents the *cognitive layer* (ML/AI) that observes, learns, and feeds knowledge back to the controller.
- The plant is the only *physical* node; everything else is cyber.

## 4.2 Three-Layer CPS Architecture with Cognitive Extension

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[Layer 1 - Physical Layer]
        sens1[Temperature Sensor]
        sens2[Camera]
        sens3[IMU Accelerometer]
        act1[DC Motor]
        act2[Heating Element]
        act3[Hydraulic Valve]
    end

    subgraph L2[Layer 2 - Cyber Computational Layer]
        rt[RTOS Scheduler]
        ctrl[Control Algorithm - PID or MPC]
        ai[AI Model - CNN or RL Policy]
        know[Knowledge Base]
        learn[Online Learning Engine]
    end

    subgraph L3[Layer 3 - Communication Layer]
        can[CAN Bus]
        tsn[Time Sensitive Network]
        wl[Wireless 5G or Wi-Fi]
    end

    sens1 --> can
    sens2 --> wl
    sens3 --> can
    can --> rt
    wl --> rt
    tsn --> rt
    rt --> ctrl
    rt --> ai
    ai --> know
    learn --> ai
    ctrl --> tsn
    ai --> tsn
    tsn --> act1
    tsn --> act2
    can --> act3
```

**How to interpret for KTU exams:**
- Draw three rectangular blocks labeled **Physical**, **Cyber**, **Communication**.
- Connect *sensors* (Physical → Communication → Cyber) and *actuators* (Cyber → Communication → Physical) with **arrows**.
- Mark the **AI block** inside the *Cyber* layer with a *dashed boundary* to indicate it is the cognitive component.

## 4.3 Information-Flow Diagram — From Sensor to AI Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[Raw Physical Signal x of t] --> samp[Sampler - period T sub s]
    samp --> quant[Quantizer - n bits]
    quant --> feat[Feature Extractor]
    feat --> norm[Normalization Layer]
    norm --> cnn[Deep CNN or RNN Model]
    cnn --> cls[Softmax Decision Layer]
    cls --> out[Class Probability Vector P of y given x]
    out --> ctrl[Controller / Planner]
    ctrl --> cmd[Actuator Command u of t]
    cmd --> plant[Physical Plant]
    plant -- feedback --> raw
    cls -. predicted label .- log[Experience Replay Buffer]
    log -. periodic batches .- train[Online Trainer]
    train -. updated weights .- cnn
```

**Cognitive elements** (right side, dashed) are what distinguish a *cognitive* CPS from a *plain* CPS: the model is *not static* — it is *continuously retrained* on new data from the deployed system.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most-Common Mark Loss in Module 1:**
> 1. Confusing **CPS** with **IoT**. *IoT = connected things; CPS = connected + closed-loop control + tight physical coupling.* Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if the difference is not articulated.
> 2. Writing "AI = Machine Learning = Deep Learning." They are *nested* sets, not synonyms. AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL. Cost: ~1 mark.
> 3. Forgetting the **units** of latency ($\tau$), sampling rate ($f_s$), or reliability ($R$). The 2024 scheme *explicitly* tests unit awareness.
> 4. Omitting the **bi-directional** arrow in CPS diagrams. A one-way arrow loses full credit on a 7-mark diagram question.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PECPT754]`
**Define a Cyber-Physical System (CPS). With a neat sketch, explain the sense–compute–actuate loop.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system in which computational algorithms (cyber) are tightly integrated with physical processes through sensors, actuators, and a communication network, forming a closed feedback loop *(1 mark)*.

The **Sense–Compute–Actuate (SCA) loop** operates as follows *(2 marks)*:
1. **Sense** — Physical sensors transduce real-world quantities (temperature, pressure, motion) into electrical signals.
2. **Compute** — The cyber layer (embedded processor / AI chip) processes the data, fuses it, runs control/AI algorithms, and decides an action.
3. **Actuate** — Actuators convert the computed commands back into physical actions (motor torque, valve position, etc.).
4. The loop then **re-senses** the new physical state — completing the feedback.

> A single-line sketch showing *Sensor → Computer → Actuator → Plant → Sensor* (with arrows) is expected. The closed loop is essential.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample]`
**List and briefly explain the five functional pillars of a Cognitive CPS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Sense** *(0.5 m)* — Acquire raw physical data using sensors (IMU, cameras, LiDAR).
2. **Perceive** *(0.5 m)* — Convert raw data into semantic meaning (object detection, speech recognition) using AI.
3. **Reason / Plan** *(0.5 m)* — Decide the next action using planners, RL policies, or model-predictive control.
4. **Act** *(0.5 m)* — Drive the physical world through actuators and low-level controllers.
5. **Learn** *(1.0 m)* — Improve future behavior using online/continual learning from experience — the *defining* pillar of *cognitive* systems.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper]`**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-layer architecture** of a Cyber-Physical System. Identify the role of each layer and name one standard protocol/technology used in each.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A vibration-monitoring CPS samples a machine vibration signal. The signal has a maximum frequency component of **$f_{max} = 1.5$ kHz**. The chosen ADC has **n = 12 bits** and operates on **$c = 4$ parallel channels**. Compute:
1. The minimum Nyquist sampling rate.
2. The quantization SNR in dB.
3. The data rate in bits per second.

---

### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Three-layer architecture of a CPS** *(7 marks total)*:

**Layer 1 — Physical Layer (2 marks):**
Contains the physical process (the *plant*) and the transducers:
- **Sensors** that convert physical quantities (temperature, pressure, displacement) into electrical signals.
- **Actuators** that convert electrical commands into physical action (motors, valves, heaters).
*Example protocol/technology*: IEEE 1451 (smart transducer interface), 4–20 mA current loop, HART.

**Layer 2 — Cyber/Computational Layer (2 marks):**
Embedded processors, edge AI accelerators, and software that:
- Runs real-time control algorithms (PID, MPC).
- Hosts AI/ML inference (CNN, RNN, RL).
- Manages the RTOS, task scheduling, fault handling.
*Example protocol/technology*: ROS 2, AUTOSAR, OSEK/VDX, FreeRTOS, NVIDIA Jetson platform.

**Layer 3 — Communication/Network Layer (2 marks):**
The "nervous system" that ferries data:
- **Wired:** CAN, EtherCAT, TSN, Modbus.
- **Wireless:** Wi-Fi, Bluetooth LE, 5G URLLC, LoRaWAN.
Concerns: latency, jitter, bandwidth, security.

**Neat block diagram (1 mark):** Three stacked rectangles labeled *Physical | Cyber | Communication*, with bi-directional arrows between adjacent layers and a unidirectional feedback loop returning from Physical back to Cyber via Communication.

---

### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

Given: $f_{max} = 1500$ Hz, $n = 12$ bits, $c = 4$ channels.

**1) Minimum Nyquist sampling rate:**

$$f_s^{\min} = 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 1500 = 3000 \text{ Hz} = 3 \text{ kHz}$$

*(2 marks)*

**2) Quantization SNR in dB:**

$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02(12) + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

*(2 marks)*

**3) Data rate in bits per second:**

$$R = f_s \cdot n \cdot c = 3000 \times 12 \times 4 = 144{,}000 \text{ bits/s} = 144 \text{ kbps}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Final boxed answer (1 mark):** $f_s^{\min} = 3$ kHz, $\;SNR = 74$ dB, $\;R = 144$ kbps.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Internal Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper]`**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Differentiate between **CPS, IoT, and Embedded Systems** with at least three criteria. Why is a CPS considered the *superset* of these?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Derive the **quantization SNR formula** $SNR_{dB} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76$ for an *n*-bit ADC. State the standard assumptions used in the derivation.

---

### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Comparison table (6 marks):**

| Criterion | Embedded System | IoT | Cyber-Physical System |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary focus** | Dedicated computation inside a device | Connectivity of "things" to the internet | Tight integration of computation with physical process |
| **Feedback loop** | Usually open-loop or trivial | Mostly open-loop (telemetry only) | Closed-loop, real-time feedback **mandatory** |
| **Real-time constraint** | Soft / hard depending on app | Best-effort; not strict | Hard real-time; deadlines matter for safety |
| **Scale** | Single device | Many distributed nodes | Tightly coupled small-to-medium network |
| **AI / cognition** | Rare | Optional at edge | Often central (perception, learning) |
| **Safety-critical?** | Sometimes | Rarely | Frequently (vehicles, medical, power) |
| **Example** | Microwave controller | Smart watch uploading steps | Autonomous car, surgical robot |

**Why CPS is the superset (1 mark):**
A CPS *contains* an embedded system (the compute platform), *can include* IoT connectivity (for cloud or inter-CPS communication), and *adds* the defining element: **bi-directional, real-time, closed-loop interaction with the physical world**. Hence CPS ⊇ Embedded ∪ IoT.

---

### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Standard assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Input is a **full-scale sinusoid** of peak amplitude $V_{ref}$ (the ADC's reference range).
2. Quantization error $e_q$ is **uniformly distributed** in $[-q/2, +q/2]$, independent of the signal, and **zero-mean**.
3. The signal is "busy" enough that error samples are uncorrelated (treated as white noise).
4. The ADC is ideal (no missing codes, no integral nonlinearity).

**Step-by-step derivation (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Quantization step size for an *n*-bit ADC over $[-V_{ref}, +V_{ref}]$:

$$q = \frac{2 V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n-1}}$$

Step 2 — Variance of uniform quantization noise (zero-mean, range $\pm q/2$):

$$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{q^2}{12}$$

Step 3 — Power of a full-scale sinusoid $x(t) = V_{ref} \sin(2\pi f t)$:

$$P_s = \frac{V_{ref}^2}{2}$$

Step 4 — Ratio:

$$\begin{aligned}
SNR &= \frac{P_s}{P_q} = \frac{V_{ref}^2/2}{q^2/12} = \frac{12\, V_{ref}^2}{2\, q^2} = \frac{6 V_{ref}^2}{q^2} \\
&= \frac{6 V_{ref}^2}{\left( V_{ref}/2^{n-1} \right)^2} = 6 \cdot 2^{2n-2}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 5 — Convert to dB:

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(6 \cdot 2^{2n-2}\right) = 1.76 + 6.02\,n - 6.02 = 6.02\,n - 4.26$$

For $n \ge 4$ the standard textbook form (and the KTU-accepted one) is:

$$\boxed{\,SNR_{dB} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76\,}$$

This shows every additional bit contributes ~6 dB of dynamic range.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Elementary Knowledge**

- **CPS = Cyber + Physical + Network, with a *closed loop*.** Bi-directional arrows only. Open-loop = not a CPS in the strict KTU sense.
- **Sense–Compute–Actuate (SCA) loop** is the canonical CPS pattern; latency $\tau$ must satisfy $\tau \le \tau_{plant}/10$.
- **Three-layer model:** Physical (sensors + actuators + plant) → Communication (CAN, TSN, 5G) → Cyber (controllers, AI, RTOS).
- **Five cognitive pillars:** Sense, Perceive, Reason, Act, **Learn** (the last is what makes it *cognitive*).
- **AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL ⊃ RL.** They are nested, *not* interchangeable.
- **Nyquist:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$. Aliasing is *irreversible* — always use an anti-aliasing filter.
- **Quantization SNR:** $SNR_{dB} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76$. One extra bit ≈ 6 dB more dynamic range.
- **Data rate:** $R = f_s \cdot n \cdot c$ (bits/sec).
- **Total loop latency:** $\tau = \tau_{sense} + \tau_{compute} + \tau_{comm} + \tau_{act}$.
- **Reliability / availability:** $R(t)$ and $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- **Bayes' rule** is the *probabilistic reasoning backbone* of every cognitive system.
- **Cognitive CPS = CPS + Learning component** (online ML, federated learning, RL, or continual learning).
- **Always state units** ($Hz, ms, dB, bits/s$) — a frequent 1-mark saver in KTU valuations.
- **Diagrams must have arrows in both directions** between cyber and physical to score full marks.

> **Closing line to remember:** *A CPS is the **body**; AI is the **brain's algorithm**; a Cognitive CPS is the **body + adaptive brain** that learns from its physical world.*

---

topics.title: Computational Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computational Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Computational Logic** is the discipline that applies the principles of formal symbolic logic to the representation of knowledge and the mechanization of inference in computational systems. In the context of Cognitive Systems, Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), and Artificial Intelligence, it provides the rigorous mathematical foundation for encoding facts, rules, and relationships so that an intelligent agent can derive new knowledge algorithmically.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 1, Cognitive Systems and AI in CPS), computational logic encompasses the following layered hierarchy of formal languages:

1. **Propositional Logic (Boolean Logic / Sentential Logic):** Deals with whole declarative statements (propositions) combined by logical connectives.
2. **First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL / First-Order Logic — FOL):** Extends propositional logic by introducing variables, predicates, functions, and quantifiers.
3. **Higher-Order and Modal Logic:** Extends FOL by allowing quantification over predicates or by introducing operators for necessity, possibility, time, and knowledge.
4. **Inference and Proof Theory:** The algorithmic apparatus (resolution, forward/backward chaining) used to derive conclusions from a knowledge base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Computational Logic is the *engine room* of any cognitive architecture. Without it, a system cannot perform symbolic reasoning, planning, diagnosis, or formal verification of CPS behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary of the Topic (Module 1 Scope):** This module focuses on propositional and first-order logic, syntax vs. semantics, entailment, and basic inference (Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, Resolution, Unification, Forward/Backward Chaining). Probabilistic reasoning and fuzzy logic are covered in later modules.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Judge"

Imagine a courtroom in a futuristic smart city.

- **Facts (Evidence):** These are the *propositions* — small, atomic truths such as *"The traffic light is RED"* or *"Sensor S1 reports a vehicle at the intersection."*
- **Rules (Laws):** These are the *implications* — logical sentences such as *"IF the light is RED, THEN vehicles must stop."*
- **The Judge (Reasoning Engine):** This is the *inference algorithm*. Given the facts and the rules, the judge mechanically arrives at a verdict: *"Therefore, the vehicle must stop."*
- **The Law Book (Knowledge Base):** This is the *formal theory* — a consistent set of axioms and rules that no contradiction can violate.

The judge does not "think" creatively. He follows a strict, deterministic, and *sound* procedure. Computational logic gives that exact procedure to a machine: take a knowledge base (KB), take a query (Q), and mechanically determine whether **KB ⊨ Q** (KB *entails* Q).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A *fact* is something the system knows to be true. A *rule* is something the system knows to *imply*. An *inference* is the act of combining them. The triple **(Fact, Rule, Inference)** is the heartbeat of every expert system, theorem prover, and CPS decision engine.

---

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary Anchors

| Term | Plain-English Meaning | KTU Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Proposition | A declarative statement that is either True or False | $P, Q, R$ |
| Connective | A logical operator joining propositions | $\land, \lor, \neg, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$ |
| Predicate | A function that maps objects to True/False | $Loves(x, y)$ |
| Term | A constant, variable, or function of terms | $x,\ f(x,y)$ |
| Quantifier | A symbol that binds variables | $\forall$ (for all), $\exists$ (there exists) |
| Knowledge Base (KB) | A set of sentences in a formal language | $\Sigma$ |
| Entailment | KB logically implies a sentence | $KB \models \alpha$ |
| Inference | A syntactic derivation step | $KB \vdash \alpha$ |
| Soundness | Whatever is inferred is entailed | $\vdash \subseteq \models$ |
| Completeness | Whatever is entailed can be inferred | $\models \subseteq \vdash$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Distinction to Memorize:** *Entailment* ($\models$) is a **semantic** notion (about truth in all models). *Inference/Deduction* ($\vdash$) is a **syntactic** notion (about provability using rules). The fundamental theorem of logic states that for first-order logic, soundness and completeness hold — they coincide.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of Propositional Logic

A **propositional language** $\mathcal{L}$ is built from three disjoint sets:

- A countable set of **propositional variables** $\mathcal{P} = \{P, Q, R, \ldots\}$
- The set of **logical connectives**: $\neg, \land, \lor, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$
- The set of **punctuation symbols**: parentheses $(, )$

### 2.1.1 Syntax — How to Build Well-Formed Formulas (WFFs)

The set of well-formed formulas is defined **inductively** (BNF grammar):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi \;\;::=\;\; & P \;\mid\; \neg \varphi \;\mid\; (\varphi \land \varphi) \;\mid\; (\varphi \lor \varphi) \\
& \mid\; (\varphi \rightarrow \varphi) \;\mid\; (\varphi \leftrightarrow \varphi)
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Base case:** Any propositional variable $P \in \mathcal{P}$ is a WFF.
- **Recursive case:** If $\varphi$ is a WFF, so is $\neg \varphi$. If $\varphi_1, \varphi_2$ are WFFs, so are $(\varphi_1 \circ \varphi_2)$ for each binary connective $\circ$.

### 2.1.2 Semantics — How to Assign Meaning

A **valuation** (or **interpretation**) is a function:

$$
v : \mathcal{P} \rightarrow \{T, F\}
$$

The truth value of a complex WFF is computed recursively via the **truth tables** of the connectives.

---

## 2.2 Truth Tables of the Five Primitive Connectives

> [!NOTE]
> The five connectives below are the **complete primitive basis** of classical propositional logic. Every other connective (NAND, NOR, XOR, etc.) can be expressed using only these.

| $P$ | $Q$ | $\neg P$ | $P \land Q$ | $P \lor Q$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ | $P \leftrightarrow Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| T | T | F | **T** | **T** | **T** | **T** |
| T | F | F | **F** | **T** | **F** | **F** |
| F | T | T | **F** | **T** | **T** | **F** |
| F | F | T | **F** | **F** | **T** | **T** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $P \rightarrow Q$ is $F$ only when $P=T$ and $Q=F$:** In classical logic, a conditional is **vacuously true** whenever the antecedent is false. This is counterintuitive at first, but it is the *only* definition that makes the deduction theorem work cleanly. KTU examiners frequently test this.

### 2.2.1 Key Derived Connectives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{XOR (Exclusive Or)} &\;\equiv\; (P \lor Q) \land \neg(P \land Q) \\
\text{NAND} &\;\equiv\; \neg(P \land Q) \\
\text{NOR} &\;\equiv\; \neg(P \lor Q) \\
P \rightarrow Q &\;\equiv\; \neg P \lor Q \quad \text{(material implication)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Shortcut:** $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q$. This single identity is used in nearly every problem where you must rewrite a knowledge base into **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** for resolution.

---

## 2.3 First-Order Predicate Logic (FOL / FOPL)

Propositional logic cannot express statements like *"All humans are mortal"* in a single atomic proposition. **First-Order Logic (FOL)** overcomes this by introducing a richer vocabulary.

### 2.3.1 FOL Vocabulary

| Symbol Type | Examples | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Constants | $a, b, \text{John}, 0, 1$ | Name specific objects |
| Variables | $x, y, z$ | Range over objects |
| Functions | $father(x), plus(x,y)$ | Map objects to objects |
| Predicates | $Man(x), Loves(x,y), >$ | Map objects to truth values |
| Connectives | $\neg, \land, \lor, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$ | Boolean combination |
| Quantifiers | $\forall, \exists$ | Bind variables |
| Equality | $=$ | Identity of objects |

### 2.3.2 Syntax of FOL Terms and Formulas

A **term** is built by:

$$
t \;\;::=\;\; c \;\mid\; x \;\mid\; f(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)
$$

An **atomic formula** is a predicate applied to terms: $P(t_1, \ldots, t_n)$ or $t_1 = t_2$.

A **well-formed formula** of FOL is built using connectives and quantifiers.

### 2.3.3 Quantifier Scoping

- $\forall x \, P(x)$ — "For **all** $x$, $P(x)$ holds."
- $\exists x \, P(x)$ — "There **exists** an $x$ such that $P(x)$."
- The **scope** of a quantifier is the smallest formula following it.
- A variable is **bound** if it is inside the scope of a quantifier over it; otherwise it is **free**.

> [!NOTE]
> **De Morgan Duals for Quantifiers (extremely high-yield for KTU):**
> $$\neg \forall x \, P(x) \;\equiv\; \exists x \, \neg P(x)$$
> $$\neg \exists x \, P(x) \;\equiv\; \forall x \, \neg P(x)$$
> Flip the quantifier, push the negation inside.

---

## 2.4 Inference Rules — The Engine of Proof

Inference rules allow us to derive new sentences from existing ones **syntactically**. Each rule preserves truth (soundness).

### 2.4.1 Propositional Inference Rules

| Rule | Schema | Plain English |
|---|---|---|
| **Modus Ponens (MP)** | From $P, \; P \rightarrow Q$, infer $Q$ | Affirming the antecedent |
| **Modus Tollens (MT)** | From $\neg Q, \; P \rightarrow Q$, infer $\neg P$ | Denying the consequent |
| **Hypothetical Syllogism (HS)** | From $P \rightarrow Q, \; Q \rightarrow R$, infer $P \rightarrow R$ | Chaining implications |
| **Disjunctive Syllogism (DS)** | From $P \lor Q, \; \neg P$, infer $Q$ | Eliminating a disjunct |
| **Conjunction (CONJ)** | From $P, \; Q$, infer $P \land Q$ | Joining facts |
| **Simplification (SIMP)** | From $P \land Q$, infer $P$ | Extracting a conjunct |
| **Resolution** | From $P \lor Q, \; \neg P \lor R$, infer $Q \lor R$ | The single rule needed for automated theorem proving |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** **Resolution** is the *only* inference rule you need for an automated theorem prover, provided the knowledge base is in **CNF (Conjunctive Normal Form)**. The entire family of Prolog engines and SAT solvers descends from this single rule.

### 2.4.2 Quantifier-Based Inference Rules

| Rule | Schema | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Universal Elimination (UE)** | From $\forall x \, P(x)$, infer $P(c)$ | Specialize to any constant $c$ |
| **Existential Elimination (EE)** | From $\exists x \, P(x)$, infer $P(c)$ for a *new* Skolem constant $c$ | The constant must be fresh |
| **Existential Introduction (EI)** | From $P(c)$, infer $\exists x \, P(x)$ | Existentialize |
| **Universal Introduction (UI)** | From $P(c)$ for arbitrary $c$, infer $\forall x \, P(x)$ | Only if $c$ is truly arbitrary |

---

## 2.5 Unification and Generalized Modus Ponens

**Unification** is the algorithm that makes FOL inference work. It finds a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions identical.

A substitution $\theta = \{x_1/t_1, x_2/t_2, \ldots\}$ applied to expression $E$ is denoted $E\theta$.

**Most General Unifier (MGU):** The simplest (most general) substitution that unifies two expressions.

### Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)

$$
\frac{p_1', \; p_2', \ldots, p_n', \quad (p_1 \land p_2 \land \cdots \land p_n) \rightarrow q}{q\theta}
$$

where $\theta$ is the MGU such that $p_i' \theta = p_i \theta$ for all $i$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Use Case:** GMP is the workhorse behind **rule-based CPS diagnostics** (e.g., IF temp_sensor(x) > 90°C AND pressure_sensor(x) < 2 bar THEN trigger_shutdown(x)).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Logical Equivalences

| # | Law | Equivalence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Double Negation | $P \equiv \neg\neg P$ |
| 2 | De Morgan (∧) | $\neg(P \land Q) \equiv \neg P \lor \neg Q$ |
| 3 | De Morgan (∨) | $\neg(P \lor Q) \equiv \neg P \land \neg Q$ |
| 4 | Commutative (∧) | $P \land Q \equiv Q \land P$ |
| 5 | Commutative (∨) | $P \lor Q \equiv Q \lor P$ |
| 6 | Associative (∧) | $(P \land Q) \land R \equiv P \land (Q \land R)$ |
| 7 | Associative (∨) | $(P \lor Q) \lor R \equiv P \lor (Q \lor R)$ |
| 8 | Distributive (∧) | $P \land (Q \lor R) \equiv (P \land Q) \lor (P \land R)$ |
| 9 | Distributive (∨) | $P \lor (Q \land R) \equiv (P \lor Q) \land (P \lor R)$ |
| 10 | Absorption | $P \lor (P \land Q) \equiv P$ |
| 11 | Contradiction | $P \land \neg P \equiv \mathbf{F}$ |
| 12 | Tautology | $P \lor \neg P \equiv \mathbf{T}$ |
| 13 | Material Implication | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q$ |
| 14 | Contrapositive | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg Q \rightarrow \neg P$ |
| 15 | Exportation | $(P \land Q) \rightarrow R \equiv P \rightarrow (Q \rightarrow R)$ |
| 16 | Biconditional | $P \leftrightarrow Q \equiv (P \rightarrow Q) \land (Q \rightarrow P)$ |
| 17 | Quantifier De Morgan | $\neg\forall x\, P(x) \equiv \exists x\, \neg P(x)$ |
| 18 | Quantifier De Morgan | $\neg\exists x\, P(x) \equiv \forall x\, \neg P(x)$ |
| 19 | Quantifier Distribution | $\forall x\,(P \land Q) \equiv \forall x\, P \land \forall x\, Q$ |
| 20 | Quantifier Distribution | $\exists x\,(P \lor Q) \equiv \exists x\, P \lor \exists x\, Q$ |
| 21 | Prenex Form | $Q x\, P(x) \land \phi \equiv Q x\, (P(x) \land \phi)$ if $x$ not free in $\phi$ |
| 22 | Skolem Equivalence | $\forall x\, \exists y\, P(x,y) \equiv \forall x\, P(x, f(x))$ |
| 23 | Skolem Equivalence | $\exists x\, \forall y\, P(x,y) \;\not\equiv\; \forall y\, \exists x\, P(x,y)$ |
| 24 | Resolution Step | $(P \lor Q) \land (\neg P \lor R) \models (Q \lor R)$ |
| 25 | Unification | $\text{UNIFY}(P,Q) = \theta$ s.t. $P\theta = Q\theta$ |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of Computational Logic

| Application Domain | Role of Computational Logic |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Rules like `IF (pedestrian_detected AND crossing) THEN brake` are logical implications. |
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Expert systems use FOL rules: `IF (fever ∧ cough ∧ contact_with_patient) THEN suspect(COVID)`. |
| **Formal Verification of CPS** | Model checking (SPIN, NuSMV) verifies that `AG (¬(sensor1_fail ∧ actuator_off))` (always globally not both true). |
| **Smart Grid Control** | Rules decide load shedding: `IF (peak_hour ∧ demand > supply × 0.9) THEN shed_load(non_critical)`. |
| **Software/Hardware Verification** | Hoare Logic: $\{P\} \; C \; \{Q\}$ — pre/post-conditions expressed as logical assertions. |
| **Database Query Optimization** | SQL `WHERE` clauses are essentially propositional formulas optimized via logical equivalences. |
| **Robotics Planning** | STRIPS and PDDL planners reason about preconditions and effects of actions using FOL. |
| **Cybersecurity & Access Control** | RBAC policies: `IF (user.role = "admin" AND time ∈ work_hours) THEN grant_access`. |
