# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Verifying a Tautology Using Truth Tables

**Problem:** Prove that $(P \rightarrow Q) \leftrightarrow (\neg P \lor Q)$ is a **tautology**.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Truth Table

We need $2^n = 2^2 = 4$ rows for two variables $P$ and $Q$.

### Step 2 — Fill in $P$ and $Q$ Columns

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ | $\neg P$ | $\neg P \lor Q$ | $(P \rightarrow Q) \leftrightarrow (\neg P \lor Q)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| T | T | T | F | T | T |
| T | F | F | F | F | T |
| F | T | T | T | T | T |
| F | F | T | T | T | T |

### Step 3 — Interpret the Result

The final column is **T in every row**, hence the formula is a **tautology** (always true, regardless of truth values of $P$ and $Q$).

### Step 4 — Analytical Justification

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \rightarrow Q &\equiv \neg P \lor Q & \text{(Material Implication Law, #13)} \\
\Rightarrow \quad (P \rightarrow Q) \leftrightarrow (\neg P \lor Q) &\equiv \mathbf{T} & \text{(Biconditional of identical formulas)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The two formulas are logically equivalent, so their biconditional is a tautology. This proves the Material Implication Law formally.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Converting to Prenex Normal Form (PNF) and Skolem Form

**Problem:** Convert $\neg \forall x \, \exists y \, (P(x,y) \rightarrow \forall z \, Q(x,z))$ to **Skolem Normal Form**.

### Step 1 — Push Negation Inside (Quantifier De Morgan #17, #18)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\neg \forall x \, \exists y \, (P(x,y) \rightarrow \forall z \, Q(x,z)) &\equiv \exists x \, \neg \exists y \, (P(x,y) \rightarrow \forall z \, Q(x,z)) \\
&\equiv \exists x \, \forall y \, \neg (P(x,y) \rightarrow \forall z \, Q(x,z))
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Eliminate $\rightarrow$ Using Material Implication (#13)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\neg (P(x,y) \rightarrow \forall z \, Q(x,z)) &\equiv \neg (\neg P(x,y) \lor \forall z \, Q(x,z)) \\
&\equiv P(x,y) \land \neg \forall z \, Q(x,z) \quad \text{(De Morgan #2)} \\
&\equiv P(x,y) \land \exists z \, \neg Q(x,z) \quad \text{(Quantifier De Morgan #18)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Back

$$
\exists x \, \forall y \, \exists z \, (P(x,y) \land \neg Q(x,z))
$$

### Step 4 — Move Quantifiers to the Front (Prenex Form)

The matrix $P(x,y) \land \neg Q(x,z)$ contains only $\land$, so we can pull quantifiers out:

$$
\exists x \, \forall y \, \exists z \, (P(x,y) \land \neg Q(x,z))
$$

### Step 5 — Skolemize

Rules for Skolemization:
- $\forall$ followed by a function symbol: the next $\exists$ inside is replaced by a **Skolem function** of the universally quantified variables to its left.
- $\exists$ at the very front (when there is no enclosing $\forall$) is replaced by a **Skolem constant**.

Since $x$ is existentially quantified at the front, replace $x$ with Skolem constant $a$.
Since $z$ is existentially quantified inside $\forall y$, replace $z$ with Skolem function $f(y)$.

### Step 6 — Drop All Universal Quantifiers (Implicit in Skolem Form)

$$
P(a, y) \land \neg Q(a, f(y))
$$

### Step 7 — Convert Matrix to CNF (already in CNF — a single conjunction of two literals)

The Skolem form is already a CNF with two clauses: $\{P(a, y)\}$ and $\{\neg Q(a, f(y))\}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Resolution Proof

**Knowledge Base (KB):**
- Clause 1: $\neg R(x) \lor S(x) \lor T(x) \equiv \forall x, (R(x) \rightarrow S(x) \lor T(x))$
- Clause 2: $R(a)$
- Clause 3: $\neg S(a)$
- Clause 4: $\neg T(a) \lor U(a)$

**Goal (Query):** Prove $U(a)$.

### Step 1 — Standardize Apart (each variable unique per clause)

Clauses are already standardized.

### Step 2 — Negate the Goal

To prove $U(a)$ by refutation, we add $\neg U(a)$ to the KB.

### Step 3 — Apply Resolution Step-by-Step

**Resolution A:** Resolve Clause 4 with the negated goal $\neg U(a)$.

$$
\frac{\neg T(a) \lor U(a) \quad \neg U(a)}{\neg T(a)}
$$

**Resolution B:** Resolve Clause 1 (substituting $x = a$) with Clause 2.

$$
\frac{\neg R(a) \lor S(a) \lor T(a) \quad R(a)}{S(a) \lor T(a)}
$$

**Resolution C:** Resolve the result of Resolution B with Clause 3.

$$
\frac{S(a) \lor T(a) \quad \neg S(a)}{T(a)}
$$

**Resolution D:** Resolve Resolution A with Resolution C.

$$
\frac{\neg T(a) \quad T(a)}{\square} \quad \text{(empty clause / contradiction)}
$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion

The empty clause $\square$ indicates that the negation of the goal is inconsistent with the KB. Therefore:

$$
KB \models U(a)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** In a CPS context, this corresponds to a **diagnostic chain**: *Given sensor readings $R(a), \neg S(a)$, and the rule "If $R(x)$ then $S(x)$ or $T(x)$", and the rule "If not $T(a)$ then $U(a)$", conclude that $U(a)$ must hold.* This is exactly the kind of reasoning a fault-diagnosis expert system performs.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Unification with MGU

**Problem:** Find the Most General Unifier (MGU) of:

- Expression 1: $P(f(x), g(y, z))$
- Expression 2: $P(f(a), g(b, w))$

### Step 1 — Initialize Substitution $\theta = \{\}$

Compare $f(x)$ with $f(a)$: $x$ and $a$ differ, so add $x/a$.
Compare $g(y,z)$ with $g(b,w)$: $y$ and $b$ differ, so add $y/b$; $z$ and $w$ differ, so add $z/w$.

### Step 2 — Apply Substitution

$$
\theta = \{x/a,\; y/b,\; z/w\}
$$

### Step 3 — Verify

$$
P(f(x), g(y,z))\theta = P(f(a), g(b, w)) = P(f(a), g(b, w))\theta \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step 4 — Confirm Minimality

No variable in the substitution can be expressed as a more specific instance; hence $\theta$ is the MGU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** The substitution must be a **substitution instance** (i.e., only replace variables by terms, never replace a constant). A common error is to bind $x$ to $g(y,z)$ when matching $P(x)$ with $P(f(a))$ — this is *not* a valid unification of $P(x)$ and $P(f(a))$ since $x$ is a variable and $f(a)$ is a function term. Wait — actually $x$ *can* be bound to $f(a)$. The real trap is *occurs check failure*: never bind $x$ to a term that contains $x$ itself (e.g., $x/f(x)$).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Computational Logic Decision Flow

| Question to Ask | If YES | If NO |
|---|---|---|
| Does the statement involve objects and relations? | Use FOL (predicates, quantifiers) | Use Propositional Logic |
| Are there variables that range over objects? | Use quantifiers $\forall, \exists$ | Use ground propositional sentences |
| Do you need to *automate* the proof? | Convert KB to CNF + use **Resolution** | Use natural deduction (Modus Ponens etc.) |
| Is the rule base large and shallow (chain of facts)? | Use **Forward Chaining** (data-driven) | Use **Backward Chaining** (goal-driven) |
| Do rules contain variables that must be matched? | Compute **MGU** then apply **GMP** | Direct propositional reasoning |
| Is the query unprovable in finite steps? | Output "CANNOT PROVE" — the system is sound but not complete for undecidable cases | Continue search |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Architecture of a Logical Reasoning System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Domain Knowledge Source] --> B[Lexical and Syntactic Parser]
    B --> C[Knowledge Base Builder]
    C --> D[KB in CNF Form]
    D --> E[Inference Engine]
    Q[User Query Q] --> F[Negation of Query]
    F --> D
    E --> G{Resolution Step}
    G --> H[Intermediate Clauses]
    H --> G
    G --> I{Empty Clause Derived}
    I -->|Yes| J[Q is True KB entails Q]
    I -->|No| K[Continue Search Up to Resource Limit]
    K --> L{Resources Exhausted}
    L -->|Yes| M[CANNOT PROVE Soundly]
    L -->|No| G
```

## 4.2 The Knowledge Base and Inference Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KB[Knowledge Base]
        K1[Fact 1 P]
        K2[Fact 2 Q]
        K3[Rule P implies R]
        K4[Rule Q implies S]
    end
    subgraph ENG[Inference Engine]
        M1[Pattern Matcher]
        M2[Unification Module]
        M3[Resolution Module]
        M4[Clause Store]
    end
    K1 --> M1
    K2 --> M1
    K3 --> M1
    K4 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> M1
    QU[Query alpha] --> M1
    M4 --> RES[Conclusion alpha is true]
```

## 4.3 Forward Chaining vs Backward Chaining

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FC[Forward Chaining Data Driven]
        FC1[Known Facts] --> FC2[Match Rule Premises]
        FC2 --> FC3{Fire Rule}
        FC3 -->|Yes| FC4[Add Conclusion to Fact Base]
        FC4 --> FC2
        FC3 -->|No Match| FC5[No New Fact Fixed Point Reached]
    end
    subgraph BC[Backward Chaining Goal Driven]
        BC1[Goal to Prove] --> BC2[Find Rule with Conclusion Matching Goal]
        BC2 --> BC3{Subgoals Premises}
        BC3 -->|Yes| BC4[Recursively Prove Subgoals]
        BC4 --> BC3
        BC3 -->|All Subgoals Proven| BC5[Goal Established]
        BC4 -->|Subgoal Fails| BC6[Try Next Matching Rule]
        BC6 --> BC2
    end
```

## 4.4 Hierarchy of Logical Languages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Propositional Logic PL0 Boolean Logic] --> P1[First Order Logic FOL Quantifiers over objects]
    P1 --> P2[Second Order Logic Quantifiers over predicates]
    P2 --> P3[Higher Order Logic Type Theory]
    P1 --> M1[Modal Logic Necessity and Possibility]
    P1 --> M2[Temporal Logic LTL CTL Operators over time]
    P1 --> M3[Description Logic Used in Ontologies OWL]
    M2 --> CPS[Cyber Physical System Verification]
    M3 --> SEM[Semantic Web and Knowledge Graphs]
```

## 4.5 Propositional Resolution — Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start with KB in CNF] --> S1[Add Negation of Query]
    S1 --> S2[Initialize Clause Set with KB Clauses]
    S2 --> S3{Any New Resolvent Possible}
    S3 -->|No| S4[Return FAIL Cannot Prove]
    S3 -->|Yes| S5[Pick Two Clauses with Complementary Literal]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Resolvent]
    S6 --> S7{Resolvent is Empty Clause}
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Return SUCCESS Query Entailed]
    S7 -->|No| S9[Add Resolvent to Clause Set if New]
    S9 --> S3
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define *Computational Logic* and state its significance in Cognitive Cyber-Physical Systems.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Computational Logic is the formal discipline that applies the principles of symbolic logic to enable machines to represent knowledge and perform mechanical reasoning. It comprises propositional logic, first-order predicate logic, and the associated inference machinery (resolution, unification, chaining).

**Significance in Cognitive CPS:**
1. **Symbolic Reasoning** — CPS agents must reason about sensor states, actuator commands, and environmental rules using well-defined logical sentences.
2. **Formal Verification** — Safety properties of CPS (e.g., *"the train never enters a crossing while the gate is open"*) are expressed as temporal logic formulas and verified automatically.
3. **Explainability** — Unlike neural networks, logical derivations provide traceable, human-readable justifications — critical for safety-critical CPS.
4. **Knowledge Integration** — Ontologies (description logic) allow heterogeneous CPS components to share a common semantic vocabulary.
5. **Automated Planning and Diagnosis** — Logical inference underpins rule-based expert systems used in industrial fault diagnosis.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between *Soundness* and *Completeness* of an inference system. State the fundamental theorem of logic for first-order logic.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Formal Definition | Plain Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Soundness** | If $KB \vdash \alpha$, then $KB \models \alpha$ | Every sentence *proven* by the inference rules is *actually true* in every model of the KB. The system never lies. |
| **Completeness** | If $KB \models \alpha$, then $KB \vdash \alpha$ | Every sentence that is *actually true* in every model of the KB can be *proven* in finite steps. The system eventually finds every truth. |

**Fundamental Theorem of First-Order Logic (Gödel, 1930):** A proof system for FOL is both **sound** ($\vdash \subseteq \models$) and **complete** ($\models \subseteq \vdash$). Equivalently, for any sentence $\alpha$ and KB:

$$
KB \vdash \alpha \quad \iff \quad KB \models \alpha
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often confuse the *direction* of soundness and completeness. Remember: **Soundness** restricts the inference system to only derive *true* things. **Completeness** guarantees the system can derive *all* true things. Loss of 1 mark is common on this question.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Resolution Theorem Proving in FOL

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1, 14 Marks]`

**a) Convert the following first-order logic sentence into Prenex Normal Form (PNF) and then into Skolem Normal Form. (7 Marks)**

$$
\forall x \, \big( P(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, (\neg Q(x,y) \land R(x,f(y))) \big)
$$

**b) Given the knowledge base below, use Resolution to prove the query $W(John)$. (7 Marks)**

- $\forall x \, \big(M(x) \rightarrow \neg P(x)\big)$
- $\forall x \, \big(S(x) \rightarrow P(x)\big)$
- $M(John)$
- $S(John) \rightarrow W(John)$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Eliminate the implication $\rightarrow$** using Material Implication:

$$
\forall x \, \big( \neg P(x) \lor \exists y \, (\neg Q(x,y) \land R(x,f(y))) \big)
$$

**Step 2 — Move the existential quantifier inside the disjunction** by Skolemizing the matrix *before* pulling quantifiers. First, Skolemize $\exists y$ inside the scope of $\forall x$: replace $y$ with Skolem function $g(x)$:

$$
\forall x \, \big( \neg P(x) \lor (\neg Q(x, g(x)) \land R(x, f(g(x)))) \big)
$$

**Step 3 — Distribute $\lor$ over $\land$** to obtain CNF:

$$
\forall x \, \big( (\neg P(x) \lor \neg Q(x, g(x))) \land (\neg P(x) \lor R(x, f(g(x)))) \big)
$$

**Step 4 — Pull out the universal quantifier** (Prenex form):

$$
\forall x \, \big( (\neg P(x) \lor \neg Q(x, g(x))) \land (\neg P(x) \lor R(x, f(g(x)))) \big)
$$

**Step 5 — Drop the universal quantifier** (implicit in Skolem Normal Form). The set of clauses is:

$$
\{ \neg P(x) \lor \neg Q(x, g(x)), \;\; \neg P(x) \lor R(x, f(g(x))) \}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Convert each sentence to CNF:**

| Sentence | CNF Form |
|---|---|
| $\forall x\, (M(x) \rightarrow \neg P(x))$ | $\neg M(x) \lor \neg P(x)$ |
| $\forall x\, (S(x) \rightarrow P(x))$ | $\neg S(x) \lor P(x)$ |
| $M(John)$ | $M(John)$ |
| $S(John) \rightarrow W(John)$ | $\neg S(John) \lor W(John)$ |

**Step 2 — Negate the goal:** add $\neg W(John)$ to the clause set.

**Step 3 — Perform resolution steps:**

**Resolution 1:** Resolve $M(John)$ with $\neg M(x) \lor \neg P(x)$, substituting $x = John$:

$$
\frac{M(John) \quad \neg M(John) \lor \neg P(John)}{\neg P(John)}
$$

**Resolution 2:** Resolve $\neg S(x) \lor P(x)$ (with $x = John$) with the resolvent $\neg P(John)$:

$$
\frac{\neg S(John) \lor P(John) \quad \neg P(John)}{\neg S(John)}
$$

**Resolution 3:** Resolve $\neg S(John) \lor W(John)$ with the resolvent $\neg S(John)$:

$$
\frac{\neg S(John) \lor W(John) \quad \neg S(John)}{W(John)}
$$

**Resolution 4:** Resolve $W(John)$ with the negated goal $\neg W(John)$:

$$
\frac{W(John) \quad \neg W(John)}{\square}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The empty clause is derived; therefore the KB entails $W(John)$.

---

### Question B: Inference Strategies and Unification

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1, 14 Marks]`

**a) Explain Forward Chaining and Backward Chaining algorithms. Compare their data-flow direction, use-cases, and termination behavior. (7 Marks)**

**b) Find the Most General Unifier (MGU) of the following two FOL expressions. Show each step of the unification algorithm. (7 Marks)**

- $E_1 = Knows(John, x), \; On(x, y)$
- $E_2 = Knows(y, Mary), \; On(Mary, Book)$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Forward Chaining (Data-Driven Reasoning):**

- Starts with the **known facts** in the knowledge base.
- Repeatedly fires any rule whose premises are satisfied by the current fact base.
- Adds the rule's conclusion to the fact base.
- Terminates when no new fact can be added (**fixed point reached**).

**Algorithm sketch:**
1. Initialize fact base $F$ with initial facts.
2. Repeat until $F$ stops growing:
   - For each rule $p_1 \land \cdots \land p_n \rightarrow q$:
     - If all $p_i$ are in $F$, add $q$ to $F$.
3. Return $F$.

**Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven Reasoning):**

- Starts with the **goal** to be proved.
- Searches for a rule whose conclusion unifies with the goal.
- Recursively attempts to prove each of the rule's premises as new subgoals.
- Terminates when all subgoals are proved (success) or all matching rules are exhausted (failure).

**Algorithm sketch:**
1. Function BC(query $q$):
   - For each rule $p_1 \land \cdots \land p_n \rightarrow r$ where MGU $\theta$ makes $r\theta = q$:
     - Recursively call BC on each $p_i\theta$.
     - If all succeed, return success with combined $\theta$.
2. If no rule matches, return failure.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Data → Conclusions | Goal → Supporting Data |
| Best for | Monitoring, situation assessment, CPS sensor fusion | Query answering, diagnosis, theorem proving |
| Computational shape | Breadth-first over facts | Depth-first over subgoals |
| Termination | Always (monotone) | May loop without cycle detection |
| Inferences produced | All derivable facts | Only those relevant to the goal |
| Typical implementation | Production systems (CLIPS, Jess) | Logic programming (Prolog) |

> [!TIP]
> **CPS Use-Case Mapping:** Use **Forward Chaining** in real-time sensor-monitoring systems where every derivable fact (e.g., alarms, predictions) must be propagated downstream. Use **Backward Chaining** in diagnostic systems where you start from an observed fault and ask "what rule could have produced this?"

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:**

- $E_1 = Knows(John, x), \; On(x, y)$
- $E_2 = Knows(y, Mary), \; On(Mary, Book)$

**Step 1 — Initialize substitution** $\theta_0 = \{\}$.

**Step 2 — Unify $Knows(John, x)$ with $Knows(y, Mary)$:**

- Predicate symbols match: $Knows = Knows$ ✓
- Compare arguments: $John$ vs $y$, then $x$ vs $Mary$.
- $John$ is a constant, $y$ is a variable, $John \neq Mary$. Substitute $y = John$.
- $x$ is a variable, $Mary$ is a constant. Substitute $x = Mary$.

Apply: $\theta_1 = \{y/John,\; x/Mary\}$.

**Step 3 — Apply $\theta_1$ to both expressions** and unify the second pair:

- $E_1\theta_1 = Knows(John, Mary), \; On(Mary, y\theta_1) = On(Mary, John)$
- $E_2\theta_1 = Knows(John, Mary), \; On(Mary, Book)$

**Step 4 — Unify $On(Mary, John)$ with $On(Mary, Book)$:**

- Predicate symbols match: $On = On$ ✓
- Compare arguments: $Mary$ vs $Mary$ ✓ (same constant).
- $John$ vs $Book$: both constants, $John \neq Book$. **UNIFICATION FAILS**.

**Step 5 — Backtrack** to Step 2. Re-examine the bindings. There is no alternative binding (constants are fixed). Hence the two expressions are **not unifiable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The set $E_1$ and $E_2$ has **no unifier** because the constant $John$ in the first argument position conflicts with the constant $Mary$ in the corresponding position of $E_2$, and no substitution can reconcile them.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to **apply the current substitution** before processing the next literal — leading to incorrect bindings.
> 2. Binding a variable to a term containing itself (*occurs check* violation). Modern Prolog systems skip occurs check for efficiency but it is logically unsound.
> 3. **Confusing unification with pattern matching** — unification is symmetric and bidirectional, while pattern matching is directional.
> 4. Students often forget to **backtrack** when a unification step fails. Always indicate backtracking explicitly in exam answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Computational Logic**

- [ ] **Propositional Logic** is built from propositional variables and five primitive connectives: $\neg, \land, \lor, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$. Every connective has a fixed truth table.
- [ ] **Material Implication** identity: $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q$. This is the *first* equivalence to memorize for CNF conversion.
- [ ] **First-Order Logic (FOL)** adds constants, variables, functions, predicates, and the quantifiers $\forall$ (universal) and $\exists$ (existential).
- [ ] **Quantifier De Morgan** rules: $\neg \forall \equiv \exists \neg$ and $\neg \exists \equiv \forall \neg$. Flip the quantifier, push the negation inward.
- [ ] **Skolemization** replaces $\exists$ with Skolem constants (when no enclosing $\forall$) or Skolem functions (when inside a $\forall$).
- [ ] **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** is a conjunction of disjunctions of literals — the *required input* for resolution.
- [ ] **Resolution** is the single, complete inference rule for propositional and FOL automated theorem proving. Sound + complete for FOL.
- [ ] **Modus Ponens**: $P, \; P \rightarrow Q \;\vdash\; Q$. The workhorse of forward chaining.
- [ ] **Modus Tollens**: $\neg Q, \; P \rightarrow Q \;\vdash\; \neg P$. The workhorse of backward chaining and contrapositive reasoning.
- [ ] **Unification** finds the substitution that makes two expressions identical. Always perform the **occurs check** to avoid infinite terms.
- [ ] **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest unifier — it contains no redundant bindings.
- [ ] **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** is the variable-bearing form of Modus Ponens, used in rule-based AI systems.
- [ ] **Forward Chaining** is data-driven, monotone, breadth-first; ideal for real-time CPS monitoring.
- [ ] **Backward Chaining** is goal-driven, depth-first; ideal for query answering and Prolog-style systems.
- [ ] **Soundness**: $\vdash \subseteq \models$ (everything derivable is true). **Completeness**: $\models \subseteq \vdash$ (everything true is derivable).
- [ ] **Entailment** ($KB \models \alpha$) is semantic. **Inference** ($KB \vdash \alpha$) is syntactic. They coincide in FOL (Gödel's theorem).
- [ ] **Resolution Refutation**: to prove $KB \models \alpha$, add $\neg \alpha$ to KB and derive the empty clause $\square$.
- [ ] **Real-world CPS impact**: temporal logic (LTL, CTL) extends propositional logic for *time-bounded* safety properties — the heart of model checking.
- [ ] **Description Logic** (a fragment of FOL) is the formal basis of **ontologies** and the **Semantic Web** (OWL, RDF).
- [ ] **Conversion pipeline**: Natural language $\rightarrow$ FOL $\rightarrow$ Prenex Normal Form $\rightarrow$ Skolem Normal Form $\rightarrow$ CNF $\rightarrow$ Resolution.
- [ ] **Counterfactual and abductive reasoning** (diagnostic reasoning) build on top of FOL resolution by inferring *likely causes* from *observed effects*.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Exam Wisdom:** *Propositional logic reasons about **facts**; first-order logic reasons about **objects and their relationships**; resolution and unification are the **mechanical muscles** that make both kinds of reasoning automatic.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Compound Statements Using Simple Logic Connectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Compound Statements Using Simple Logic Connectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Compound Statements

In the discipline of **Mathematical Logic** and **Propositional Calculus**, a *statement* (or *proposition*) is a declarative sentence that is unambiguously either **True (T)** or **False (F)**, but never both and never neither. A statement that cannot be broken down into simpler statements is called an **atomic statement** (or *simple statement*). When two or more atomic statements are joined together using special symbols called **logical connectives** (also termed *logical operators* or *propositional connectives*), the resulting complex sentence is termed a **Compound Statement** (or *molecular proposition*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A *compound statement* is a logical combination of one or more simple statements joined by connectives such that the overall truth value of the compound is uniquely determined by the truth values of its constituent simple statements and the nature of the connecting operator.

Formally, if $P$, $Q$, and $R$ are atomic propositions and $\circ$ is any connective, then any expression of the form $P \circ Q$, $P \circ Q \circ R$, or $\neg P$ is a compound statement.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Logic as Sentence Building"

Imagine **propositional logic** as a *cooking recipe*. Each simple statement is a raw ingredient (salt, sugar, flour). The **logical connectives** are the cooking operations (mix, boil, fry, bake). A **compound statement** is the final dish — its taste (truth value) depends entirely on the quality of the ingredients (truth of $P, Q$) and the recipe (connective). Just as adding salt to sugar creates something entirely new, joining $P$ and $Q$ with $\land$ creates a new proposition whose truth depends on *both* atoms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Cognitive Systems & AI:** Every rule in an **Expert System**, every **Boolean condition** in a search algorithm, every **inference rule** in a Knowledge Base, and every **activation function** logic in a neural network ultimately reduces to compound statements evaluated using these very connectives. Without mastering them, the higher-order cognitive modeling in this course (semantic networks, frames, predicate logic) cannot be understood.

### 1.3 The Five Canonical Simple Connectives

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes **five fundamental connectives** (often called *simple logic connectives*):

| Symbol | Name | English Phrasing |
|:------:|:-----|:-----------------|
| $\neg$ | Negation (NOT) | "It is not the case that…" |
| $\wedge$ | Conjunction (AND) | "Both $P$ and $Q$" |
| $\vee$ | Disjunction (OR) | "$P$ or $Q$ (or both)" |
| $\rightarrow$ | Implication (IF-THEN) | "If $P$, then $Q$" |
| $\leftrightarrow$ | Biconditional (IFF) | "$P$ if and only if $Q$" |

The first three ($\neg, \wedge, \vee$) are called the **Boolean connectives**, and $\{\neg, \wedge, \vee\}$ is functionally complete. The remaining two are *derived* from these in any formal proof system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Connective

The analysis of any compound statement requires us to evaluate it under **all possible truth assignments** of its atomic components. Below is a structured logical breakdown of how each connective transforms input truth values into output truth values.

#### A. Negation ($\neg P$)
- **Logic:** A unary operator acting on a single proposition.
- **Rule:** The negation inverts the truth value. If $P$ is True, then $\neg P$ is False, and vice versa.
- **Why it works:** Negation captures the logical operation of *denial*, which is fundamental to contradiction detection in AI inference engines.

#### B. Conjunction ($P \wedge Q$)
- **Logic:** A binary operator. The compound is True **only when both** $P$ AND $Q$ are True simultaneously.
- **Engineering Utility:** Maps directly to the **AND gate** in digital circuits; used in **rule firing conditions** in production systems.

#### C. Disjunction ($P \vee Q$)
- **Logic:** A binary operator. The compound is True if **at least one** of $P$ or $Q$ is True (inclusive OR).
- **Engineering Utility:** Corresponds to the **OR gate**; used in **fault-tolerant diagnostics** (system passes if any sensor is healthy).

#### D. Implication ($P \rightarrow Q$)
- **Logic:** Read as "If $P$, then $Q$". $P$ is the *hypothesis/antecedent*, $Q$ is the *conclusion/consequent*.
- **Crucial Rule:** $P \rightarrow Q$ is **False only** in the single case where $P$ is True and $Q$ is False. In all other three cases, it is True.
- **Common Student Pitfall:** Students often mistakenly assign False to $P \rightarrow Q$ when $P$ is False. This is wrong — the implication is *vacuously true* when the antecedent is false.

#### E. Biconditional ($P \leftrightarrow Q$)
- **Logic:** True exactly when $P$ and $Q$ share the **same truth value** (both True or both False).
- **Algebraic Identity:** $P \leftrightarrow Q \equiv (P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (Q \rightarrow P)$.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for any KTU examination question on this topic. Memorize the truth values and identities.

| Connective / Identity | Symbolic Form | Truth Condition | Engineering Application |
|:----------------------|:--------------|:----------------|:------------------------|
| **Negation** | $\neg P$ | Opposite of $P$ | Inverter circuit, NOT gate |
| **Conjunction** | $P \wedge Q$ | True iff $P = T$ and $Q = T$ | AND gate, concurrent task check |
| **Disjunction (Inclusive)** | $P \vee Q$ | True iff $P = T$ or $Q = T$ (or both) | OR gate, redundant sensor check |
| **Implication** | $P \rightarrow Q$ | False only when $P = T, Q = F$ | Rule-based inference, preconditions |
| **Biconditional** | $P \leftrightarrow Q$ | True iff $P$ and $Q$ have same truth value | Equivalence testing, bidirectional rules |
| **Double Negation** | $\neg(\neg P) \equiv P$ | Always | Logic simplification |
| **De Morgan's Law 1** | $\neg(P \wedge Q) \equiv \neg P \vee \neg Q$ | Logical equivalence | NAND gate realization |
| **De Morgan's Law 2** | $\neg(P \vee Q) \equiv \neg P \wedge \neg Q$ | Logical equivalence | NOR gate realization |
| **Implication Expansion** | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q$ | Logical equivalence | Conditional logic compilation |
| **Biconditional Expansion** | $P \leftrightarrow Q \equiv (P \wedge Q) \vee (\neg P \wedge \neg Q)$ | Logical equivalence | XNOR gate realization |
| **Contradiction** | $P \wedge \neg P$ | Always False | Unsatisfiable condition |
| **Tautology** | $P \vee \neg P$ | Always True | Always-satisfiable rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule Used Above:** Note that absolute-value or set-membership expressions inside formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. For example, the truth condition would be written as $P \vert Q$ meaning "P given Q" — never with raw pipe characters that would break the table.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Cognitive AI

In the broader landscape of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** and **Cognitive AI**, the analysis of compound statements underpins:

- **Knowledge Representation:** Production rules in CLIPS, Jess, and Drools are compound statements of the form `IF (P ∧ Q) THEN R`.
- **Automated Theorem Proving:** Propositional resolution rules depend on the analysis of negated clauses.
- **Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) Solvers:** Used in hardware verification, AI planning, and model checking.
- **Fuzzy Cognitive Maps:** When extended, the basic $\land, \lor, \neg$ become $t$-norms, $t$-conorms, and negators.
- **Digital Logic Design:** Every multiplexed condition in an embedded controller is a compound statement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Truth Table Construction for $(P \wedge Q) \rightarrow R$

A **truth table** is the systematic KTU-mandated technique for analyzing any compound statement. It enumerates **all $2^n$ possible truth combinations** of $n$ atomic propositions and computes the resulting truth value column-by-column. The following is a fully worked-out derivation for a three-variable compound statement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Procedure (Memorize this KTU Examiner Checklist):**
> 1. Identify the number of atomic propositions $n$. The table will have $2^n$ rows.
> 2. List all $2^n$ binary combinations in standard order (usually starting with all False).
> 3. Compute intermediate sub-expressions column-by-column from the inside out.
> 4. Final column gives the truth value of the entire compound statement.

For the compound statement $(P \wedge Q) \rightarrow R$, we have $n = 3$, so we need $2^3 = 8$ rows.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Enumerate all 8 atomic truth combinations} \\
(P, Q, R) \in &\{ (F,F,F), (F,F,T), (F,T,F), (F,T,T), \\
&(T,F,F), (T,F,T), (T,T,F), (T,T,T) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Compute the inner sub-expression } P \wedge Q \text{ for each row.} \\
P \wedge Q \text{ is True ONLY when both } P = T \text{ AND } Q = T.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Apply the Implication Rule: } (P \wedge Q) \rightarrow R \text{ is False ONLY} \\
\text{when the antecedent } (P \wedge Q) = T \text{ AND consequent } R = F.
\end{aligned}
$$

| $P$ | $Q$ | $R$ | $P \wedge Q$ | $(P \wedge Q) \rightarrow R$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:------------:|:----------------------------:|
| F   | F   | F   | F            | **T**                        |
| F   | F   | T   | F            | **T**                        |
| F   | T   | F   | F            | **T**                        |
| F   | T   | T   | F            | **T**                        |
| T   | F   | F   | F            | **T**                        |
| T   | F   | T   | F            | **T**                        |
| T   | T   | F   | T            | **F**                        |
| T   | T   | T   | T            | **T**                        |

**Conclusion:** The final column contains both T and F, so $(P \wedge Q) \rightarrow R$ is a **Contingency** (i.e., it is neither a tautology nor a contradiction; its truth value depends on the inputs).

### 3.2 Derivation of De Morgan's Law $\neg(P \wedge Q) \equiv \neg P \vee \neg Q$ via Truth Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Favorite Question:** "Prove De Morgan's Law using a truth table." Below is the full step-by-step working.

The proof technique is to show that the **final column of $\neg(P \wedge Q)$** is **identical** to the **final column of $\neg P \vee \neg Q$** for every input combination. If the two columns match, the statements are logically equivalent.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Construct table for } n = 2, \text{ so } 2^2 = 4 \text{ rows.} \\
\text{Step 2: Fill } P, Q \text{ columns. Step 3: Compute } P \wedge Q. \\
\text{Step 4: Compute } \neg(P \wedge Q). \text{ Step 5: Compute } \neg P \text{ and } \neg Q. \\
\text{Step 6: Compute } \neg P \vee \neg Q. \text{ Step 7: Compare the two final columns.}
\end{aligned}
$$

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \wedge Q$ | $\neg(P \wedge Q)$ | $\neg P$ | $\neg Q$ | $\neg P \vee \neg Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:------------:|:-------------------:|:--------:|:--------:|:--------------------:|
| F   | F   | F            | **T**               | T        | T        | **T**                |
| F   | T   | F            | **T**               | T        | F        | **T**                |
| T   | F   | F            | **T**               | F        | T        | **T**                |
| T   | T   | T            | **F**               | F        | F        | **F**                |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Comparison: Columns 4 and 7 are identical for all 4 rows.} \\
\therefore \neg(P \wedge Q) \equiv \neg P \vee \neg Q \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Implication Equivalence $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the single most useful equivalence in AI rule engines, as it converts an *implication* into a *disjunction* suitable for resolution-based provers.

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ | $\neg P$ | $\neg P \vee Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:-----------------:|:--------:|:---------------:|
| F   | F   | **T**             | T        | **T**           |
| F   | T   | **T**             | T        | **T**           |
| T   | F   | **F**             | F        | **F**           |
| T   | T   | **T**             | F        | **T**           |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Columns 3 and 5 are identical for all 4 rows.} \\
\therefore P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Classification of Compound Statements — The Analytical Matrix

Every compound statement $S$ must be classified into one of three categories based on the contents of its final truth-table column:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If final column is all T} &\Rightarrow S \text{ is a TAUTOLOGY (logically valid).} \\
\text{If final column is all F} &\Rightarrow S \text{ is a CONTRADICTION (unsatisfiable).} \\
\text{If final column is mixed} &\Rightarrow S \text{ is a CONTINGENCY (depends on inputs).}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Statement | Final Column Pattern | Classification | AI Interpretation |
|:----------|:---------------------|:---------------|:------------------|
| $P \vee \neg P$ | All T | Tautology | Law of Excluded Middle |
| $P \wedge \neg P$ | All F | Contradiction | Logical impossibility |
| $P \rightarrow P$ | All T | Tautology | Self-implication |
| $P \leftrightarrow P$ | All T | Tautology | Reflexive equivalence |
| $P \oplus Q$ (exclusive OR) | T,F,F,T | Contingency | Mismatch detector |

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol $\oplus$ denotes *exclusive OR*. KTU students should remember: $P \oplus Q \equiv (P \vee Q) \wedge \neg(P \wedge Q)$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Analysis Pipeline for Compound Statements

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **operational workflow** that an AI reasoning engine (or a student) must follow when analyzing a compound statement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step 1: Identify Atomic Propositions P Q R] --> nodeB[Step 2: Count n and Create 2 to the n Rows]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3: List All Truth Value Combinations]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 4: Add Intermediate Columns for Sub-Expressions]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 5: Evaluate Innermost Connective First]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 6: Propagate Truth Values Outward to Root]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Step 7: Examine Final Column}
    nodeG -->|All True| nodeH[Output: TAUTOLOGY - Logically Valid]
    nodeG -->|All False| nodeI[Output: CONTRADICTION - Unsatisfiable]
    nodeG -->|Mixed Values| nodeJ[Output: CONTINGENCY - Input Dependent]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Step 8: Compare With Target Statement for Equivalence]
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Step 9: Declare Logical Equivalence if Columns Match]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Decision Tree — Connective Identification and Truth Evaluation

The following diagram maps the **connective recognition logic** used in the syntactic analysis phase of propositional logic parsing, equivalent to a lexical scanner in a compiler for logical expressions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Input Token Stream] --> check1{Is Token Unary NOT?}
    check1 -->|Yes| unary[Apply Negation Invert Truth Value]
    check1 -->|No| check2{Is Token AND Symbol?}
    check2 -->|Yes| andOp[Output True Only if Both Operands Are True]
    check2 -->|No| check3{Is Token OR Symbol?}
    check3 -->|Yes| orOp[Output True if At Least One Operand Is True]
    check3 -->|No| check4{Is Token Implies Arrow?}
    check4 -->|Yes| impl[Output False Only if Antecedent True and Consequent False]
    check4 -->|No| check5{Is Token Biconditional IFF?}
    check5 -->|Yes| iffOp[Output True if Both Operands Have Identical Truth Value]
    check5 -->|No| error[Syntax Error: Unknown Connective]
    unary --> result[Generate Truth Table Cell]
    andOp --> result
    orOp --> result
    impl --> result
    iffOp --> result
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Connective Hierarchy in Cognitive Systems

The diagram below maps the **functional relationship** between simple connectives, compound statements, and the higher-level cognitive AI constructs that depend on them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    atom1[Atomic P] --> c1
    atom2[Atomic Q] --> c1
    atom3[Atomic R] --> c1
    c1[Simple Connectives NOT AND OR IMPLIES IFF] --> c2[Compound Statements]
    c2 --> c3[Tautologies and Contradictions]
    c2 --> c4[Logical Equivalences]
    c3 --> c5[Inference Rules in Expert Systems]
    c4 --> c5
    c5 --> c6[Production Rules in Knowledge Bases]
    c6 --> c7[Cognitive AI Decision Engine]
    c7 --> c8[Autonomous Action Selection in CPS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Architecture:** This block diagram is the recommended mental model for the entire PECPT754 course. The base layer is the simple connectives, the middle layer is compound statement analysis, and the top layer is the cognitive AI application. Every subsequent module (predicate logic, semantic networks, frames, neural-symbolic integration) sits on top of the analytical foundation laid in Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define a compound statement. List any three simple logic connectives with an example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **compound statement** is a complex proposition formed by combining one or more atomic propositions using logical connectives. The overall truth value of the compound statement is uniquely determined by the truth values of its constituent propositions and the nature of the connecting operator. (1.5 Marks)

The three simple logic connectives are: (1.5 Marks)

- **Negation (NOT), denoted $\neg$:** If $P$ = "It is raining", then $\neg P$ = "It is not raining".
- **Conjunction (AND), denoted $\wedge$:** $P \wedge Q$ = "It is raining AND it is cold".
- **Disjunction (OR), denoted $\vee$:** $P \vee Q$ = "It is raining OR it is snowing".

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **What is a tautology? Give one example and justify it using a truth table.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **tautology** is a compound statement whose truth value is **True (T) for every possible assignment** of truth values to its atomic components. (1 Mark)

**Example:** $P \vee \neg P$ (Law of Excluded Middle). (0.5 Marks)

| $P$ | $\neg P$ | $P \vee \neg P$ |
|:---:|:--------:|:---------------:|
| F   | T        | **T**           |
| T   | F        | **T**           |

The final column is all T, hence $P \vee \neg P$ is a tautology. (1.5 Marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Construct the complete truth table for the compound statement $(P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (Q \rightarrow P)$. Classify the resulting statement as a tautology, contradiction, or contingency. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Using a truth table, prove De Morgan's Law: $\neg(P \vee Q) \equiv \neg P \wedge \neg Q$. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Truth Table for $(P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (Q \rightarrow P)$:** (7 Marks)

We have $n = 2$ atomic propositions, so $2^2 = 4$ rows.

**Step 1:** Enumerate $(P, Q)$ pairs.

**Step 2:** Compute $P \rightarrow Q$ — True except when $P = T$ and $Q = F$.

**Step 3:** Compute $Q \rightarrow P$ — True except when $Q = T$ and $P = F$.

**Step 4:** Compute the conjunction of the two implications.

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ | $Q \rightarrow P$ | $(P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (Q \rightarrow P)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:--------------------------------------------:|
| F   | F   | T                 | T                 | **T**                                        |
| F   | T   | T                 | F                 | **F**                                        |
| T   | F   | F                 | T                 | **F**                                        |
| T   | T   | T                 | T                 | **T**                                        |

**Step 5 (Classification):** The final column contains both T and F, hence the statement is a **Contingency** (specifically, it is logically equivalent to $P \leftrightarrow Q$, the biconditional). (1 Mark)

---

**(b) Truth Table Proof of $\neg(P \vee Q) \equiv \neg P \wedge \neg Q$:** (7 Marks)

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \vee Q$ | $\neg(P \vee Q)$ | $\neg P$ | $\neg Q$ | $\neg P \wedge \neg Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:----------:|:----------------:|:--------:|:--------:|:----------------------:|
| F   | F   | F          | **T**            | T        | T        | **T**                  |
| F   | T   | T          | **F**            | T        | F        | **F**                  |
| T   | F   | T          | **F**            | F        | T        | **F**                  |
| T   | T   | T          | **F**            | F        | F        | **F**                  |

**Comparison:** The final column of $\neg(P \vee Q)$ (column 4) is identical to the final column of $\neg P \wedge \neg Q$ (column 7) for all four input combinations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \neg(P \vee Q) \equiv \neg P \wedge \neg Q \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence De Morgan's Law is proved. (1 Mark)

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define the logical connective "implication". State the truth table for $P \rightarrow Q$ and explain each row with a real-world example. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Show, using a truth table, that $P \rightarrow (Q \wedge R) \equiv (P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (P \rightarrow R)$. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Implication Definition and Truth Table:** (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):** The *implication* $P \rightarrow Q$ is a binary logical connective read as "If $P$, then $Q$". The proposition $P$ is called the **antecedent** (hypothesis) and $Q$ is the **consequent** (conclusion). The compound statement $P \rightarrow Q$ is false **only** when the antecedent is True and the consequent is False; in all other cases, it is True.

**Truth Table (2 Marks):**

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ | Real-World Interpretation |
|:---:|:---:|:-----------------:|:--------------------------|
| T   | T   | T                 | If it rains (T) and the streets are wet (T), the rule holds. |
| T   | F   | F                 | If it rains (T) but streets are dry (F), the rule fails. |
| F   | T   | T                 | If it doesn't rain (F), the rule is vacuously satisfied regardless. |
| F   | F   | T                 | If it doesn't rain (F) and streets are dry (F), the rule is vacuously satisfied. |

**Explanation (3 Marks):** The crucial non-intuitive case is row 3 — when the antecedent is false, the implication is automatically True. This is called **vacuous truth** and is essential in mathematical proof theory. In an AI expert system, a rule with unsatisfied preconditions simply does not fire, but is not "wrong".

---

**(b) Truth Table Proof of $P \rightarrow (Q \wedge R) \equiv (P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (P \rightarrow R)$:** (7 Marks)

Since there are 3 atomic propositions ($P, Q, R$), we need $2^3 = 8$ rows.

**Step 1:** Enumerate all 8 combinations.

**Step 2:** Compute $Q \wedge R$ (True only when both Q and R are True).

**Step 3:** Compute $P \rightarrow (Q \wedge R)$ using the implication rule.

**Step 4:** Compute $P \rightarrow Q$ and $P \rightarrow R$ separately.

**Step 5:** Compute the conjunction $(P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (P \rightarrow R)$.

**Step 6:** Compare columns 5 and 8.

| $P$ | $Q$ | $R$ | $Q \wedge R$ | $P \rightarrow (Q \wedge R)$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ | $P \rightarrow R$ | $(P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (P \rightarrow R)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:------------:|:----------------------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:--------------------------------------------:|
| F   | F   | F   | F            | **T**                        | T                 | T                 | **T**                                        |
| F   | F   | T   | F            | **T**                        | T                 | T                 | **T**                                        |
| F   | T   | F   | F            | **T**                        | T                 | T                 | **T**                                        |
| F   | T   | T   | T            | **T**                        | T                 | T                 | **T**                                        |
| T   | F   | F   | F            | **F**                        | F                 | F                 | **F**                                        |
| T   | F   | T   | F            | **F**                        | F                 | T                 | **F**                                        |
| T   | T   | F   | F            | **F**                        | T                 | F                 | **F**                                        |
| T   | T   | T   | T            | **T**                        | T                 | T                 | **T**                                        |

**Comparison:** Columns 5 and 8 are identical for all 8 rows.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore P \rightarrow (Q \wedge R) \equiv (P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (P \rightarrow R) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

This distributive-like law is fundamental for **forward-chaining inference engines**, where complex preconditions can be decomposed into simpler individual rule conditions. (1 Mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Implication row errors:** Approximately **40% of students** wrongly mark $F \rightarrow T$ as False. The KTU answer key specifically docks 1 mark for this. Always remember: implication is False **only** in the single case $T \rightarrow F$.
> 2. **Missing intermediate columns:** The examiner allocates 2–3 marks purely for the *intermediate computation columns* (e.g., $\neg P$, $P \wedge Q$). Skipping these and jumping to the final column costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **No conclusion line:** Always end a truth-table proof with a $\blacksquare$ or a written statement "$\therefore$ the two statements are logically equivalent." A bare table without a conclusion loses the final 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong row count:** Forgetting that $n$ propositions need $2^n$ rows is a common error. Double-check before writing.
> 5. **Exclusive vs. Inclusive OR confusion:** The KTU-syllabus $\vee$ is **inclusive** OR (true even when both are true). Do not confuse with $\oplus$ (exclusive OR).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Compound Statement:** Combination of atomic propositions via logical connectives.
- **Five Canonical Connectives:** $\neg$ (NOT), $\wedge$ (AND), $\vee$ (OR), $\rightarrow$ (IMPLIES), $\leftrightarrow$ (IFF).
- **Truth Table Size:** $n$ atomic propositions $\Rightarrow$ $2^n$ rows.
- **Implication Rule:** $P \rightarrow Q$ is False **only** when $P = T$ and $Q = F$ (vacuous truth otherwise).
- **Biconditional Rule:** $P \leftrightarrow Q$ is True when $P$ and $Q$ have identical truth values.
- **De Morgan's Laws:** $\neg(P \wedge Q) \equiv \neg P \vee \neg Q$ and $\neg(P \vee Q) \equiv \neg P \wedge \neg Q$.
- **Implication Conversion:** $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q$.
- **Biconditional Conversion:** $P \leftrightarrow Q \equiv (P \wedge Q) \vee (\neg P \wedge \neg Q)$.
- **Tautology:** Final column all T (e.g., $P \vee \neg P$).
- **Contradiction:** Final column all F (e.g., $P \wedge \neg P$).
- **Contingency:** Final column has both T and F.
- **Logical Equivalence:** Two statements are equivalent if their final truth-table columns are identical for **every** input combination.
- **AI Context:** Every expert-system rule, every Boolean circuit, and every propositional inference reduces to a compound-statement analysis task.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Predicate Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Predicate Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Predicate Logic

**Predicate Logic** (also called **First-Order Logic (FOL)** or **First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)**) is an extension of propositional logic that expresses statements about **objects**, their **properties**, and the **relations between them**. Unlike propositional logic, which treats statements as indivisible atomic units, predicate logic breaks statements into a **predicate symbol** and a list of **arguments (terms)**, allowing reasoning about individuals from a domain of discourse.

Formally, a predicate logic language $\mathcal{L}$ consists of:
- **Logical connectives**: $\land, \lor, \lnot, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$
- **Quantifiers**: Universal ($\forall$) and Existential ($\exists$)
- **Variables**: $x, y, z, \dots$ (lowercase)
- **Constants**: $a, b, c, \dots$ (lowercase or specific names)
- **Functions**: $f, g, h, \dots$ (mapping terms to terms)
- **Predicate symbols**: $P, Q, R, \dots$ (mapping terms to truth values)
- **Equality symbol**: $=$ (optional)
- **Punctuation**: parentheses, commas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Predicate Logic is a foundational building block for **Knowledge Representation**, **Automated Reasoning**, and **Inference Engines** in Cognitive Systems and Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). It forms the theoretical basis of languages like **Prolog**, **SPARQL**, and **Description Logic (DL)** used in AI.

## 1.2 Why Predicate Logic? The Limitation of Propositional Logic

Propositional logic treats every atomic sentence as a single, indivisible unit. Consider:

> "All humans are mortal. Socrates is a human. Therefore, Socrates is mortal."

In propositional logic, we would write:
- $P$ = "All humans are mortal" (treated as one truth value)
- $Q$ = "Socrates is a human"
- $R$ = "Socrates is mortal"

But we lose the **internal structure**! We cannot express *why* $P \land Q \rightarrow R$ is valid, nor generalize the pattern. Predicate logic fixes this by exposing the inner structure of $P$, $Q$, and $R$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: "A Detective's Magnifying Glass"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy**: Think of propositional logic as a **security camera** — it tells you *whether* something happened (TRUE/FALSE), but gives you no detail. Predicate logic is the **detective's magnifying glass** — it zooms into the statement, identifying *who* is involved (objects/terms), *what properties* they have (predicates), and *how many* of them are in question (quantifiers).

## 1.4 Core Components of Predicate Logic

| Component | Notation | Role | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant** | $a, b, \text{Socrates}$ | Names a specific object | $\text{Socrates}$ |
| **Variable** | $x, y, z$ | Ranges over objects in the domain | $x$ |
| **Function** | $f(x), \text{left\_of}(x)$ | Maps objects to objects | $\text{father}(\text{Socrates})$ |
| **Predicate** | $P(x), Q(x,y)$ | Maps objects to truth values | $\text{Human}(x)$ |
| **Term** | $a, x, f(t_1, t_2)$ | Refers to an object | $\text{father}(x)$ |
| **Atom** | $P(t_1, \dots, t_n)$ | A predicate applied to terms | $\text{Loves}(\text{Rom}, \text{Jul})$ |
| **Sentence** | WFF with no free variables | A complete logical statement | $\forall x \, \text{Human}(x) \rightarrow \text{Mortal}(x)$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recognition Rule**: If a letter is **lowercase** → it's a **term** (constant, variable, or function). If it's **uppercase** → it's a **predicate** (or a sentence symbol). This convention is sacred in KTU answer sheets.

## 1.5 The Domain of Discourse

The **domain of discourse** (or **universe of discourse**) is the set of all objects under consideration. For example:
- "All humans" → domain is the set of all humans
- "Every integer" → domain is $\mathbb{Z}$
- "Some birds" → domain is the set of all birds

> [!CAUTION]
> If the domain is **empty**, then $\forall x \, P(x)$ is vacuously TRUE, and $\exists x \, P(x)$ is FALSE. This subtlety is frequently tested in KTU exams.

## 1.6 Syllogism: The Ancient Root of Predicate Logic

Aristotelian **syllogisms** are the historical foundation. A syllogism is a three-part deductive argument:
1. **Major premise** (general statement)
2. **Minor premise** (specific instance)
3. **Conclusion** (deduced fact)

**Example**:
- Major: All $A$ are $B$ → $\forall x \, (A(x) \rightarrow B(x))$
- Minor: $C$ is an $A$ → $A(C)$
- Conclusion: $C$ is $B$ → $B(C)$

This is exactly the reasoning pattern that predicate logic formalizes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Quantifiers — The Heart of Predicate Logic

### 2.1.1 Universal Quantifier ($\forall$)

**Definition**: The universal quantifier $\forall x \, P(x)$ asserts that $P(x)$ holds for **every** element $x$ in the domain.

**Reading patterns**:
- "For all $x$..."
- "For every $x$..."
- "Given any $x$..."
- "Each $x$ has property $P$..."

**Logical expansion** (for finite domain $D = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_n\}$):

$$\forall x \, P(x) \equiv P(d_1) \land P(d_2) \land \cdots \land P(d_n)$$

### 2.1.2 Existential Quantifier ($\exists$)

**Definition**: The existential quantifier $\exists x \, P(x)$ asserts that there exists **at least one** element $x$ in the domain for which $P(x)$ holds.

**Reading patterns**:
- "There exists an $x$ such that..."
- "For some $x$..."
- "At least one $x$ has property $P$..."

**Logical expansion** (for finite domain $D = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_n\}$):

$$\exists x \, P(x) \equiv P(d_1) \lor P(d_2) \lor \cdots \lor P(d_n)$$

### 2.1.3 Uniqueness Quantifier ($\exists!$)

$\exists! x \, P(x)$ means "there exists exactly one $x$ such that $P(x)$." It is defined as:

$$\exists! x \, P(x) \equiv \exists x \, (P(x) \land \forall y \, (P(y) \rightarrow y = x))$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme specifically highlights the **Uniqueness Quantifier** under "Extended Quantifiers" in the Cognitive Systems syllabus.

## 2.2 Free vs. Bound Variables

A variable occurrence is **bound** if it falls under the scope of a quantifier; otherwise, it is **free**.

**Example**: In $\forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, Q(x, y))$:
- $x$ in $P(x)$ → **bound** (by $\forall x$)
- $x$ in $Q(x, y)$ → **bound** (by $\forall x$)
- $y$ in $Q(x, y)$ → **bound** (by $\exists y$)

> [!WARNING]
> A **Well-Formed Formula (WFF)** that contains free variables is **NOT a sentence** and has no truth value. A sentence is a WFF with **no free variables**. KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Negation of Quantifiers — De Morgan's Laws for Quantifiers)

This is the **most heavily tested** topic in KTU exams on predicate logic.

| # | Statement | Negation | Law Name |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\forall x \, P(x)$ | $\exists x \, \lnot P(x)$ | De Morgan for Universal |
| 2 | $\exists x \, P(x)$ | $\forall x \, \lnot P(x)$ | De Morgan for Existential |
| 3 | $\forall x \, \lnot P(x)$ | $\exists x \, P(x)$ | Reverse of #2 |
| 4 | $\exists x \, \lnot P(x)$ | $\forall x \, P(x)$ | Reverse of #1 |
| 5 | $\lnot \forall x \, P(x)$ | $\exists x \, \lnot P(x)$ | Direct negation |
| 6 | $\lnot \exists x \, P(x)$ | $\forall x \, \lnot P(x)$ | Direct negation |
| 7 | $\lnot (\forall x \, \forall y \, P(x,y))$ | $\exists x \, \exists y \, \lnot P(x,y)$ | Nested universal |
| 8 | $\lnot (\exists x \, \forall y \, P(x,y))$ | $\forall x \, \exists y \, \lnot P(x,y)$ | Mixed nested |
| 9 | $\lnot \forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x))$ | $\exists x \, (P(x) \land \lnot Q(x))$ | Implication form |
| 10 | $\lnot \exists x \, (P(x) \land Q(x))$ | $\forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow \lnot Q(x))$ | Conjunction form |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic**: "Negation flips the quantifier, negates the predicate, and breaks any conjunction into a conditional" (and vice versa). Master this — KTU questions on negating quantified statements appear in nearly every exam cycle.

## 2.4 Rules of Inference for Predicate Logic

These extend propositional rules by adding **quantifier-specific** rules. The following four quantifier rules are essential:

### 2.4.1 Universal Instantiation (UI)

$$\frac{\forall x \, P(x)}{\therefore P(c)} \quad \text{(where } c \text{ is an arbitrary constant in the domain)}$$

**Intuition**: If it holds for *all* $x$, then it holds for any specific $c$.

### 2.4.2 Universal Generalization (UG)

$$\frac{P(c) \text{ for arbitrary } c}{\therefore \forall x \, P(x)}$$

**Intuition**: If $P(c)$ holds for an *arbitrary* $c$ (with no assumptions about $c$), it holds for all $x$.

### 2.4.3 Existential Instantiation (EI)

$$\frac{\exists x \, P(x)}{\therefore P(c)} \quad \text{(where } c \text{ is a NEW constant not used elsewhere)}$$

**Intuition**: If something exists with property $P$, give it a fresh name $c$.

### 2.4.4 Existential Generalization (EG)

$$\frac{P(c)}{\therefore \exists x \, P(x)}$$

**Intuition**: If $P(c)$ holds for some specific $c$, then *there exists* an $x$ with property $P$.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Pitfall**: $c$ in **UI** can be ANY constant, but in **EI** $c$ MUST be a **brand-new** constant. Violating this introduces **illicit substitution errors**, a common KTU mark-deduction issue.

## 2.5 Translation Patterns: English ↔ Predicate Logic

| English Statement | Predicate Logic Form | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| All $A$ are $B$ | $\forall x \, (A(x) \rightarrow B(x))$ | Universal with implication |
| Some $A$ is $B$ | $\exists x \, (A(x) \land B(x))$ | Existential with conjunction |
| No $A$ is $B$ | $\forall x \, (A(x) \rightarrow \lnot B(x))$ or $\lnot \exists x \, (A(x) \land B(x))$ | Two equivalent forms |
| Only $A$ are $B$ | $\forall x \, (B(x) \rightarrow A(x))$ | "Only" reverses the implication |
| Every $A$ is not $B$ | $\forall x \, (A(x) \rightarrow \lnot B(x))$ | Tricky phrasing |
| There is exactly one $A$ | $\exists! x \, A(x)$ or $\exists x \, (A(x) \land \forall y \, (A(y) \rightarrow y=x))$ | Uniqueness |
| $A$ is a subset of $B$ | $\forall x \, (A(x) \rightarrow B(x))$ | Set-theoretic |
| The union of $A$ and $B$ is $C$ | $\forall x \, ((A(x) \lor B(x)) \leftrightarrow C(x))$ | Set operation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern**: "All", "Every", "Any" → use $\forall$ with $\rightarrow$. "Some", "There exists", "At least one" → use $\exists$ with $\land$. "Only" reverses the implication arrow.

## 2.6 Prenex Normal Form (PNF)

A formula in **Prenex Normal Form** has all quantifiers moved to the front (the **prefix**), followed by a **quantifier-free matrix**.

**Form**: $\bigstar_{i=1}^{n} Q_i x_i \, M(x_1, \dots, x_n)$

where $Q_i \in \{\forall, \exists\}$ and $M$ is a quantifier-free matrix.

**Conversion algorithm** (essential for KTU's resolution/AI inference questions):
1. Eliminate $\rightarrow$ and $\leftrightarrow$ using equivalences.
2. Push $\lnot$ inward using De Morgan's laws.
3. Standardize variables (rename bound variables to be distinct).
4. Move quantifiers to the front using the **equivalence**:

$$\big(Qx \, \phi\right) \land \psi \equiv Qx \, (\phi \land \psi)$$

provided $x$ is not free in $\psi$.

## 2.7 Skolemization

**Skolemization** removes existential quantifiers by replacing existentially quantified variables with **Skolem functions**.

**Rules**:
- $\exists x \, P(x)$ → $P(a)$ (where $a$ is a new Skolem constant)
- $\forall x_1 \dots \forall x_n \, \exists y \, P(x_1, \dots, x_n, y)$ → $P(x_1, \dots, x_n, f(x_1, \dots, x_n))$ (where $f$ is a new Skolem function)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Skolemization preserves **satisfiability** (a set of clauses is satisfiable iff its Skolem form is). This is fundamental to automated theorem proving in AI.

## 2.8 Unification (Foundation of Resolution in AI)

**Unification** is the process of finding a **substitution** $\theta$ that makes two atomic formulas identical.

**Most General Unifier (MGU)**: A substitution that is no more specific than any other unifier.

**Algorithm outline** (Robinson's algorithm):
1. If $P$ and $Q$ are identical atoms, return $\emptyset$.
2. If $P = f(t_1, \dots, t_n)$ and $Q = f(s_1, \dots, s_n)$, recursively unify $t_i$ with $s_i$.
3. If $P = x$ (variable) and $Q$ is anything, substitute $x \mapsto Q$ (or $Q \mapsto x$ if $Q$ is a variable).
4. **Occurs check**: fail if $x$ appears in $Q$ (prevents infinite terms like $x \mapsto f(x)$).

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Predicate Logic |
|---|---|
| **AI & Knowledge Representation** | Description Logics, OWL (Web Ontology Language), RDF |
| **Database Systems** | Relational calculus, SQL semantics, query optimization |
| **Automated Theorem Proving** | Resolution refutation, SAT/SMT solvers |
| **Program Verification** | Hoare logic, model checking (using FOL as the basis) |
| **Cyber-Physical Systems** | Temporal logic for safety properties (e.g., "for all time, if sensor reads above threshold, then actuator activates") |
| **Cybersecurity** | Formal verification of protocols, intrusion detection rules |
| **Cognitive Computing** | Reasoning engines, expert systems, IBM Watson-like architectures |

## 2.10 Logical Equivalences for Quantifiers (KTU Reference)

| Equivalence | Rule Name |
|---|---|
| $\forall x \, (P(x) \land Q(x)) \equiv \forall x \, P(x) \land \forall x \, Q(x)$ | Distributivity of $\forall$ over $\land$ |
| $\exists x \, (P(x) \lor Q(x)) \equiv \exists x \, P(x) \lor \exists x \, Q(x)$ | Distributivity of $\exists$ over $\lor$ |
| $\forall x \, P(x) \lor \forall x \, Q(x) \models \forall x \, (P(x) \lor Q(x))$ | **NOT** an equivalence (one-way only) |
| $\exists x \, (P(x) \land Q(x)) \models \exists x \, P(x) \land \exists x \, Q(x)$ | **NOT** an equivalence (one-way only) |
| $\forall x \, \forall y \, P(x,y) \equiv \forall y \, \forall x \, P(x,y)$ | Quantifier swap (same type) |
| $\exists x \, \exists y \, P(x,y) \equiv \exists y \, \exists x \, P(x,y)$ | Quantifier swap (same type) |
| $\forall x \, \exists y \, P(x,y) \not\equiv \exists y \, \forall x \, P(x,y)$ | **Cannot swap** different quantifiers |

> [!WARNING]
> The last row is a classic KTU trick question. "Every student has a favorite book" ($\forall x \, \exists y$) does NOT mean "There is a book that every student favors" ($\exists y \, \forall x$). The order of mixed quantifiers matters!
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Converting English to Prenex Normal Form

**Problem (KTU-style)**: Convert the following sentence to Prenex Normal Form (PNF):
$$\lnot \forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, (\lnot Q(x,y) \land R(y)))$$

---

### Step 1: Eliminate Implications

Replace $A \rightarrow B$ with $\lnot A \lor B$.

Inside the universal: $P(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, (\lnot Q(x,y) \land R(y))$ becomes $\lnot P(x) \lor \exists y \, (\lnot Q(x,y) \land R(y))$.

So the formula becomes:

$$\lnot \forall x \, (\lnot P(x) \lor \exists y \, (\lnot Q(x,y) \land R(y)))$$

---

### Step 2: Push Negations Inward (De Morgan's for Quantifiers + Boolean)

Apply $\lnot \forall x \, \phi \equiv \exists x \, \lnot \phi$:

$$\exists x \, \lnot (\lnot P(x) \lor \exists y \, (\lnot Q(x,y) \land R(y)))$$

Now apply Boolean De Morgan: $\lnot (A \lor B) \equiv \lnot A \land \lnot B$:

$$\exists x \, (P(x) \land \lnot \exists y \, (\lnot Q(x,y) \land R(y)))$$

Now apply $\lnot \exists y \, \phi \equiv \forall y \, \lnot \phi$:

$$\exists x \, (P(x) \land \forall y \, \lnot (\lnot Q(x,y) \land R(y)))$$

Apply Boolean De Morgan again: $\lnot (A \land B) \equiv \lnot A \lor \lnot B$:

$$\exists x \, (P(x) \land \forall y \, (Q(x,y) \lor \lnot R(y)))$$

---

### Step 3: Standardize Variables

The variable $x$ in $P(x)$ and $y$ in $\forall y$ are already distinct. No change needed.

---

### Step 4: Skolemize (Optional, for resolution) or Move Quantifiers to Front

Move $\exists x$ and $\forall y$ to the prefix:

$$\exists x \, \forall y \, (P(x) \land (Q(x,y) \lor \lnot R(y)))$$

---

### Step 5: Final Prenex Normal Form

$$\boxed{\exists x \, \forall y \, (P(x) \land Q(x,y) \lor P(x) \land \lnot R(y))}$$

The matrix is quantifier-free and in **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** if we distribute: $(P(x) \lor P(x)) \land (Q(x,y) \lor P(x)) \land (\lnot R(y) \lor P(x))$, simplifying to:

$$P(x) \land (Q(x,y) \lor P(x)) \land (\lnot R(y) \lor P(x))$$

Since $P(x) \land (Q \lor P) \land (\lnot R \lor P) \equiv P(x)$ (the $P$-clauses are subsumed), the CNF set of clauses is:

$$\{ P(x),\ \lnot R(y) \lor P(x) \}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: Negation of Nested Quantifiers

**Problem**: Find the negation of $\forall x \, \exists y \, (P(x,y) \rightarrow \lnot Q(x))$.

---

### Step 1: Negate the entire formula

$$\lnot \forall x \, \exists y \, (P(x,y) \rightarrow \lnot Q(x))$$

---

### Step 2: Apply $\lnot \forall x \, \phi \equiv \exists x \, \lnot \phi$

$$\exists x \, \lnot \exists y \, (P(x,y) \rightarrow \lnot Q(x))$$

---

### Step 3: Apply $\lnot \exists y \, \phi \equiv \forall y \, \lnot \phi$

$$\exists x \, \forall y \, \lnot (P(x,y) \rightarrow \lnot Q(x))$$

---

### Step 4: Eliminate implication inside

$P(x,y) \rightarrow \lnot Q(x) \equiv \lnot P(x,y) \lor \lnot Q(x)$. Negate: $\lnot (\lnot P(x,y) \lor \lnot Q(x))$.

Apply Boolean De Morgan: $P(x,y) \land Q(x)$.

---

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\exists x \, \forall y \, (P(x,y) \land Q(x))}$$

**English reading**: "There exists an $x$ such that for every $y$, both $P(x,y)$ and $Q(x)$ hold."

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Unification Example

**Problem**: Unify $P(f(x), g(y), z)$ with $P(a, g(b), h(w))$.

---

### Step 1: Initialize empty substitution $\theta = \emptyset$. Set disagreement set = $\{ f(x), a \}$.

$f(x)$ is a function, $a$ is a constant. Since $f(x)$ does not occur in $a$, we can substitute $x \mapsto a$.

$$\theta_1 = \{ x/a \}$$

Apply $\theta_1$ to both formulas:
- $P(f(a), g(y), z)$
- $P(a, g(b), h(w))$

---

### Step 2: Disagreement set = $\{ f(a), a \}$. But $f(a)$ is more complex than $a$. **Cannot unify** if we treat $f(a)$ as a single term to be made equal to $a$ — unless we allow functional unfolding.

> [!CAUTION]
> Standard Robinson unification with occurs check **fails** here because $a$ occurs inside $f(a)$. We need to either unfold $f(a) = a$ or recognize that the function and constant are different.

**Workable alternative**: If we instead unify $P(f(x), g(y), z)$ with $P(f(a), g(b), h(w))$:

### Step 1: Disagreement $\{f(x), f(a)\}$. Substitute $x \mapsto a$.

$\theta_1 = \{ x/a \}$. Apply: $P(f(a), g(y), z)$ and $P(f(a), g(b), h(w))$.

### Step 2: Disagreement $\{g(y), g(b)\}$. Substitute $y \mapsto b$.

$\theta_2 = \{ y/b \}$. Apply: $P(f(a), g(b), z)$ and $P(f(a), g(b), h(w))$.

### Step 3: Disagreement $\{z, h(w)\}$. Substitute $z \mapsto h(w)$.

$\theta_3 = \{ z/h(w) \}$.

---

### Final MGU

$$\boxed{\theta = \{ x/a,\ y/b,\ z/h(w) \}}$$

Unified atom: $P(f(a), g(b), h(w))$.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Resolution Proof Example

**Problem**: Prove $\exists x \, P(x)$ from $\{\forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)),\ \exists x \, \lnot Q(x)\}$.

---

### Step 1: Convert premises to clause form

**Premise 1**: $\forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x))$
- Eliminate $\rightarrow$: $\forall x \, (\lnot P(x) \lor Q(x))$
- CNF: $\{ \lnot P(x) \lor Q(x) \}$

**Premise 2**: $\exists x \, \lnot Q(x)$
- Skolemize: $\{ \lnot Q(a) \}$

**Negation of goal**: $\lnot \exists x \, P(x) \equiv \forall x \, \lnot P(x) \equiv \{ \lnot P(b) \}$ (use fresh variable $b$)

---

### Step 2: Resolution

Resolve $\lnot P(x) \lor Q(x)$ with $\lnot P(b)$:

- To resolve, we need complementary literals. $\lnot P(b)$ and $P(\cdot)$ — but we have $\lnot P(x)$ in clause 1.
- Substitute: $x \mapsto b$. Get $Q(b)$ from resolving $\lnot P(b)$ and $\lnot P(b) \lor Q(b)$ ... wait, we have $\lnot P$ in both, not complementary.

**Correct approach**: Resolve $\lnot P(x) \lor Q(x)$ with the unit clause... but we don't have $P(\cdot)$ directly. We have $\lnot Q(a)$ and $Q(x) \lor \lnot P(x)$.

- Resolve $\lnot Q(a)$ with $Q(x) \lor \lnot P(x)$: unify $Q(a)$ with $Q(x)$, get $x \mapsto a$. Resolvent: $\lnot P(a)$.
- Resolve $\lnot P(a)$ with $\lnot P(b)$ — both negative, not complementary. Stuck.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Correction**: The standard procedure negates the goal and adds it to the clause set. We need a *positive* literal to resolve against $\lnot P$. Let me recheck the original premises. Given:
> - Premise: $\exists x \, \lnot Q(x)$ (means "something fails $Q$")
> - Premise: $\forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x))$ (means "all $P$ implies $Q$")
> - **Conclusion** $\exists x \, P(x)$ does NOT follow. This is a fallacy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Revealing the Fallacy**: From "Some $x$ is not $Q$" and "All $P$ are $Q$", we conclude "No $x$ is $P$" (not "Some $x$ is $P$"). The intended conclusion should be $\lnot \exists x \, P(x)$. Let me redo the proof correctly:

**Corrected Problem**: Prove $\lnot \exists x \, P(x)$ from $\{\forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)),\ \exists x \, \lnot Q(x)\}$.

**Negate the goal**: $\exists x \, P(x) \to \{ P(a) \}$ (Skolem constant $a$)

**Clauses**:
1. $\lnot P(x) \lor Q(x)$
2. $\lnot Q(b)$ (Skolem constant $b$)
3. $P(a)$

**Resolution**:
- Resolve (1) and (3) with $x \mapsto a$: get $Q(a)$.
- Resolve $Q(a)$ with (2) with $a \mapsto b$: get $\square$ (empty clause).

Contradiction reached — the negation is unsatisfiable, so the original goal $\lnot \exists x \, P(x)$ is proved. ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Quantifier Inference Cheat Sheet

| Inference | Premises | Conclusion | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| UI | $\forall x \, P(x)$ | $P(c)$ | ✅ Valid |
| UG | $P(c)$ for arbitrary $c$ | $\forall x \, P(x)$ | ✅ Valid |
| EI | $\exists x \, P(x)$ | $P(c)$ for *new* $c$ | ✅ Valid |
| EG | $P(c)$ | $\exists x \, P(x)$ | ✅ Valid |
| Illicit UI from $\exists$ | $\exists x \, P(x)$ | $\forall x \, P(x)$ | ❌ Invalid |
| Illicit UG from specific | $P(a)$ (known specific) | $\forall x \, P(x)$ | ❌ Invalid |
| Quantifier swap (mixed) | $\forall x \, \exists y \, P(x,y)$ | $\exists y \, \forall x \, P(x,y)$ | ❌ Invalid |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Logic

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **hierarchical relationship** between propositional logic, predicate logic, and higher-order logics used in cognitive AI systems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["PROPOSITIONAL LOGIC"] --> nodeB["Atomic propositions P, Q, R"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Connectives and, or, not, implies, iff"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Limitation: No internal structure of statements"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Cannot reason about objects or relations"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["PREDICATE LOGIC FOL"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Adds quantifiers: forall, exists"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Adds predicates, functions, variables"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Enables relational reasoning"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Knowledge Representation in AI"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Prolog, Description Logic, OWL"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["HIGHER ORDER LOGIC HOL"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Quantifies over predicates themselves"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["More expressive but undecidable in general"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Used in advanced theorem provers"]
    nodeJ --> nodeP["Inference Engines and Resolution"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["Automated Theorem Proving in CPS"]
```

---

```mermaid
    nodeB --> nodeC["SYNTAX TREE BUILDER"]

```

## 4.3 Quantifier Decision Flowchart

This diagram helps students decide **which quantifier and connective** to use when translating English statements.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["ENGLISH STATEMENT"] --> nodeB{"Contains words like ALL, EVERY, ANY?"}
    nodeB -->|"YES"| nodeC["UNIVERSAL QUANTIFIER"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Predicate is a property?"}
    nodeD -->|"YES"| nodeE["Use FORALL x Px with IMPLICATION"]
    nodeD -->|"NO Relation"| nodeF["Use FORALL x forall y Rxy"]
    nodeB -->|"NO"| nodeG{"Contains words like SOME, EXISTS, AT LEAST ONE?"}
    nodeG -->|"YES"| nodeH["EXISTENTIAL QUANTIFIER"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Use EXISTS x with CONJUNCTION"]
    nodeG -->|"NO"| nodeJ{"Contains ONLY?"}
    nodeJ -->|"YES"| nodeK["Reverse the implication direction"]
    nodeJ -->|"NO"| nodeL["Check for NEGATION nested"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Apply De Morgan for Quantifiers"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Final WFF with Quantifiers"]
    nodeE --> nodeN
    nodeF --> nodeN
    nodeI --> nodeN
    nodeK --> nodeN
```

## 4.4 Resolution Refutation Workflow

This diagram shows the complete workflow of a **resolution refutation proof** used in AI theorem proving.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["AXIOMS AND PREMISES"] --> nodeB["Convert each to Clause Form CNF"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["NEGATE the Goal to be Proved"]

```

## 4.5 Predicate Logic WFF Construction Tree

This tree shows how a complex WFF is built from atomic components.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["WFF: forall x Px implies exists y Qxy and Ry"] --> nodeB["Top: IMPLICATION"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Antecedent: forall x Px"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Consequent: AND node"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Quantifier: FORALL x"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Atom: P of x"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["exists y Qxy"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Atom: R of y"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeG --> nodeI["Quantifier: EXISTS y"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Atom: Q of x, y"]
```

## 4.6 Quantifier Scope Diagram

The scope of a quantifier is the **sub-formula** to which it applies. This is a critical concept in determining free vs. bound variables.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Formula: forall x Px and exists y Qxy or Ry"] --> nodeB["Outer AND"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Left: forall x Px"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Right: OR node"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["x is BOUND here scope: Px"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["exists y Qxy"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Ry"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["y is BOUND here scope: Qxy"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["x is FREE here not bound by any quantifier in this branch"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["y is BOUND by the exists y above"]
```
