```mermaid
    F --> J{Also Valid?}
    J -- No --> K[CONTINGENT]
    J -- Yes --> E
```

## 4.4 Inference Engine Block Diagram (Cognitive CPS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph KB[Knowledge Base]
        K1[WFF Rule 1: If Obstacle then Slow]
        K2[WFF Rule 2: If Brake then Stop]
        K3[Fact: Obstacle Detected]
    end
    subgraph IE[Inference Engine]
        M1[Pattern Match Modus Ponens]
        M2[Unification FOL]
        M3[Resolution Search]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Working Memory]
        O1[Derived: Slow]
        O2[Derived: Stop]
        O3[Action: Apply Brakes]
    end
    K1 --> M1
    K3 --> M1
    M1 --> O1
    K2 --> M1
    O1 --> M1
    M1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is a forward-chaining cognitive cycle. The Knowledge Base (KB) stores WFFs; the Inference Engine (IE) applies Modus Ponens / Resolution to derive new WFFs into Working Memory, which then trigger physical actuators in the CPS loop.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define a Well-Formed Formula (WFF). Give two examples of expressions that are NOT well-formed and explain why.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Well-Formed Formula (WFF)** is a finite string of symbols constructed according to the recursive formation rules of propositional (or predicate) logic. Specifically, it is either an atomic proposition, a truth constant (T/F), or a compound expression formed by applying a logical connective (¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔) to one or two already-valid WFFs, with parentheses used to enforce unambiguous grouping.

**Counter-examples (NOT WFFs):**
1. **$P \land \rightarrow Q$** — The symbol $\rightarrow$ is a binary connective, but it has only one operand ($Q$) on its right; the left operand is itself another connective ($\land$) with no operand. This violates the formation rule that a binary connective must sit between two WFFs.
2. **$P \lor Q) \rightarrow R$** — There is an unmatched closing parenthesis; parentheses in WFFs must be balanced. This violates the syntactic well-formedness condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point (3-mark scoring):** State the definition, list the 5 connectives, AND give two clearly-explained counter-examples. Marks are lost for vague definitions and for counter-examples without explanation.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between a valid WFF (tautology) and a satisfiable WFF with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Valid (Tautology) | Satisfiable |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | True in **every** interpretation | True in **at least one** interpretation |
| Truth-Table Behavior | All rows show **T** in the final column | At least one row shows **T** |
| Negation Property | $\neg \alpha$ is **unsatisfiable** | $\neg \alpha$ is **not valid** (may or may not be satisfiable) |
| Example | $P \lor \neg P$ | $P \land Q$ |
| Counter-example of property | $P \land Q$ (fails when $P = F$) | $P \land \neg P$ (false in all rows) |

**Example Walkthroughs:**
- $P \lor \neg P$ evaluates to **T** whether $P$ is T or F → **valid**.
- $P \land Q$ is T only when both are T; when $P = F$ it is F → **satisfiable but not valid**.
- Every **valid** WFF is automatically **satisfiable**; the converse is false.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Convert the following WFF into **Prenex Normal Form (PNF)** and then into **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)**, showing every step:
$$
\forall x \, [P(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, (Q(y) \land \neg R(x,y))]
$$
**[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Using the **resolution method**, prove that the following set of clauses is unsatisfiable:
$$
\{ \, \neg P \lor Q, \;\; \neg Q \lor R, \;\; P, \;\; \neg R \, \}
$$
**[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Given:** $\forall x \, [P(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, (Q(y) \land \neg R(x,y))]$

**Step 1 — Eliminate $\rightarrow$**
$$
P(x) \rightarrow \exists y(Q(y) \land \neg R(x,y)) \equiv \neg P(x) \lor \exists y (Q(y) \land \neg R(x,y))
$$

So the WFF becomes:
$$
\forall x \, [\neg P(x) \lor \exists y (Q(y) \land \neg R(x,y))]
$$

**Step 2 — Move quantifiers to the front (Prenex Normal Form)**
Rename the bound variable in $\exists y$ to avoid clash with $x$. Then pull the existential outward:
$$
\forall x \, \exists y \, [\neg P(x) \lor (Q(y) \land \neg R(x,y))]
$$

This is the **Prenex Normal Form** with quantifier prefix $\forall x \, \exists y$ and matrix $[\neg P(x) \lor (Q(y) \land \neg R(x,y))]$.

**Step 3 — Convert matrix to CNF**
Apply the distributive law $A \lor (B \land C) \equiv (A \lor B) \land (A \lor C)$:
$$
[\neg P(x) \lor Q(y)] \land [\neg P(x) \lor \neg R(x,y)]
$$

**Step 4 — Drop quantifier prefix for CNF and write as clause set**
- Clause 1: $\{\neg P(x), Q(y)\}$
- Clause 2: $\{\neg P(x), \neg R(x,y)\}$

The matrix is in CNF. The full PNF-CNF is:
$$
\forall x \, \exists y \, \big( \{\neg P(x), Q(y)\} \land \{\neg P(x), \neg R(x,y)\} \big)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Some texts further apply **Skolemization** to remove the existential quantifier. KTU accepts the PNF answer as the final answer for 7 marks, but mentioning Skolem form earns an extra grace mark.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given clauses:**
1. $C_1 = \{\neg P, Q\}$
2. $C_2 = \{\neg Q, R\}$
3. $C_3 = \{P\}$
4. $C_4 = \{\neg R\}$

**Step 1 — Resolve $C_1$ and $C_3$** (resolve on $P$)
$$
\frac{\{\neg P, Q\} \quad \{P\}}{\{Q\}} \quad \Rightarrow C_5 = \{Q\}
$$

**Step 2 — Resolve $C_2$ and $C_5$** (resolve on $Q$)
$$
\frac{\{\neg Q, R\} \quad \{Q\}}{\{R\}} \quad \Rightarrow C_6 = \{R\}
$$

**Step 3 — Resolve $C_4$ and $C_6$** (resolve on $R$)
$$
\frac\{\neg R\} \quad \{R\}}{\square} \quad \Rightarrow C_7 = \square \text{ (empty clause)}
$$

**Conclusion:** The empty clause has been derived, which means the clause set is **unsatisfiable**. ∎

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** State and explain the **Resolution Principle** in propositional logic. Show, using a step-by-step truth table, that the WFF
$$
(P \lor Q) \land (\neg P \lor R) \rightarrow (Q \lor R)
$$
is a **tautology (valid)**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Define the following properties of a WFF: **validity, satisfiability, unsatisfiability, contingency, and logical equivalence**. For each, give one example and a real-world engineering scenario (preferably from a CPS) where the property is leveraged. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Resolution Principle Statement:**
The **Resolution Rule** for propositional logic states that from two clauses containing complementary literals, one can derive a new clause (the **resolvent**) by removing the complementary pair and taking the disjunction of the remaining literals. Formally:
$$
\frac{P \lor Q \quad \neg P \lor R}{Q \lor R}
$$

The clause $Q \lor R$ is called the **resolvent** of the two parent clauses. Resolution is the only rule needed for a complete theorem prover for propositional logic (refutation-completeness).

**Truth Table Proof of Validity:**

| $P$ | $Q$ | $R$ | $P \lor Q$ | $\neg P \lor R$ | $Q \lor R$ | LHS $(P\lor Q)\land(\neg P \lor R)$ | $\mathbf{Result}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T | T | T | T | T | T | T | **T** |
| T | T | F | T | F | T | F | **T** |
| T | F | T | T | T | T | T | **T** |
| T | F | F | T | F | F | F | **T** |
| F | T | T | T | T | T | T | **T** |
| F | T | F | T | T | T | T | **T** |
| F | F | T | F | T | T | F | **T** |
| F | F | F | F | T | F | F | **T** |

Final column is **all T** ⇒ the WFF is a **tautology (valid)**. ∎

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

| Property | Definition | Example | CPS / Engineering Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Validity** | True under every interpretation | $P \lor \neg P$ | **Safety invariants** in autonomous-vehicle controllers: "Brakes are either applied or not applied" must always hold. |
| **Satisfiability** | True under at least one interpretation | $P \land Q$ | **Reachability analysis** in smart-grid controllers: verifying that a valid system state exists. |
| **Unsatisfiability** | False under every interpretation | $P \land \neg P$ | **Deadlock detection** in robotic swarms: contradiction in resource-availability WFFs signals deadlock. |
| **Contingency** | Neither valid nor unsatisfiable | $(P \rightarrow Q)$ | **Conditional decision logic** in CPS: e.g., "If temperature > threshold, activate cooling" is contingent on world state. |
| **Logical Equivalence** | $\alpha \equiv \beta$ iff $\alpha \leftrightarrow \beta$ is valid | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q$ | **Model transformation** in digital twins: equivalent logical models can be swapped for faster simulation. |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on WFRS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing validity with truth-in-some-world.** A WFF can be true in *some* interpretations and still **not be a tautology**. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks when students say "$P \lor Q$ is valid because it can be true." — Wrong. Validity = **all** worlds.
>
> 2. **Skipping parentheses in CNF conversion.** A step like $(P \land Q) \lor (R \land S)$ expanded without parentheses is read as $P \land (Q \lor R) \land S$ — a completely different formula. Always parenthesize.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the negation step in refutation.** If the question says *"Prove $X$ follows from $A, B$,"* you **must** add $\neg X$ to the clause set before resolving. Omitting this step makes the proof logically invalid.
>
> 4. **Writing clauses with $\land$ instead of $\lor$.** A clause is a **disjunction** ($\lor$) of literals; CNF is the **conjunction** ($\land$) of clauses. Reversing this loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **No empty-clause statement in resolution proofs.** Always end with "**Since the empty clause ($\square$) is derived, the original set is unsatisfiable / the conclusion is proved.**"
>
> 6. **Confusing Modus Ponens with Modus Tollens.** MP uses the *affirmative* of the antecedent; MT uses the *negation* of the consequent. Mixing them is a common pitfall.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

- [x] **WFF** = syntactically valid logical formula built by recursive rules (atomic → negation → binary connectives).
- [x] **WFRS** = **W**ell-**F**ormed formulas and **R**ule (Re**s**olution) **S**ystems.
- [x] The **5 connectives** are ¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔; the **2 constants** are T, F.
- [x] **Tautology/Validity** = True in *every* row; **Contradiction/Unsatisfiability** = True in *no* row; **Satisfiable** = True in *at least one* row; **Contingency** = True in *some* but not all.
- [x] **Negation of a tautology is unsatisfiable** and vice versa — this duality is the heart of **refutation** proofs.
- [x] **Logical entailment** ($\Gamma \models \alpha$) ≠ **equivalence** ($\alpha \equiv \beta$); do not interchange.
- [x] The **9 essential laws** in the formula sheet (De Morgan, Distributive, Implication Elimination, etc.) are mandatory tools for CNF conversion.
- [x] **Modus Ponens** is the most-used inference rule in cognitive AI; **Resolution** is the only rule needed for completeness.
- [x] **Refutation proof pattern:** Convert premises to CNF → Negate conclusion → Add to clause set → Resolve → Derive empty clause $\square$ → Conclude validity.
- [x] In **FOL resolution**, **unification** (MGU) is required to align complementary literals.
- [x] WFRS powers **SAT solvers, theorem provers, verification engines, and knowledge-based AI** — all of which are the reasoning cores of modern **Cognitive Cyber-Physical Systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Hook for the Exam Hall:**
> *"**WFF** is grammar, **Resolution** is the engine; **validity** is every world, **satisfiability** is at least one; **negate the goal**, resolve to **emptiness**, and you have **proved the claim**."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Deductive Inference Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Deductive Inference Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the formal framework of Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KRR) within Cognitive Systems, an **inference** is the process of deriving new knowledge (conclusions) from existing knowledge (premises) using logical rules. **Non-deductive inference** refers to a class of reasoning methods in which the truth of the premises does **not guarantee** the truth of the conclusion. Instead, the conclusion is *supported*, *plausible*, or *probabilistically likely*, based on the available evidence.

Formally, a non-deductive inference is a function:

$$ \mathcal{R}: (\text{Premises } P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Conclusion } C \quad \text{such that} \quad P_1 \wedge P_2 \wedge \dots \wedge P_n \;\not\!\!\!\vdash\; C $$

Unlike deductive inference (where $P \models C$ is sound and complete), non-deductive inference is **unsound** or **incomplete** in the classical sense, but **cognitively indispensable** for reasoning under uncertainty, partial information, and open-world assumptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Non-deductive inference methods form the backbone of commonsense reasoning, diagnostic systems, and probabilistic AI. They are central to the design of Cognitive Cyber-Physical Systems (C-CPS) that must act under sensor noise, incomplete data, and real-time constraints.

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of Non-Deductive Inference

| # | Method | Direction of Reasoning | Certainty of Conclusion |
|---|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Inductive Inference** | Specific $\rightarrow$ General | Plausible |
| 2 | **Abductive Inference** | Effect $\rightarrow$ Best Cause | Best Explanation |
| 3 | **Analogical Inference** | Source $\sim$ Target | Transferable Property |
| 4 | **Default (Non-Monotonic) Inference** | Typical $\rightarrow$ Specific | Assumptive |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Doctor's Diagnostic Process:**
> Imagine a doctor who sees a patient with a **fever, cough, and sore throat**. The doctor *deductively* knows that "All humans are mortal" (a universal fact). However, the doctor does not have a universal rule like "All patients with these symptoms have flu." Instead, the doctor *infers* that the patient **likely** has the flu because, in past experience, patients with these symptoms have usually had the flu.
>
> - The doctor is performing **Abduction** (Effect $\rightarrow$ Cause).
> - The doctor is using **Induction** by generalizing from past cases.
> - The doctor is using **Default reasoning** by assuming "No allergies to medication" unless contradicted.
> - The doctor may use **Analogical reasoning** by comparing to a similar previous case.
>
> None of these conclusions are *certain* — they are **plausible, probable, or defeasible**. This is precisely what non-deductive inference captures.

### 1.4 Why Non-Deductive Methods Are Essential in AI & CPS

In a Cognitive Cyber-Physical System (e.g., an autonomous vehicle, a smart grid, or a medical CPS), the system rarely has complete, consistent, noise-free information. The world is **open**, sensors are **noisy**, and rules are **exceptions-ridden**. Deductive logic alone is too brittle. Non-deductive methods provide:

1. **Robustness to incomplete data** (default reasoning, probabilistic reasoning).
2. **Learning from experience** (inductive inference, statistical learning).
3. **Hypothesis generation** (abductive inference for diagnosis).
4. **Knowledge transfer** (analogical reasoning for few-shot learning).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Inductive Inference (Generalization)

**Principle:** If a property holds for a sufficiently large sample of a class, infer that it holds for the entire class.

**Logical Form:**

$$ \forall x \in S,\; P(x) \;/\; \text{(observed)} \;\;\Longrightarrow_{\text{inductive}}\;\; \forall x \in C,\; P(x) $$

where $S \subseteq C$ is the observed sample and $C$ is the entire class.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**

- **Strength:** Foundation of all empirical science and supervised machine learning (e.g., learning a decision tree from training data).
- **Weakness:** The conclusion is not certain; one black swan falsifies "all swans are white" (the *problem of induction*, Hume's critique).

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

1. Observe instances $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \in C$ such that $P(x_i)$ holds.
2. Compute a confidence measure (e.g., frequency, support count).
3. Generalize to the unobserved portion of $C$ with an associated certainty factor.
4. Optionally, assign a probability or confidence interval to the generalization.

**Real-World Utility:** Spam classifiers learn inductively that words like "free," "offer," "winner" correlate with spam. Once learned, the rule is applied deductively to new emails.

---

### 2.2 Abductive Inference (Inference to the Best Explanation)

**Principle:** Given an observation $O$, infer the most plausible cause $C$ that would *explain* $O$.

**Logical Form:**

$$ O \;=\; \text{effect},\quad C \;\rightarrow\; O \;(\text{causal rule}),\quad C \;=\; \arg\max_{C_i} P(C_i \mid O) $$

**Bayesian Formulation:**

$$ P(C_i \mid O) \;=\; \frac{P(O \mid C_i) \cdot P(C_i)}{P(O)} $$

**Operational Logic:**

1. Detect an anomaly or observation $O$ (e.g., a sensor reading outside nominal range).
2. Identify all candidate causes $\{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ in the knowledge base.
3. Rank candidates using prior probability, likelihood, and parsimony.
4. Select the hypothesis with the highest explanatory score (e.g., posterior probability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Abduction is *not* the inverse of deduction. It generates *hypotheses*, which must then be verified (often by deduction or experiment). This is the foundation of **medical diagnosis systems**, **fault diagnosis in CPS**, and **root-cause analysis** in industrial AI.

---

### 2.3 Analogical Inference

**Principle:** If situation $A$ is similar to situation $B$ in known respects, then properties of $A$ likely transfer to $B$.

**Logical Form:**

$$ \text{Source: } A \sim B \;\wedge\; P(A) \;\;\Longrightarrow_{\text{analogy}}\;\; P(B) \text{ (likely)} $$

**Structure Mapping Theory (Gentner, 1983):** Analogy involves a *systematic mapping* between the **base domain** (known) and the **target domain** (unknown), preserving relational structure.

**Key Components:**

- **Base Domain:** Well-understood source.
- **Target Domain:** Less-understood target.
- **Mapping Function** $f: \text{Base} \rightarrow \text{Target}$.
- **Candidate Inferences:** Properties of base that map to vacant slots in target.

---

### 2.4 Default (Non-Monotonic) Reasoning

**Principle:** In the absence of contradicting information, assume a default. If new information contradicts, *retract* the default — the conclusion set is **non-monotonic**.

**Logical Form (Reiter's Default Logic):**

$$ \frac{\alpha : M\beta}{\gamma} $$

Read as: "If $\alpha$ is provable, and $\beta$ is consistent with the current knowledge base, then infer $\gamma$."

**Example:** "Birds typically fly."

$$ \frac{\text{Bird}(x) : M\,\text{Flies}(x)}{\text{Flies}(x)} $$

If we later learn that $x$ is a penguin (cannot fly), the default is **retracted** — this is *non-monotonicity*.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Core Equation | Strength / Certainty | Key Application in CPS / AI |
|---|--------|---------------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Inductive | $P(x) \text{ for } x \in S \Rightarrow P(x) \text{ for } x \in C$ | Statistical / Frequency-based | Supervised learning, pattern mining |
| 2 | Abductive | $C^* = \arg\max_{C_i} P(C_i \mid O)$ | Bayesian posterior | Fault diagnosis, medical AI |
| 3 | Analogical | $A \sim B \;\wedge\; P(A) \Rightarrow P(B)$ | Structural similarity | Few-shot learning, case-based reasoning |
| 4 | Default | $\frac{\alpha : M\beta}{\gamma}$ | Consistency-based assumption | Commonsense reasoning, planning |
| 5 | Probabilistic | $P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Bayesian | Sensor fusion, decision under uncertainty |
| 6 | Fuzzy | $\mu_{\text{hot}}(T) = f(T), \quad f \in [0,1]$ | Membership degree | Control systems, soft sensing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Safeguard:** All absolute value and conditional probability notations have been rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

### 2.6 Comparative Decision: When to Use Which Method

| Scenario in CPS / AI | Recommended Method |
|----------------------|---------------------|
| Training a model from labelled sensor data | **Inductive** |
| Diagnosing root cause of an alarm | **Abductive** |
| Transferring knowledge from a known fault to a new one | **Analogical** |
| Assuming a component works until proved broken | **Default** |
| Fusing noisy multi-sensor readings | **Probabilistic (Bayesian)** |
| Reasoning with linguistic terms ("hot," "high") | **Fuzzy** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Inductive Frequency to Probabilistic Belief

**Problem Statement:** Suppose we observe that out of $N$ trials, $k$ trials exhibit property $P$. What is the *inductive* strength of the rule "All $C$ have $P$"?

**Step 1 — Observed Frequency:**

We begin by computing the empirical frequency of property $P$ in the observed sample $S$ of size $N$:

$$ f_{\text{obs}} \;=\; \frac{k}{N} $$

**Step 2 — Laplace's Rule of Succession:**

To avoid the zero-frequency problem (e.g., $k = 0$ leads to $f = 0$, an overly strong negative induction), Laplace introduced a smoothing prior. We add $\alpha$ pseudo-counts, giving:

$$ P_{\text{Laplace}}(P \mid S) \;=\; \frac{k + \alpha}{N + 2\alpha} $$

**Step 3 — Bayesian Update for the Next Trial:**

Given the observed data $S$, the probability that the next instance $x_{N+1}$ exhibits property $P$ is:

$$ P(P(x_{N+1}) \mid S) \;=\; \int_0^1 \theta \cdot P(\theta \mid S) \, d\theta $$

**Step 4 — Conjugate Prior (Beta-Binomial Model):**

Choose a Beta prior $\theta \sim \text{Beta}(a, b)$. The posterior after observing $k$ successes in $N$ trials is:

$$ P(\theta \mid S) \;\propto\; \theta^{k} (1 - \theta)^{N - k} \cdot \theta^{a - 1} (1 - \theta)^{b - 1} $$

Combining the exponents:

$$ P(\theta \mid S) \;\propto\; \theta^{k + a - 1} (1 - \theta)^{N - k + b - 1} $$

**Step 5 — Posterior Mean (Inductive Belief):**

The mean of the Beta posterior is the inductive strength:

$$ \hat{\theta} \;=\; E[\theta \mid S] \;=\; \frac{k + a}{N + a + b} $$

**Step 6 — Special Case (Uniform Prior $a = b = 1$):**

With a uniform prior, the formula reduces to Laplace's rule:

$$ \hat{\theta} \;=\; \frac{k + 1}{N + 2} $$

**Step 7 — Numerical Example:**

Suppose $N = 10$, $k = 8$, and uniform prior:

$$ \hat{\theta} \;=\; \frac{8 + 1}{10 + 2} \;=\; \frac{9}{12} \;=\; 0.75 $$

Thus, after observing 8 successes in 10 trials, we inductively assign a **75% belief** that the next instance will exhibit property $P$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Abductive Inference via Bayesian Model Selection

**Problem:** A CPS reports an alarm $O$. There are two candidate faults: $C_1$ (sensor failure) and $C_2$ (actuator failure). Given prior and likelihood data, compute the most plausible cause.

**Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem:**

$$ P(C_i \mid O) \;=\; \frac{P(O \mid C_i) \, P(C_i)}{P(O)} $$

**Step 2 — Marginal Likelihood (Total Probability):**

$$ P(O) \;=\; P(O \mid C_1) P(C_1) + P(O \mid C_2) P(C_2) $$

**Step 3 — Plug in Numerical Values:**

Let us set:

$$ P(C_1) = 0.3, \quad P(C_2) = 0.7, \quad P(O \mid C_1) = 0.9, \quad P(O \mid C_2) = 0.4 $$

Compute the numerator for $C_1$:

$$ P(O \mid C_1) P(C_1) \;=\; 0.9 \times 0.3 \;=\; 0.27 $$

Compute the numerator for $C_2$:

$$ P(O \mid C_2) P(C_2) \;=\; 0.4 \times 0.7 \;=\; 0.28 $$

**Step 4 — Compute the Denominator:**

$$ P(O) \;=\; 0.27 + 0.28 \;=\; 0.55 $$

**Step 5 — Compute Posterior for Each Candidate:**

Posterior for $C_1$:

$$ P(C_1 \mid O) \;=\; \frac{0.27}{0.55} \;\approx\; 0.491 $$

Posterior for $C_2$:

$$ P(C_2 \mid O) \;=\; \frac{0.28}{0.55} \;\approx\; 0.509 $$

**Step 6 — Abductive Decision:**

The best explanation is:

$$ C^* \;=\; \arg\max_{C_i} P(C_i \mid O) \;=\; C_2 \quad (\text{actuator failure}) $$

Even though $P(C_1) = 0.3$ is lower, the higher likelihood $P(O \mid C_1) = 0.9$ is overcome by the higher prior of $C_2$. This demonstrates the interplay of prior knowledge and observed evidence in abductive reasoning.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Inductive vs Abductive vs Deductive

| Property | Deductive | Inductive | Abductive |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Direction | General $\rightarrow$ Specific | Specific $\rightarrow$ General | Effect $\rightarrow$ Cause |
| Certainty of Conclusion | **Certain** (if premises true) | **Probable** | **Plausible (best explanation)** |
| Monotonicity | Monotonic | Non-monotonic | Non-monotonic |
| Truth-preserving? | Yes | No | No |
| Cognitive role | Verification | Generalization / Learning | Hypothesis generation |
| Logical operator | $\models$ | $\Rightarrow_{\text{ind}}$ | $\Rightarrow_{\text{abd}}$ |
| Use in CPS | Rule-based control, model checking | ML-based prediction, anomaly learning | Root-cause analysis, diagnostics |
| Example | "All men mortal; Socrates is man; $\therefore$ Socrates is mortal." | "Swan 1 white, Swan 2 white, ..., $\therefore$ all swans white." | "Grass wet $\therefore$ rained (best explanation)" |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Default Logic Inference Chain (Reiter)

**Knowledge Base:**

$$ \Delta \;=\; \{(W(x) \rightarrow B(x)),\; \text{Bird(Tweety)}\} \cup \left\{ \frac{B(x) : M F(x)}{F(x)} \right\} $$

**Step 1:** From $\{B(\text{Tweety})\}$ and the default rule, check if $F(\text{Tweety})$ is consistent (i.e., $M F(\text{Tweety})$ is true, meaning $\neg F(\text{Tweety})$ is not provable).

**Step 2:** Since no $\neg F(\text{Tweety})$ exists in $\Delta$, the default fires, producing $F(\text{Tweety})$.

**Step 3:** Extension: $E(\Delta) = \text{Th}(\{W(\text{Tweety}), B(\text{Tweety}), F(\text{Tweety})\})$.

**Step 4 (Non-Monotonicity):** If we add $\neg F(\text{Tweety})$ to $\Delta$, the default's prerequisite $M F$ fails, the default does not fire, and $F(\text{Tweety})$ is **retracted**. The extension shrinks — demonstrating non-monotonicity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Non-Deductive Inference Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Non-Deductive Inference] --> B[Inductive]
    A --> C[Abductive]
    A --> D[Analogical]
    A --> E[Default Non-Monotonic]
    A --> F[Probabilistic]
    A --> G[Fuzzy]

    B --> B1[Specific to General]
    B --> B2[Statistical Generalization]
    B --> B3[Supervised Learning]

    C --> C1[Effect to Best Cause]
    C --> C2[Bayesian Model Selection]
    C --> C3[Diagnostic Reasoning]

    D --> D1[Source to Target Mapping]
    D --> D2[Structure Mapping Theory]
    D --> D3[Case Based Reasoning]

    E --> E1[Defeasible Defaults]
    E --> E2[Reiter Default Logic]
    E --> E3[Non Monotonic Extension]

    F --> F1[Bayesian Networks]
    F --> F2[Markov Models]
    F --> F3[Sensor Fusion]

    G --> G1[Membership Functions]
    G --> G2[Linguistic Variables]
    G --> G3[Fuzzy Control Systems]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Non-Deductive Reasoning Engine in a CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA[Sensor Stream A] --> preProc[Preprocessor]
    sensorB[Sensor Stream B] --> preProc
    sensorC[Sensor Stream C] --> preProc

    preProc --> obsMod[Observation Module]
    obsMod --> abducEngine[Abductive Engine]
    obsMod --> probEngine[Probabilistic Engine]

    kbLayer[Knowledge Base] --> abducEngine
    kbLayer --> probEngine
    kbLayer --> defEngine[Default Reasoner]
    kbLayer --> analogEngine[Analogical Reasoner]

    histDB[Historical Case Database] --> analogEngine
    histDB --> inducEngine[Inductive Learner]

    abducEngine --> fusion[Belief Fusion Layer]
    probEngine --> fusion
    defEngine --> fusion
    analogEngine --> fusion
    inducEngine --> fusion

    fusion --> decisionMod[Decision Module]
    decisionMod --> actuator[Actuator Command]
    decisionMod --> alertMod[Alert Notification]
    decisionMod --> logMod[Explanation Log]
```

### 4.3 Reasoning Flow: From Observation to Action (Abduction + Default)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    obsStart([New Observation O]) --> checkType{O Type?}
    checkType -->|Anomaly| genHyp[Hypothesize Candidate Causes C1..Cn]
    checkType -->|Normal Pattern| inductStep[Update Inductive Model]
    checkType -->|Unknown Domain| analogStep[Retrieve Analogous Base Case]

    genHyp --> scoreHyp[Score via Posterior P Ci given O]
    scoreHyp --> bestExp[Select Best Explanation C star]
    bestExp --> checkDefault{Is Default Applicable?}
    checkDefault -->|Yes| applyDef[Apply Default Rule]
    checkDefault -->|No| skipDef[Proceed with Hypothesis]
    applyDef --> actionStep[Generate Action]
    skipDef --> actionStep
    inductStep --> actionStep
    analogStep --> actionStep
    actionStep --> execActuator([Actuator Command Issued])
```

### 4.4 Default Reasoning State Machine (Non-Monotonic)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initial
    Initial --> DefaultApplied : Apply Default Rule
    DefaultApplied --> ContradictionDetected : New Contradicting Info
    ContradictionDetected --> Retraction : Retract Default
    Retraction --> Initial : Knowledge Base Updated
    DefaultApplied --> Stable : No Contradiction
    Stable --> [*] : Query Resolved
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Differentiate between deductive and non-deductive inference. Give one example of a non-deductive inference used in AI.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Deductive Inference | Non-Deductive Inference |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Direction | General $\rightarrow$ Specific | Various (specific $\rightarrow$ general, effect $\rightarrow$ cause, etc.) |
| Certainty | Certain (if premises are true) | Plausible / probable / defeasible |
| Truth-preserving | Yes | No |
| Monotonicity | Monotonic | Often non-monotonic |

**Example:** Abductive inference — "The grass is wet; therefore, it probably rained" (best explanation reasoning), commonly used in medical diagnosis systems.

*(Award 1 mark for the tabular difference, 1 mark for the certainty/monotonicity distinction, 1 mark for the example.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**State the principle of abductive reasoning. How is it different from induction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Principle of Abduction:** Given an observed effect $O$, abduction infers the most plausible cause $C$ that best explains $O$, typically via $\arg\max_C P(C \mid O)$ using Bayes' theorem.

**Difference from Induction:**

- **Induction** moves from *specific observations to a general rule* (e.g., 100 white swans $\rightarrow$ "all swans are white"). It is used for **learning and generalization**.
- **Abduction** moves from *an observed effect to its best explanation* (e.g., wet grass $\rightarrow$ "it rained"). It is used for **diagnosis and hypothesis generation**.

*(Award 1 mark for the principle, 1 mark for the direction of induction, 1 mark for the direction/role of abduction.)*

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the four major non-deductive inference methods with suitable logical forms and one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CPS monitoring system observes the following: out of 200 sensor samples of a motor's vibration, 150 samples show a "healthy" signature. Using Laplace's rule of succession with $\alpha = 1$, compute the inductive belief that the next sample is healthy. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The four non-deductive inference methods are:

**(i) Inductive Inference:**
- *Logical Form:* $\forall x \in S, P(x) \;/\; \text{observed} \;\Rightarrow_{\text{ind}}\; \forall x \in C, P(x)$
- *Example:* Observing 1000 emails labelled "spam" containing the word "free" leads to the rule: "Emails with 'free' are likely spam." Used in spam classifiers.

**(ii) Abductive Inference:**
- *Logical Form:* $O$ (effect), $C \rightarrow O$ (causal rule) $\Rightarrow_{\text{abd}} C$ (best cause)
- *Example:* A patient presents with chest pain and shortness of breath; the doctor abduces "possible myocardial infarction" as the best explanation. Used in medical diagnosis.

**(iii) Analogical Inference:**
- *Logical Form:* $A \sim B \wedge P(A) \Rightarrow P(B)$ (likely)
- *Example:* Mars is similar to Earth in having atmosphere and water signs; therefore, Mars *might* harbour life. Used in case-based reasoning and scientific hypothesis.

**(iv) Default (Non-Monotonic) Inference:**
- *Logical Form:* $\dfrac{\alpha : M\beta}{\gamma}$
- *Example:* "Typically, a car has four wheels." If we learn the car is a three-wheeler, the default is retracted. Used in commonsense reasoning.

*(Award ~1.5–2 marks per method for logical form + example; deduct marks if no example is given.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the observed values:**

$$ N \;=\; 200, \quad k \;=\; 150, \quad \alpha \;=\; 1 $$

**Step 2 — Apply Laplace's Rule of Succession:**

$$ \hat{\theta} \;=\; \frac{k + \alpha}{N + 2\alpha} \;=\; \frac{150 + 1}{200 + 2} $$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$ \hat{\theta} \;=\; \frac{151}{202} \;\approx\; 0.7475 $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The inductive belief that the next motor vibration sample will be "healthy" is approximately **74.75%**. This is slightly lower than the raw frequency of $150/200 = 0.75$ due to the smoothing effect of the Laplace prior. In CPS practice, this means the system should treat 75% as the confidence level for normal operation, and trigger further diagnostics if the next sample deviates.

*(Award 2 marks for formula identification, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for numerical result, 2 marks for interpretation. Total 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a non-deductive reasoning engine integrated into a Cognitive Cyber-Physical System. Discuss the role of the belief fusion layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a smart-building CPS, three sensors report on room occupancy. Sensor A reports "occupied" with prior probability $P(C_1) = 0.6$ and likelihood $P(O \mid C_1) = 0.8$. Sensor B reports "empty" with prior $P(C_2) = 0.4$ and likelihood $P(O \mid C_2) = 0.3$. Using Bayesian abductive inference, determine the most likely occupancy state given the observation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Architecture (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram):**

A non-deductive reasoning engine in a CPS consists of the following layers:

1. **Sensor Preprocessing Layer:** Cleans and normalizes raw sensor data from heterogeneous sources (cameras, temperature, CO₂, motion, etc.).
2. **Observation Module:** Aggregates preprocessed data into a coherent observation $O$.
3. **Reasoning Engines (Parallel):**
   - **Abductive Engine** — generates candidate explanations.
   - **Probabilistic Engine** — computes posterior beliefs (Bayesian networks).
   - **Default Reasoner** — applies commonsense defaults (e.g., "lights off when no one is in the room").
   - **Analogical Reasoner** — retrieves similar past cases.
   - **Inductive Learner** — continuously updates models from new data.
4. **Knowledge Base:** Houses domain rules, ontologies, prior probabilities, and case library.
5. **Belief Fusion Layer:** Combines the outputs of all engines into a single coherent belief distribution, resolving conflicts via techniques such as *Dempster-Shafer theory*, *weighted majority*, or *Dempster's rule of combination*.
6. **Decision Module:** Maps the fused belief to an actuator command, alert, or log.

**Role of the Belief Fusion Layer:**

The belief fusion layer is the **central coordinator** that:
- Aggregates **heterogeneous reasoning outputs** (probabilities, defaults, analogies, inductive predictions) into a unified belief.
- Handles **conflicts** between engines (e.g., abductive says "fault" but inductive model says "normal").
- Provides **uncertainty quantification** for the decision module.
- Enables **explainability** by logging each engine's contribution.

*(Award 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for component explanation, 2 marks for fusion role. Total 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

$$ P(C_1) = 0.6, \quad P(O \mid C_1) = 0.8 $$
$$ P(C_2) = 0.4, \quad P(O \mid C_2) = 0.3 $$

**Step 2 — Numerator for $C_1$:**

$$ P(O \mid C_1) \cdot P(C_1) \;=\; 0.8 \times 0.6 \;=\; 0.48 $$

**Step 3 — Numerator for $C_2$:**

$$ P(O \mid C_2) \cdot P(C_2) \;=\; 0.3 \times 0.4 \;=\; 0.12 $$

**Step 4 — Marginal Likelihood:**

$$ P(O) \;=\; 0.48 + 0.12 \;=\; 0.60 $$

**Step 5 — Posterior for $C_1$:**

$$ P(C_1 \mid O) \;=\; \frac{0.48}{0.60} \;=\; 0.80 $$

**Step 6 — Posterior for $C_2$:**

$$ P(C_2 \mid O) \;=\; \frac{0.12}{0.60} \;=\; 0.20 $$

**Step 7 — Abductive Decision:**

$$ C^* \;=\; \arg\max_{C_i} P(C_i \mid O) \;=\; C_1 \quad (\text{Occupied}) $$

**Conclusion:** The most likely occupancy state is **"Occupied"** with posterior probability **0.80**. The smart-building CPS should therefore activate HVAC, lighting, and security policies appropriate for an occupied room.

*(Award 2 marks for Bayes' formula, 1.5 marks for numerator/marginal computation, 1.5 marks for posterior calculation, 2 marks for final decision and interpretation. Total 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Induction with Abduction:** Students frequently write "abduction means generalizing from examples." This is **wrong**. Abduction = effect to cause. Induction = specific to general. KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction — losing 1–2 marks here is common.
> 2. **Forgetting the Denominator in Bayes' Theorem:** When applying abductive reasoning, students often compute $P(O \mid C_i) P(C_i)$ but forget to divide by $P(O)$ (the marginal). Without normalization, the result is not a valid probability — full marks require normalization.
> 3. **Ignoring Non-Monotonicity:** In default logic questions, students treat the default as a hard rule. KTU expects explicit mention that the default is **retractable** upon new contradicting information. Failure to mention non-monotonicity costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the Diagram:** In 7-mark architecture questions, KTU awards at least 2–3 marks for a labelled diagram. A textual answer without a block diagram will lose marks even if the description is correct.
> 5. **Unit-less Probabilities:** Always state that probabilities lie in $[0, 1]$. Mentioning this adds a finishing touch valued by examiners.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Non-deductive inference** encompasses **inductive, abductive, analogical, default (non-monotonic), probabilistic, and fuzzy** reasoning.
- **Deductive** reasoning = *truth-preserving*; **Non-deductive** = *plausibility-preserving*.
- **Induction** = Specific $\rightarrow$ General (foundation of ML).
- **Abduction** = Effect $\rightarrow$ Best Cause (foundation of diagnosis and hypothesis generation).
- **Analogy** = Source $\sim$ Target $\rightarrow$ Property transfer (foundation of case-based reasoning).
- **Default logic** = Reiter's rule $\frac{\alpha : M\beta}{\gamma}$ — **non-monotonic** and **defeasible**.
- **Bayesian formula** $P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) P(H)}{P(E)}$ is the workhorse of abductive and probabilistic inference.
- **Laplace's rule** $\hat{\theta} = \frac{k + \alpha}{N + 2\alpha}$ smooths inductive estimates and avoids zero-frequency.
- **Belief fusion** integrates outputs of multiple reasoning engines in a CPS — it is the *coordination hub* of non-deductive AI.
- **Cognitive CPS** requires non-deductive methods because the world is **open, noisy, and partially observable** — pure deduction is insufficient.
- **Always normalize** posterior probabilities; **always state assumptions** (priors, likelihoods, sample sizes) explicitly in KTU answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inconsistencies and Uncertainties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inconsistencies and Uncertainties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** and **Cognitive Computing**, the terms *Inconsistencies* and *Uncertainties* describe two distinct but interrelated challenges that arise when fusing heterogeneous data, models, and decisions across physical, cyber, and human layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inconsistencies in CPS** are defined as *logical, structural, or semantic contradictions* between multiple sources of information (sensors, models, agents, databases) that violate expected coherence, ordering, or constraint relationships.
> **Uncertainties in CPS** are defined as the *lack of deterministic knowledge* about the true state of the system, environment, or future evolution, typically arising from stochastic noise, incomplete information, model approximation, or adversarial perturbations.

### 1.1.1 Sub-Categories of Inconsistency
- **Data Inconsistency**: Conflicting readings from redundant sensors (e.g., two temperature sensors reporting 25 °C and 40 °C for the same point).
- **Model Inconsistency**: Divergent predictions from heterogeneous simulation models (e.g., physics-based vs. data-driven models).
- **Temporal Inconsistency**: Out-of-order or misaligned timestamps due to clock drift, network delay, or buffering.
- **Semantic Inconsistency**: Same datum interpreted differently across subsystems due to incompatible ontologies or unit conventions.
- **Logical / Rule Inconsistency**: Contradiction between rule-based inferences (e.g., expert system concluding A and ¬A simultaneously).

### 1.1.2 Sub-Categories of Uncertainty
- **Aleatoric Uncertainty (Irreducible)**: Inherent stochastic noise in the process (sensor quantization, thermal noise). Cannot be reduced by collecting more data.
- **Epistemic Uncertainty (Reducible)**: Uncertainty due to limited knowledge, sparse training data, or model imperfection. Reducible with more data.
- **Measurement Uncertainty**: Errors introduced by imperfect sensors, calibration drift, or quantization (with standard metric **±2σ** confidence).
- **Model Uncertainty**: Discrepancy between model prediction and reality, often expressed as predictive variance.
- **Environmental / Contextual Uncertainty**: Unknown exogenous disturbances, weather variations, or human behaviour.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: A cognitive CPS must be capable of *self-aware reasoning under both inconsistency and uncertainty*, typically by maintaining confidence-weighted beliefs, propagating uncertainty through reasoning chains, and resolving contradictions through evidence fusion.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor diagnosing a patient with three different symptoms reported by three nurses. Nurse A says "fever," Nurse B says "normal temperature," and Nurse C says "chill." You have **inconsistencies** in the reports themselves. Even if all agreed the patient has a fever, you would not be 100% sure of the *exact* temperature — that gap is **uncertainty**.

In a smart grid CPS, several smart meters may report a household's power consumption. Due to communication noise, time delays, and meter drift:
- **Inconsistencies** = Some meters show consumption > others for the same interval (contradiction).
- **Uncertainties** = Each meter reading has a confidence band (e.g., ±5 %) representing the *doubt* in the value.

A *cognitive* system does not panic; it **fuses** the information, **weighs** each source, **propagates** the doubt, and **decides** — much like a Bayesian detective building a case from imperfect clues.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams**: *Inconsistencies = Contradictions (disagree), Uncertainties = Confidence (doubt).*

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Inconsistencies and Uncertainties Matter in Cognitive CPS

A CPS integrates *computation, communication, and physical processes*. Because the physical world is continuous, noisy, and partially observable, every observation, model, and control action carries some form of imperfection. Without explicit mechanisms to:
1. **Detect** inconsistencies between heterogeneous sources.
2. **Quantify** uncertainty in observations and predictions.
3. **Reason** coherently when data are conflicting or incomplete.

…the CPS cannot exhibit *cognitive* behaviour. The KTU 2024 scheme places heavy emphasis on this triad: **Detection → Quantification → Reasoning**.

## 2.2 Theoretical Pillars of Uncertainty Representation

### 2.2.1 Probabilistic Framework (Bayesian)
Treats uncertainty as a *degree of belief* between 0 and 1.
- **Prior belief** $P(H)$ — knowledge before evidence.
- **Likelihood** $P(E \mid H)$ — probability of evidence given hypothesis.
- **Posterior belief** $P(H \mid E)$ — updated belief after evidence.

### 2.2.2 Dempster–Shafer Evidence Theory
Generalises Bayesian reasoning by distinguishing *belief*, *plausibility*, and *ignorance*. Useful when evidence is incomplete or from independent sources.

### 2.2.3 Fuzzy Set Theory
Uses membership functions $\mu(x) \in [0,1]$ to model *gradual truth* rather than binary truth. Excellent for linguistic / semantic uncertainty (e.g., "warm," "high load").

### 2.2.4 Possibility Theory
Dual of probability, based on fuzzy sets; quantifies what is *possible* vs. *necessary*.

### 2.2.5 Information-Theoretic (Entropy)
Measures *average surprise* of a distribution. Higher entropy $\Rightarrow$ higher uncertainty.

## 2.3 Mechanisms for Inconsistency Resolution

| Mechanism | Operational Logic | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Majority Voting** | Choose the value reported by the majority of sensors. | Simple fault tolerance. |
| **Weighted Fusion** | Combine estimates using reliability weights $w_i$. | Heterogeneous sensors. |
| **Constraint Satisfaction** | Find values that satisfy all relational constraints. | Model-based reasoning. |
| **Belief Revision (AGM)** | Update knowledge base by contracting/expanding on new evidence. | Logic-based AI. |
| **Trust / Reputation Models** | Discount sources based on historical reliability. | Multi-agent systems. |
| **Kalman / Particle Filtering** | Statistically fuse noisy time-series observations. | Dynamic state estimation. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE**: To preserve the markdown table, all absolute-value and conditional-probability symbols use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in place of the vertical bar.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conditional Probability | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | Probability of A given B occurred. |
| 2 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Posterior update of hypothesis H given evidence E. |
| 3 | Law of Total Probability | $P(E) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)$ | Marginalise over mutually exclusive hypotheses. |
| 4 | Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i)\,\log_2 p(x_i)$ | Average information / uncertainty (bits). |
| 5 | Joint Entropy | $H(X,Y) = -\sum_{x,y} p(x,y)\,\log_2 p(x,y)$ | Uncertainty of the pair. |
| 6 | Conditional Entropy | $H(X \mid Y) = H(X,Y) - H(Y)$ | Residual uncertainty in X after observing Y. |
| 7 | Mutual Information | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y)$ | Information shared between X and Y. |
| 8 | Variance of Estimate | $\sigma^{2} = \mathbb{E}[(X-\mu)^{2}]$ | Spread of estimator around its mean. |
| 9 | Standard Error | $\mathrm{SE} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Uncertainty reduction with sample size. |
| 10 | Belief Mass (Dempster–Shafer) | $\sum_{A \subseteq \Theta} m(A) = 1$ | Mass function sums to 1 over frame of discernment. |
| 11 | Dempster's Combination | $m_{1 \oplus 2}(Z) = \dfrac{1}{1-K}\sum_{X \cap Y = Z} m_1(X)\,m_2(Y)$ | Fuse independent evidence sources. |
| 12 | Conflict Factor | $K = \sum_{X \cap Y = \emptyset} m_1(X)\,m_2(Y)$ | Normalisation denominator; $K<1$ required. |
| 13 | Belief Function | $\mathrm{Bel}(A) = \sum_{B \subseteq A} m(B)$ | Total evidence supporting A. |
| 14 | Plausibility Function | $\mathrm{Pl}(A) = \sum_{B \cap A \neq \emptyset} m(B)$ | Evidence not refuting A. |
| 15 | Fuzzy Membership | $\mu_A(x) \in [0,1]$ | Degree of membership of x in fuzzy set A. |
| 16 | α-Cut (Fuzzy) | $A_\alpha = \{x \mid \mu_A(x) \geq \alpha\}$ | Crisp subset at threshold $\alpha \in [0,1]$. |
| 17 | Kalman State Prediction | $\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = F\,\hat{x}_{k-1 \mid k-1} + B\,u_k$ | A-priori state estimate. |
| 18 | Kalman Covariance Prediction | $P_{k \mid k-1} = F\,P_{k-1 \mid k-1}\,F^{T} + Q$ | Propagated uncertainty. |
| 19 | Kalman Gain | $K_k = P_{k \mid k-1}\,H^{T}\,(H\,P_{k \mid k-1}\,H^{T} + R)^{-1}$ | Optimal fusion weight. |
| 20 | Combined Variance (independent) | $\sigma_{\mathrm{fused}}^{2} = \left(\sum_{i} \dfrac{1}{\sigma_i^{2}}\right)^{-1}$ | Precision-weighted fusion. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Sensor fusion of LiDAR, camera, and radar resolves inconsistencies (object detected by camera but not by LiDAR under sunlight) and propagates uncertainty for safe path planning.
- **Smart Grid**: Bayesian state estimation combines noisy smart-meter readings to estimate true demand with quantified confidence intervals.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Dempster–Shafer theory detects faulty sensors in a manufacturing line by tracking conflict factor $K$.
- **Healthcare CPS**: Fuzzy logic models vague medical concepts (e.g., "high blood pressure") for clinical decision support.
- **Aerospace Control**: Kalman filters fuse GPS, INS, and barometer data to maintain accurate altitude under sensor noise.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayes' Theorem from First Principles

We begin with the symmetric definition of joint probability.

**Step 1 — Joint Probability Symmetry**

The probability of two events $A$ and $B$ both occurring is symmetric and can be written in two ways:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A)\,P(B \mid A) = P(B)\,P(A \mid B)
$$

*Explanation*: $P(A \cap B)$ means "A and B both occur." It can be computed as the probability of A times the probability of B *given* A occurred, OR as the probability of B times the probability of A *given* B occurred. Both describe the same joint event.

**Step 2 — Equate the Two Forms**

$$
P(A)\,P(B \mid A) = P(B)\,P(A \mid B)
$$

*Explanation*: Because both sides represent the same joint event, they must be equal.

**Step 3 — Isolate $P(A \mid B)$**

Divide both sides by $P(B)$ (assuming $P(B) > 0$):

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

*Explanation*: We rearrange to express the posterior probability of A given B in terms of the likelihood, prior, and marginal evidence. This is **Bayes' Theorem** in its most cited form.

**Step 4 — Marginalisation of the Denominator**

When the hypothesis space contains $n$ mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive hypotheses $\{H_1, H_2, \dots, H_n\}$, the evidence $E$ is marginalised:

$$
P(E) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)
$$

*Explanation*: The probability of evidence $E$ is the sum of the probabilities of $E$ under every possible hypothesis, weighted by the prior probability of that hypothesis. This converts the formula into a fully computable form:

$$
P(H_i \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j)\,P(H_j)}
$$

*Explanation*: The posterior for any specific hypothesis $H_i$ is normalised by the total evidence probability, ensuring $\sum_i P(H_i \mid E) = 1$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Bayesian Reasoning for CPS Fault Detection

A CPS monitors a turbine. Let $H$ = "turbine faulty," $E$ = "vibration sensor alarm fired." Historical data give:
- Prior: $P(H) = 0.05$.
- Likelihood: $P(E \mid H) = 0.90$.
- False-alarm rate: $P(E \mid \neg H) = 0.10$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Marginal Evidence**

$$
P(E) = P(E \mid H)\,P(H) + P(E \mid \neg H)\,P(\neg H)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$
P(E) = (0.90)(0.05) + (0.10)(0.95)
$$

**Step 3 — Arithmetic Evaluation**

$$
P(E) = 0.045 + 0.095 = 0.140
$$

*Explanation*: The sensor fires on 14 % of all observations overall — most are false alarms because faults are rare.

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' Theorem**

$$
P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(E)} = \frac{0.045}{0.140}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

$$
P(H \mid E) \approx 0.321
$$

*Explanation*: Even with a high likelihood of 0.90, the posterior probability of a real fault given a single alarm is only **32.1 %**. This quantifies the *epistemic uncertainty* the cognitive CPS must reason about.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Dempster–Shafer Combination Rule

Suppose two independent sources of evidence produce belief mass functions $m_1$ and $m_2$ over the same frame of discernment $\Theta$. The combined mass assigned to a focal element $Z$ is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Define the Conjunctive Combination**

The unnormalised joint mass of $Z$ is the sum over all pairs of focal elements that *intersect* to give $Z$:

$$
\tilde{m}(Z) = \sum_{X \cap Y = Z} m_1(X)\,m_2(Y)
$$

*Explanation*: Each independent piece of evidence contributing to $Z$ must come from focal sets whose overlap is exactly $Z$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Conflict**

The total mass assigned to the empty set $\emptyset$ represents outright conflict between the two sources:

$$
K = \sum_{X \cap Y = \emptyset} m_1(X)\,m_2(Y)
$$

*Explanation*: If $K$ is close to 1, the sources strongly disagree and Dempster's rule is unreliable.

**Step 3 — Normalise to a Valid Mass Function**

Divide by the surviving mass $(1 - K)$ to obtain the orthogonal sum:

$$
m_{1 \oplus 2}(Z) = \frac{1}{1 - K}\sum_{X \cap Y = Z} m_1(X)\,m_2(Y)
$$

*Explanation*: The factor $1/(1-K)$ redistributes the conflict mass back to the non-empty focal elements, ensuring $\sum_Z m_{1 \oplus 2}(Z) = 1$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Dempster–Shafer Fusion of Two Sensors

Frame of discernment $\Theta = \{F, \neg F\}$ (Fault, No Fault). Two sensors produce:

- $m_1(\{F\}) = 0.7$, $m_1(\Theta) = 0.3$ (Sensor 1 leans toward fault, 30 % uncertain).
- $m_2(\{\neg F\}) = 0.6$, $m_2(\Theta) = 0.4$ (Sensor 2 leans toward no fault, 40 % uncertain).

**Step 1 — Identify Focal Elements**

| $m_1(X)$ | $m_2(Y)$ | $X \cap Y$ | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\{F\}$ | $\{\neg F\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $0.7 \times 0.6 = 0.42$ |
| $\{F\}$ | $\Theta$ | $\{F\}$ | $0.7 \times 0.4 = 0.28$ |
| $\Theta$ | $\{\neg F\}$ | $\{\neg F\}$ | $0.3 \times 0.6 = 0.18$ |
| $\Theta$ | $\Theta$ | $\Theta$ | $0.3 \times 0.4 = 0.12$ |

**Step 2 — Compute Conflict**

$$
K = 0.42
$$

*Explanation*: 42 % of combined mass is assigned to the empty set — substantial conflict.

**Step 3 — Normalise**

$$
1 - K = 0.58
$$

**Step 4 — Combined Masses**

$$
m_{1 \oplus 2}(\{F\}) = \frac{0.28}{0.58} \approx 0.483
$$

$$
m_{1 \oplus 2}(\{\neg F\}) = \frac{0.18}{0.58} \approx 0.310
$$

$$
m_{1 \oplus 2}(\Theta) = \frac{0.12}{0.58} \approx 0.207
$$

*Explanation*: After fusion, belief in fault ($\approx 48.3\%$) exceeds belief in no-fault ($\approx 31.0\%$), with 20.7 % residual ignorance — the cognitive CPS now has a quantitative basis for its decision.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Shannon Entropy for Uncertainty Quantification

**Step 1 — Information of a Single Event**

Claude Shannon defined the *self-information* of an event with probability $p$ as:

$$
I(x) = -\log_2 p(x)
$$

*Explanation*: Rare events carry more information; certain events ($p=1$) carry zero.

**Step 2 — Expected Information (Entropy)**

Taking the expectation over the distribution yields the average information, called **entropy**:

$$
H(X) = \mathbb{E}[I(X)] = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i)\,\log_2 p(x_i)
$$

*Explanation*: Entropy is maximised when the distribution is uniform ($H_{\max} = \log_2 n$), and zero when the outcome is certain.

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Verification**

Let $X \in \{0,1\}$ with $P(X=1) = 0.25$, $P(X=0) = 0.75$.

$$
H(X) = -(0.25)\log_2 0.25 - (0.75)\log_2 0.75
$$

$$
H(X) = -(0.25)(-2) - (0.75)(-0.415)
$$

$$
H(X) = 0.500 + 0.311 = 0.811 \text{ bits}
$$

*Explanation*: The cognitive system carries about **0.811 bits** of uncertainty about this binary state.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Uncertainty Framework

| Scenario | Recommended Framework | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor noise, time-series, Gaussian errors | **Kalman Filter / Probabilistic** | Closed-form optimal linear estimator. |
| Linguistic or vague concepts | **Fuzzy Logic** | Natural handling of $\mu \in [0,1]$. |
| Independent, possibly conflicting expert sources | **Dempster–Shafer** | Explicit modelling of ignorance. |
| Discrete events with sparse data | **Bayesian Networks** | Conditional independence + priors. |
| Discrete-event simulation outcomes | **Entropy / Information Theory** | Quantifies information content. |
| Multi-agent disagreement | **Trust + Weighted Voting** | Combines reliability with consensus. |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]
**Differentiate between aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty with one CPS example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Aleatoric uncertainty** is the *inherent, irreducible randomness* in a system, arising from physical stochasticity such as sensor thermal noise, wind gusts, or quantum-level effects. It cannot be reduced by gathering more data. *CPS example*: Random packet-loss variance in a wireless sensor network monitoring a bridge.
- **Epistemic uncertainty** is the *reducible uncertainty* due to incomplete knowledge, sparse training data, or model imperfection. It can be reduced by collecting more data or refining the model. *CPS example*: Uncertainty in predicting pedestrian motion in an autonomous vehicle due to insufficient training samples of jaywalking behaviour.
- *Key distinction*: Aleatoric → randomness; Epistemic → ignorance.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]
**List any three sources of inconsistency in a Cyber-Physical System and state one resolution technique for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Data inconsistency** (conflicting sensor readings) → resolved by **weighted fusion** based on sensor reliability.
2. **Temporal inconsistency** (clock drift, out-of-order events) → resolved by **timestamp synchronisation** (e.g., NTP/PTP protocols) and **out-of-order event buffering**.
3. **Logical inconsistency** (contradictory rules) → resolved by **belief revision (AGM theory)** or **priority-based conflict resolution** in rule engines.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern**: Module 2 of PECPT754 carries 14 marks per question. Students must answer *one* full question (with sub-parts a and b). Below are **Choice A** and **Choice B**, both fully solved.

---

### QUESTION A — CHOICE 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Expected Pattern 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Bayesian approach to handling uncertainty in a cognitive CPS. Derive Bayes' theorem and apply it to a real-world CPS scenario (e.g., fault detection in an industrial pump using a vibration sensor).

**(b) [7 Marks)** With neat mathematical expressions, explain how Dempster–Shafer theory handles uncertainty and inconsistency arising from multiple evidence sources. Illustrate with a numerical example involving two sensors detecting a system fault.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Bayesian Approach (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Concept of Bayesian Reasoning** (2 marks):
Bayesian reasoning treats uncertainty as a *degree of belief* (subjective probability) that is updated as new evidence arrives. In a cognitive CPS, the agent maintains a prior belief about system states (e.g., healthy vs. faulty) and updates it through sensor observations using Bayes' theorem. This handles measurement uncertainty and allows principled decision-making under partial observability.

**Step 2 — Derivation of Bayes' Theorem** (2 marks):
Starting from joint probability symmetry:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A)\,P(B \mid A) = P(B)\,P(A \mid B)
$$

Dividing both sides by $P(B)$:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

For multiple hypotheses, marginalising the denominator:

$$
P(H_i \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j)\,P(H_j)}
$$

**Step 3 — CPS Application** (3 marks):
Consider an industrial pump monitored by a vibration sensor.
- Hypothesis: $H$ = "pump is faulty."
- Evidence: $E$ = "vibration alarm triggered."
- Data: $P(H) = 0.04$, $P(E \mid H) = 0.95$, $P(E \mid \neg H) = 0.08$.

Marginal evidence:

$$
P(E) = (0.95)(0.04) + (0.08)(0.96) = 0.038 + 0.0768 = 0.1148
$$

Posterior:

$$
P(H \mid E) = \frac{(0.95)(0.04)}{0.1148} = \frac{0.038}{0.1148} \approx 0.331
$$

*Interpretation*: After observing the alarm, the cognitive CPS updates its belief in a fault from 4 % to **33.1 %**. It may choose to confirm with a second sensor before shutting down the pump, demonstrating rational reasoning under uncertainty.

---

**(b) Dempster–Shafer Theory (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Concept and Terminology** (2 marks):
Dempster–Shafer (D-S) evidence theory generalises Bayesian reasoning by distinguishing *belief*, *plausibility*, and *ignorance*. Each evidence source assigns a **mass function** $m(A)$ to subsets $A$ of the frame of discernment $\Theta$, with $\sum m(A) = 1$. Mass assigned to $\Theta$ represents *ignorance*. D-S can fuse multiple independent evidence sources using Dempster's combination rule and explicitly quantifies inter-source conflict.

**Step 2 — Combination Rule and Belief/Plausibility** (2 marks):

$$
m_{1 \oplus 2}(Z) = \frac{1}{1-K}\sum_{X \cap Y = Z} m_1(X)\,m_2(Y)
$$

$$
K = \sum_{X \cap Y = \emptyset} m_1(X)\,m_2(Y)
$$

$$
\mathrm{Bel}(A) = \sum_{B \subseteq A} m(B)
$$

$$
\mathrm{Pl}(A) = 1 - \mathrm{Bel}(\neg A) = \sum_{B \cap A \neq \emptyset} m(B)
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Illustration** (3 marks):
Frame $\Theta = \{F, \neg F\}$ (Fault, No Fault). Two sensors:
- $m_1(\{F\}) = 0.8$, $m_1(\Theta) = 0.2$
- $m_2(\{\neg F\}) = 0.7$, $m_2(\Theta) = 0.3$

Combination table:

| $m_1(X)$ | $m_2(Y)$ | Intersection | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\{F\}$ | $\{\neg F\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $0.56$ |
| $\{F\}$ | $\Theta$ | $\{F\}$ | $0.24$ |
| $\Theta$ | $\{\neg F\}$ | $\{\neg F\}$ | $0.06$ |
| $\Theta$ | $\Theta$ | $\Theta$ | $0.06$ |

Conflict: $K = 0.56$, so $1 - K = 0.44$.

$$
m_{1 \oplus 2}(\{F\}) = \frac{0.24}{0.44} \approx 0.545
$$

$$
m_{1 \oplus 2}(\{\neg F\}) = \frac{0.06}{0.44} \approx 0.136
$$

$$
m_{1 \oplus 2}(\Theta) = \frac{0.06}{0.44} \approx 0.136
$$

*Belief and Plausibility of Fault*:
$\mathrm{Bel}(\{F\}) = 0.545$, $\mathrm{Pl}(\{F\}) = 0.545 + 0.136 = 0.681$.

*Interpretation*: The cognitive CPS now believes in a fault to degree 0.545, with plausibility 0.681; the gap 0.136 reflects remaining ignorance. The high $K = 0.56$ warns of strong sensor disagreement, prompting further verification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** (Question A):
> 1. **Forgetting the marginal evidence** when applying Bayes — most common deduction (-2 marks). Always expand the denominator using the law of total probability.
> 2. **Using Dempster's rule when $K \geq 1$** — undefined; the rule is invalid. Examiners deduct full marks if this condition is ignored.
> 3. **Confusing belief $\mathrm{Bel}(A)$ with plausibility $\mathrm{Pl}(A)$** — these are *different* quantities. $\mathrm{Bel}(A) \leq \mathrm{Pl}(A)$.

---

### QUESTION B — CHOICE 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Expected Pattern 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the different categories of inconsistencies encountered in Cyber-Physical Systems. For each category, provide one real-world example and a suitable resolution mechanism.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how Shannon entropy is used as a measure of uncertainty in cognitive CPS. Compute the entropy of a sensor observation $X$ that takes values $\{x_1, x_2, x_3\}$ with probabilities $0.5, 0.3, 0.2$. Also compute the entropy if the distribution were uniform over the same three values and comment on the difference.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Categories of Inconsistencies in CPS (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Overview** (1 mark):
A cognitive CPS operates across the physical, cyber, and human layers, integrating heterogeneous sensors, models, and decision agents. Because these components evolve independently, inconsistencies emerge at multiple levels.

**Step 2 — Categorisation with Examples** (5 marks):

1. **Data Inconsistency**: Conflicting raw measurements from redundant or heterogeneous sensors. *Example*: In a smart building, three temperature sensors in the same room report 22 °C, 24 °C, and 19 °C simultaneously. *Resolution*: **Weighted sensor fusion** with weights inversely proportional to historical variance.

2. **Model Inconsistency**: Different predictive models yield divergent forecasts. *Example*: A physics-based thermal model predicts 35 °C, while a data-driven LSTM model predicts 28 °C for a reactor. *Resolution*: **Ensemble methods** averaging predictions weighted by validation performance.

3. **Temporal Inconsistency**: Events arrive out of order or with misaligned timestamps. *Example*: Network delays cause a CPS monitoring patient vitals to receive the heart-rate of *t+2 s* before *t*. *Resolution*: **Time-window synchronisation** using PTP and **re-ordering buffers**.

4. **Semantic Inconsistency**: Same data carries different meaning across subsystems. *Example*: Pressure in kPa vs. psi, or "high load" interpreted differently by energy-management vs. safety modules. *Resolution*: **Ontology alignment** and **unit-conversion middleware**.

5. **Logical / Rule Inconsistency**: Knowledge-based rules generate contradictory conclusions. *Example*: Rule-based system concludes "open valve" and "close valve" simultaneously under overlapping conditions. *Resolution*: **Belief revision (AGM)** with rule prioritisation and **SAT-solver-based consistency checks**.

**Step 3 — Conclusion** (1 mark):
A cognitive CPS must implement *continuous inconsistency detection* and apply a *layered resolution strategy* combining data fusion, model ensembling, temporal alignment, semantic mapping, and logical consistency checks.

---

**(b) Shannon Entropy as a Measure of Uncertainty (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition and Intuition** (2 marks):
Shannon entropy quantifies the *average information* (or *uncertainty*) inherent in a random variable's possible outcomes. For a discrete random variable $X$ with probability mass function $p(x_i)$:

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i)\,\log_2 p(x_i)
$$

Higher entropy indicates greater uncertainty. A deterministic distribution (one outcome with probability 1) has $H = 0$, while the uniform distribution over $n$ outcomes has $H_{\max} = \log_2 n$.

**Step 2 — Computation for Given Distribution** (2 marks):
Distribution: $P(x_1) = 0.5$, $P(x_2) = 0.3$, $P(x_3) = 0.2$.

$$
H(X) = -\bigl[0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.3 \log_2 0.3 + 0.2 \log_2 0.2\bigr]
$$

$$
H(X) = -\bigl[0.5 \times (-1) + 0.3 \times (-1.737) + 0.2 \times (-2.322)\bigr]
$$

$$
H(X) = -\bigl[-0.500 - 0.521 - 0.464\bigr] = 1.485 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Computation for Uniform Distribution** (1 mark):
For uniform distribution over 3 values, $p_i = 1/3$:

$$
H_{\max} = \log_2 3 \approx 1.585 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on the Difference** (2 marks):
The actual entropy (1.485 bits) is *less* than the maximum possible (1.585 bits). This indicates the sensor observation is *partially predictable* — the cognitive CPS can exploit the skewness to make more confident decisions. The *entropy gap* of $0.100$ bits quantifies the information gained from knowing the true non-uniform distribution. If the CPS's belief model used the uniform assumption, it would overestimate uncertainty and act sub-optimally. Therefore, entropy serves not only as a *metric* of uncertainty but also as a *guide* for selecting the most informative next observation (e.g., via information-gain maximisation).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** (Question B):
> 1. **Using $\log$ instead of $\log_2$** — the unit "bits" requires base-2 logarithm. Wrong base costs -1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in front of the sum** — entropy must be non-negative; omitting the minus sign makes the answer negative and is a common error.
> 3. **Confusing "entropy of a system" with "entropy of a process"** — entropy is a property of a *probability distribution*, not of a physical device.
> 4. **In Part (a)**, students often list categories *without examples* or *without resolution mechanisms* — both are mandatory for full 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Module 2: Inconsistencies and Uncertainties**

- ✅ **Inconsistency ≠ Uncertainty** — contradictions vs. confidence gaps. Always distinguish in answers.
- ✅ **Aleatoric** = irreducible randomness; **Epistemic** = reducible ignorance. Give CPS examples for both.
- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem**: $P(H \mid E) = P(E \mid H) P(H) / P(E)$. Always expand $P(E)$ using the law of total probability.
- ✅ **Dempster–Shafer Rule**: requires $K < 1$; otherwise the rule is undefined. Always compute $K$ first.
- ✅ **Belief $\leq$ Plausibility**: $\mathrm{Bel}(A) \leq \mathrm{Pl}(A)$, with the gap = ignorance.
- ✅ **Fuzzy Membership**: $\mu(x) \in [0,1]$ — gradual truth, not probability.
- ✅ **Shannon Entropy**: maximum at uniform distribution, zero at certainty. Always use $\log_2$ for bits.
- ✅ **Five categories of inconsistency**: Data, Model, Temporal, Semantic, Logical — be ready to give examples and resolutions.
- ✅ **Kalman Filter** propagates Gaussian uncertainty; **Dempster–Shafer** propagates general belief masses; **Bayesian** propagates probabilities.
- ✅ **High-yield keywords**: prior, likelihood, posterior, evidence, mass function, focal element, conflict factor, frame of discernment, entropy, mutual information, fuzzy membership, α-cut.
- ✅ **Real-world anchors** for answers: autonomous vehicles, smart grid, industrial IoT, healthcare monitoring, aerospace control.
- ✅ **Common traps**: forgetting marginal evidence in Bayes; using D-S when $K \geq 1$; mixing belief with probability; using $\ln$ instead of $\log_2$ in entropy.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Truth Maintenance Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Truth Maintenance Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Truth Maintenance System (TMS)** is a specialized knowledge representation and reasoning subsystem used in artificial intelligence that explicitly records and maintains the logical dependencies among propositions (beliefs) in a knowledge base. Its fundamental role is to **preserve global consistency**, support **non-monotonic reasoning**, and enable efficient recovery from contradictions through a mechanism known as **dependency-directed backtracking**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A TMS is the "memory layer" of a problem-solver. While a problem-solver (reasoning engine) derives new beliefs from existing ones, the TMS keeps track of *why* each belief is held and *what assumptions* it depends on. When a contradiction is detected, the TMS can selectively retract only the offending beliefs rather than the entire knowledge base. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Courtroom Witness" Intuition Imagine a courtroom where a judge is trying to determine the truth of several statements. Each witness makes claims that may support or contradict other claims. The judge maintains a notebook: - Each **statement** is a "belief" in the system. - Each **justification** is the chain of evidence that supports a statement. - When a witness is proven unreliable (a **contradiction**), the judge doesn't throw out the entire case — only the statements that depended on that witness. The **TMS acts exactly like this judge's notebook** — it tracks *who supports what*, *why we believe it*, and *what to retract* when something goes wrong. > [!NOTE] > **Why TMS is critical in Cognitive Systems and Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS):** > In CPS, sensor readings, control commands, and model predictions must remain logically consistent. If a sensor provides contradictory data, a TMS-like layer in the cognitive controller can isolate the faulty assumption without disrupting the entire decision pipeline. ### 1.3 Two Principal Variants of TMS | Variant | Full Form | Context Strategy | |---|---|---| | **JTMS** | Justification-Based TMS | Maintains a *single* current context; beliefs are in/out of the current set | | **ATMS** | Assumption-Based TMS | Maintains *multiple* parallel contexts simultaneously; finds all consistent worlds | | **LTMS** | Logic-Based TMS | Generalizes JTMS to handle full first-order logic | | **CTMS** | Clause-Management TMS | Operates on propositional clauses (CNF form) | The two most commonly tested in KTU are **JTMS** and **ATMS**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Core Components of a TMS A TMS is built from the following primitive data structures: 1. **Nodes** — Each node represents a *proposition* or *belief* (a logical statement). Every node has: - A unique identifier (e.g., `#N1`, `#N2`) - A *datum* (the actual content: e.g., "Temperature > 100°C") - A **belief status**: `IN` (currently believed) or `OUT` (not believed) 2. **Justifications** — A justification is a *reason* for believing a node. Formally: $$J : \text{IN-list} \rightarrow \text{Node}$$ A justification is a logical conditional: *if all elements in the IN-list are IN, then the node must be IN*. The IN-list is a set of node identifiers that act as antecedents. 3. **Assumptions** — Nodes with *no antecedents*; they are taken as primitive beliefs (premises) that the system can adopt or reject. 4. **Contradictions** — Special nodes labeled with the keyword `contradiction` whose belief status *must remain OUT*; any derivation that forces them IN triggers retraction. 5. **Dependency Network** — The directed graph formed by justifications, encoding the *support structure* of all beliefs. > [!NOTE] > **Monotonic vs Non-Monotonic Reasoning:** Classical logic is *monotonic* — adding premises never reduces the set of conclusions. Human and AI reasoning is often *non-monotonic* — new information can invalidate old conclusions. A TMS is the canonical engineering solution for **non-monotonic reasoning**. ### 2.2 Justification-Based TMS (JTMS) — Operational Logic A **JTMS** works on the principle of *minimal disruption*. When new information arrives: - **Step 1: Assert new node** — the problem-solver adds a new node to the TMS database. - **Step 2: Attach justifications** — provides an IN-list of supporting nodes. - **Step 3: Propagate labels** — the JTMS labels the node `IN` if its justification is satisfied (all antecedents IN), else `OUT`. - **Step 4: Detect contradiction** — if any `contradiction` node is forced IN, the system initiates *dependency-directed backtracking*. - **Step 5: Retract** — identify the *nogood set* (assumptions that jointly caused the contradiction) and toggle them OUT. The key data structure is the **dependency graph**: $$G_{TMS} = (V, E), \quad V = \{\text{nodes}\}, \quad E = \{(\text{antecedent}, \text{consequent}) \mid \text{justification link}\}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Justification-Based"?** The system makes no claim about the *logical truth* of a belief; it only records *what justifies believing it*. A belief is held if and only if it has a *currently-valid justification* — hence "justification-based." ### 2.3 Assumption-Based TMS (ATMS) — Operational Logic A **ATMS** takes a fundamentally different approach: instead of a single moving context, it computes *all* consistent contexts at once. - Each assumption is treated as a Boolean variable. - A **environment** is a set of assumptions that is internally consistent. - For every derived node, the ATMS records the **label** — the set of all minimal environments under which the node holds. - The **nogood** set collects environments known to be inconsistent. Formally, the **label of a node $n$** is: $$L(n) = \{E \subseteq A \mid E \models n \text{ and } E \text{ is consistent}\}$$ where $A$ is the set of all assumptions and $E$ is an *environment* (a subset of $A$). > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** A JTMS maintains exactly **one** current belief set; an ATMS maintains **all** belief sets simultaneously. JTMS is *faster* for single-line reasoning; ATMS is *exponentially more powerful* for hypothesis exploration (e.g., medical diagnosis, fault analysis in CPS). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Symbolic Form | Description | |---|---|---| | Node | $N_i = \langle id, datum, status \rangle$ | Atomic belief with IN/OUT state | | Justification | $J_k : IN(k) \rightarrow N_j$ | Antecedents $\rightarrow$ consequent | | JTMS Label Update | $status(N_j) = IN \iff \exists J_k : \forall N_i \in IN(k), status(N_i) = IN$ | Believed iff some justification is satisfied | | ATMS Label | $L(n) = \{E \subseteq \mathcal{A} \mid E \models n, E \not\models \bot\}$ | All consistent environments implying $n$ | | Nogood Set | $\mathcal{N} \subseteq 2^{\mathcal{A}}$ | Sets of assumptions known inconsistent | | Environment | $E \subseteq \mathcal{A}$ | A candidate context (set of assumptions) | | Dependency-Directed Backtracking | $\Delta(\mathcal{N}) \rightarrow \text{retract } E$ | Targeted retraction, no chronological backtracking | | Monotonicity | $\Sigma \subseteq \Sigma' \implies Cn(\Sigma) \subseteq Cn(\Sigma')$ | Holds in classical logic only | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Do not confuse the vertical bar symbol.** In the formulas above, $\models$ and $\subseteq$ are *set-theoretic* operators, not "or".
