Always use $\vert$ in LaTeX when writing $|x|$ or $\mathcal{A} \mid E$ — never the raw pipe `|`, which breaks markdown tables. ### 2.5 Real-World Utility of TMS in Engineering 1. **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS):** Reconciling conflicting sensor streams; fault-isolating cognitive controllers. 2. **Autonomous Vehicles:** Maintaining consistent beliefs about road state, traffic, and other agents. 3. **Medical Diagnosis AI:** ATMS allows simultaneous exploration of multiple differential diagnoses. 4. **Database Systems:** Maintaining integrity constraints with view-update transactions. 5. **Automated Planning & Scheduling:** Replanning after a goal conflict without rebuilding the plan. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — JTMS Belief Propagation **Scenario:** A cognitive CPS controller in a smart building must decide whether to *activate the fire alarm*. It holds the following beliefs: | Node | Datum | Justification (IN-list → Node) | |---|---|---| | `#N1` | Smoke detected | Assumption (primitive) | | `#N2` | Heat sensor high | Assumption (primitive) | | `#N3` | Fire probable | $\{{N1}, {N2}\} \rightarrow #N3$ | | `#N4` | Activate alarm | $\{{N3}\} \rightarrow #N4$ | | `#N5` | Test mode active | Assumption (primitive) | | `#N6` | Suppress alarm | $\{{N5}\} \rightarrow #N6$ | | `#C1` | Contradiction (alarm AND suppressed) | $\{{N4}, {N6}\} \rightarrow #C1$ | **Step 1:** Initially, the user asserts assumptions `#N1 = IN`, `#N2 = IN`, `#N5 = IN`. **Step 2:** The JTMS propagates labels. Since `#N1` and `#N2` are both IN, justification for `#N3` is satisfied: $$status(\#N3) = IN \iff \{status(\#N1)=IN \land status(\#N2)=IN\}$$ $$status(\#N3) = IN \iff \{IN \land IN\} = IN \checkmark$$ **Step 3:** Since `#N3 = IN`, justification for `#N4` is satisfied: $$status(\#N4) = IN \iff \{status(\#N3)=IN\} = IN \checkmark$$ **Step 4:** Since `#N5 = IN`, justification for `#N6` is satisfied: $$status(\#N6) = IN \iff \{status(\#N5)=IN\} = IN \checkmark$$ **Step 5:** Contradiction check — both `#N4` and `#N6` are IN, so `#C1`'s justification is satisfied. A contradiction is flagged. **Step 6:** Dependency-directed backtracking. The JTMS walks the dependency graph backward from `#C1` and identifies the *minimal nogood set*: $$\mathcal{N} = \{\{\#N1, \#N2, \#N5\}\}$$ **Step 7:** The system toggles the *cheapest* assumption to OUT. If `#N5` (test mode) is a low-cost assumption to retract, the system flips `#N5 = OUT`, which then propagates: $$status(\#N6) = OUT \quad (\text{since its only antecedent } \#N5 = OUT)$$ $$status(\#C1) = OUT \quad (\text{justification no longer satisfied})$$ **Result:** The system now consistently believes `{Smoke, Heat, Fire, Alarm}` and `{Test_mode_retracted, No_suppression}`. > [!NOTE] > **Why this is "intelligent" rollback:** A naive system would either re-derive from scratch or use chronological backtracking. The TMS performs a *targeted* retraction costing $O(\text{depth of dependency chain})$, completely avoiding the combinatorial explosion of trying all combinations of assumptions. ### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — JTMS vs ATMS | Parameter | JTMS | ATMS | |---|---|---| | **Contexts maintained** | Exactly one (current) | All consistent contexts simultaneously | | **Primary data structure** | Single dependency graph | Label lattice over assumption sets | | **Update operation** | Retract beliefs, re-propagate | Augment labels with new environments | | **Best suited for** | Single-line deductive reasoning | Hypothesis search, diagnosis, planning | | **Computational cost** | Linear in graph size | Exponential in worst case ($2^{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$) | | **Contradiction handling** | Dependency-directed backtrack | Nogood caching | | **Information loss** | Possible (retracted beliefs lost from current context) | None (all consistent views preserved) | | **Typical use case** | Real-time CPS controllers | Medical AI, VLSI verification | | **Introduced by** | Doyle (1979) | de Kleer (1986) | | **In KTU 2024 syllabus** | Yes — core topic | Yes — core topic | ### 3.3 Proof Sketch — ATMS Label Monotonicity **Claim:** If new assumptions are added, the label of a node can only *grow* (more environments become possible), never shrink, modulo the removal of nogoods. **Derivation:** Let $\mathcal{A}' = \mathcal{A} \cup \{a_{\text{new}}\}$ be the enlarged assumption set, and $L'(n)$ the new label. $$L'(n) = \{E' \subseteq \mathcal{A}' \mid E' \models n,\ E' \not\models \bot\}$$ For any environment $E \subseteq \mathcal{A}$ in the old label, consider $E' = E \cup \{a_{\text{new}}\}$ in the new label: $$E' \subseteq \mathcal{A}', \quad E' \supseteq E \implies E' \models n \text{ (since } E \models n \text{ and } a_{\text{new}} \text{ is irrelevant)}$$ If $E'$ is consistent (not in $\mathcal{N}'$), then $E' \in L'(n)$. Therefore: $$L(n) \subseteq L'(n) \quad \text{(modulo nogood pruning)}$$ This is the **monotonicity of the ATMS label under assumption augmentation**, a critical property guaranteeing that the ATMS does not lose valid contexts as the system evolves. $\blacksquare$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to *prove* any property of a TMS, always anchor the proof to the formal label definition $L(n) = \{E \subseteq \mathcal{A} \mid E \models n\}$. The label is the single most important mathematical object in TMS theory. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a TMS-Enabled Reasoner ```mermaid flowchart TB KB[Knowledge Base
Domain Facts and Rules] PS[Problem Solver
Inference Engine] TMS[Truth Maintenance System
Dependency Tracker] DG[Dependency Graph
Nodes and Justifications] NG[Nogood Cache
Inconsistent Environments] LB[Label Store
Belief Status Table] CON[Contradiction Detector] RET[Retraction Engine
Dependency Directed Backtracking] OUT[Reasoner Output
Consistent Belief Set] KB -->|Facts| PS PS -->|Assert node with justification| TMS TMS -->|Read/Write| DG TMS -->|Update| LB DG -->|Trigger on conflict| CON CON -->|Report nogood| NG NG -->|Select minimal set| RET RET -->|Toggle assumptions| LB RET -->|Re-propagate| DG LB -->|Current IN set| PS PS -->|Derived belief| OUT NG -->|Inform| TMS ``` ### 4.2 JTMS Belief Propagation State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Out Out --> In: justification satisfied
all antecedents IN In --> Out: justification lost
antecedent retracted Out --> Contradiction: contradiction node forced IN In --> Contradiction: contradiction node forced IN Contradiction --> Out: dependency directed backtrack
nogood assumption toggled OUT Out --> [*]: assumption explicitly retracted In --> [*]: assumption explicitly retracted note right of Contradiction Triggers nogood identification and selective retraction end note ``` ### 4.3 ATMS Multiple-Context Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Assumption A1] A2[Assumption A2] A3[Assumption A3] AX[Assumption An] N1[Node N1
label L1] N2[Node N2
label L2] N3[Node N3
label L3] NM[Node Nm
label Lm] ENV1[Environment E1 equals A1,A2] ENV2[Environment E2 equals A1,A3] ENV3[Environment E3 equals A2,A3] CONS[Consistent Contexts] NG[Nogood Environments
Cached and Pruned] A1 --> N1 A2 --> N1 A1 --> N2 A3 --> N2 A2 --> N3 A3 --> N3 A3 --> NM AX --> NM N1 --> CONS N2 --> CONS N3 --> CONS NM --> CONS A1 --> ENV1 A2 --> ENV1 A1 --> ENV2 A3 --> ENV2 A2 --> ENV3 A3 --> ENV3 ENV1 --> NG ENV2 --> NG ENV3 --> NG CONS -->|All E in Ln| OUT_ATMS[ATMS Output
Multi Context Belief Map] NG -->|Prune| OUT_ATMS ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading guide for the diagrams:** In the architecture diagram, the **TMS sits between the problem-solver and the knowledge base**, acting as a memory and consistency layer. In the ATMS diagram, each environment is a *hypothetical world*; the ATMS keeps all worlds alive in parallel and lets the consumer choose the appropriate one for the task. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** **Q1. Define a Truth Maintenance System. Why is it needed in cognitive systems?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Truth Maintenance System (TMS) is a knowledge representation subsystem that explicitly records the justifications and dependencies among beliefs in a knowledge base, enabling the system to maintain global consistency, perform non-monotonic reasoning, and recover efficiently from contradictions through dependency-directed backtracking. It is needed in cognitive systems because real-world reasoning is non-monotonic — new evidence may invalidate prior conclusions — and a naive system would either retain contradictions or suffer from a combinatorial explosion during retraction. The TMS provides a principled mechanism for *selective* belief revision, which is essential for autonomous decision-making in CPS, robotics, and diagnostic AI. **(3 marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** **Q2. Distinguish between a JTMS and an ATMS in two points.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. A **JTMS (Justification-Based TMS)** maintains only the *current* set of beliefs in a single context, and retracts beliefs when their justifications are violated. An **ATMS (Assumption-Based TMS)** maintains *all* consistent belief sets simultaneously by computing a label (set of environments) for every node. 2. A JTMS uses **dependency-directed backtracking** for contradiction recovery, while an ATMS uses **nogood caching and label intersection** — never retracting, only pruning inconsistent environments. JTMS is suitable for single-line reasoning; ATMS is suitable for hypothesis-space exploration. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme, Model]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the architecture of a Justification-Based Truth Maintenance System (JTMS) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of *justifications* and the *dependency-directed backtracking* mechanism. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A Justification-Based TMS, introduced by **Doyle (1979)**, is a memory and consistency layer attached to a problem-solver. Its architecture consists of the following blocks: 1. **Node Store** — holds all propositions (beliefs). Each node has a unique identifier (e.g., `#N1`), a datum, and a belief status `IN` or `OUT`. 2. **Justification Table** — for each non-primitive node, stores one or more justifications of the form $J : IN\text{-list} \rightarrow N$. 3. **Dependency Graph** — a directed graph $G=(V,E)$ where each justification is an edge from antecedents to consequent. 4. **Label Propagation Engine** — repeatedly enforces the rule: a node is `IN` if and only if at least one of its justifications has all its antecedents `IN`. 5. **Contradiction Detector** — monitors `contradiction` nodes; if any are forced `IN`, it triggers retraction. 6. **Retraction Engine** — performs **dependency-directed backtracking**: walks the dependency graph backward from the contradiction, identifies the *nogood set* (the minimal set of assumptions that jointly caused the contradiction), and toggles one or more of them to `OUT`. *Role of Justifications:* A justification is not a logical proof; it is a *record of why the problem-solver currently believes a node*. As long as the antecedents remain `IN`, the node stays `IN`. The moment any antecedent flips, the JTMS re-evaluates and may flip the consequent. *Dependency-Directed Backtracking:* Unlike chronological backtracking (which undoes the most recent choice and tries the next), dependency-directed backtracking follows the *dependency chain* directly to the offending assumption. This avoids the *thrashing* problem of chronological backtracking and is exponentially more efficient in deeply nested reasoning. **(7 marks — full marks for explaining all six blocks, the role of justifications, and the contrast with chronological backtracking)** **(b)** Consider the following knowledge base in a JTMS. Trace the belief propagation and explain what happens when a contradiction is detected. | Node | Datum | Justification | |---|---|---| | `#N1` | Pressure_high | Assumption | | `#N2` | Valve_stuck | Assumption | | `#N3` | System_overload | `{N1, N2} → N3` | | `#N4` | Emergency_shutdown | `{N3} → N4` | | `#N5` | Maintenance_mode | Assumption | | `#N6` | Override_shutdown | `{N5} → N6` | | `#C1` | Contradiction | `{N4, N6} → C1` | Initial assertions: `#N1 = IN`, `#N2 = IN`, `#N5 = IN`. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Initial assertions:** The problem-solver asserts the three assumptions: pressure is high (`#N1 = IN`), valve is stuck (`#N2 = IN`), and maintenance mode is active (`#N5 = IN`). **Step 2 — Forward propagation of `#N3`:** The justification for `#N3` is satisfied because both antecedents `#N1` and `#N2` are `IN`. Therefore `status(#N3) = IN`. **Step 3 — Propagation of `#N4`:** The justification for `#N4` requires `#N3 = IN`, which holds. So `status(#N4) = IN`. **Step 4 — Propagation of `#N6`:** The justification for `#N6` requires `#N5 = IN`, which holds. So `status(#N6) = IN`. **Step 5 — Contradiction detection:** The justification for `#C1` requires both `#N4 = IN` and `#N6 = IN`. Both are true, so `#C1` is forced `IN`. The contradiction detector is triggered. **Step 6 — Nogood identification:** The JTMS walks the dependency graph backward from `#C1`. The two branches converge on assumptions `{N1, N2, N5}`. The minimal nogood set is identified as: $$\mathcal{N} = \{\{N1, N2, N5\}\}$$ **Step 7 — Targeted retraction:** The JTMS selects the *cheapest* assumption in the nogood set to flip. Since `#N5` (maintenance mode) is a low-impact assumption (the controller can simply log and continue), the system toggles `status(#N5) = OUT`. **Step 8 — Re-propagation:** The flip of `#N5` invalidates the justification of `#N6` (its sole antecedent is now `OUT`). So `status(#N6) = OUT`. With `#N6 = OUT`, the justification of `#C1` is no longer satisfied, so `status(#C1) = OUT`. The contradiction is resolved. **Final consistent belief set:** `{N1, N2, N3, N4} = IN` and `{N5, N6, C1} = OUT`. The system consistently believes: *pressure is high, valve is stuck, system is overloaded, emergency shutdown is active*, and maintenance mode has been retracted. **(7 marks — full marks for stepwise propagation, correct nogood identification, and correct final state)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Describe the structure and operational principles of an **Assumption-Based Truth Maintenance System (ATMS)**. How does it differ from a JTMS in handling multiple hypotheses? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** An Assumption-Based TMS, introduced by **de Kleer (1986)**, abandons the single-context model of JTMS in favor of *concurrent multi-context reasoning*. Its structure consists of: 1. **Assumption Set $\mathcal{A}$** — a finite set of primitive, declarable propositions. Each assumption is a candidate hypothesis. 2. **Node Set** — derived propositions, each carrying a **label** $L(n)$ — a set of environments under which the node holds. 3. **Environment** $E \subseteq \mathcal{A}$ — a set of assumptions defining a hypothetical world. 4. **Justifications** — logical rules that propagate environments from antecedents to consequents. 5. **Nogood Set $\mathcal{N}$** — a cached set of environments known to be inconsistent. 6. **Label Update Rules** — when a new justification is added, the label of the consequent is updated by: $$L(n) \leftarrow \{E \cup E_a \mid E \in L(n), E_a \in L(a_1) \times \cdots \times L(a_k)\} \setminus \mathcal{N}$$ **Operational principle:** Every time a new assumption is declared or a new justification is added, the ATMS *augments* labels rather than retracting them. A node is *believed* in environment $E$ iff $E \in L(n)$. Contradictions are *pruned* (the offending environment is added to the nogood set) but never *forgotten*. **Difference from JTMS in handling multiple hypotheses:** A JTMS cannot natively consider two contradictory hypotheses in the same database — it must commit to one and retract the other. An ATMS, by contrast, can keep *both* hypotheses alive by recording them in disjoint environments. For example, in a medical diagnosis, a JTMS must choose between "Disease A" and "Disease B" upon conflicting evidence; an ATMS computes $L(\text{Disease A}) = \{\{A_{1}\}, \{A_{1}, A_{2}\}\}$ and $L(\text{Disease B}) = \{\{B_{1}\}\}$, allowing downstream reasoning to select the appropriate context. **(7 marks — full marks for ATMS components, label update rule, and clear contrast with JTMS)** **(b)** For the following ATMS, compute the label of every node. Assumptions: $A_1 =$ "M1 is OK", $A_2 =$ "M2 is OK", $A_3 =$ "M3 is OK". | Node | Datum | Justification | |---|---|---| | `#N1` | M1 works | $A_1 \rightarrow N1$ | | `#N2` | M2 works | $A_2 \rightarrow N2$ | | `#N3` | M3 works | $A_3 \rightarrow N3$ | | `#N4` | System_works | $(\{N1, N2, N3\}) \rightarrow N4$ | | `#N5` | Sensor_fault | $A_1 \rightarrow N5$ | | `#C1` | Contradiction | $(\{N4, N5\}) \rightarrow C1$ | Nogood: $\{A_1, A_2, A_3, A_1\} \Rightarrow \{A_1\}$ (i.e., $A_1$ alone is inconsistent with $N4$ and $N5$ together). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Initial labels for assumptions:** $$L(A_1) = \{\{A_1\}\}, \quad L(A_2) = \{\{A_2\}\}, \quad L(A_3) = \{\{A_3\}\}$$ **Step 2 — Labels for `#N1, #N2, #N3, #N5`:** $$L(\#N1) = \{\{A_1\}\}$$ $$L(\#N2) = \{\{A_2\}\}$$ $$L(\#N3) = \{\{A_3\}\}$$ $$L(\#N5) = \{\{A_1\}\}$$ **Step 3 — Label of `#N4`:** The justification $\{N1, N2, N3\} \rightarrow N4$ requires *all three* antecedents. The cross-product of their environments is: $$L(\#N1) \times L(\#N2) \times L(\#N3) = \{\{A_1\}\} \times \{\{A_2\}\} \times \{\{A_3\}\} = \{\{A_1, A_2, A_3\}\}$$ Therefore $L(\#N4) = \{\{A_1, A_2, A_3\}\}$. **Step 4 — Label of `#C1`:** The justification $\{N4, N5\} \rightarrow C1$ requires both antecedents: $$L(\#N4) \times L(\#N5) = \{\{A_1, A_2, A_3\}\} \times \{\{A_1\}\} = \{\{A_1, A_2, A_3\}\}$$ So $L(\#C1) = \{\{A_1, A_2, A_3\}\}$ *before* nogood application. **Step 5 — Nogood application:** The environment $\{A_1, A_2, A_3\}$ is added to $\mathcal{N}$ (it forces both `#N4 = IN` and `#N5 = IN`, which contradicts the domain rule that the system cannot simultaneously be working and have a sensor fault under assumption $A_1$). So: $$L(\#C1) \leftarrow \{\{A_1, A_2, A_3\}\} \setminus \mathcal{N} = \emptyset$$ **Step 6 — Final labels:** | Node | Final Label | |---|---| | `#N1` | $\{\{A_1\}\}$ | | `#N2` | $\{\{A_2\}\}$ | | `#N3` | $\{\{A_3\}\}$ | | `#N4` | $\{\{A_1, A_2, A_3\}\}$ (still in label, just not in a consistent world) | | `#N5` | $\{\{A_1\}\}$ | | `#C1` | $\emptyset$ (no consistent environment supports it) | **Interpretation:** `#N4` is *only* derivable in the inconsistent environment $\{A_1, A_2, A_3\}$; once that environment is pruned, `#N4` has no consistent label. In a JTMS, this would have caused retraction; in the ATMS, the label is preserved but effectively empty, allowing the system to "remember" that this derivation exists. **(7 marks — full marks for correct cross-products, nogood pruning, and final labels)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:** > 1. **Confusing node IDs with content** — never write `IN(N1) = Pressure_high`; write `status(#N1) = IN`, datum = "Pressure_high". Examiners explicitly check for correct notation. > 2. **Forgetting the cross-product in ATMS** — when a justification has multiple antecedents, you must compute the **Cartesian product** of their labels, not the union. > 3. **Skipping the nogood step** — after computing $L(\#C1)$, students often stop. You must *explicitly subtract* $\mathcal{N}$ from the label. Marks are deducted if the nogood application is omitted. > 4. **Mixing up JTMS retraction with ATMS pruning** — JTMS *removes* beliefs from the current context; ATMS *preserves* all labels and only prunes inconsistent environments. Confusing these is a 3-mark penalty. > 5. **Using chronological backtracking in explanations** — the whole point of TMS is *dependency-directed* backtracking. Examiners look for the word "dependency-directed." > 6. **Using raw `|` in formulas** — in markdown tables, this breaks rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **TMS = belief-dependency tracker** attached to a problem-solver for non-monotonic reasoning. - **Two main types:** JTMS (single context, justification-based) and ATMS (multi-context, assumption-based). - **Core elements of every TMS:** nodes, justifications, assumptions, contradictions, dependency graph. - **Justification format:** $J : \{\text{IN-list}\} \rightarrow N$. A node is `IN` iff at least one justification is fully satisfied. - **JTMS conflict resolution:** *Dependency-directed backtracking* identifies the *nogood set* and retracts only the offending assumption. - **ATMS key data structure:** the **label** $L(n) = \{E \subseteq \mathcal{A} \mid E \models n,\ E \not\models \bot\}$. - **ATMS contradiction handling:** *Nogood caching* — inconsistent environments are stored in $\mathcal{N}$ and subtracted from all labels. - **JTMS = fast, single-line; ATMS = powerful, hypothesis-space search.** - **JTMS introduced by Doyle (1979); ATMS by de Kleer (1986).** - **Monotonicity of ATMS labels:** adding assumptions can only grow labels (modulo nogood pruning). - **Real-world uses:** CPS fault isolation, autonomous driving, medical diagnosis, automated planning, database integrity. - **Key KTU words to use in answers:** *justification, dependency, nogood, environment, label, non-monotonic, dependency-directed backtracking*. - **Always show propagation step-by-step** in derivations — KTU examiners reward clean, traceable logic. - **Cartesian product rule for ATMS:** $L(\text{consequent}) \supseteq L(a_1) \times L(a_2) \times \cdots \times L(a_k)$, then intersect/union with existing label, then subtract $\mathcal{N}$. - **LTMS and CTMS** are logical and clause-based extensions — know their existence for completeness questions. - **TMS is essential for cognitive systems** because human-like reasoning is fundamentally non-monotonic; classical logic engines are insufficient.

topics.title: Default Reasoning and Closed World Assumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Default Reasoning and Closed World Assumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Default Reasoning — Formal Definition

**Default Reasoning** is a form of *non-monotonic reasoning* introduced by **Raymond Reiter (1980)** that permits the derivation of plausible conclusions based on the **absence of contrary evidence**. It allows an intelligent system to "jump to conclusions" using *default rules* of the form:

$$\frac{\alpha : \beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots, \beta_n}{\gamma}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ is the **prerequisite** (must be known/derivable),
- $\beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots, \beta_n$ are the **justifications** (assumed to be consistent/possible),
- $\gamma$ is the **consequent** (what we conclude by default).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Default rules are *defeasible* — meaning they can be *retracted* (defeated) if new contradictory information arrives. This is the hallmark of **non-monotonic** logic, contrasting with classical first-order logic where the set of theorems only grows.

## 1.2 Closed World Assumption (CWA) — Formal Definition

The **Closed World Assumption (CWA)** is a *non-monotonic inference principle* introduced by **Reiter (1978)**. It postulates that *any atomic sentence that cannot be logically derived from the knowledge base is assumed to be **false***.

Formally, for a knowledge base $\Sigma$:

$$\text{CWA}(\Sigma) = \Sigma \cup \{\neg P \mid P \text{ is a ground atom and } \Sigma \nvdash P\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negation as Failure (NAF):** In CWA, the inability to prove a statement is treated as evidence *for* its negation. This principle underlies **Prolog's `not` operator** and relational database query semantics.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Analogy 1 — Default Reasoning: "The Rule of Thumb Lawyer"
Imagine a **lawyer in a courtroom** who, by default, *assumes the defendant is innocent* unless proven otherwise. The rule looks like:

> *"If the accused has a clean record **AND** there is no evidence of guilt, **THEN** assume innocence."*

- The **prerequisite** (`clean record`) must be established.
- The **justification** (`no evidence of guilt`) is checked for *consistency*, not truth.
- The **consequent** (`innocence`) is drawn by default.
- If a witness suddenly testifies for the prosecution, the default is *defeated* — the rule retracts.

### Analogy 2 — CWA: "The Strict School Teacher"
Imagine a **teacher's attendance register**. The teacher starts with a *blank assumption* — every student is **absent** — and then *only* marks the present ones. Any student *not* marked present is **assumed absent**.

> *"If 'Ram is present' is not provable from the register, **then** conclude 'Ram is absent'."*

This is the essence of CWA: **what is not known to be true is taken to be false**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance in CPS (Cyber-Physical Systems):** In autonomous vehicles, smart grids, and industrial IoT, CWA enables *rapid decision-making* under incomplete sensor data — "if no collision is detected, assume the path is clear." Default reasoning handles *anomaly-tolerant control* — "use PID control by default unless the system shows chaotic behavior."

---

## 1.4 Relationship with Open World Assumption (OWA)

| Aspect | **Closed World Assumption (CWA)** | **Open World Assumption (OWA)** |
|---|---|---|
| Unknown atom | Treated as **FALSE** | Treated as **UNKNOWN** (may be true or false) |
| Use case | **Databases, Prolog, expert systems** | **Semantic Web, OWL/RDF, ontologies** |
| Reasoning style | Non-monotonic | Monotonic (safe) |
| Adds negation? | Yes (active inference) | No (remains neutral) |

> [!NOTE]
> In **Cognitive CPS**, default reasoning combined with CWA underpins the *cognitive layer* of systems like **IBM Watson, autonomous drones, and medical decision-support systems**, where plausible reasoning under uncertainty is mission-critical.

---

## 1.5 Why These Concepts Matter in Cognitive AI

Classical first-order logic is **monotonic**: adding more axioms can only *increase* the set of theorems, never retract them. However, **human cognition is non-monotonic** — we revise beliefs constantly. Default reasoning and CWA are foundational pillars that bridge this gap, enabling:

1. **Plausible reasoning** (commonsense inference)
2. **Efficient decision-making** (negation as failure)
3. **Belief revision** in dynamic knowledge bases
4. **Cognitive modeling** in CPS for robust autonomy

These topics map directly to **KTU Course Outcome CO2** — *Apply non-monotonic reasoning techniques in cognitive CPS architectures*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reiter's Default Logic — Operational Mechanics

A **default theory** $\Delta$ is the pair:

$$\Delta = \langle D, W \rangle$$

where:
- $W$ is a set of **first-order logic formulas** (the "axioms" or facts).
- $D$ is a set of **default rules** of the form:
  $$d = \frac{\alpha : \beta}{\gamma}$$

### 2.1.1 Extension of a Default Theory

An **extension** $E$ of $\Delta = \langle D, W \rangle$ is a fixed point of the operator $\Gamma$:

$$E = \Gamma(E)$$

where $\Gamma(E)$ is the smallest set of formulas satisfying:
1. $W \subseteq \Gamma(E)$
2. $\Gamma(E)$ is *deductively closed* (closed under logical consequence)
3. For any default $\frac{\alpha : \beta}{\gamma} \in D$ such that $\alpha \in \Gamma(E)$ and $\neg \beta \notin E$, then $\gamma \in \Gamma(E)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** A default rule is *applicable* if the prerequisite $\alpha$ is already known in $E$, and the *negation* of the justification $\beta$ cannot be derived from $E$. The "**:**" denotes "is consistent with" — not strict entailment.

### 2.1.2 Types of Default Rules

| Type | Syntax | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal** | $\frac{\alpha : \beta}{\gamma}$ | Justification = consequent ($\beta = \gamma$) | Most common; "birds fly" |
| **Semi-normal** | $\frac{\alpha : \beta \wedge \gamma}{\gamma}$ | Extra condition added | "Birds typically fly **unless penguins**" |
| **Unrestricted** | $\frac{\alpha : \beta}{\gamma}$ | General case | Rare, complex semantics |
| **Prerequisite-free** | $\frac{:\beta}{\gamma}$ | No prerequisite | "Assume cooperation" |
| **Justification-free** | $\frac{\alpha :}{\gamma}$ | Inferential default | CWA-like behavior |

## 2.2 Closed World Assumption — Deep Mechanics

### 2.2.1 Formal Definition

Given a knowledge base $\Sigma$ in first-order logic, the CWA closure is:

$$\text{CWA}(\Sigma) = \Sigma \cup \{\neg P(\vec{a}) \mid P(\vec{a}) \text{ is a ground atom}, \Sigma \nvdash P(\vec{a})\}$$

The set of *ground atoms that are not entailed* is often called the **negation set**:

$$\text{Neg}(\Sigma) = \{A \mid A \text{ is a ground atom and } \Sigma \nvdash A\}$$

Then:
$$\text{CWA}(\Sigma) = \Sigma \cup \text{Neg}(\Sigma)$$

### 2.2.2 Properties of CWA

1. **Decidability:** If $\Sigma$ is *definite* (no function symbols, only Horn clauses), CWA is decidable.
2. **Inconsistency Issue:** CWA can yield **inconsistency** when the knowledge base has *implicit dependencies* (e.g., two complementary axioms both unprovable).
3. **Generalized CWA (GCWA):** Proposed to handle inconsistencies by only negating atoms whose *negation is consistent* with $\Sigma$.

> [!NOTE]
> **GCWA Formula:** For atom $A$, add $\neg A$ to the closure **only if** $\Sigma \cup \{\neg A\}$ is consistent. This avoids contradiction in cyclic knowledge bases.

### 2.2.3 Variants of CWA

| Variant | Acronym | Key Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard CWA** | CWA | Negate all non-provable atoms |
| **Generalized CWA** | GCWA | Negate only if consistency is preserved |
| **Extended CWA** | ECWA | Adds unique-name assumption and handles disjunctions |
| **Disjunctive CWA** | DCWA | Handles disjunctive databases |
| **Careful CWA** | CCWA | Cautious negation; never negates *relevant* atoms |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready reference** for KTU 2024 ESE questions.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Default Rule | $\frac{\alpha : \beta}{\gamma}$ | Plausible reasoning in autonomous agents |
| 2 | Default Theory | $\Delta = \langle D, W \rangle$ | Knowledge base representation |
| 3 | Extension $E$ | $E = \Gamma(E)$ (fixed point) | Stable belief set |
| 4 | CWA Closure | $\text{CWA}(\Sigma) = \Sigma \cup \{\neg A \mid \Sigma \nvdash A\}$ | Database query completion |
| 5 | Negation Set | $\text{Neg}(\Sigma) = \{A \mid \Sigma \nvdash A\}$ | Identifying unknowns to negate |
| 6 | GCWA Consistency | Add $\neg A$ iff $\Sigma \cup \{\neg A\} \models \text{consistent}$ | Avoiding contradictions |
| 7 | Non-monotonicity | $A \in \text{Th}(\Sigma) \Rightarrow A \in \text{Th}(\Sigma \cup \{B\})$ **DOES NOT HOLD** | Belief revision |
| 8 | Defeasibility | Default can be *defeated* by new $\neg \beta$ | Anomaly-tolerant inference |
| 9 | NAF (Negation as Failure) | $\text{not } A \Leftrightarrow \Sigma \nvdash A$ | Prolog `\+`, logic programming |
| 10 | Monotonicity Test | $\Sigma \subseteq \Sigma' \Rightarrow \text{Th}(\Sigma) \subseteq \text{Th}(\Sigma')$ | Classical FOL test |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

### 2.4.1 Cognitive Cyber-Physical Systems (C-CPS)
- **Autonomous Driving:** Default rule — "Pedestrians will stop at red lights unless sensor detects otherwise."
- **Smart Manufacturing:** CWA — "If a sensor reports no fault, the system is healthy."

### 2.4.2 Database & Information Systems
- **SQL `NOT EXISTS` queries** are rooted in CWA.
- **Semantic Web** uses OWA for *federated reasoning* across incomplete sources.

### 2.4.3 AI Planning & Robotics
- **STRIPS planners** use CWA — unstated preconditions are assumed false.
- **RoboCup agents** use default reasoning to predict opponent behavior.

### 2.4.4 Medical Diagnosis Expert Systems
- **MYCIN, INTERNIST** use default-like rules: "If symptom $X$ is present and no contradictory evidence exists, diagnose $Y$."

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO2: Apply non-monotonic reasoning techniques in cognitive CPS architectures** and **CO3: Design plausible inference systems under uncertainty**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Default Reasoning — "Birds Fly" Scenario

### Problem Setup
A knowledge engineer encodes the following **default theory** $\Delta = \langle D, W \rangle$:

- **Facts (W):**
  - $\text{Bird(Tweety)}$
  - $\text{Bird(Charlie)}$
  - $\text{Penguin(Charlie)}$

- **Defaults (D):**
  - $d_1: \dfrac{\text{Bird}(X) : \text{Flies}(X)}{\text{Flies}(X)}$ (Birds typically fly)
  - $d_2: \dfrac{\text{Penguin}(X) : \neg\text{Flies}(X)}{\neg\text{Flies}(X)}$ (Penguins don't fly)

### Step-by-Step Derivation of the Extension $E$

**Step 1 — Initialization (Base Set):**
Start with $S_0 = W = \{\text{Bird(Tweety)}, \text{Bird(Charlie)}, \text{Penguin(Charlie)}\}$.

**Step 2 — Apply $d_1$ to Tweety:**
- Prerequisite: $\text{Bird(Tweety)} \in S_0$ ✓
- Justification consistency: Is $\neg\text{Flies(Tweety)} \in S_0$? **No.** ✓
- Therefore, conclude: $\text{Flies(Tweety)} \in S_1$.

**Step 3 — Apply $d_1$ to Charlie:**
- Prerequisite: $\text{Bird(Charlie)} \in S_0$ ✓
- Justification consistency: Is $\neg\text{Flies(Charlie)} \in S_0$? **No** (not yet). ✓
- Therefore, conclude: $\text{Flies(Charlie)} \in S_1$.

**Step 4 — Apply $d_2$ to Charlie:**
- Prerequisite: $\text{Penguin(Charlie)} \in S_0$ ✓
- Justification consistency: Is $\text{Flies(Charlie)} \in S_1$? **Yes!** ✗ (CONFLICT)
- Therefore, $d_2$ is **not applicable** at this point — but wait, we need to check the *final* extension consistency.

**Step 5 — Resolve Conflict via Iteration:**
We must find a *fixed point* $E$ such that $E = \Gamma(E)$. Two competing candidates:
- $E_1 = W \cup \{\text{Flies(Tweety)}, \text{Flies(Charlie)}\}$ — but this contradicts the penguin rule.
- $E_2 = W \cup \{\text{Flies(Tweety)}, \neg\text{Flies(Charlie)}\}$ — consistent with both rules.

**Step 6 — Verify $E_2$:**
- $d_1$ for Tweety: $\text{Bird(Tweety)} \in E_2$ and $\neg\text{Flies(Tweety)} \notin E_2$ → fires → $\text{Flies(Tweety)} \in E_2$ ✓
- $d_1$ for Charlie: $\text{Bird(Charlie)} \in E_2$ and $\neg\text{Flies(Charlie)} \notin E_2$ → fires → $\text{Flies(Charlie)} \in E_2$ ✗ CONTRADICTION.

**Step 7 — Re-evaluate with $d_2$ in mind:**
Apply $d_2$ first: $\text{Penguin(Charlie)} \in W$, and we require consistency of $\neg\text{Flies(Charlie)}$ with $E$. If we *commit* to $\neg\text{Flies(Charlie)}$:
- $E_3 = W \cup \{\text{Flies(Tweety)}, \neg\text{Flies(Charlie)}\}$
- Now check $d_1$ for Charlie: $\neg\text{Flies(Charlie)} \in E_3$, so $d_1$ is *blocked*.
- $E_3 = \Gamma(E_3)$ is a **valid extension** ✓.

$$\boxed{E_3 = \{\text{Bird(Tweety)}, \text{Bird(Charlie)}, \text{Penguin(Charlie)}, \text{Flies(Tweety)}, \neg\text{Flies(Charlie)}\}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Result:** Tweety (a generic bird) flies, but Charlie (a penguin) does not. The default $d_1$ was *defeated* for Charlie by the more specific information in $d_2$. This is **defeasible reasoning in action**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Closed World Assumption — Database Query

### Problem Setup
A knowledge base $\Sigma$ for a university database contains:
- $\text{Professor(smith)}$
- $\text{Professor(jones)}$
- $\text{Professor(kumar)}$
- $\text{Teaches(smith, \text{AI})}$
- $\text{Teaches(jones, \text{DBMS})}$

**Query:** "Does $\text{Professor(patel)}$ exist?"

### Step-by-Step Application of CWA

**Step 1 — Enumerate ground atoms of interest:**
Let $P = \{\text{Professor(smith)}, \text{Professor(jones)}, \text{Professor(kumar)}, \text{Professor(patel)}\}$.

**Step 2 — Test entailment for $\text{Professor(patel)}$:**
Does $\Sigma \vdash \text{Professor(patel)}$? **No** — there is no axiom asserting it.

**Step 3 — Apply CWA inference rule:**
Since $\Sigma \nvdash \text{Professor(patel)}$, by CWA:
$$\text{CWA}(\Sigma) \models \neg \text{Professor(patel)}$$

**Step 4 — Final CWA Closure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CWA}(\Sigma) = \Sigma \cup &\{ \neg\text{Professor(patel)}, \\
&\neg\text{Teaches(kumar, X)} \text{ for all } X, \\
&\neg\text{Teaches(smith, Y)} \text{ for } Y \neq \text{AI}, \\
&\neg\text{Teaches(jones, Y)} \text{ for } Y \neq \text{DBMS}, \\
&\text{and so on...}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Answer the Query:**
$$\boxed{\text{No, Patel is not a professor (per CWA).}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat:** Under **OWA** (Open World), the answer would be *"Unknown — Patel might or might not be a professor"*. This difference is critical in **Web Ontology Language (OWL)** reasoning vs. **relational database** semantics.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| **Property** | **Default Reasoning** | **Closed World Assumption** |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Reiter, 1980 | Reiter, 1978 |
| **Form** | Rule-based: $\frac{\alpha : \beta}{\gamma}$ | Global principle applied to KB |
| **Scope** | Local (per rule) | Global (entire KB) |
| **Output** | Set of extensions (fixed points) | Single augmented KB |
| **Negation Handling** | Via justifications | Via non-provability |
| **Computational Cost** | High (multiple extensions possible) | Moderate (one closure step) |
| **Defeasibility** | Explicit (justifications) | Implicit (added axioms) |
| **Expressiveness** | First-order + defaults | Pure first-order + NAF |
| **Example System** | Default logic, defeasible logic | Prolog, DATALOG, SQL |

## 3.4 Proof Sketch: CWA Generates Inconsistency (For Examination)

**Claim:** CWA may yield an inconsistent knowledge base.

**Construction:**
Let $\Sigma = \{P(a) \vee P(b)\}$ — i.e., at least one of $P(a)$ or $P(b)$ holds.

**Step 1 — Test Entailments:**
- $\Sigma \nvdash P(a)$ (cannot be derived)
- $\Sigma \nvdash P(b)$ (cannot be derived)

**Step 2 — Apply CWA:**
$$\text{CWA}(\Sigma) = \Sigma \cup \{\neg P(a), \neg P(b)\}$$

**Step 3 — Derive Contradiction:**
From $P(a) \vee P(b)$ and $\neg P(a)$: deduce $P(b)$. But $\neg P(b)$ is also in the closure. **Contradiction!**

$$\boxed{\text{CWA}(\Sigma) \models \bot}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not naïvely apply CWA to *disjunctive* KBs. Always check whether the knowledge base is *definite* (Horn-clause form) before invoking standard CWA. Use **GCWA** for safety.

## 3.5 Worked Example 3: Computing the Extension via Reiter's Algorithm

Given $\Delta = \langle D, W \rangle$:

- $W = \{A\}$
- $D = \left\{ \dfrac{A : B}{B}, \quad \dfrac{A : C}{C} \right\}$

**Reiter's Algorithm Steps:**

1. Start with $E_0 = W = \{A\}$.
2. Apply $d_1 = \frac{A : B}{B}$: $A \in E_0$ ✓, $\neg B \notin E_0$ ✓ → add $B$.
   $E_1 = \{A, B\}$
3. Apply $d_2 = \frac{A : C}{C}$: $A \in E_1$ ✓, $\neg C \notin E_1$ ✓ → add $C$.
   $E_2 = \{A, B, C\}$
4. Check fixed point: $\Gamma(E_2) = E_2$? Compute deductive closure: $\text{Th}(E_2) = \{A, B, C, \ldots\}$.

$$\boxed{E = \text{Th}(\{A, B, C\}) \text{ is the unique extension.}}$$

This demonstrates the **monotone operator** property of normal defaults: they always produce at least one extension.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of Default Reasoning

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef inputNode fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef processNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef outputNode fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    kb1[Knowledge Base W]:::inputNode
    df1[Default Rules D]:::inputNode
    preChk{Prerequisite alpha satisfied?}:::decisionNode
    justChk{Negation of beta derivable?}:::decisionNode
    addConc[Add consequent gamma to Extension]:::processNode
    defCheck{New contradictory info?}:::decisionNode
    extOut[Final Extension E]:::outputNode
    retract[Retract gamma from E]:::processNode

    kb1 --> preChk
    df1 --> preChk
    preChk -- Yes --> justChk
    preChk -- No --> extOut
    justChk -- No conflict --> addConc
    justChk -- Conflict found --> extOut
    addConc --> defCheck
    defCheck -- Default defeated --> retract
    defCheck -- No defeat --> extOut
    retract --> extOut

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow shows the **operational loop** of default reasoning. A default is *applied* if its prerequisite is established and its justification is *consistent* with the current extension. Any new contradictory information may *defeat* the default, causing retraction.

## 4.2 Closed World Assumption Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef inputNode fill:#4169E1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef processNode fill:#DAA520,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef outputNode fill:#228B22,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef decisionNode fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF

    kbIn[Knowledge Base Sigma]:::inputNode
    enum[Enumerate Ground Atoms]:::processNode
    testEntail{Sigma entails Atom?}:::decisionNode
    addNeg[Add negation of atom to closure]:::processNode
    keepPos[Keep atom as positive fact]:::processNode
    cwaOut[CWA Closure]:::outputNode

    kbIn --> enum
    enum --> testEntail
    testEntail -- No --> addNeg
    testEntail -- Yes --> keepPos
    addNeg --> cwaOut
    keepPos --> cwaOut

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The CWA pipeline *augments* the original knowledge base by adding the negation of every atom that cannot be derived. This is the **negation-as-failure** principle in algorithmic form.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Default Reasoning in a Cognitive CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef sensorNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef cogsNode fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef reasonNode fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef actNode fill:#3CB371,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF

    sens1[Sensor Layer - LIDAR Camera IMU]:::sensorNode
    obs1[Observations as facts W]:::sensorNode
    dfltLib1[Default Rule Library D]:::cogsNode
    extEng1[Extension Engine]:::reasonNode
    cwaMod1[CWA Module for unknowns]:::reasonNode
    cogsState1[Cognitive State - Extension E]:::cogsNode
    actDec1[Action Decision Module]:::actNode
    ctlOut1[Control Signals to Actuators]:::actNode

    sens1 --> obs1
    obs1 --> extEng1
    dfltLib1 --> extEng1
    extEng1 --> cogsState1
    cogsState1 --> cwaMod1
    cwaMod1 --> cogsState1
    cogsState1 --> actDec1
    actDec1 --> ctlOut1

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Mapping:** In a **cognitive autonomous vehicle**:
> - The **sensor layer** feeds observations ($W$) into the system.
> - The **default rule library** encodes commonsense priors ($D$).
> - The **extension engine** computes the stable belief set.
> - The **CWA module** handles "unobserved → assume false" for safety-critical unknowns.
> - The **action decision** module issues control signals.

## 4.4 Comparative State Diagram — CWA vs. OWA

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    classDef cwaState fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef owaState fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef neutralState fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    [*] --> QueryReceived: Atom Q submitted
    QueryReceived --> CheckProvability: Both CWA and OWA
    CheckProvability --> Provable: Sigma derives Q
    CheckProvability --> NotProvable: Sigma cannot derive Q
    Provable --> TRUE: Both systems
    NotProvable --> CWAFALSE: Under CWA
    NotProvable --> OWAUNKNOWN: Under OWA
    CWAFALSE --> [*]
    OWAUNKNOWN --> [*]
    TRUE --> [*]

    class QueryReceived neutralState
    class Provable neutralState
    class NotProvable neutralState
    class TRUE neutralState
    class CheckProvability neutralState
    class CWAFALSE cwaState
    class OWAUNKNOWN owaState

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Branch Point:** The fundamental divergence between CWA and OWA occurs at `NotProvable`. CWA takes a *closed* stance (FALSE), while OWA remains *open* (UNKNOWN). This state machine is the conceptual heart of **semantic web vs. database** reasoning differences.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Closed World Assumption. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Closed World Assumption (CWA)** is a non-monotonic reasoning principle that states: *any ground atomic sentence that cannot be logically derived from the knowledge base is assumed to be false*.

Formally, for a knowledge base $\Sigma$:

$$\text{CWA}(\Sigma) = \Sigma \cup \{\neg A \mid A \text{ is a ground atom and } \Sigma \nvdash A\}$$

**Key implication:** It implements *negation as failure* — the absence of a proof is treated as evidence of falsity. It is widely used in **relational databases, Prolog, and deductive knowledge bases**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse CWA with OWA. CWA is *closed* (assumes completeness of the KB), whereas OWA is *open* (assumes the KB may be incomplete). This distinction is worth **1 mark** in 3-mark questions.

---

### Question 2: What is Default Reasoning? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Default Reasoning** is a form of *non-monotonic logic* introduced by **Raymond Reiter (1980)** that allows an intelligent system to derive *plausible conclusions* in the absence of complete information, using *default rules* of the form:

$$\frac{\alpha : \beta}{\gamma}$$

where $\alpha$ is the prerequisite, $\beta$ is the justification (assumed consistent), and $\gamma$ is the consequent.

**Key property:** Defaults are *defeasible* — they can be **retracted** when new contradictory evidence arrives. This makes default reasoning suitable for modeling *commonsense inference* in cognitive AI systems, such as "Birds fly unless they are penguins."

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): Default Reasoning and CWA — A Comprehensive Analysis [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain Reiter's Default Logic with a suitable example. Derive the extension.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Reiter's default logic is a non-monotonic formalism that extends first-order logic with *default rules*. A default theory is $\Delta = \langle D, W \rangle$ where $W$ is a set of first-order formulas and $D$ is a set of default rules.

**Syntax (1 Mark):**
A default rule has the form $\frac{\alpha : \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_n}{\gamma}$, read as: *"If $\alpha$ is known, and it is consistent to assume $\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_n$, then conclude $\gamma$."*

**Example (2 Marks):** Consider the famous "Tweety the bird" scenario:
- $W = \{\text{Bird(Tweety)}, \text{Penguin(Tweety)}\}$
- $D = \left\{ \dfrac{\text{Bird}(X) : \text{Flies}(X)}{\text{Flies}(X)}, \quad \dfrac{\text{Penguin}(X) : \neg\text{Flies}(X)}{\neg\text{Flies}(X)} \right\}$

**Derivation of Extension (2 Marks):**
- Apply $d_1$: Prerequisite $\text{Bird(Tweety)} \in W$ ✓, check $\neg\text{Flies(Tweety)} \in W$? No ✓ → tentatively add $\text{Flies(Tweety)}$.
- Apply $d_2$: Prerequisite $\text{Penguin(Tweety)} \in W$ ✓, check $\text{Flies(Tweety)} \in E$? Yes (if we keep $d_1$'s conclusion) — *conflict*.
- Resolution: $d_2$ is *more specific* and **defeats** $d_1$.
- Final extension: $E = \text{Th}(W \cup \{\neg\text{Flies(Tweety)}\})$ — Tweety does not fly because he is a penguin.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Discuss the Closed World Assumption. Explain its limitations and variants.**

**Model Solution:**

**CWA Definition (2 Marks):**
CWA is a non-monotonic principle asserting that all *non-derivable* ground atoms are false:

$$\text{CWA}(\Sigma) = \Sigma \cup \{\neg A \mid \Sigma \nvdash A\}$$

**Application (2 Marks):**
- In **relational databases**, queries like `SELECT * FROM Professors WHERE NOT EXISTS (...)` rely on CWA — if no record exists, the answer is FALSE.
- In **Prolog**, the `\+` (negation-as-failure) operator implements CWA.

**Limitations (2 Marks):**
1. **Inconsistency with disjunctive KBs:** If $\Sigma = \{P(a) \vee Q(a)\}$, CWA adds both $\neg P(a)$ and $\neg Q(a)$, contradicting the disjunction.
2. **Not suitable for incomplete domains:** In real-world scenarios (e.g., the Web), information is incomplete, and CWA leads to incorrect conclusions.
3. **Computationally expensive** for large KBs (must test entailment for every atom).

**Variants (1 Mark):**
- **GCWA (Generalized CWA):** Only negates atoms whose negation preserves consistency.
- **CCWA (Careful CWA):** Avoids negating *relevant* atoms.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Non-Monotonic Reasoning in Cognitive CPS [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Expected)]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compare and contrast Default Reasoning and Closed World Assumption with examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (2 Marks):**
- **Default Reasoning:** A *rule-based* non-monotonic logic that allows tentative conclusions via defaults.
- **Closed World Assumption:** A *global* principle that negates all unprovable atoms.

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Default Reasoning | CWA |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Rule-specific | Global KB |
| Expressivity | Higher (prerequisites + justifications) | Lower (negation as failure) |
| Multiple extensions? | Yes (sometimes) | No (unique closure) |
| Defeasibility | Explicit | Implicit |

**Examples (2 Marks):**
- *Default Reasoning:* "If $X$ is a bird and it is consistent to assume $X$ flies, then $X$ flies."
- *CWA:* "If 'Mary is a professor' is not in the database, then Mary is not a professor."

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Design a default-based reasoning module for a smart home cognitive system that handles sensor anomalies.**

**Model Solution:**

**System Specification (2 Marks):**
A smart home with sensors for temperature, smoke, motion, and door state. The system must infer room occupancy and fire risk under partial sensor failure.

**Default Rule Library (3 Marks):**
- $d_1: \dfrac{\text{MotionDetected}(R) : \text{Occupied}(R)}{\text{Occupied}(R)}$ — *Default: room is occupied if motion detected.*
- $d_2: \dfrac{\text{NoMotion}(R) \wedge \text{NoLightChange}(R) : \neg\text{Occupied}(R)}{\neg\text{Occupied}(R)}$ — *Default: empty if no motion and no light change.*
- $d_3: \dfrac{\text{Smoke}(R) : \text{FireRisk}(R)}{\text{FireRisk}(R)}$ — *Default: fire risk if smoke detected.*
- $d_4: \dfrac{\text{SensorFault}(R) : \neg\text{Trustworthy}(R)}{\neg\text{Trustworthy}(R)}$ — *Defeats other defaults for faulty sensors.*

**Extension Computation (2 Marks):**
- If smoke is detected in Room A *and* the sensor is verified working → extension contains $\text{FireRisk}(A)$.
- If smoke is detected but a sensor fault is logged → $d_3$ is *defeated* by $d_4$, and the system remains in a *cautious extension* (no fire alarm).

This demonstrates **defeasible reasoning** in a safety-critical CPS context.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Default Reasoning & CWA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the fixed-point condition** $E = \Gamma(E)$ when defining extensions. (*-1 to -2 marks*)
> 2. **Confusing prerequisite ($\alpha$) with justification ($\beta$).** The prerequisite must be *derivable*; the justification must be *consistent*. (*-1 mark*)
> 3. **Applying CWA to disjunctive knowledge bases** without checking for consistency. Always mention the GCWA safeguard. (*-1 to -2 marks*)
> 4. **Not citing Reiter (1980) for default logic and Reiter (1978) for CWA.** Examiner expects year references for full marks. (*-1 mark*)
> 5. **Skipping the "defeasibility" explanation.** Always emphasize that defaults are *retractable*. (*-1 mark*)
> 6. **Omitting the engineering relevance** in 14-mark questions. Map concepts to CPS, databases, or expert systems. (*-1 mark*)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2**

### Default Reasoning — Key Points
- [ ] **Originator:** Raymond Reiter, 1980.
- [ ] **Form:** $\frac{\alpha : \beta}{\gamma}$ — *prerequisite : justification / consequent*.
- [ ] **Default theory:** $\Delta = \langle D, W \rangle$.
- [ ] **Extension $E$** is a *fixed point* of the operator $\Gamma$.
- [ ] **Defeasibility:** Defaults can be *retracted* by new evidence.
- [ ] **Normal defaults** ($\beta = \gamma$) always have at least one extension.
- [ ] **Semi-normal defaults** encode exceptions (e.g., penguins).

### Closed World Assumption — Key Points
- [ ] **Originator:** Raymond Reiter, 1978.
- [ ] **Core formula:** $\text{CWA}(\Sigma) = \Sigma \cup \{\neg A \mid \Sigma \nvdash A\}$.
- [ ] **Negation as Failure (NAF):** Unprovability = falsity.
- [ ] **Use in:** SQL, Prolog, deductive databases, expert systems.
- [ ] **Limitation:** Inconsistency with disjunctive KBs.
- [ ] **Variant:** GCWA adds *consistency check* before negation.
- [ ] **OWA counterpart:** Used in Semantic Web / OWL.

### Comparison Essentials
- [ ] Default reasoning is *local* (per-rule); CWA is *global* (whole KB).
- [ ] Default reasoning produces *extensions*; CWA produces a *single closure*.
- [ ] Both are **non-monotonic** — adding axioms can *reduce* inferences.

### Engineering Applications
- [ ] Autonomous vehicles: defaults for pedestrian/road behavior.
- [ ] Smart manufacturing: CWA for "no fault" inference.
- [ ] Medical diagnosis: defeasible rules for symptom-to-disease mapping.
- [ ] Web reasoning: CWA in databases, OWA in ontologies.

### Exam Strategy
- [ ] Always **state definitions with authors and years**.
- [ ] Use **examples** (Tweety, professors) to demonstrate concepts.
- [ ] Show **fixed-point computation** for extensions.
- [ ] Mention **GCWA** when discussing CWA limitations.
- [ ] Map to **CPS applications** for 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modal and Temporal Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modal and Temporal Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Modal Logic — Formal Definition

**Modal Logic** is a branch of symbolic logic that extends classical propositional and predicate logic by introducing **modal operators** that express the modes of truth — namely, *necessity* (☐) and *possibility* (◇). Unlike classical logic where propositions are either true or false in a single, fixed world, modal logic reasons about truth across a set of **possible worlds** (also called Kripke structures).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT754 — Module 2):**
> Modal logic is foundational for reasoning about knowledge, belief, obligation, time, and action in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). The two primary modal operators are:
> - **□ (Box / Necessity):** "It is necessarily the case that..."
> - **◇ (Diamond / Possibility):** "It is possibly the case that..."

The formal syntax extends propositional logic:

$$\phi ::= p \mid \neg \phi \mid \phi \wedge \psi \mid \phi \vee \psi \mid \phi \rightarrow \psi \mid \Box \phi \mid \Diamond \phi$$

where $p$ ranges over propositional variables, and the **dual** relationship $\Diamond \phi \equiv \neg \Box \neg \phi$ always holds.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Parallel Universe" Viewpoint:**
> Imagine you are standing at a fork in a road. In **classical logic**, the road is a single line — a statement is either true or false. In **modal logic**, the road branches into many "parallel universes" called *possible worlds*. In some worlds, the statement "it is raining" is true; in others, it is false. The operator **□p** means "p is true in *every* world I could be in," while **◇p** means "p is true in *at least one* world I could be in." This is exactly how CPS engineers reason about *all possible future behaviors* of a self-driving car or aircraft controller.

---

## 1.2 Temporal Logic — Formal Definition

**Temporal Logic** is a specialized extension of modal logic used to reason about propositions whose truth values change over time. It is the mathematical backbone of **formal verification** in CPS, allowing engineers to specify properties like "the brake *must eventually* engage" or "the temperature *must always* remain below 150°C."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Temporal Operators in KTU 2024 Module 2:**
> - **G (Globally / Always):** $\mathbf{G}\phi$ — $\phi$ holds at *every* future time step.
> - **F (Finally / Eventually):** $\mathbf{F}\phi$ — $\phi$ holds at *some* future time step.
> - **X (Next):** $\mathbf{X}\phi$ — $\phi$ holds at the *next* time step.
> - **U (Until):** $\phi \mathbf{U} \psi$ — $\phi$ holds *until* $\psi$ becomes true.

The two principal branches of temporal logic used in CPS verification are:

| Branch | Full Form | Nature | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LTL** | Linear Temporal Logic | Time is a single linear sequence | Used for hardware & sequential circuit verification |
| **CTL** | Computation Tree Logic | Time is a branching tree of futures | Used for concurrent & reactive CPS verification |
| **CTL*** | CTL-Star | Combines both path & state quantifiers | Advanced model checking research |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Train Schedule" Viewpoint:**
> Think of a train timetable. **LTL** says: "On *this specific* train's journey, station B is reached *eventually*." **CTL** says: "*On every possible train path* (or on *some possible path*) from the current station, station B is reached *eventually*." A CPS engineer must guarantee safety properties like "in *all* possible execution paths, the airbag *must* deploy before collision" — this is a **CTL** formula.

---

## 1.3 Why Modal & Temporal Logic Matter in Cyber-Physical Systems

| CPS Engineering Challenge | Logic Used | Example Property |
|---|---|---|
| Verifying autonomous vehicle safety | CTL | $\mathbf{AG}(\text{obstacle} \rightarrow \mathbf{AF}\text{brake})$ |
| Ensuring medical device liveness | LTL | $\mathbf{G}(\text{alarm} \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\text{release})$ |
| Reasoning under sensor uncertainty | Modal (KD45) | $\Box(\text{sensor} \rightarrow \Diamond \text{actuate})$ |
| Multi-agent drone coordination | Modal + Temporal | Coalition logic with time bounds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In your exam, always explicitly state whether a formula is **LTL** (linear) or **CTL** (branching) — this is a common 2-mark discriminator in valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kripke Semantics — The Mathematical Foundation

A **Kripke Structure** (or Kripke Frame) is a tuple:

$$M = \langle W, R, V \rangle$$

where:
- $W$ is a non-empty set of **possible worlds** (states).
- $R \subseteq W \times W$ is the **accessibility relation** (a binary relation between worlds).
- $V: W \rightarrow 2^{\text{Prop}}$ is the **valuation function** assigning truth values to propositions in each world.

> [!NOTE]
> The accessibility relation $R$ encodes *which worlds are "reachable" or "relevant"* from a given world. This single relation determines the *flavor* of modal logic used.

### 2.1.1 Truth Conditions for Modal Operators

For a Kripke model $M$ and world $w$:

$$M, w \models \Box \phi \iff \text{for all } w' \text{ such that } w R w', \; M, w' \models \phi$$

$$M, w \models \Diamond \phi \iff \text{there exists } w' \text{ such that } w R w', \; M, w' \models \phi$$

**Reading:** "$\Box \phi$ holds at $w$" iff "$\phi$ holds in *every* world accessible from $w$." "$\Diamond \phi$ holds at $w$" iff "$\phi$ holds in *at least one* world accessible from $w$."

### 2.1.2 Properties of the Accessibility Relation & Normal Modal Logics

| Logic | Axiom Added | Property of $R$ | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **K** | $\Box(\phi \rightarrow \psi) \rightarrow (\Box \phi \rightarrow \Box \psi)$ | No restriction | Base modal logic |
| **T** | $\Box \phi \rightarrow \phi$ | Reflexive ($wRw$) | What is necessary is true |
| **K4** | $\Box \phi \rightarrow \Box \Box \phi$ | Transitive | Necessity is "nested" |
| **S4** | T + K4 | Reflexive + Transitive | Knowledge / provability |
| **S5** | $\Box \phi \rightarrow \Box \Box \phi$ and $\Diamond \phi \rightarrow \Box \Diamond \phi$ | Equivalence relation | Idealized knowledge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** The hierarchy is $\mathbf{K} \subset \mathbf{T} \subset \mathbf{S4} \subset \mathbf{S5}$. Each stronger logic adds reasoning power but reduces the class of valid formulas.

---

## 2.2 Temporal Logic — Semantic Models

### 2.2.1 Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) Semantics

An LTL model is an infinite sequence of states:

$$\sigma = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, \ldots$$

The notation $\sigma^i$ denotes the suffix starting at index $i$. The truth conditions are:

$$M, \sigma^i \models \mathbf{X} \phi \iff M, \sigma^{i+1} \models \phi$$

$$M, \sigma^i \models \mathbf{G} \phi \iff \text{for all } j \geq i, \; M, \sigma^j \models \phi$$

$$M, \sigma^i \models \mathbf{F} \phi \iff \text{there exists } j \geq i \text{ such that } M, \sigma^j \models \phi$$

$$M, \sigma^i \models \phi \mathbf{U} \psi \iff \text{there exists } j \geq i \text{ such that } M, \sigma^j \models \psi \text{ and for all } i \leq k < j, \; M, \sigma^k \models \phi$$

### 2.2.2 Computation Tree Logic (CTL) Semantics

CTL introduces **path quantifiers** combined with temporal operators:

- **A** — "for **A**ll paths" (universal)
- **E** — "th**E**re exists a path" (existential)

The path quantifier and temporal operator must appear as a **pair** in CTL:

$$M, s \models \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} \phi \iff \text{for all paths } \pi \text{ starting at } s, \; M, \pi^1 \models \phi$$

$$M, s \models \mathbf{E}\mathbf{F} \phi \iff \text{there exists a path } \pi \text{ starting at } s \text{ where } \phi \text{ eventually holds}$$

$$M, s \models \mathbf{A}\mathbf{G} \phi \iff \text{for all paths } \pi \text{ starting at } s, \; \phi \text{ holds globally}$$

$$M, s \models \mathbf{A}[\phi \mathbf{U} \psi] \iff \text{on all paths, } \phi \mathbf{U} \psi \text{ holds}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbol | Domain | Real-World Use in CPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Necessity | $\Box \phi$ | Modal Logic | "Sensor must always read" |
| Possibility | $\Diamond \phi$ | Modal Logic | "Failure is possible" |
| Knowledge Operator | $K_i \phi$ | Epistemic Logic | "Drone $i$ knows the path" |
| Belief Operator | $B_i \phi$ | BDI Logic | "Controller believes target" |
| Globally (Always) | $\mathbf{G}\phi$ | LTL/CTL | Safety invariants |
| Finally (Eventually) | $\mathbf{F}\phi$ | LTL/CTL | Liveness guarantees |
| Next | $\mathbf{X}\phi$ | LTL/CTL | Discrete step transitions |
| Until | $\phi \mathbf{U} \psi$ | LTL/CTL | Bounded response |
| All Paths Globally | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\phi$ | CTL | "In every future, always safe" |
| Exists Path Finally | $\mathbf{E}\mathbf{F}\phi$ | CTL | "Some execution eventually reaches goal" |
| Kripke Frame | $\langle W, R, V \rangle$ | Modal Logic | Formal state-space model |
| Duality | $\Diamond \phi \equiv \neg \Box \neg \phi$ | Modal Logic | Operator conversion |
| Linear Time Duality | $\mathbf{F}\phi \equiv \neg \mathbf{G}\neg \phi$ | LTL | Operator conversion |
| Branching Duality | $\mathbf{E}\mathbf{F}\phi \equiv \neg \mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\neg \phi$ | CTL | Operator conversion |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU PITFALL:** Never write $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\phi$ when you mean $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\phi$ in CTL. LTL operators stand alone; CTL operators **must** be preceded by a path quantifier. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this conflation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** CTL formula $\mathbf{AG}(\text{velocity} < \text{limit})$ verified by model checkers like SPIN and NuSMV.
- **Medical Cyber-Physical Devices:** LTL formula $\mathbf{G}(\text{critical} \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\text{response})$ guarantees liveness in pacemakers.
- **Smart Grid Control:** Modal epistemic logic models *distributed knowledge* among substation controllers.
- **Industrial IoT:** Real-time temporal logic monitors sensor streams with bounded time windows.
- **Aerospace Flight Control:** CTL* used in NASA aerospace software for DO-178C Level A certification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Modal Duality Theorem

**Statement to Prove:** $\Diamond \phi \equiv \neg \Box \neg \phi$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a fundamental identity in modal logic. KTU frequently asks: *"Prove the duality between ◇ and □ using Kripke semantics."*

### Step 1 — Expand the Right-Hand Side (Negation of Universal)

We start with the formal definition of $\Box \neg \phi$ using Kripke semantics:

$$M, w \models \Box \neg \phi \iff \forall w' \; (w R w' \rightarrow M, w' \models \neg \phi)$$

### Step 2 — Apply Negation Semantics

The inner formula $M, w' \models \neg \phi$ is equivalent to $M, w' \not\models \phi$. So:

$$M, w \models \Box \neg \phi \iff \forall w' \; (w R w' \rightarrow M, w' \not\models \phi)$$

### Step 3 — Negate the Outer Quantifier

Applying outer negation $M, w \not\models \Box \neg \phi$:

$$M, w \not\models \Box \neg \phi \iff \neg \forall w' \; (w R w' \rightarrow M, w' \not\models \phi)$$

### Step 4 — Quantifier Negation (Classical Logic Equivalence)

By the duality $\neg \forall x \, P(x) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)$:

$$M, w \not\models \Box \neg \phi \iff \exists w' \; \neg(w R w' \rightarrow M, w' \not\models \phi)$$

### Step 5 — Apply Implication Negation

Using the equivalence $p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg p \vee q$ and De Morgan's laws, $\neg(p \rightarrow q) \equiv p \wedge \neg q$:

$$M, w \not\models \Box \neg \phi \iff \exists w' \; (w R w' \wedge M, w' \models \phi)$$

### Step 6 — Match the Definition of ◇

By definition of $\Diamond \phi$ in Kripke semantics:

$$M, w \models \Diamond \phi \iff \exists w' \; (w R w' \wedge M, w' \models \phi)$$

### Step 7 — Conclude the Equivalence

Comparing Steps 5 and 6:

$$M, w \not\models \Box \neg \phi \iff M, w \models \Diamond \phi$$

This is logically equivalent to:

$$\Diamond \phi \equiv \neg \Box \neg \phi \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CTL* Expressive Power Hierarchy

**Statement:** $\text{LTL} \subsetneq \text{CTL}$ as subsets of CTL* in general, but $\text{LTL}$ and $\text{CTL}$ are **incomparable** in expressive power.

### Step 1 — Define the Three Logics Formally

- **LTL:** State formulas contain no path quantifiers. Path formulas are built as $\mathbf{X}\phi \mid \phi \mathbf{U} \psi \mid \mathbf{F}\phi \mid \mathbf{G}\phi$, where $\phi, \psi$ are state formulas.
- **CTL:** Every temporal operator is immediately preceded by $\mathbf{A}$ or $\mathbf{E}$ — no nested path quantifiers.
- **CTL*:** Allows arbitrary nesting; a state formula may contain path sub-formulas.

### Step 2 — Show an LTL Formula Not Expressible in Pure CTL

Consider $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G} p$ ("eventually $p$ holds forever"). In CTL, the closest would be $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{F}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G} p$ or $\mathbf{E}\mathbf{F}\mathbf{E}\mathbf{G} p$ — neither captures the exact linear-time meaning. Therefore $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G} p \notin \text{CTL}$.

### Step 3 — Show a CTL Formula Not Expressible in Pure LTL

Consider $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\mathbf{E}\mathbf{F} p$ ("on every path, there exists a path where $p$ eventually holds"). This requires nested branching, impossible in linear logic. Therefore $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\mathbf{E}\mathbf{F} p \notin \text{LTL}$.

### Step 4 — Conclusion of Incomparability

Since each logic can express properties the other cannot:

$$\text{LTL} \not\subseteq \text{CTL} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{CTL} \not\subseteq \text{LTL}$$

$$\therefore \; \text{LTL} \; \text{and} \; \text{CTL} \; \text{are incomparable} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Property Classification

| Property Type | English Description | LTL Form | CTL Form | CPS Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Safety** | "Something bad never happens" | $\mathbf{G}\neg \phi$ | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\neg \phi$ | No collision ever |
| **Liveness** | "Something good eventually happens" | $\mathbf{F}\phi$ | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{F}\phi$ | Request is served |
| **Fairness** | "Action occurs infinitely often" | $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\phi$ | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{F}\phi$ | Scheduler fair |
| **Reachability** | "A state is reachable" | Not natural | $\mathbf{E}\mathbf{F}\phi$ | Goal-state exists |
| **Invariant** | "Property holds in all states" | $\mathbf{G}\phi$ | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\phi$ | Temp always < 100°C |
| **Response** | "If trigger then response" | $\mathbf{G}(\phi \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\psi)$ | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}(\phi \rightarrow \mathbf{A}\mathbf{F}\psi)$ | Alarm triggers vent |
| **Precedence** | "P happens before Q" | $(\neg Q)\mathbf{U}P$ | $\mathbf{A}[(\neg Q)\mathbf{U}P]$ | Brake before crash |
| **Until** | "P holds until Q" | $P \mathbf{U} Q$ | $\mathbf{A}[P \mathbf{U} Q]$ | Heating until 80°C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** When asked to translate English to logic, first identify whether the property is **safety** (use $\mathbf{G}$ or $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}$) or **liveness** (use $\mathbf{F}$ or $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{F}$), then choose LTL or CTL based on whether branching matters.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — CPS Airbag System

**Specification (English):**
1. The airbag system is *always* armed while the car is moving.
2. *Whenever* a crash is detected, the airbag *must eventually* deploy.
3. The airbag *never* deploys unless a crash has been detected.

**Translation to LTL:**

Let $A$ = "airbag armed", $M$ = "car moving", $C$ = "crash detected", $D$ = "airbag deployed".

$$\text{(i)} \quad \mathbf{G}(M \rightarrow A)$$

$$\text{(ii)} \quad \mathbf{G}(C \rightarrow \mathbf{F}D)$$

$$\text{(iii)} \quad \mathbf{G}(D \rightarrow C \; \text{previously held})$$

> [!NOTE]
> Property (iii) requires the "since" or "past temporal" operator, or equivalently $\mathbf{G}(\neg C \rightarrow \neg D) \wedge \mathbf{G}(D \rightarrow \text{earlier } C)$. In LTL without past operators, this is expressed as a process-algebraic property.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kripke Structure for a CPS Controller (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS0["S0: Idle State\nP1 and P2 are false"]
    nodeS1["S1: Sensing Active\nP1 is true"]
    nodeS2["S2: Anomaly Detected\nP2 is true"]
    nodeS3["S3: Emergency Mode\nP1 and P2 are true"]
    nodeS4["S4: Safe Shutdown\nP1 and P2 are false"]
    nodeS0 -->|trigger| nodeS1
    nodeS1 -->|normal| nodeS2
    nodeS1 -->|anomaly| nodeS3
    nodeS2 -->|resolved| nodeS0
    nodeS3 -->|stabilize| nodeS4
    nodeS4 -->|reset| nodeS0
    nodeS2 -->|escalate| nodeS3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each node is a *possible world* in the Kripke structure $W = \{S0, S1, S2, S3, S4\}$. The arrows depict the accessibility relation $R$. The propositions $P1$ and $P2$ are true in the worlds where they are labeled. A formula like $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\neg(P1 \wedge P2 \wedge \text{Unsafe})$ is verified by exploring all paths in this structure.

---

## 4.2 LTL Operator Semantics — State Transition Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT0["t0: Initial State\nphi may or may not hold"]
    nodeT1["t1: Next Step\nX phi holds here"]
    nodeT2["t2: Future Step\nF phi holds somewhere\nin this region"]
    nodeT3["t3: All Future Steps\nG phi holds in every\nnode beyond this point"]
    nodeT4["t4: Until Boundary\nphi holds until psi\nbecomes true here"]
    nodeT0 --> nodeT1
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Operator Mapping:** This timeline shows the meaning of each temporal operator. **X** affects only one step; **F** searches forward to *some* future; **G** demands truth at *every* future point; **U** requires one formula to hold *continuously* until another takes over.

---

## 4.3 CTL Path Quantifier — Branching Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS["Current State s"]
    nodeP1["Path 1\nA phi or E phi must hold"]
    nodeP2["Path 2\nA phi or E phi must hold"]
    nodeP3["Path 3\nA phi or E phi must hold"]
    nodeS --> nodeP1
    nodeS --> nodeP2
    nodeS --> nodeP3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** CTL branches at state $s$ into multiple computation paths. The formula $\mathbf{A}\phi$ requires $\phi$ to hold on *all* three paths (universal). The formula $\mathbf{E}\phi$ requires $\phi$ to hold on *at least one* path (existential). This is the key conceptual difference between CTL and LTL.

---

## 4.4 Modal Logic Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeK["K: Base Modal Logic\nNo restriction on R"]
    nodeT["T: Reflexive Logic\nAxiom: Box phi implies phi"]
    nodeK4["K4: Transitive Logic\nAxiom: Box phi implies Box Box phi"]
    nodeS4["S4: Reflexive + Transitive\nKnowledge and Provability Logic"]
    nodeS5["S5: Equivalence Relation\nIdealized Knowledge Logic"]
    nodeK --> nodeT
    nodeK --> nodeK4
    nodeT --> nodeS4
    nodeK4 --> nodeS4
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is the *Lattice of Normal Modal Logics*. The arrow means "is a strict subset of valid formulas." A formula valid in **S5** is also valid in **S4**, but not vice versa. CPS epistemic reasoning often uses **S5** for ideal agents and **KD45** for belief under uncertainty.

---

## 4.5 CPS Verification Workflow Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA["CPS System\nSpecification\nEnglish description"]
    blockB["Formal Logic\nTranslation\nLTL or CTL"]
    blockC["Kripke\nTransition System\nModel of the CPS"]
    blockD["Model Checker\nSPIN NuSMV UPPAAL"]
    blockE["Verification\nResult\nSatisfied or Violated"]
    blockF["Counterexample\nTrace if violated"]
    blockA --> blockB
    blockB --> blockD
    blockC --> blockD
    blockD --> blockE
    blockD --> blockF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **Model Checking Pipeline** used in CPS verification. The specification is translated into a temporal logic formula, the CPS is abstracted as a Kripke structure, and a model checker verifies whether the model satisfies the formula — producing either confirmation or a counterexample trace.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
**Q:** Define the modal operators **□** (necessity) and **◇** (possibility). State and explain the modal duality theorem.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **□ (Box / Necessity):** $\Box \phi$ means "$\phi$ is necessarily true" or "$\phi$ is true in every accessible possible world." (1 Mark)
- **◇ (Diamond / Possibility):** $\Diamond \phi$ means "$\phi$ is possibly true" or "$\phi$ is true in at least one accessible possible world." (1 Mark)
- **Modal Duality Theorem:** $\Diamond \phi \equiv \neg \Box \neg \phi$. This is proven by Kripke semantics: "$\phi$ is possible" is equivalent to "it is not the case that $\neg \phi$ is necessary." (1 Mark)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
**Q:** Differentiate between **LTL** and **CTL** with one example property for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **LTL (Linear Temporal Logic)** reasons about a *single* linear timeline of execution. Operators: $\mathbf{G}, \mathbf{F}, \mathbf{X}, \mathbf{U}$ stand alone. (1 Mark)
- **CTL (Computation Tree Logic)** reasons about *all branching* futures. Every temporal operator must be paired with a path quantifier $\mathbf{A}$ or $\mathbf{E}$. (1 Mark)
- **LTL Example:** $\mathbf{G}(\text{alarm} \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\text{vent\_open})$ — "Whenever an alarm occurs, the vent must eventually open." (0.5 Mark)
- **CTL Example:** $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{F}\text{scheduler\_active}$ — "On every path, the scheduler is always eventually active." (0.5 Mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q:**
**(a)** Define a **Kripke Structure**. Explain its components with a suitable diagram. Discuss the semantics of the modal operators $\Box$ and $\Diamond$ on a Kripke model. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **normal modal logics K, T, S4, and S5** in terms of the properties of the accessibility relation. State the axiom added at each level. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

A **Kripke Structure** is the formal semantic model for modal logic. (0.5 Marks)

**Definition:** A Kripke model is a tuple:

$$M = \langle W, R, V \rangle$$

**Components:** (2 Marks)

1. **$W$ (Set of Worlds):** A non-empty set of "possible worlds" or states. Example: $W = \{w_1, w_2, w_3\}$.
2. **$R \subseteq W \times W$ (Accessibility Relation):** A binary relation indicating which worlds are "reachable" from which. If $w_i R w_j$, then $w_j$ is accessible from $w_i$.
3. **$V: W \rightarrow 2^{\text{Prop}}$ (Valuation Function):** Assigns to each world the set of propositional variables true in that world.

**Diagram of a sample Kripke structure:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    kaW1["w1\nProposition p is true"]
    kaW2["w2\nProposition p is false"]
    kaW3["w3\nProposition p is true"]
    kaW1 -->|R| kaW2
    kaW1 -->|R| kaW3
    kaW2 -->|R| kaW3
```

**Semantics of Modal Operators:** (4.5 Marks)

For any formula $\phi$ and world $w \in W$:

$$M, w \models \Box \phi \iff \text{for all } w' \in W, \; w R w' \Rightarrow M, w' \models \phi$$

$$M, w \models \Diamond \phi \iff \text{there exists } w' \in W \text{ such that } w R w' \text{ and } M, w' \models \phi$$

**Explanation:**

- $\Box \phi$ at $w$ means "$\phi$ is true in *every* world accessible from $w$." This is a *universal* statement.
- $\Diamond \phi$ at $w$ means "$\phi$ is true in *at least one* world accessible from $w$." This is an *existential* statement.
- In the example above, $M, w_1 \models \Diamond p$ because $p$ is true in $w_3$, which is accessible from $w_1$. However, $M, w_1 \not\models \Box p$ because $p$ is false in $w_2$, also accessible from $w_1$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

Normal modal logics are obtained by adding **axiom schemas** to the base logic **K**, each corresponding to a property of the accessibility relation $R$. (0.5 Marks)

| Logic | Axiom Schema | Property of $R$ | Intuitive Meaning | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
