# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define fuzzy matching. How does it differ from exact matching in a Cyber-Physical System?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fuzzy matching is a technique that finds *approximately* equivalent strings or patterns rather than requiring exact equality, by computing a *similarity score* in $[0, 1]$ between an input observation and stored templates. (1 mark)

Unlike exact matching, which is binary (a match exists or does not), fuzzy matching returns a graded confidence value. (1 mark)

In a CPS, fuzzy matching is critical because physical-sensor data is inherently noisy — e.g., OCR cameras reading license plates, voice microphones transcribing commands, RFID scanners under interference — and the cognitive layer must robustly correlate such noisy inputs to canonical knowledge representations. (1 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any THREE fuzzy matching metrics and state one CPS application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Levenshtein Distance** — minimum number of single-character insert/delete/substitute operations; used in license-plate recognition with OCR noise. (1 mark)
2. **Jaccard Similarity** — ratio of intersecting to union of $n$-gram sets; used for tag reconciliation in IIoT (e.g., `Boiler_Temp_3` vs `boiler-temp-3`). (1 mark)
3. **Jaro–Winkler Similarity** — prefix-boosted edit similarity; used in patient-record linkage across healthcare CPS where name spelling varies. (1 mark)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Derive the Levenshtein distance recurrence relation. Compute the Levenshtein distance between the strings `"CAFE"` and `"COFFEE"` using the dynamic-programming matrix. Show all intermediate steps. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Levenshtein distance $L(i,j)$ between prefixes $a_1 \ldots a_i$ and $b_1 \ldots b_j$ is the minimum of three operations: (1 mark)

$$L(i,j) = \min \begin{cases} L(i-1,j-1) + \mathbb{1}_{a_i \ne b_j} & \text{(substitute / match)} \\ L(i,j-1) + 1 & \text{(insert } b_j\text{)} \\ L(i-1,j) + 1 & \text{(delete } a_i\text{)} \end{cases}$$

with base cases $L(0,j) = j$ and $L(i,0) = i$. (1 mark)

The recurrence is derived by considering that the *last* edit in any optimal sequence must be one of these three operations, and optimal substructure holds because each sub-problem is independent. (1 mark)

**DP matrix for $L(\text{"CAFE"},\ \text{"COFFEE"})$:**

Dimensions: $|a| = 4$, $|b| = 6$, so matrix is $5 \times 7$. Initialize row 0 = $[0,1,2,3,4,5,6]$ and column 0 = $[0,1,2,3,4]$. (1 mark)

Fill the matrix by row-major order:

|   | ε | C | O | F | F | E | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ε** | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| **C** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| **A** | 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| **F** | 3 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| **E** | 4 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 3 |

**Cell-by-cell verification:**
- $L(1,1)$: $C$ vs $C$ match $\Rightarrow L(0,0) + 0 = 0$. (1 mark)
- $L(1,2)$: $C$ vs $O$ mismatch $\Rightarrow \min(L(0,1)+1, L(0,2)+1, L(1,1)+1) = \min(2,3,1) = 1$.
- $L(1,3)$: $C$ vs $F$ mismatch $\Rightarrow \min(2,3,2) = 2$.
- $L(1,4)$: $C$ vs $F$ mismatch $\Rightarrow \min(3,4,3) = 3$.
- $L(1,5)$: $C$ vs $E$ mismatch $\Rightarrow \min(4,5,4) = 4$.
- $L(1,6)$: $C$ vs $E$ mismatch $\Rightarrow \min(5,6,5) = 5$.
- $L(2,1)$: $A$ vs $C$ mismatch $\Rightarrow \min(2,2,2) = 2 \to$ corrected as 1. Apply correctly: $L(2,1) = \min(L(1,0)+1, L(1,1)+1, L(2,0)+1) = \min(2,1,3) = 1$.
- (Continue similarly for remaining cells.)

**Result:**

$$L(\text{"CAFE"},\ \text{"COFFEE"}) = 3$$

The edit sequence is: substitute $A \to O$, substitute $E \to F$, insert $E$ (1 mark for verification).

**(b) Explain the Jaccard similarity and Sørensen–Dice coefficient. With $a =$ `"weather"` and $b =$ `"whether"`, compute both metrics using bigrams ($n=2$). Comment on which is more 'optimistic' and why. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Jaccard Similarity** $J(A,B)$ is the ratio of the size of the intersection of two sets to the size of their union: (1 mark)

$$J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$

**Sørensen–Dice Coefficient** $D_S(A,B)$ gives double weight to the intersection, originally derived for ecological community sampling: (1 mark)

$$D_S(A,B) = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$$

**Bigrams of $a =$ `"weather"`:**
$$N_2(a) = \{we, ea, at, th, he, er\}, \quad \vert A \vert = 6$$

**Bigrams of $b =$ `"whether"`:**
$$N_2(b) = \{wh, he, et, th, he, er\}, \quad \vert B \vert = 6$$

(Yes, bigrams can repeat — `"he"` appears twice in `whether`.)

**Intersection:** $A \cap B = \{th, he, er\}$ (1 mark)
- Note: `he` appears in both, even if we count multiplicities; cardinality $\vert A \cap B \vert = 3$.

**Union:** $A \cup B = \{we, ea, at, th, he, er, wh, et\}$ (1 mark)
- $\vert A \cup B \vert = 8$

**Jaccard:**

$$J = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

**Sørensen–Dice:**

$$D_S = \frac{2 \cdot 3}{6 + 6} = \frac{6}{12} = 0.5$$

**Commentary:** $D_S = 0.5 > J = 0.375$, so the Sørensen–Dice coefficient is more "optimistic" about the similarity. (1 mark)

This is because the Dice coefficient implicitly up-weights the intersection (the matching evidence) by a factor of 2 relative to the union, which mathematically corresponds to: (1 mark)

$$D_S = \frac{2J}{1 + J}$$

For CPS applications, Dice is preferred when missing a true match is more costly than a false alarm (e.g., patient identification), while Jaccard is preferred when both error types are equally costly.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) Explain the Jaro and Jaro–Winkler similarity metrics with formulas. Given $a =$ `"MARTHA"` and $b =$ `"MARHTA"`, compute the Jaro similarity and the Jaro–Winkler similarity (using $p = 0.1$). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Jaro Similarity** measures agreement between two strings on a per-character basis using a sliding matching window. (1 mark)

Given strings $a$ and $b$, let $m$ = number of matching characters, $t$ = number of transpositions (counted as half the number of mismatched-position matches). Two characters are *matching* if they are the same and their positions differ by at most $\lfloor \max(\vert a \vert, \vert b \vert)/2 \rfloor$. (1 mark)

$$J_{aro}(a,b) = \frac{1}{3}\!\left(\frac{m}{\vert a \vert} + \frac{m}{\vert b \vert} + \frac{m - t}{m}\right)$$

**Jaro–Winkler** boosts the Jaro score when the strings share a common prefix of length $\ell$ (capped at 4): (1 mark)

$$J_W(a,b) = J_{aro} + \ell \cdot p \cdot (1 - J_{aro})$$

**Computation for $a =$ `"MARTHA"`, $b =$ `"MARHTA"`:**

- $\vert a \vert = 6$, $\vert b \vert = 6$.
- Matching window: $\lfloor 6/2 \rfloor = 3$.

**Matching characters $m$:**
- $a_1 = M$ matches $b_1 = M$.
- $a_2 = A$ matches $b_2 = A$.
- $a_3 = R$ matches $b_3 = R$.
- $a_4 = T$ matches $b_5 = T$ (distance 1 ≤ 3, OK).
- $a_5 = H$ matches $b_4 = H$ (distance 1 ≤ 3, OK).
- $a_6 = A$ matches $b_6 = A$.

So $m = 6$. (1 mark)

**Transpositions $t$:**
- $a_4 = T$ matched $b_5$ instead of $b_4$.
- $a_5 = H$ matched $b_4$ instead of $b_5$.

These are out of order by 1 position: 1 transposition pair. Per Jaro convention, $t = 1/2$ (or count raw mismatches $\to t = 2 \to t/2 = 1$ in some texts). (1 mark)

Using the standard convention $t = $ number of transposed matching-character pairs / 2 = $2/2 = 1$:

$$J_{aro} = \frac{1}{3}\!\left(\frac{6}{6} + \frac{6}{6} + \frac{6 - 1}{6}\right) = \frac{1}{3}\!\left(1 + 1 + \frac{5}{6}\right) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{17}{6} = \frac{17}{18} \approx 0.944$$

(1 mark for the substitution)

**Jaro–Winkler with $p = 0.1$:**

Common prefix: `"MAR"` of length $\ell = 3$.

$$J_W = 0.944 + 3 \cdot 0.1 \cdot (1 - 0.944) = 0.944 + 0.3 \cdot 0.056 = 0.944 + 0.0168 \approx 0.961$$

(1 mark)

**Interpretation:** A score of $\approx 0.961$ indicates very high similarity, suitable for confident record-linkage acceptance in CPS cognitive layers.

**(b) Describe the role of fuzzy matching in a Cognitive Cyber-Physical System. Discuss its application in (i) smart transportation, (ii) healthcare record linkage, and (iii) voice-controlled IoT. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Fuzzy matching is a **perception-layer cognitive function** in a C-CPS that allows the system to bridge the gap between noisy, ambiguous physical-sensor inputs and the canonical symbolic knowledge stored in cognitive memory. (1 mark)

Unlike deterministic rule-based matching, fuzzy matching supports *graded inference* — the cognitive layer can reason about *how much* an observation matches a known concept, enabling decisions under uncertainty. (1 mark)

**Application (i) — Smart Transportation:** (1.5 marks)
- License-plate recognition systems receive OCR readings that vary due to dirt, glare, fonts, and camera angles. Fuzzy matching consolidates multiple noisy readings of the *same* physical vehicle into a single canonical identity. For example, `"KL-01-AB-1234"`, `"KL01AB1234"`, and `"KL O1 A8 1234"` are all matched as the same vehicle. Levenshtein or Jaro–Winkler metrics are typically used with $T \approx 0.85$.

**Application (ii) — Healthcare Record Linkage:** (1.5 marks)
- When patient records are merged across hospitals, name spelling varies (`"George"` vs `"Georges"`), DOB may have day-month ambiguity, and addresses contain typographic errors. Fuzzy matching identifies *probabilistic* identity matches. Sorensen–Dice and Jaro–Winkler dominate this domain because they handle partial token overlap and prefix agreement.

**Application (iii) — Voice-Controlled IoT:** (1.5 marks)
- ASR engines transcribe speech imperfectly, especially under noise. Fuzzy matching maps noisy transcripts like `"turn on the lyt"` to the canonical command `"turn on the light"`. Phonetic algorithms (Metaphone) combined with Levenshtein distance are commonly used. Threshold $T \approx 0.75$ is typical.

**Conclusion:** Fuzzy matching is *the* essential enabler of cognitive robustness in CPS — without it, every layer downstream of the sensor would be brittle to the irreducible noise of the physical world. (0.5 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Confusing distance and similarity:** Levenshtein is a *distance* (lower = more similar); Jaccard is a *similarity* (higher = more similar). Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting base cases:** Writing the recurrence without $L(0,j) = j$ and $L(i,0) = i$ loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong bigram counting:** Students often forget that bigrams can be **repeated** (multiset), e.g., `"he"` appears twice in `"whether"`. This skews the Jaccard computation.
> 4. **Transposition convention in Jaro:** The number of transpositions $t$ must be **halved** (each transposition contributes 2 mismatches). Using the raw count is a common error.
> 5. **Threshold mis-specification:** Students often forget to specify the cognitive threshold $T$ when describing a fuzzy-matching system. KTU examiners look for the *decision rule* $S \ge T$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fuzzy matching** = approximate, score-based pattern matching; returns a *similarity* in $[0,1]$ or a *distance* normalized to $[0,1]$.
- **Levenshtein distance** uses dynamic programming with the recurrence $L(i,j) = \min(\text{delete}, \text{insert}, \text{substitute})$ and base cases $L(0,j) = j$, $L(i,0) = i$.
- **Damerau–Levenshtein** extends Levenshtein to include *transposition* of adjacent characters — useful for typographic errors.
- **Hamming distance** only works on *equal-length* strings and counts substitutions only.
- **Jaccard similarity** is $\vert A \cap B \vert \,/\, \vert A \cup B \vert$ over $n$-gram sets.
- **Sørensen–Dice** is $2 \vert A \cap B \vert \,/\, (\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert)$ — always $\ge$ Jaccard, so more "optimistic".
- **Cosine similarity** measures the angle between TF–IDF or character-frequency vectors.
- **Jaro–Winkler** boosts Jaro by $\ell \cdot p \cdot (1 - J_{aro})$ when strings share a common prefix (capped at $\ell = 4$, $p = 0.1$ typical).
- **Phonetic algorithms** (Soundex, Metaphone) collapse homophones — critical for voice-driven CPS.
- **Threshold rule** $S(a,b) \ge T$ governs the cognitive acceptance decision; typical $T \in [0.7, 0.9]$ depending on application criticality.
- **CPS applications:** license-plate recognition, patient-record linkage, voice-IoT commands, IIoT tag reconciliation, biometric re-identification.
- **Always state** the **base cases** when writing the DP recurrence — KTU examiners explicitly test this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RETE Matching Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** RETE Matching Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **RETE Matching Algorithm** (Latin/Italian for *"network"*) is a high-performance, **incremental pattern-matching algorithm** developed by **Dr. Charles L. Forgy** in **1974 at Carnegie Mellon University** for the efficient implementation of **Production Rule-Based Systems** (a foundational component of Cognitive Systems and Rule-Based Expert Systems).

In a rule-based production system, the *Recognize–Act Cycle* (also called the *Inference Cycle*) must repeatedly match thousands of *Working Memory Elements (WME)* — i.e., the current **facts** — against the conditional parts (*Left-Hand Side, LHS*) of hundreds or thousands of rules stored in **Production Memory (PM)**. A naïve re-evaluation of every fact against every rule on every cycle is computationally catastrophic, with worst-case complexity **O(R · F<sup>P</sup>)**, where *R* is the number of rules, *F* is the number of facts, and *P* is the average number of patterns (conditions) per rule.

The RETE algorithm solves this scalability bottleneck by:

1. **Compiling** the LHS of all rules into a single, shared, rooted, directed acyclic graph (DAG) called the **RETE Network**.
2. **Memoizing** (caching) the partial matches at every internal node.
3. **Incrementally updating** the network only when Working Memory changes (a fact is *ADDed*, *REMOVE*d, or *MODIFY*-ed), avoiding full re-matching.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RETE is the canonical match engine in classical expert-system shells such as **CLIPS, Jess, Drools, OPS5, and LISP-based production systems**. Any question on "matching in production systems," "rule firing cycle," or "expert system inference engine" at KTU implicitly targets the RETE family of algorithms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Filing Clerks" Intuition

Imagine a **government office** where citizens constantly file new applications (facts entering Working Memory) and a clerk must identify which set of laws (rules) applies to each application. A naïve clerk would, for every new application, walk to the massive legal library and re-read *every single law* from page 1. That clerk would collapse from exhaustion within an hour.

A **smart clerk** instead:

- Pre-builds a **decision tree (the RETE network)** that sorts applications by attributes (age, income, type) at intermediate desks (**alpha and beta nodes**).
- Keeps **open folders on every desk** showing which applications are currently passing which partial checks (**tokens stored in node memories**).
- When a *new* application arrives, the clerk only walks the **affected branch** of the tree and **updates the open folders in place** — never re-reading the whole library.

That smart clerk **IS the RETE algorithm.** The library is *Production Memory*, the application stack is *Working Memory*, the decision tree is the *RETE Network*, and the open folders are the *node memories (alpha and beta memories)*.

## 1.3 The Two Memory Stores RETE Operates On

| Memory Store | Symbol | Contents | Read/Write |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Production Memory** | PM | The set of all *rules* (IF–THEN productions). Compiled **once** into the network. | Read-only at runtime |
| **Working Memory** | WM | The set of all currently true *facts* (Working Memory Elements, WMEs). | Constantly modified by *assert*, *retract*, *modify* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:** RETE does **not** fire rules. It only *matches* them. The matched rule instances (called **activations** or **instantiations**) are placed in the **Conflict Set**, from which a *Conflict Resolution Strategy* (e.g., RECENCY, SPECIFICITY, LEX, MEA, or priority) selects exactly one rule to *fire* in the *Act* phase of the cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Recognize–Act Cycle (Where RETE Lives)

The RETE algorithm is the **Recognize** phase of the production-system inference cycle. The cycle has three steps:

1. **Match** — RETE compares WM against PM and produces the *Conflict Set* of all satisfied rule instances.
2. **Resolve** — A *Conflict Resolution Strategy* picks one activation from the Conflict Set.
3. **Act** — The chosen rule's *Right-Hand Side (RHS)* executes, which may *assert*, *retract*, or *modify* WMEs, triggering the cycle to repeat.

## 2.2 The Two Logical Sub-Networks

The RETE network is logically partitioned into two cascaded sub-networks:

### A. Alpha Network — Intra-Element Tests
- Performs **constant tests on a single fact** (one WME at a time).
- Each **alpha node** represents one *attribute–value test*, e.g., `(x.age > 18)`, `(x.color == red)`.
- Successfully tested WMEs are stored in the node's **Alpha Memory (α-memory)**.
- Complexity: **O(F)** per assertion/retraction.

### B. Beta Network — Inter-Element (Join) Tests
- Performs **join tests across pairs/tuples of facts from different alpha memories**.
- Each **beta node (join node)** combines the partial matches of its left and right inputs.
- Successfully joined tuples are stored in the node's **Beta Memory (β-memory)**.
- The **right input** is the *new* WME being propagated; the **left input** is the cached state of the partner memory — this is called the **"right-activated, left-memoized"** discipline and is the *core optimization* of RETE.
- A specialized **NOT node** performs *negated* conditions; a **EXISTS node** checks for existence without binding the variable.

## 2.3 Node Types — Complete Taxonomy

| Node Type | Symbol | Function | Memory? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Root Node** | R | Single entry point for all WMEs | No |
| **Alpha Node** | α | Single-WME constant test | Yes (α-memory) |
| **Two-Input Join Node** | β | Joins partial matches from two inputs | Yes (β-memory) |
| **NOT Node** | ¬ | Tests for *absence* of a matching WME | Yes |
| **Terminal Node** | T | Marks the end of a rule's LHS chain; produces an *activation* | No |
| **Memory (Storage) Node** | M | Pure storage holding tokens passing its parent test | Yes |

## 2.4 Token Model — The Unit of Flow

A **token** is a unit of state flowing through the network:

- **WME token** — a single asserted fact entering the root.
- **Token** — a tagged tuple: `(tag, WME_1, WME_2, ..., WME_n)`, where `tag ∈ {+ (positive), - (negative)}` indicates whether this is an *add* or *remove* update.
- Tokens are **propagated downward** as long as they pass tests, and **stored** in node memories to avoid re-computation.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Complexity Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Naïve Re-Match | RETE Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Worst-case match per cycle | $O(R \cdot F^P)$ | $O(F \cdot P \cdot k)$ amortized |
| Update on a single WME change | Re-scan everything | $O(P)$ nodes touched per change |
| Memory overhead | None | Stores all partial matches |
| Compilation | None (re-parse every cycle) | One-time cost to build network |
| Best use case | Small systems (<50 rules) | Large systems (100s–1000s of rules) |

> Symbols: *R* = number of rules, *F* = number of facts, *P* = patterns per rule, *k* = constant test cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** The space–time trade-off is the heart of RETE. You pay a **one-time compilation cost and permanent memory overhead** in exchange for **near-constant-time incremental updates**. This makes RETE the workhorse of real-time expert systems in manufacturing, telecom fault diagnosis, medical decision support, and cognitive cyber-physical diagnostics.

## 2.6 Step-by-Step Operation of the Match Phase

1. **Build Phase (compile-time, once):** Translate every rule's LHS into a sub-network; share common prefixes across rules by merging alpha and beta nodes.
2. **Initialize:** Insert all initial WMEs one-by-one through the root.
3. **Run Phase (runtime, every cycle):**
   - On `assert(w)`: propagate a `+` token for *w* down the network.
   - On `retract(w)`: propagate a `-` token for *w* down the network, removing cached tuples.
   - On `modify(w)`: equivalent to a retract followed by an assert.
4. **Activation Generation:** When a `+` token reaches a terminal node, an *activation* is added to the Conflict Set; a `-` token removes an existing activation.

## 2.7 Key Engineering Optimizations in RETE

- **Node Sharing (Common Sub-expression Elimination):** If two rules share an alpha test `(x > 10)`, only **one** alpha node is created and shared.
- **Right-Activation Discipline:** Only the *new/right* input is iterated; the *left* input is passively consulted from its memory — yielding **O(N + M)** joins instead of **O(N · M)**.
- **Memory Indexing:** Beta memories are typically indexed on the join variable to reduce the per-token lookup cost.
- **Unlinking / Lazy Evaluation:** Empty beta memories can be temporarily unlinked from the network to skip propagation — modern descendants like **Rete-II, Rete-OO, Rete-NT, and TREAT** extend this idea.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Building a RETE Network

**Rule Base** (two rules, shared and distinct patterns):

$$
R_1: \text{IF } (x.color = \text{red}) \land (x.shape = \text{cube}) \land (y.on = x) \;\text{ THEN } \text{stack}(x,y)
$$

$$
R_2: \text{IF } (x.color = \text{red}) \land (z.left = x) \;\text{ THEN } \text{tag}(z)
$$

**Initial Working Memory (WM):**

| WME | Fact |
|---|---|
| W1 | `(B1 color red shape cube)` |
| W2 | `(B2 color red shape sphere)` |
| W3 | `(B1 left-of B2)` |
| W4 | `(B2 on B1)` |

### Step 1 — Parse Patterns into α-nodes

For each unique constant test, create an α-node with its α-memory:

| α-node | Test | α-memory contents (after init) |
|---|---|---|
| α1 | `?x.color = red` | {W1, W2} |
| α2 | `?x.shape = cube` | {W1} |
| α3 | `?y.on = ?x` | {W4} |
| α4 | `?z.left = ?x` | {W3} |

### Step 2 — Wire β-nodes (Join Nodes) for $R_1$

- **β1** = join(α1, α2) on common variable `?x`. Produces tuples where the *same* WME passes both color-red AND shape-cube.

  Result: `{ (W1, W1) }` (only W1 satisfies both).

- **β2** = join(β1, α3) on `?x` of β1's first element and `?y.on = ?x` of α3.

  This requires `?x` from β1 to equal the first slot of α3's WME.
  - β1 holds `(W1)`. α3 holds `(W4: (B2 on B1))` — so `B1 == B1` ✓.
  - **Result:** token `(W1, W4)` stored in β2's memory.

- **Terminal T1** receives this token → produces **activation of R1**.

### Step 3 — Wire β-node for $R_2$

- **β3** = join(α1, α4) on `?x`. Joins color-red WMEs with left-of WMEs whose second slot equals `?x`.

  - α1 = {W1, W2}; α4 = {W3: (B1 left-of B2)}.
  - For W1 (B1): matches W3 (B1 left-of B2)? → `?x = B1` ✓.
  - For W2 (B2): matches W3? → `?x = B2` ≠ `B1` ✗.
  - **Result:** token `(W1, W3)`.

- **Terminal T2** receives this token → produces **activation of R2**.

### Step 4 — Conflict Set After Initialization

$$
\text{Conflict Set} = \{ R_1(W1, W4),\; R_2(W1, W3) \}
$$

### Step 5 — Incremental Update Demonstration

Suppose we now **assert** a new fact W5 = `(B3 color red shape cube)`.

- W5 enters at root; passes α1 (color red) → α1-memory becomes {W1, W2, W5}.
- W5 passes α2 (shape cube) → α2-memory becomes {W1, W5}.
- W5 propagates into β1 with `+` token. β1 consults its left input memory (α1) — but in **right-activated discipline**, β1 iterates the *new right* W5 against its **left** memory of color-red tokens: {W1, W2}.
  - Join with W1 (B1) on `?x` → already exists, so no new tuple (RETE **deduplicates**).
  - Join with W2 (B2) → shapes don't match (W2 is sphere). Reject.
- Result: **no new activation produced** — the network efficiently determined W5 cannot complete any rule without touching any other node.

Now suppose we **assert** W6 = `(B4 on B3)`.

- W6 passes α3 (the `on` test) → α3-memory becomes {W4, W6}.
- W6 propagates into β2 as right input. β2 consults its left memory of `(W1)` and `(W5)` from β1.
  - Join with W5 (B3): `?x = B3` and W6's first slot is `B4`, second slot is `B3` → `B3 == B3` ✓.
  - New token `(W5, W6)` flows to Terminal T1 → **new activation R1(W5, W6) added to Conflict Set**.
- Total nodes touched: **2** (α3 and β2) — exactly the optimization RETE promises.

## 3.2 Complexity Derivation — Why RETE Is Fast

Let:
- $R$ = number of rules
- $F$ = number of WMEs
- $P$ = average patterns per rule
- $k$ = cost of one test
- $N_\alpha$ = number of alpha nodes (≈ number of unique tests)
- $N_\beta$ = number of beta nodes (≈ number of joins)

**Naïve Approach — per cycle:**

$$
T_{\text{naive}} = R \cdot F^P \cdot k
$$

Because each of the $R$ rules must consider all $F^P$ possible $P$-tuples of WMEs. This explodes combinatorially.

**RETE Approach — amortized per WME change:**

$$
T_{\text{RETE}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_\alpha + N_\beta} c_i \;\approx\; (N_\alpha + N_\beta) \cdot k
$$

Each changed WME is propagated through **at most one path per pattern it appears in**, and a beta join touches only the indexed portion of its left memory. Empirically and asymptotically:

$$
T_{\text{RETE per update}} = O\!\left(P \cdot \log F\right) \quad \text{(with indexing)}
$$

$$
T_{\text{RETE per update}} = O(P \cdot F) \quad \text{(worst, un-indexed)}
$$

For typical expert systems where $P \ll F$ and most alpha tests pre-filter the data, the **constant factor of RETE is dramatically smaller** than $R \cdot F^P$, often by 2–4 orders of magnitude.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Naïve vs RETE Cost

Let $R = 500$ rules, $F = 200$ facts, $P = 4$ patterns/rule, $k = 1\ \mu s$.

$$
T_{\text{naive}} = 500 \cdot 200^4 \cdot 1\,\mu s = 500 \cdot 1.6 \times 10^9 \cdot 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 8 \times 10^5\ \text{s} \approx 9.26\ \text{days}
$$

$$
T_{\text{RETE per update}} \approx P \cdot \log_2 F \cdot k = 4 \cdot \log_2(200) \cdot 1\,\mu s \approx 4 \cdot 7.6 \cdot 1\,\mu s \approx 30\ \mu s
$$

> That is a **speedup factor of roughly 2.7 × 10<sup>10</sup>** (≈ 27 billion) for the same logical workload. This is precisely why RETE is the de-facto algorithm in industrial rule engines like **Drools, Jess, and CLIPS**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the RETE Engine

The diagram below maps the **information flow** inside a RETE-based production system — from raw facts in Working Memory, through the alpha and beta networks, down to the Conflict Set and the Recognize–Act cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Working Memory WM] -->|assert/retract/modify| B[Root Node R]
    B --> C1[Alpha Node a1 test color red]
    B --> C2[Alpha Node a2 test shape cube]
    B --> C3[Alpha Node a3 test on relation]
    B --> C4[Alpha Node a4 test left relation]

    C1 -->|positive tokens| M1[Alpha Memory AM1]
    C2 -->|positive tokens| M2[Alpha Memory AM2]
    C3 -->|positive tokens| M3[Alpha Memory AM3]
    C4 -->|positive tokens| M4[Alpha Memory AM4]

    M1 -->|right input| J1[Beta Join Node b1 join a1 and a2 on x]
    M2 -->|left input| J1

    M1 -->|right input| J2[Beta Join Node b2 join a1 and a4 on x]
    M4 -->|left input| J2

    J1 -->|partial matches| J3[Beta Join Node b3 join b1 and a3 on x]
    M3 -->|right input| J3

    J1 -->|partial matches| BM1[Beta Memory BM1]
    J2 -->|partial matches| BM2[Beta Memory BM2]
    J3 -->|partial matches| BM3[Beta Memory BM3]

    J3 --> T1[Terminal Node T1 end of Rule R1]
    J2 --> T2[Terminal Node T2 end of Rule R2]

    T1 -->|emits activation| CS[Conflict Set of Activations]
    T2 -->|emits activation| CS

    CS --> CRS[Conflict Resolution Strategy e.g. RECENCY SPECIFICITY LEX]
    CRS --> SEL[Selected Activation]

    SEL --> ACT[Act Phase execute RHS actions]
    ACT -->|may assert retract modify| A
```

## 4.2 Right-Activated / Left-Memoized Discipline — Visual Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LI[Beta Memory Left Input cached tuples] -->|consulted on demand| JN[Beta Join Node]
    RI[New Token Right Input] -->|drives propagation| JN
    JN -->|output tuples| BO[Beta Memory Output]
    JN -->|failed tuples| DROP[Discarded]

    style LI fill:#ffd9a8,stroke:#cc6600
    style RI fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99
    style JN fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#1f7a1f
    style BO fill:#e8d5f5,stroke:#5f1f7a
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The orange box is *passive* (only consulted); the blue box *drives* the join. This is the *exact* asymmetry that gives RETE its speed — at most one operand is iterated per update.

## 4.3 Token Propagation Sequence on a New Assertion

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant WM as Working Memory
    participant ROOT as Root Node
    participant ALPHA as Alpha Network
    participant BETA as Beta Network
    participant TERM as Terminal Nodes
    participant CS as Conflict Set

    WM->>ROOT: assert W5
    ROOT->>ALPHA: forward W5
    ALPHA->>ALPHA: test each alpha node
    Note over ALPHA: matched nodes update their alpha memories
    ALPHA->>BETA: forward + tokens
    BETA->>BETA: perform indexed joins against left memories
    Note over BETA: right-activated left-memoized discipline
    BETA->>TERM: forward new joined tuples
    TERM->>CS: add or remove activations
    Note over CS: Conflict Resolution picks one rule to fire
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the RETE algorithm. Why is it preferred over naïve re-matching in production systems? (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**
RETE is an **incremental pattern-matching algorithm** introduced by **Charles Forgy (1974)** that compiles all rule LHSs into a shared **directed acyclic network**. It maintains **alpha and beta memories** to cache partial matches, so that when a fact is asserted or retracted, only the *affected path* of the network is updated, rather than re-scanning all rules against all facts. It is preferred because its amortized update cost is **linear in the number of changed facts and rule patterns**, whereas naïve re-matching is **exponential** in the number of patterns per rule.

### Q2. Distinguish between Alpha Memory and Beta Memory in a RETE network. (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Alpha Memory (α-memory):** Stores **individual WMEs** that have passed a **single-constant test** (an alpha node). It holds *tokens* of size 1.
- **Beta Memory (β-memory):** Stores **tuples of WMEs** that have passed a **join (beta) test** combining partial matches from two child memories. It holds *tokens* of size ≥ 2 and represents *inter-fact relationships* required by the rule LHS.

In short, alpha memory deals with **intra-fact** tests, beta memory deals with **inter-fact** join results.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of the RETE algorithm**. Describe the role of the **alpha network** and **beta network** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

The RETE network is a **rooted, directed acyclic graph** compiled from the LHSs of all production rules. It has three logical layers:

1. **Root Node (R):** The single entry point through which every WME entering or leaving Working Memory is funneled.

2. **Alpha Network:** A tree of **alpha nodes**, each performing a *constant test* on a single WME attribute (e.g., `?x.color == red`). A WME that passes an alpha node's test is stored in that node's **alpha memory (α-memory)**. The alpha network filters out WMEs that cannot possibly satisfy any rule as early as possible.

3. **Beta Network:** A tree of **beta (join) nodes**, each merging two input streams — typically a beta memory on the left and a freshly arriving token on the right. The join test checks that the *join variables* (shared variables) are bound to the same value across the two input WMEs. Successful join results are stored in the node's **beta memory (β-memory)**.

4. **Terminal Nodes (T):** Mark the end of a rule's LHS. When a token reaches a terminal node, the corresponding rule becomes an **activation** and is added to the **Conflict Set**.

The **role of the alpha network** is to perform *intra-fact constant tests* cheaply, reducing the working set of facts the beta network must consider. The **role of the beta network** is to perform *inter-fact join tests*, combining partial matches into progressively larger tuples that approach the full set of WMEs required to satisfy a rule's LHS.

> *(Diagram — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**(b)** Explain the **Recognize–Act cycle** of a production system. How does the RETE algorithm make the *Recognize* phase efficient? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Recognize–Act cycle** is the perpetual inference loop of a rule-based system:

$$
\text{Match} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Resolve} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Act} \;\rightarrow\; (\text{loop back to Match})
$$

1. **Match:** Compare the current *Working Memory* against the LHSs of all rules in *Production Memory* to determine the **Conflict Set** of all satisfied rule instances (activations).
2. **Resolve:** Apply a **Conflict Resolution Strategy** (e.g., RECENCY, SPECIFICITY, LEX, MEA, or a user-defined priority) to select *one* activation from the Conflict Set.
3. **Act:** Execute the RHS of the selected rule, which typically *asserts*, *retracts*, or *modifies* WMEs in Working Memory, restarting the cycle.

The RETE algorithm makes the **Match** phase efficient through three principles:

- **Network Compilation:** All rule LHSs are compiled into a single shared network *once*; common prefixes are merged, avoiding redundant testing.
- **State Caching (Memory):** Partial matches are stored in alpha and beta memories, so they are not recomputed on every cycle.
- **Incremental Update:** When a single WME changes, RETE propagates a single *+* or *−* token through **only the affected path**, updating the conflict set in **O(P)** time where *P* is the number of patterns in the affected rules.

The result is a dramatic reduction in the cost of the Recognize phase, especially for large rule bases with many facts.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**Q. (a)** What is meant by **incremental matching** in RETE? Explain the **right-activated, left-memoized** discipline with an example. **(7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

**Incremental matching** means that the RETE engine does **not** rebuild the entire conflict set on every cycle. Instead, it updates the conflict set by propagating only the *delta* caused by a single WM change (assert, retract, or modify).

The **right-activated, left-memoized** discipline is the *mechanism* that makes this efficient at every beta (join) node:

- The **left input** is the *passive, cached* state — the contents of a beta memory holding all previously computed partial matches.
- The **right input** is the *newly arriving* token (the WME or partial tuple just propagated from above).
- The join node iterates **only over the right input** and consults the left input via **indexed lookup** on the join variable.

**Example:** Suppose beta node *β2* joins color-red WMEs with left-of WMEs, and its left memory already holds the tuple `(W1: B1 color red)`. When a new fact W7 = `(B1 left-of B3)` is asserted, it is the *right input* to *β2*. The join iterates over the *single* new right token W7 and looks up matching left tuples where `?x = B1`, finding `(W1)`. The result is a new tuple `(W1, W7)`. Only **one** new token is processed, not the full Cartesian product of all left-of × color-red facts.

**(b)** Compare **RETE** with the **TREAT** algorithm. List the **advantages and limitations** of RETE. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**RETE vs TREAT (Miranker, 1987):**

| Feature | RETE | TREAT |
|---|---|---|
| Memory of partial matches | **Full** (caches all partial tuples) | **None** for non-terminal joins |
| Update cost on WME change | Low — touches only the affected path | Higher — may re-join from scratch when a join variable's binding changes |
| Memory footprint | High | Low |
| Best suited for | Large rule bases, many shared patterns, frequent activations | Small rule bases, highly dynamic WM, or memory-constrained systems |
| Indexing | Optional but typical | Required for acceptable speed |
| Successors | Rete-II, Rete-OO, Rete-NT | Phased-Match, Gator |

**Advantages of RETE:**

1. **Speed** — incremental updates avoid full re-matching.
2. **Sharing** — common sub-patterns across rules share network nodes, reducing duplication.
3. **Predictable performance** — empirically linear in WM changes for well-indexed implementations.
4. **Maturity** — decades of production use in CLIPS, Jess, Drools.

**Limitations of RETE:**

1. **High memory consumption** — every partial match is stored indefinitely until removed.
2. **Garbage / staleness** — for rules with *negation* (NOT nodes), memory management becomes complex.
3. **Poor scaling with very high WM churn** — constant inserts and deletes thrash beta memories.
4. **Recompilation cost** — when rules change, the entire network must be rebuilt.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in RETE questions at KTU 2024:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Match with Fire:** RETE *matches* rules; it does **not** fire them. Mentioning the *Conflict Resolution* step is mandatory to earn full marks on architecture questions.
> 2. **Omitting the incremental property:** A common 2-mark loss is describing RETE as "a network that matches facts to rules" *without* emphasizing the *caching + incremental update* aspect — the entire point of the algorithm.
> 3. **Mixing up α and β memories:** Examiners frequently test this. Remember: **alpha = single-WME intra-fact test; beta = joined-tuple inter-fact test.**
> 4. **Forgetting node sharing:** Many answers describe each rule as having its own independent chain. The *sharing* of common alpha and beta sub-networks across rules is a defining feature; missing it costs marks.
> 5. **No complexity comparison:** Always state the **naïve O(R·F<sup>P</sup>) vs RETE O(P·F)** contrast — it is a free 2 marks and almost always asked.
> 6. **Negation handling:** If a question includes `not` in a rule LHS, you *must* discuss the **NOT node** and its memory semantics — a very common pitfall.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RETE = network.** It is a *compiled DAG* of all rule LHSs, *not* a re-scanning engine.
- **Two sub-networks:** **Alpha network** (intra-fact constant tests) + **Beta network** (inter-fact join tests).
- **Two memory types:** **α-memory** (single WMEs passing alpha tests) + **β-memory** (tuples of WMEs passing join tests).
- **Incremental update:** Only the *affected path* is re-evaluated on each WM change — the central optimization.
- **Right-activated / Left-memoized:** The right input drives the join; the left input is passively consulted from memory — gives O(N + M) per join instead of O(N·M).
- **Node sharing:** Common patterns across rules share nodes in the compiled network — saves both time and space.
- **Token model:** Every state change is a tagged `(+/−, WME_1, ..., WME_n)` token propagated downward.
- **Naïve vs RETE complexity:** Naïve is **O(R · F<sup>P</sup>)**; RETE amortizes to **O(P · F)** (or **O(P · log F)** with indexing) per WM change.
- **RETE matches; resolution & act complete the cycle.** The output of RETE is the **Conflict Set**; a separate **Conflict Resolution Strategy** (RECENCY, SPECIFICITY, LEX, MEA) selects the next rule.
- **Common variants to know:** **Rete-II, Rete-OO, Rete-NT, TREAT** — know the trade-off (memory vs. recompilation cost).
- **Real-world engines built on RETE:** **CLIPS, Jess, Drools, OPS5, Soar** — name-dropping these in a KTU answer signals strong understanding and impresses examiners.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Knowledge Organization: Indexing and Retrieval Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Knowledge Organization: Indexing and Retrieval Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Knowledge Organization (KO)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Knowledge Organization (KO) is the systematic discipline of structuring, classifying, indexing, and arranging knowledge artifacts (documents, sensor data, ontologies, semantic graphs) to enable efficient storage, discovery, retrieval, and reasoning. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, KO bridges the digital-information layer (cyber) with the physical-world layer (sensors, actuators, machines) by ensuring that the right knowledge reaches the right decision node at the right time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Knowledge Organization in CPS is not just about *data*. It is the **cognitive scaffolding** that makes a Cyber-Physical System "aware", "context-sensitive", and "intelligently responsive". Without KO, a CPS is merely a dumb sensor-actuator loop.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Library:**
Imagine a massive library with **10 million books** but no catalog, no shelves labelled by subject, and no librarian. Finding a book on "fault diagnosis in induction motors" would take years.
- **Indexing** is like the **Dewey Decimal System** — it labels and tags every book so its location becomes computable.
- **Retrieval** is the **librarian** — when you ask a question, the librarian uses the index to fetch the most relevant books.
- **Knowledge Organization** is the *entire system* — the shelves, the catalog cards, the classification rules, the librarian's training, and the cross-reference system.
- In a **CPS** (say, a smart factory), the "books" are sensor logs, maintenance records, control policies, and domain ontologies. KO is what makes the factory *cognitive*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of KO in CPS:**
> 1. **Representation** — How knowledge is encoded (vectors, graphs, symbols).
> 2. **Indexing** — How knowledge is stored for fast lookup.
> 3. **Retrieval** — How the right knowledge is fetched when needed.

---

### 1.2 Indexing

**Formal Definition:**
Indexing is the process of creating auxiliary data structures (**indexes**) that map *terms, concepts, or features* to their locations in a knowledge base, enabling sub-linear time retrieval. An index transforms an $O(N)$ scan into an $O(\log N)$ or $O(1)$ lookup.

**Standard Indexing Metrics (KTU-relevant):**
- **Index Size Overhead:** typically **15%–30%** of raw data size.
- **Build Time Complexity:** $O(N \cdot L)$ where $L$ is average document length.
- **Query Time Complexity:** $O(\log N)$ for tree-based, $O(1)$ for hash-based.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of the **index at the back of a textbook**. Instead of reading the entire book to find "Newton's Second Law", you flip to the index, locate the page numbers, and jump directly. The index "sacrifices" extra pages to save enormous search time.

---

### 1.3 Retrieval

**Formal Definition:**
Retrieval is the algorithmic process of locating and ranking knowledge units (documents, facts, embeddings) that are *relevant* to a given query, where relevance is mathematically modelled via similarity, probability, or semantic distance functions.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Retrieval is what **Google** does in milliseconds — it does not "understand" your question the way a human does, but it computes a *relevance score* across billions of pages and returns the best matches in ranked order. In CPS, retrieval is what allows a self-driving car to instantly match a camera-frame to a stored "pedestrian-near-crossing" knowledge pattern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Exam Favorite):**
> - **Data Retrieval** → exact match (DBMS, SQL).
> - **Information Retrieval (IR)** → best-match ranking, handles ambiguity and partial relevance.
> - **Knowledge Retrieval** → semantic, context-aware, uses ontologies and inference.
> CPS cognitive systems demand **Knowledge Retrieval**, not just IR.

---

### 1.4 Why Indexing & Retrieval Matter in CPS

| CPS Sub-system | Role of Indexing & Retrieval |
|---|---|
| Smart Grid | Retrieves fault-handling policies indexed by sensor signature |
| Autonomous Vehicles | Retrieves traffic-rule knowledge indexed by visual context |
| Smart Healthcare | Retrieves patient-history records indexed by symptoms |
| Industrial IoT | Retrieves maintenance logs indexed by equipment telemetry |
| Smart Buildings | Retrieves HVAC control rules indexed by occupancy pattern |

> [!NOTE]
> In a CPS, the **latency budget** for retrieval is often **< 10 ms** (hard real-time) or **< 100 ms** (soft real-time). This makes the choice of indexing technique a *safety-critical* engineering decision, not merely a software convenience.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Indexing Techniques

Indexing techniques are classified along three orthogonal axes:

**Axis A — Logical Structure:**
- **Flat (Linear) Indexing** — sequential scan, e.g., linear search over logs.
- **Tree-based Indexing** — B-Tree, B+ Tree, R-Tree, KD-Tree.
- **Hash-based Indexing** — Static and Dynamic Hashing.
- **Inverted Indexing** — term-to-document mapping (the backbone of search engines).
- **Graph-based Indexing** — adjacency lists, Knowledge Graph indices.
- **Vector-based Indexing** — HNSW, IVF, Annoy (for embeddings).

**Axis B — Granularity:**
- **Word-level** — token-based (TF-IDF, BM25).
- **Sentence-level** — passage retrieval.
- **Document-level** — whole-doc retrieval.
- **Concept-level** — semantic concept indices.

**Axis C — Construction Method:**
- **Manual Indexing** — human expert assigns terms (expensive, high quality).
- **Automatic Indexing** — algorithmic (cheap, scalable, may have noise).
- **Hybrid Indexing** — human + machine (best of both, used in legal/medical CPS).

---

### 2.2 Deep Dive — Inverted Index (The Workhorse of IR)

An **inverted index** is a mapping from **terms → postings list**.

- **Term Dictionary:** vocabulary of all unique terms.
- **Postings List:** for each term, the list of documents containing it, often with term-frequency and positional data.

**Why is it "inverted"?** Because the natural forward mapping is *document → terms it contains*. We invert this to get *term → documents containing it*, which is exactly what a query needs.

**Logical Structure of an Inverted Index:**
```
term        →  postings list (doc_id, term_frequency[, positions])
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
"transformer"  →  [(D3, 2, [45, 89]), (D7, 1, [12]), (D12, 4, [...])]
"voltage"      →  [(D3, 1, [102]), (D5, 3, [33, 67, 90]), (D7, 2, [...])]
"fault"        →  [(D1, 1, [200]), (D5, 2, [10, 88]), (D12, 3, [...])]
```

**Operations supported:**
- **Boolean AND** → intersect postings lists.
- **Boolean OR** → union postings lists.
- **Boolean NOT** → set difference.
- **Phrase query** → use positional indexes for adjacency checks.

> [!NOTE]
> In a **smart-grid CPS**, the inverted index for sensor logs would have terms like "overcurrent", "voltage_drop", "trip_signal" mapping to timestamps and equipment IDs. A query "show all overcurrent events in transformer T-7 last month" becomes a fast postings-list intersection.

---

### 2.3 Deep Dive — Tree-Based Indexing (B+ Tree)

**B+ Tree** is the dominant index in databases and time-series CPS data.

**Properties (must memorize for KTU):**
- All data lives in **leaf nodes** (leaf nodes are linked for range queries).
- Internal nodes store only **keys + child pointers**.
- **Order $m$** tree: each node has between $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ and $m$ children.
- Tree is **always balanced** — all leaves at the same depth.
- **Search time:** $O(\log_m N)$.
- **Insert/Delete time:** $O(\log_m N)$ with node splits/merges.
- **Range query:** $O(\log_m N + k)$ where $k$ is result size.

**Why B+ Tree for CPS time-series?**
CPS generates massive time-stamped sensor data. Queries like "all sensor readings between $t_1$ and $t_2$" are *range queries* — B+ Tree's linked-leaf structure makes this extremely efficient.

---

### 2.4 Deep Dive — Vector (Embedding) Indexing

Modern cognitive CPS use **dense vector embeddings** (from BERT, Sentence-Transformers, etc.) and need **Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN)** search.

**Key ANN Indices:**
- **HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World):** graph-based, $O(\log N)$ search, high recall.
- **IVF (Inverted File Index):** clusters vectors via k-means, search top clusters.
- **LSH (Locality-Sensitive Hashing):** hash-based probabilistic, $O(1)$ expected.
- **Annoy (Approximate Nearest Neighbors Oh Yeah):** random-projection trees, $O(\log N)$ with disk-friendly memory mapping.

---

### 2.5 Retrieval Models — The Theoretical Core

#### A. Boolean Retrieval Model
- Document $D$ and Query $Q$ are sets of terms.
- Relevance is **binary** ($1$ if matches Boolean expression, else $0$).
- **No ranking** — every matched document is equally "relevant".
- Fast, predictable, but no partial credit.

#### B. Vector Space Model (VSM)
- Document and query represented as vectors in $\mathbb{R}^V$ ($V$ = vocabulary size).
- Relevance = **cosine similarity** between query and document vectors.
- Supports ranking, but assumes term independence (the "orthogonality" assumption).

#### C. Probabilistic Model (BM25)
- Relevance is the **probability** that a document is relevant given the query.
- BM25 is the de-facto industry standard (used in Elasticsearch, Lucene, OpenSearch).
- Handles **term-frequency saturation** and **document-length normalization**.

#### D. Semantic / Neural Retrieval
- Uses **dense embeddings** from neural networks.
- Captures **synonymy, paraphrase, contextual meaning**.
- Powers modern RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) systems.

#### E. Knowledge-Graph Retrieval
- Traverses an ontology/graph to answer queries via inference.
- Used heavily in medical CPS, legal CPS, industrial standards compliance.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorize cheat sheet** for KTU 2024 ESE / Module tests. Every formula below is examinable. The vertical pipe `|` has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` for markdown safety.

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Symbols / Units** | **Where Used** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Term Frequency (raw) | $\text{TF}(t,d) = f_{t,d}$ | $f_{t,d}$ = count of term $t$ in doc $d$ | Raw weighting |
| 2 | Term Frequency (normalized) | $\text{TF}(t,d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Dimensionless ratio in $[0,1]$ | VSM, TF-IDF |
| 3 | Inverse Document Frequency | $\text{IDF}(t) = \log\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ | $N$ = total docs, $\text{df}(t)$ = docs containing $t$ | TF-IDF |
| 4 | TF-IDF weight | $w_{t,d} = \text{TF}(t,d) \times \text{IDF}(t)$ | Scalar weight | Ranking, VSM |
| 5 | Cosine similarity | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert\vert\vec{q}\vert\vert \, \vert\vert\vec{d}\vert\vert}$ | $\theta$ in $[0,\pi]$; similarity in $[-1,1]$ | VSM ranking |
| 6 | BM25 score | $\text{BM25}(t,d) = \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \dfrac{f_{t,d}(k_1+1)}{f_{t,d} + k_1\left(1 - b + b\dfrac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}$ | $k_1 \approx 1.2$, $b \approx 0.75$, $\vert d \vert$ = doc length, $\text{avgdl}$ = avg doc length | Modern IR engines |
| 7 | BM25 IDF (Lucene variant) | $\text{IDF}_{\text{BM25}}(t) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N - \text{df}(t) + 0.5}{\text{df}(t) + 0.5} + 1\right)$ | Bounded, non-negative | BM25 implementation |
| 8 | Jaccard similarity | $J(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $A,B$ = sets of terms; $J \in [0,1]$ | Boolean / set retrieval |
| 9 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $TP$ = true positives, $FP$ = false positives | Retrieval evaluation |
| 10 | Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $FN$ = false negatives | Retrieval evaluation |
| 11 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ | Harmonic mean of $P$ and $R$ | Single-metric IR eval |
| 12 | Mean Average Precision | $\text{MAP} = \dfrac{1}{\vert Q \vert}\sum_{q \in Q} \text{AP}(q)$ | Averaged across queries | Standard IR benchmark |
| 13 | Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain | $\text{nDCG}_k = \dfrac{\text{DCG}_k}{\text{IDCG}_k}$ | $\text{DCG}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{\text{rel}_i}{\log_2(i+1)}$ | Ranked retrieval quality |
| 14 | HNSW search complexity | $O(\log N)$ expected | $N$ = number of indexed vectors | ANN retrieval |
| 15 | B+ Tree search complexity | $O(\log_m N)$ | $m$ = tree order, $N$ = keys | Range / point queries |
| 16 | LSH collision probability (sign-random-projection) | $P[\text{collision}] = 1 - \dfrac{\theta}{\pi}$ | $\theta$ = angle between vectors | ANN via hashing |
| 17 | Shannon entropy (information content) | $H(t) = -\log_2 P(t)$ | Bits per symbol | Term informativeness |
| 18 | Pointwise Mutual Information | $\text{PMI}(x,y) = \log\dfrac{P(x,y)}{P(x)P(y)}$ | Captures co-occurrence | Collocation, semantic index |

---

### 2.7 Real-World Utility Summary

| **Domain** | **Indexing Used** | **Retrieval Used** | **Why** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Google Search | Inverted + ANN over embeddings | BM25 + Neural Reranking | Web-scale sub-second |
| Smart Grid SCADA | Time-series B+ Tree, Inverted | Boolean + Temporal | Hard real-time alarms |
| Autonomous Driving | Vector (HNSW) over driving scenes | k-NN over embeddings | Sub-50 ms perception |
| Medical Diagnosis CPS | Knowledge Graph + Inverted | Inference + BM25 | Explainable, auditable |
| Industrial Predictive Maintenance | Inverted over log events | TF-IDF + Cosine | Cross-equipment pattern match |
| Smart Building HVAC | Hash over sensor IDs | Rule-based retrieval | Low-power edge CPS |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask *"Why is BM25 preferred over raw TF-IDF?"* The model answer: **BM25 saturates term-frequency growth** (a term appearing 100 times is not 100× more important than 1 time) and **normalizes for document length** (a long doc mentioning a term once is not as relevant as a short doc mentioning it once). This makes BM25 empirically superior on most IR benchmarks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the TF-IDF Weight

We derive the TF-IDF weighting from **information-theoretic first principles** so a student can reproduce this in an exam.

**Step 1 — Define raw term frequency.**
Let $f_{t,d}$ be the raw count of term $t$ in document $d$.

**Step 2 — Normalize by document length.**
A document with 10,000 words mentioning "transformer" 5 times should *not* be treated the same as a 100-word document mentioning it 5 times. Normalize:

$$
\text{TF}(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}
$$

**Step 3 — Define document frequency.**
Let $\text{df}(t)$ be the number of documents in the collection $C$ that contain term $t$. Let $N = \vert C \vert$ be the total number of documents.

**Step 4 — Define the informativeness of a term.**
A term that appears in *every* document (e.g., "the", "is") carries **no discriminative power**. A term that appears in *one* document is **maximally discriminative**. This is captured by the **inverse document frequency**:

$$
\text{IDF}(t) = \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}
$$

*Justification:* Using Shannon's self-information $H(t) = -\log_2 P(t)$, where $P(t) = \text{df}(t)/N$ is the probability a random document contains $t$, we get $H(t) = -\log_2(\text{df}(t)/N) = \log_2(N/\text{df}(t))$. IDF is essentially self-information in disguise.

**Step 5 — Combine via multiplication.**
The final weight multiplies local importance (TF) by global importance (IDF):

$$
\boxed{\,w_{t,d} = \text{TF}(t,d) \times \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\,}
$$

This is the **TF-IDF weight** of term $t$ in document $d$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — TF-IDF on a CPS Knowledge Base

Consider a CPS knowledge base of $N = 4$ maintenance documents:

- **$D_1$:** "check voltage sensor and replace fuse"
- **$D_2$:** "voltage drop caused transformer trip"
- **$D_3$:** "replace fuse and reset breaker"
- **$D_4$:** "transformer overload triggered sensor alarm"

**Step 1 — Build vocabulary.**
$V = \{\text{check}, \text{voltage}, \text{sensor}, \text{and}, \text{replace}, \text{fuse}, \text{drop}, \text{caused}, \text{transformer}, \text{trip}, \text{reset}, \text{breaker}, \text{overload}, \text{triggered}, \text{alarm}\}$

**Step 2 — Compute document frequencies $\text{df}(t)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{df}(\text{voltage}) &= 2 \quad (D_1, D_2) \\
\text{df}(\text{sensor}) &= 2 \quad (D_1, D_4) \\
\text{df}(\text{transformer}) &= 2 \quad (D_2, D_4) \\
\text{df}(\text{fuse}) &= 2 \quad (D_1, D_3) \\
\text{df}(\text{replace}) &= 2 \quad (D_1, D_3) \\
\text{df}(\text{overload}) &= 1 \quad (D_4) \\
\text{df}(\text{trip}) &= 1 \quad (D_2) \\
\text{df}(\text{check}) &= 1 \quad (D_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute IDF (base-10, but base doesn't matter for ranking).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IDF}(\text{voltage}) &= \log\frac{4}{2} = \log 2 \approx 0.301 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{transformer}) &= \log\frac{4}{2} = 0.301 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{overload}) &= \log\frac{4}{1} = \log 4 \approx 0.602 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{trip}) &= 0.602 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{check}) &= 0.602
\end{aligned}
$$

**Observation:** "overload" is rarer → higher IDF → more discriminative. ✓

**Step 4 — Compute TF for $D_4$ (length 5 words).**

$$
\text{TF}(\text{transformer}, D_4) = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20, \quad \text{TF}(\text{overload}, D_4) = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20
$$

**Step 5 — Compute TF-IDF for $D_4$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{transformer}, D_4} &= 0.20 \times 0.301 = 0.0602 \\
w_{\text{overload}, D_4} &= 0.20 \times 0.602 = 0.1204
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** In $D_4$, "overload" is weighted **twice as high** as "transformer" because it is rarer in the corpus — exactly the discriminative behaviour we want from a CPS fault-diagnosis system.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the BM25 Scoring Function

The BM25 (Best Match 25) formula is derived from the **Binary Independence Model (BIM)** of probabilistic retrieval, with two heuristic refinements added to handle term-frequency saturation and document-length normalization.

**Step 1 — Start with the Robertson–Sparck Jones BIM weight.**

In BIM, the relevance weight of term $t$ is:

$$
w^{(1)}(t) = \log\frac{p(t \mid R) \cdot (1 - p(t \mid \bar{R}))}{p(t \mid \bar{R}) \cdot (1 - p(t \mid R))}
$$

where $p(t \mid R)$ is the probability of $t$ appearing in a *relevant* document, and $p(t \mid \bar{R})$ is the probability in a *non-relevant* document. After smoothing assumptions:

$$
w^{(1)}(t) \approx \log\frac{N - \text{df}(t) + 0.5}{\text{df}(t) + 0.5}
$$

**Step 2 — Add term-frequency saturation.**
Raw $f_{t,d}$ grows unboundedly. A natural saturating function is the **11BM** form:

$$
\text{TF-sat}(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{f_{t,d} + k_1}
$$

When $f_{t,d} \to 0$, this $\to 0$. When $f_{t,d} \to \infty$, this $\to 1$. The parameter $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$ controls *how fast* saturation occurs.

**Step 3 — Add document-length normalization.**
A document whose length $\vert d \vert$ is shorter than the average document length $\text{avgdl}$ should be *boosted* (concentration of terms in short doc = more relevant). A longer doc should be *penalized*. Linear interpolation:

$$
\text{TF-BM25}(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}(k_1 + 1)}{f_{t,d} + k_1 \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}
$$

Here $b \in [0, 1]$ interpolates between no normalization ($b=0$) and full normalization ($b=1$).

**Step 4 — Final BM25 score for a multi-term query.**

For query $Q = \{q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n\}$:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{BM25}(d, Q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IDF}(q_i) \cdot \frac{f_{q_i, d}(k_1 + 1)}{f_{q_i, d} + k_1\left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}\,}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked BM25 Example

**Setup:** Collection of $N = 3$ CPS documents; query $Q = \{\text{voltage}, \text{fault}\}$; parameters $k_1 = 1.5$, $b = 0.75$.

- **$D_1$** (length 6): "voltage sensor fault alarm voltage"
- **$D_2$** (length 4): "voltage drop fault"
- **$D_3$** (length 5): "current sensor alarm"

**Average document length:**

$$
\text{avgdl} = \frac{6 + 4 + 5}{3} = 5
$$

**Document frequencies:**

$$
\text{df}(\text{voltage}) = 2 \quad (D_1, D_2), \qquad \text{df}(\text{fault}) = 2 \quad (D_1, D_2)
$$

**BM25 IDF (Lucene smoothed form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IDF}(\text{voltage}) &= \ln\!\left(\frac{3 - 2 + 0.5}{2 + 0.5} + 1\right) = \ln(1.6) \approx 0.470 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{fault}) &= \ln(1.6) \approx 0.470
\end{aligned}
$$

**Score for $D_1$ (length 6):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BM25 term component for "voltage"} &= \frac{2 \times (1.5+1)}{2 + 1.5 \cdot \left(1 - 0.75 + 0.75 \cdot \frac{6}{5}\right)} \\
&= \frac{5.0}{2 + 1.5 \cdot (0.25 + 0.9)} \\
&= \frac{5.0}{2 + 1.5 \cdot 1.15} \\
&= \frac{5.0}{2 + 1.725} = \frac{5.0}{3.725} \approx 1.342
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BM25 term component for "fault"} &= \frac{1 \times 2.5}{1 + 1.5 \cdot (0.25 + 0.9)} \\
&= \frac{2.5}{1 + 1.725} = \frac{2.5}{2.725} \approx 0.917
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{BM25}(D_1, Q) = 0.470 \times 1.342 + 0.470 \times 0.917 \approx 0.631 + 0.431 = 1.062
$$

**Score for $D_2$ (length 4):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TF component for "voltage"} &= \frac{1 \times 2.5}{1 + 1.5 \cdot (0.25 + 0.75 \cdot \frac{4}{5})} \\
&= \frac{2.5}{1 + 1.5 \cdot (0.25 + 0.6)} = \frac{2.5}{1 + 1.275} = \frac{2.5}{2.275} \approx 1.099
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TF component for "fault"} &= \frac{1 \times 2.5}{1 + 1.275} \approx 1.099
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{BM25}(D_2, Q) = 0.470 \times 1.099 + 0.470 \times 1.099 \approx 2 \times 0.516 = 1.033
$$

**Score for $D_3$ (no match):**

$$
\text{BM25}(D_3, Q) = 0
$$

**Ranking:** $D_1 \succ D_2 \succ D_3$ (D₁ ranks first despite $D_2$ being shorter, because $D_1$ has *double* the term frequency of "voltage".)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for students:** $D_2$ would have outranked $D_1$ *if* the document-length normalization were stronger (i.e., $b \to 1$) — this is precisely the *trade-off* BM25 exposes and is a classic viva question.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Indexing-Retrieval Pair

| **Query Type** | **Latency Need** | **Data Scale** | **Recommended Index** | **Recommended Retrieval** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exact point lookup | < 1 ms | $10^9$ records | Hash | $O(1)$ direct |
| Time-range scan | < 10 ms | $10^8$ events | B+ Tree on time | Range iterator |
| Boolean keyword | < 50 ms | $10^6$ docs | Inverted | Posting-list intersection |
| Ranked top-$k$ | < 100 ms | $10^6$ docs | Inverted + BM25 | BM25 ranking |
| Semantic similarity | < 200 ms | $10^7$ embeddings | HNSW or IVF | ANN k-NN |
| Relational inference | < 500 ms | $10^5$ triples | Knowledge Graph | SPARQL / graph traversal |
| Hybrid (keyword + semantic) | < 300 ms | $10^7$ items | Inverted + HNSW | BM25 → Rerank with embeddings |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Indexing & Retrieval Pipeline in a CPS Cognitive Loop

The following Mermaid block shows the **complete functional architecture** of how raw CPS data flows through indexing, storage, retrieval, and finally reaches a downstream AI decision module.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rawA["Raw Sensor Streams<br>Voltage Current Temperature Vibration"]
    rawB["Maintenance Logs<br>PDFs Free Text"]
    rawC["Domain Ontologies<br>OWL RDF Schemas"]
    token["Tokenizer<br>Lowercase Stopword Removal"]
    norm["Normalizer<br>Stemming Lemmatization"]
    fwdIdx["Forward Index<br>Doc ID to Term List"]
    invIdx["Inverted Index<br>Term to Posting List"]
    vecEmb["Neural Embedder<br>BERT or MiniLM Encoder"]
    vecStore["Vector Store<br>HNSW or IVF Index"]
    kgStore["Knowledge Graph Store<br>Triples Subject Predicate Object"]
    queryIn["CPS Query<br>Symbolic Text Multimodal"]
    parser["Query Parser<br>Boolean Expansion"]
    bm25["BM25 Retriever<br>Lexical Ranking"]
    ann["ANN Retriever<br>Cosine over Embeddings"]
    rerank["Cross Encoder Reranker<br>Fine Grained Scoring"]
    fusion["Reciprocal Rank Fusion<br>Combines Lexical and Semantic"]
    decM["Decision Module<br>Anomaly Detection Actuation"]
    actOut["Actuator Command<br>Trip Reset Alarm Display"]

    rawA --> token
    rawB --> token
    rawC --> kgStore
    token --> norm
    norm --> fwdIdx
    fwdIdx --> invIdx
    norm --> vecEmb
    vecEmb --> vecStore
    queryIn --> parser
    parser --> bm25
    parser --> ann
    invIdx --> bm25
    vecStore --> ann
    bm25 --> rerank
    ann --> rerank
    rerank --> fusion
    kgStore --> fusion
    fusion --> decM
    decM --> actOut
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left column** is the *indexing pipeline* (offline / batch).
- The **middle column** is the *storage layer* (inverted index, vector store, knowledge graph).
- The **right column** is the *retrieval pipeline* (online / real-time).
- The **bottom row** is the *decision and actuation* — the physical-world closure of the CPS loop.

> [!NOTE]
> In **edge CPS** (e.g., a smart sensor with limited memory), the inverted index may live on-device while the vector store and knowledge graph live in the cloud. The *fusion* module then runs at a fog node. This is a classic KTU essay question: *"Discuss the edge–fog–cloud split for indexing in CPS."*

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Inverted Index Lookup Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    q["Query Term<br>transformer fault"]
    pre["Query Preprocessor<br>Tokenize Stem"]
    dict["Term Dictionary Lookup<br>Hash Map O(1)"]
    pl1["Postings List<br>transformer to D2 D4 D5"]
    pl2["Postings List<br>fault to D1 D2 D4 D7"]
    inter["Intersection Algorithm<br>Merging Sorted Lists"]
    res["Result Set<br>D2 D4"]
    rank["BM25 Score<br>Ranking by Relevance"]

    q --> pre --> dict
    dict --> pl1
    dict --> pl2
    pl1 --> inter
    pl2 --> inter
    inter --> res --> rank
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — BM25 Score Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    t1["Input<br>Query Term q and Document d"]
    t2["Fetch f_qd<br>Term Frequency in d"]
    t3["Fetch N and df q<br>Corpus Statistics"]
    t4["Compute IDF q<br>Log N over df"]
    t5["Compute Length Norm<br>1 minus b plus b times d over avgdl"]
    t6["Combine Numerator<br>f qd times k1 plus 1"]
    t7["Combine Denominator<br>f qd plus k1 times Length Norm"]
    t8["Compute TF Saturation<br>Numerator over Denominator"]
    t9["Final BM25 Score<br>IDF q times TF Saturation"]

    t1 --> t2
    t1 --> t3
    t3 --> t4
    t2 --> t6
    t2 --> t7
    t1 --> t5
    t5 --> t7
    t6 --> t8
    t7 --> t8
    t4 --> t9
    t8 --> t9
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — HNSW Vector Index Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    entry["Entry Point<br>Top Layer Node"]
    l1["Layer 2<br>Few Long Range Links"]
    l2["Layer 1<br>Medium Density"]
    l3["Layer 0<br>Dense Base Layer<br>All Vectors"]
    q["Query Vector q"]
    n1["Neighbor Set"]
    n2["Refined Neighbor Set"]
    n3["Top k Nearest Neighbors"]

    entry --> l1
    l1 --> l2
    l2 --> l3
    q --> entry
    l1 --> n1
    l2 --> n2
    l3 --> n3
```

**Reading:** HNSW search starts at the *sparse top layer* (long-range jumps), greedily descends, and refines at the *dense base layer*. This gives **logarithmic expected search** with **high recall** — the gold standard for embedding-based retrieval in CPS.

---
