# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Expert System Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User / Operator] -->|Query / HMI| B[User Interface Subsystem]
    B --> C[Working Memory - Dynamic Facts]
    C --> D[Inference Engine]
    E[Knowledge Base - Rules and Facts] --> D
    D -->|Fired Rule Trace| F[Explanation Subsystem]
    F -->|Justification| B
    D -->|Derived Conclusions| C
    D -->|Actuation Signal| G[Actuator / CPS Controller]
    H[Knowledge Engineer] -->|Acquisition| I[Knowledge Acquisition Subsystem]
    I --> E
    G -->|Feedback Telemetry| C
```

### Block-Level Interpretation

- **User Interface** — Front-end gateway (HMI, voice, AR).
- **Working Memory** — Scratchpad for current session facts.
- **Inference Engine** — Reasoning core applying forward/backward chaining.
- **Knowledge Base** — Persistent rule store.
- **Explanation Subsystem** — Generates human-readable justifications.
- **Knowledge Acquisition** — Ingest pipeline for new expert heuristics.
- **Actuator** — Closes the CPS loop by acting on derived conclusions.

---

## 4.2 Cognitive Communication Loop (OODA for CPS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor Array] -->|Raw Signals| S2[Perception Module]
    S2 -->|Feature Vectors| S3[Orientation - Context Interpretation]
    S3 -->|Situation Assessment| S4[Decision Module - Expert System]
    S4 -->|Action Command| S5[Actuator Layer]
    S5 -->|Physical Effect| S6[Environment]
    S6 -->|Feedback| S1
    S4 -.->|Explanation| S7[Operator Dashboard]
```

### Functional Description

- **Sense** → Sensors acquire physical state.
- **Orient** → Cognitive layer interprets data semantically.
- **Decide** → Expert system reasons over KB.
- **Act** → Actuator executes physical change.
- **Loop closure** → Feedback re-enters the system (true CPS property).

---

## 4.3 Multi-Agent Communication using FIPA-ACL

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Agent 1 - Diagnostician] -->|inform| A2[Agent 2 - Planner]
    A2 -->|request| A3[Agent 3 - Executor]
    A3 -->|agree| A2
    A1 -->|cfp Call for Proposal| A4[Agent 4 - Resource Manager]
    A4 -->|propose| A1
    A1 -->|accept-proposal| A4
    A2 -->|failure| A1
```

### Interpretation

- **`inform`** — Asserting a fact.
- **`request`** — Asking for an action.
- **`cfp`** — Initiating a contract-net protocol.
- **`propose`** — Bid in response to cfp.
- **`accept-proposal`** / **`reject-proposal`** — Contract finalization.
- **`failure`** — Reporting task failure.

This is the **FIPA Contract Net Protocol**, a high-yield KTU concept.

---

## 4.4 Forward Chaining — Rete Network Style

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Fact Temperature gt 90] --> J1[Junction Node AND]
    F2[Fact Vibration equals High] --> J1
    J1 --> R1[Rule 1 - Bearing Fault]
    F3[Fact Bearing Fault] --> R2[Rule 2 - Shut Down Motor]
    R1 --> F3
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> A1[Action - Motor Shutdown]
```

### Functional Mapping

The Rete network is an efficient pattern-matching graph used by production systems (e.g., CLIPS, Jess). Each **junction node** tests multiple conditions; successful matches propagate tokens down the rule nodes.

---

## 4.5 Cognitive Radio Spectrum Sharing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Primary User PU] -.->|Licensed Band| S[Spectrum Pool]
    SU1[Secondary User 1 - CR] -->|Sense| S
    SU2[Secondary User 2 - CR] -->|Sense| S
    SU1 -->|Decision| DA[Dynamic Spectrum Allocator]
    SU2 -->|Decision| DA
    DA -->|Allocate Vacant Hole| SU1
    DA -->|Allocate Vacant Hole| SU2
```

### Explanation

Cognitive Radios sense **spectrum holes** (white spaces) and opportunistically transmit. The decision engine is essentially an expert system operating on RF-domain rules.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an Expert System. List and briefly explain its main components.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation)

An **Expert System (ES)** is a computer program that emulates the decision-making ability of a human expert in a specific, narrow domain by applying a structured **knowledge base** and an **inference engine** to derive conclusions from facts. **(1 mark)**

**Main Components: (2 marks — 0.5 each)**

1. **Knowledge Base** — Repository of domain facts and heuristics encoded as rules.
2. **Inference Engine** — Reasoning mechanism that applies forward or backward chaining.
3. **Working Memory** — Stores session-specific dynamic facts.

> *(Additional valid components: Explanation Subsystem, Knowledge Acquisition Subsystem)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Forward Chaining and Backward Chaining in expert systems.**

#### Model Answer

| Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Data-driven (antecedent → consequent) | Goal-driven (consequent → antecedent) |
| **Search Strategy** | Breadth-first | Depth-first |
| **Use Case** | Monitoring, real-time CPS control | Diagnosis, hypothesis testing |
| **Goal** | Unknown — derives all conclusions | Known — verifies hypothesis |

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Instructions:** Answer **ONE** full question. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) ⭐

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an Expert System with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the Inference Engine and Knowledge Base in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CPS expert system monitors a chemical reactor. The following rules and facts are given:

**Rules:**
- $R_1$: IF `Pressure > 5 bar` AND `Temp > 150°C` → `Exothermic_Runaway (CF = 0.9)`
- $R_2$: IF `Exothermic_Runaway` → `Emergency_Cooling (CF = 0.85)`
- $R_3$: IF `Coolant_Valve = Closed` → `Emergency_Cooling fails (CF = -0.7)`

**Facts:**
- Pressure = 6 bar, Temp = 160°C, Coolant_Valve = Open

Apply **forward chaining** and determine the final CF for `Emergency_Cooling`. Also discuss what happens if the coolant valve is found closed. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Architecture Diagram:** *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid block)*

**Key Explanation Points:**

- **Knowledge Base (2 marks):** Encodes domain expertise as production rules `IF <antecedent> THEN <conclusion>`. May also use frames, semantic nets, or ontologies. In modern CPS, knowledge is often represented in **RDF/OWL** for semantic interoperability.

- **Inference Engine (2 marks):** The reasoning core. Applies either **forward chaining** (data-driven) or **backward chaining** (goal-driven). Uses **pattern matching** (e.g., Rete algorithm) for efficient rule lookup.

- **Other components (3 marks):**
  - *Working Memory* — Dynamic fact store.
  - *Explanation Subsystem* — Generates "why" and "how" traces.
  - *User Interface* — HMI for operator queries.
  - *Knowledge Acquisition Subsystem* — Ingests expert knowledge.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Step-by-Step Forward Chaining**

**Step 1:** Initialize working memory.

$$
\text{WM}_0 = \{P = 6\,\text{bar}, T = 160^\circ\text{C}, V = \text{Open}\}
$$

**Step 2:** Match antecedents.

$R_1$ matches: $P = 6 > 5$ ✓ AND $T = 160 > 150$ ✓
$R_3$ does **not** match (Coolant_Valve = Open, not Closed).

**Step 3:** Fire $R_1$.

$$
\text{CF}(\text{Exothermic\_Runaway}) = \text{CF}(\text{antecedent}) \times \text{CF}(R_1) = 1.0 \times 0.9 = 0.9
$$

**Step 4:** Update WM.

$$
\text{WM}_1 = \text{WM}_0 \cup \{\text{Exothermic\_Runaway (CF = 0.9)}\}
$$

**Step 5:** Fire $R_2$.

$$
\text{CF}(\text{Emergency\_Cooling}) = 0.9 \times 0.85 = 0.765
$$

**Step 6:** Final state.

$$
\boxed{\text{CF}(\text{Emergency\_Cooling}) = 0.765}
$$

**Step 7: Counterfactual analysis — if Coolant_Valve = Closed:**

$R_3$ fires:

$$
\text{CF}(\text{Emergency\_Cooling fails}) = 1.0 \times (-0.7) = -0.7
$$

Now combine with the positive CF of 0.765 from $R_2$ using the **opposite-sign combination rule**:

$$
\text{CF}_{\text{combined}} = \dfrac{\text{CF}_1 + \text{CF}_2}{1 - \min(\vert \text{CF}_1 \vert, \vert \text{CF}_2 \vert)}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{0.765 + (-0.7)}{1 - \min(0.765, 0.7)} = \dfrac{0.065}{1 - 0.7} = \dfrac{0.065}{0.3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CF}_{\text{combined}} \approx 0.217}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** Emergency cooling is **triggered but unreliable** (CF = 0.217), warranting a secondary fail-safe such as manual override or inert gas purging.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **communication** in Cognitive Cyber-Physical Systems. Explain the **OODA loop** and **FIPA-ACL** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Shannon channel capacity** for a wireless CPS sensor transmitting with bandwidth $B = 2$ MHz and $\text{SNR} = 15$ dB. Also explain how **cognitive radios** use dynamic spectrum access. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Role of Communication in CPS (3 marks):**
- Enables **data ingestion** from distributed sensors.
- Supports **actuator command dispatch** (downstream).
- Facilitates **multi-agent coordination** (horizontal).
- Carries **explainability traces** to operators (feedback).
- Foundation for **OODA loop closure**.

**OODA Loop (2 marks):**
- **Observe** — Sensors capture physical state.
- **Orient** — Cognitive layer interprets semantics.
- **Decide** — Expert system / AI module reasons.
- **Act** — Actuator executes physical change.
- This loop is the **temporal heartbeat** of any cognitive CPS.

**FIPA-ACL (2 marks):**
- **Foundation for Intelligent Physical Agents — Agent Communication Language.**
- Standardized set of **22 communicative acts** (performatives) such as `inform`, `request`, `cfp`, `propose`, `accept-proposal`, `failure`.
- Example: In a drone swarm, the leader sends a `cfp` (Call for Proposal) for surveillance tasks; drones reply with `propose` bids; leader issues `accept-proposal` to the winner.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Shannon Capacity + Cognitive Radio**

**Step 1: Convert SNR to linear scale.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{15/10} = 10^{1.5} \approx 31.62
$$

**Step 2: Apply Shannon formula.**

$$
C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 2 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 31.62)
$$

$$
= 2 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(32.62)
$$

**Step 3: Compute $\log_2(32.62)$.**

$$
\log_2(32.62) = \dfrac{\ln(32.62)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{3.4845}{0.6931} \approx 5.027
$$

**Step 4: Final capacity.**

$$
C = 2 \times 10^6 \times 5.027
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 10.054 \text{ Mbits/s}}
$$

**Cognitive Radio Explanation (3 marks):**

- A **Cognitive Radio (CR)** is an SDR (Software Defined Radio) enhanced with a **cognition engine** (often an expert system).
- Performs **spectrum sensing** to detect unused bands ("white spaces").
- Uses **dynamic spectrum access** to opportunistically transmit without harming primary (licensed) users.
- Decision cycle: *Sense → Analyze → Decide → Share → Move*.
- Real-world deployment: **IEEE 802.22 Wireless Regional Area Networks (WRAN)** for TV white-space broadband.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing the CF combination rules** — Sequential vs. converging evidence. *Always state which scenario applies and justify.*
> 2. **Forgetting the multiplication step** — When a rule fires, $\text{CF}(\text{output}) = \text{CF}(\text{input}) \times \text{CF}(\text{rule})$. Many students omit the rule's CF.
> 3. **Missing the OODA loop label** — Examiners award a separate 1 mark for correctly identifying all four stages and the loop closure.
> 4. **No diagram in long answers** — A neat block diagram of the expert system architecture is worth **2 marks** by itself; skipping it is a guaranteed deduction.
> 5. **FIPA-ACL misnaming** — Writing "FIPA agent language" or "FIPA protocol" instead of **FIPA-ACL (Agent Communication Language)** loses terminology marks.
> 6. **Shannon formula error** — Forgetting to convert SNR from dB to linear scale before substitution is the most common numerical error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

- ✅ An **Expert System** mimics a human expert in a narrow domain using a **Knowledge Base + Inference Engine**.
- ✅ **Forward Chaining** = data-driven (bottom-up); **Backward Chaining** = goal-driven (top-down).
- ✅ The **Certainty Factor (CF)** model (Shortliffe–Buchanan) handles uncertain reasoning; range $[-1, +1]$.
- ✅ **Sequential CF**: $\text{CF}_1 + \text{CF}_2 - \text{CF}_1 \cdot \text{CF}_2$ (both $\geq 0$).
- ✅ **Converging CF (same sign, +)**: $\text{CF}_1 + \text{CF}_2 (1 - \text{CF}_1)$.
- ✅ **Converging CF (opposite signs)**: $\dfrac{\text{CF}_1 + \text{CF}_2}{1 - \min(\vert \text{CF}_1 \vert, \vert \text{CF}_2 \vert)}$.
- ✅ **Rule application CF**: multiply the antecedent CF by the rule's CF.
- ✅ **OODA Loop**: Observe → Orient → Decide → Act — the cognitive CPS heartbeat.
- ✅ **FIPA-ACL** has 22 performatives; commonly tested: `inform`, `request`, `cfp`, `propose`, `accept-proposal`, `failure`.
- ✅ **Shannon Capacity**: $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$; remember **dB → linear** conversion.
- ✅ **Cognitive Radio** senses spectrum holes and dynamically reallocates — decision engine is an expert system.
- ✅ Classical examples: **MYCIN** (medical), **DENDRAL** (chemistry), **XCON** (configuration).
- ✅ The **Explanation Subsystem** is the precursor of modern **Explainable AI (XAI)**.
- ✅ In CPS, **communication = nervous system**; **expert system = brain** — never decouple them in exam answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Linguistics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview of Linguistics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Linguistics

**Linguistics** is the rigorous, scientific study of human language. It investigates the structure, development, acquisition, and use of natural languages through empirical observation, theoretical modeling, and computational analysis. In the context of **Cognitive Systems and AI in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, linguistics forms the foundational pillar for **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, **Speech Recognition**, and **Human–Machine Interaction (HMI)** within intelligent autonomous systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECPT754):**
> Linguistics is the systematic, rule-governed study of language, encompassing **phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, and pragmatics** — collectively referred to as the *levels of linguistic analysis*.

## 1.2 Key Branches of Linguistics (Syllabus Backbone)

Linguistics is decomposed into several analytical branches, each examining a specific dimension of human language:

| Branch | Focus Area | CPS/AI Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Phonetics** | Physical production & acoustic properties of speech sounds | Speech-to-text in voice-controlled CPS |
| **Phonology** | Sound patterns & rules governing phonemes | Acoustic modeling in ASR systems |
| **Morphology** | Internal structure of words (roots, affixes) | Tokenization & lemmatization |
| **Syntax** | Sentence structure & grammatical rules | Parsers, grammar formalisms |
| **Semantics** | Meaning of words & sentences | Knowledge graphs, word embeddings |
| **Pragmatics** | Context-dependent meaning | Dialogue systems, intent recognition |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Language Engineering" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of language as a **layered building**.
> - The **ground floor** is **Phonetics/Phonology** (the raw sounds a speaker produces).
> - The **first floor** is **Morphology** (the bricks — words, built from smaller meaningful pieces).
> - The **second floor** is **Syntax** (the blueprint — how bricks are arranged into walls/sentences).
> - The **third floor** is **Semantics** (the meaning of the rooms inside).
> - The **roof** is **Pragmatics** (what the whole building *communicates* in its environment).
>
> A CPS agent like a smart home robot must navigate *every floor* to understand a command like "Switch off the bedroom light gently" — it needs phonology (hear the sounds), morphology (parse "switch" as a verb), syntax (know the command structure), semantics (know that "switch" means toggle), and pragmatics (infer "gently" implies dimming, not abrupt cutoff).

## 1.4 The Five Levels of Linguistic Analysis — KTU-Focused Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Hierarchy (Mnemonic: P-M-S-S-P → "Please Make Sentences Sound Perfect"):**
> 1. **Phonology** → smallest *sound* units (phonemes)
> 2. **Morphology** → smallest *meaning* units (morphemes)
> 3. **Syntax** → *structure* of sentences
> 4. **Semantics** → *literal meaning*
> 5. **Pragmatics** → *intended/contextual meaning*

## 1.5 Linguistics vs. Natural Language Processing (NLP) — A Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Linguistics | NLP (AI Subfield) |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Understand language scientifically | Build systems that process language |
| **Output** | Theories, rules, grammars | Algorithms, models, applications |
| **Method** | Descriptive, theoretical | Computational, statistical, neural |
| **Relation to CPS** | Provides theoretical foundation | Implements real-time language-aware CPS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bottom Line for KTU Exam:** Linguistics is to NLP what **physics** is to **electrical engineering** — one provides the theory, the other builds the artifact.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Functional Levels of Linguistic Analysis — Detailed Breakdown

### 🔹 Level 1: Phonetics
- Deals with the **physical production (articulatory), transmission (acoustic), and perception (auditory)** of speech sounds.
- **International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)** is the standard transcription system.
- In CPS, phonetics enables acoustic feature extraction (MFCCs — Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients).

### 🔹 Level 2: Phonology
- Studies the **abstract sound system** of a language: which sounds are distinct, how they pattern, and the rules governing their combination.
- Example: In English, /p/ and /b/ are distinct phonemes (pin vs. bin), but in some languages they are allophones.
- **Minimal Pairs** are diagnostic tools for phoneme identification.

### 🔹 Level 3: Morphology
- Examines the **minimal meaningful units (morphemes)** and how they combine to form words.
- Two key processes:
  - **Inflection** → grammatical variation (walk → walking)
  - **Derivation** → new word creation (happy → happiness)
- Fundamental to **tokenization** and **lemmatization** in NLP pipelines.

### 🔹 Level 4: Syntax
- Concerned with **sentence structure** and the rules governing the combination of words into grammatical sequences.
- Formalized through **Generative Grammar (Chomsky hierarchy)**, **Phrase Structure Grammars**, and **Dependency Grammars**.
- Output: **Parse Trees** representing grammatical relations.

### 🔹 Level 5: Semantics
- Studies the **literal meaning** of words, phrases, and sentences.
- Distinguished from syntax: "The dog bit the boy" vs. "The boy bit the dog" — same syntax, different semantics.
- Computational tools: **WordNet, ontologies, semantic role labeling, distributional semantics**.

### 🔹 Level 6: Pragmatics
- Concerned with **meaning in context** — what a speaker intends to convey beyond literal words.
- Handles phenomena: **speech acts, deixis, implicatures, presuppositions**.
- Central to **dialogue systems** and **intent classification** in CPS.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are essential for KTU 2024 numerical/analytical questions in PECPT754.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notation & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **N-gram Probability (Chain Rule)** | $P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ | $w_1^n$ = sequence from word 1 to n |
| 2 | **Bigram (2-gram) Probability** | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$ | $C(\cdot)$ = count in corpus |
| 3 | **Unigram Probability** | $P(w_i) = \dfrac{C(w_i)}{N}$ | $N$ = total tokens |
| 4 | **Bayes' Theorem (NLP relevance)** | $P(C \mid W) = \dfrac{P(W \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(W)}$ | $C$ = class, $W$ = word sequence |
| 5 | **TF-IDF Weighting** | $\text{tf-idf}(t, d) = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \text{idf}(t)$ | $t$ = term, $d$ = document |
| 6 | **Inverse Document Frequency** | $\text{idf}(t) = \log \left( \dfrac{N}{\vert \{d : t \in d\} \vert} \right)$ | $N$ = total documents |
| 7 | **Zipf's Law (Word Frequency)** | $f \cdot r \approx C$ | $f$ = frequency, $r$ = rank, $C$ = constant |
| 8 | **Perplexity (Language Model Eval)** | $PP(W) = 2^{H(W)} = P(w_1 w_2 \dots w_N)^{-1/N}$ | Lower is better |
| 9 | **Cosine Similarity (Semantic)** | $\text{sim}(A, B) = \dfrac{A \cdot B}{\Vert A \Vert \cdot \Vert B \Vert}$ | Range: $[-1, 1]$ |
| 10 | **Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI)** | $\text{PMI}(x, y) = \log \dfrac{P(x, y)}{P(x) P(y)}$ | Association measure |

> **Note:** All logarithms are base-2 or base-$e$ depending on convention; specify in KTU exam answers.

## 2.3 Chomsky Hierarchy — The Backbone of Formal Language Theory

This is **HIGH-YIELD for KTU** as it links linguistics to computational systems:

| Type | Grammar | Language Recognizer | CPS/AI Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type-3** | Regular Grammar | Finite Automaton (FA) | Lexical analyzers, regex |
| **Type-2** | Context-Free Grammar (CFG) | Pushdown Automaton (PDA) | Parsers, syntax analysis |
| **Type-1** | Context-Sensitive Grammar | Linear-Bounded Automaton | NLP for natural languages |
| **Type-0** | Unrestricted (Turing) | Turing Machine | Theoretical limit of computation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Natural languages** (English, Malayalam, Hindi) are *not* context-free in the strict sense but are often modeled using **Context-Free Grammars augmented with semantics and pragmatics** for tractable computation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Voice-command NLP (e.g., "Turn left at the next signal") |
| **Smart Healthcare CPS** | Clinical NLP for Electronic Health Records (EHR) |
| **Industrial IoT** | Conversational interfaces for factory floor robots |
| **Smart Cities** | Multilingual citizen grievance processing |
| **Assistive Robotics** | Pragmatic intent inference for elderly care |

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Level

- **Why Phonology matters in CPS:** Embedded microphones capture *acoustic* signals; without phonological abstraction, the system cannot distinguish "ship" from "sheep."
- **Why Morphology matters:** Reduces vocabulary dimensionality; "running," "ran," "runs" all map to lemma *run*.
- **Why Syntax matters:** Disambiguates meaning in compositional sentences; critical for robot command parsing.
- **Why Semantics matters:** Resolves lexical ambiguity (e.g., "bank" — river vs. money).
- **Why Pragmatics matters:** "Can you pass the salt?" is a *request*, not a *yes/no question*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bigram Probability from a Sample Corpus

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU Numerical):**
Given a tiny corpus: *"<s> I love NLP I love AI </s> <s> I love linguistics </s>"*, compute the bigram probability $P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love})$.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify all unique unigrams (vocabulary).**
$$V = \{ <s>, I, love, NLP, AI, linguistics, </s> \}$$

**Step 2 — Count unigram occurrences of the context word *love*.**
$$C(\text{love}) = 3$$
*(appears in: "love NLP", "love AI", "love linguistics")*

**Step 3 — Count the bigram (love, NLP).**
$$C(\text{love}, \text{NLP}) = 1$$

**Step 4 — Apply the bigram probability formula.**
$$P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) = \frac{C(\text{love}, \text{NLP})}{C(\text{love})}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the counts.**
$$P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** Out of every three times the word "love" appears, the next word is "NLP" with probability $\frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$.

> **Conversion Logic:** This derivation uses the **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** principle: estimate probabilities directly from observed frequency counts in a training corpus.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: TF-IDF Computation

**Problem (KTU 14-mark style):**
Compute the **TF-IDF** of the word *"cyber"* in a document containing 100 words where "cyber" appears 5 times, given a corpus of 1000 documents in which "cyber" appears in 50 documents.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Define the document and corpus sizes.**
$$N_d = 100, \quad N = 1000$$

**Step 2 — Compute Term Frequency (TF).**
The raw count is $5$. Using raw frequency:
$$\text{tf}(\text{cyber}, d) = 5$$

> **Alternative normalized form:** $\text{tf}(t, d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}} = \dfrac{5}{100} = 0.05$

**Step 3 — Compute Inverse Document Frequency (IDF).**
$$\text{idf}(\text{cyber}) = \log\left(\frac{N}{\text{df}(\text{cyber})}\right) = \log\left(\frac{1000}{50}\right) = \log(20)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm.**
$$\log(20) \approx 2.996 \quad \text{(using base-10)}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply TF and IDF.**
$$\text{tf-idf}(\text{cyber}, d) = 5 \times 2.996 \approx 14.98$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** The word "cyber" has high importance in this document because it appears frequently *and* is relatively rare across the corpus — a strong indicator of topical relevance.

> **Conversion Logic:** IDF penalizes words that appear in *too many* documents (e.g., "the," "is"), while rewarding discriminative terms.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bayes' Theorem Application — Spam Classification

**Problem:** In a CPS email gateway, 40% of incoming mails are spam. The word "free" appears in 60% of spam and 5% of ham. What is $P(\text{spam} \mid \text{free})$?

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — State the known probabilities.**
$$P(\text{spam}) = 0.40, \quad P(\text{ham}) = 0.60$$
$$P(\text{free} \mid \text{spam}) = 0.60, \quad P(\text{free} \mid \text{ham}) = 0.05$$

**Step 2 — Apply the law of total probability.**
$$
P(\text{free}) = P(\text{free} \mid \text{spam}) P(\text{spam}) + P(\text{free} \mid \text{ham}) P(\text{ham})
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**
$$
P(\text{free}) = (0.60 \times 0.40) + (0.05 \times 0.60) = 0.24 + 0.03 = 0.27
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' Theorem.**
$$
P(\text{spam} \mid \text{free}) = \frac{P(\text{free} \mid \text{spam}) \cdot P(\text{spam})}{P(\text{free})}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.**
$$
P(\text{spam} \mid \text{free}) = \frac{0.60 \times 0.40}{0.27} = \frac{0.24}{0.27} = \frac{8}{9} \approx 0.889
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** An incoming email containing the word "free" has an **88.9% probability** of being spam.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Zipf's Law — Rank-Frequency Relationship

**Problem:** In a corpus, the most frequent word has frequency $f_1 = 1000$. Using Zipf's law with $C = 1000$, find the frequency of the 10th-ranked word.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — State Zipf's Law.**
$$f_r = \frac{C}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $C = 1000$, $r = 10$.**
$$f_{10} = \frac{1000}{10} = 100$$

**Step 3 — Verification with the general Zipf-Mandelbrot extension.**
$$f_r = \frac{C}{(r + \beta)^{\alpha}}, \quad \text{where } \alpha \approx 1, \beta = 0$$
For our simplified case: $f_{10} = 100$ confirms the result.

> **Conversion Logic:** Zipf's law implies a *highly skewed* word distribution — a small number of words account for the majority of text occurrences. This insight is crucial for stop-word removal and language model compression.

## 3.5 Perplexity Calculation

**Problem:** A unigram language model assigns probability $1/1000$ to a test sentence of 10 words. Compute its perplexity.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Sentence probability.**
$$P(W) = \left(\frac{1}{1000}\right)^{10} = 10^{-30}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the perplexity formula.**
$$PP(W) = P(W)^{-1/N} = (10^{-30})^{-1/10} = 10^{3} = 1000$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.** Perplexity equals the vocabulary size (1000) — meaning the model is as "confused" as if it were choosing uniformly from 1000 alternatives. **Lower perplexity = better model.**

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Level-to-Computation Mapping

| Linguistic Level | Formal Tool | Output Artifact | CPS Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phonology | Acoustic model, HMM, CTC | Phoneme lattice | Voice assistants (Alexa, Siri in CPS) |
| Morphology | Finite-state transducer | Lemma, stem | Search engines in industrial CPS |
| Syntax | CFG parser (e.g., Earley, CYK) | Parse tree | Robot command interpreters |
| Semantics | Word2Vec, BERT, ontologies | Embedding, knowledge graph | Context-aware decision support |
| Pragmatics | Reinforcement learning, RNNs | Intent vector | Conversational AI in healthcare CPS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Five Levels of Linguistic Analysis (Hierarchical Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acoustic Input or Text] --> B[Level 1 Phonology]
    B --> C[Level 2 Morphology]
    C --> D[Level 3 Syntax]
    D --> E[Level 4 Semantics]
    E --> F[Level 5 Pragmatics]
    F --> G[CPS Action or Response]

    B1[Phonemes and Sounds] --> B
    C1[Morphemes and Word Forms] --> C
    D1[Parse Tree and Grammar] --> D
    E1[Literal Meaning Vectors] --> E
    F1[Intent and Context] --> F
    G1[Actuator Control or Decision Output] --> G

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff9900,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00838f,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Description:** The diagram maps the bottom-up linguistic processing pipeline. Each level transforms the input representation into a higher-level abstraction, ultimately producing a CPS-actionable response.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Linguistics Branch Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Linguistics] --> L1[Phonetics]
    L --> L2[Phonology]
    L --> L3[Morphology]
    L --> L4[Syntax]
    L --> L5[Semantics]
    L --> L6[Pragmatics]
    L --> L7[Discourse Analysis]

    L1 --> L1a[Articulatory]
    L1 --> L1b[Acoustic]
    L1 --> L1c[Auditory]

    L3 --> L3a[Inflectional]
    L3 --> L3b[Derivational]

    L4 --> L4a[Phrase Structure Grammar]
    L4 --> L4b[Dependency Grammar]

    L5 --> L5a[Lexical Semantics]
    L5 --> L5b[Compositional Semantics]

    L6 --> L6a[Speech Acts]
    L6 --> L6b[Implicature]
    L6 --> L6c[Deixis]

    style L fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:3px
    style L1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style L2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style L3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L4 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c
    style L5 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style L6 fill:#b2ebf2,stroke:#006064
    style L7 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: NLP Processing Pipeline in a CPS Context

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User Voice Command] --> V1[Speech Recognition ASR]
    V1 --> V2[Phoneme to Text]
    V2 --> V3[Tokenizer and Morphological Analyzer]
    V3 --> V4[POS Tagger and Parser]
    V4 --> V5[Semantic Role Labeler]
    V5 --> V6[Pragmatic Intent Classifier]
    V6 --> V7[Dialogue Manager]
    V7 --> V8[CPS Action Controller]
    V8 --> V9[Physical Actuator or Response]

    V3 --> K1[Morphology Module]
    V4 --> K2[Syntax Module]
    V5 --> K3[Semantics Module]
    V6 --> K4[Pragmatics Module]

    style U fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style V8 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    style V9 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    style K1 fill:#fff9c4
    style K2 fill:#fff9c4
    style K3 fill:#fff9c4
    style K4 fill:#fff9c4
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Chomsky Hierarchy — Grammar Classes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Type 0 Unrestricted Grammar] --> T1[Type 1 Context Sensitive]
    T1 --> T2[Type 2 Context Free]
    T2 --> T3[Type 3 Regular]

    T0 --> R0[Recursively Enumerable Language]
    T1 --> R1[Context Sensitive Language]
    T2 --> R2[Context Free Language]
    T3 --> R3[Regular Language]

    R0 --> M0[Turing Machine]
    R1 --> M1[Linear Bounded Automaton]
    R2 --> M2[Pushdown Automaton]
    R3 --> M3[Finite State Automaton]

    style T0 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style T1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style T2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style T3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style M0 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style M1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style M2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17

```
