# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define semantic analysis and list its four main subtasks with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**
Semantic analysis is the phase of NLP that maps syntactically parsed input to a formal, machine-interpretable meaning representation. Its four main subtasks are:
1. **Lexical disambiguation** — selecting the correct sense of polysemous words.
2. **Semantic role labeling** — identifying predicate-argument structure (agent, patient, instrument, etc.).
3. **Compositional interpretation** — combining word meanings into sentence meaning via $\lambda$-calculus.
4. **Knowledge grounding and inference** — linking meanings to ontologies and deriving entailments.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between a semantic network and a frame with two points of contrast.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Semantic Network | Frame |
|--------|------------------|-------|
| Structure | Labeled directed graph of nodes and edges | Object with named slots and faceted values |
| Inheritance | Via `is-a` edges traversed at query time | Via slot default values inherited from parent frame |
| Default reasoning | Not supported | Supported (slot defaults, daemons) |
| Formal semantics | Often informal | Can be formalized in description logics |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of a description logic–based ontology. With a suitable example, demonstrate how the concept inclusion axiom $C \sqsubseteq D$ is used to express subsumption in OWL 2 DL.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction [1 mark]:** Description Logic (DL) is the formal foundation of OWL 2 DL. It provides a set of *concept constructors* (conjunction $\sqcap$, disjunction $\sqcup$, existential restriction $\exists R.C$, universal restriction $\forall R.C$, negation $\neg$), *role constructors* (inverse $R^{-}$, transitive closure $R^{+}$), and a *terminological box (TBox)* for class axioms and an *assertional box (ABox)* for instance assertions.

**Architecture Diagram (in text) [2 marks]:**

```
DL SYSTEM
   +-----------------------------+
   | CONSTRUCTORS (syntax)       |
   |   - Concept: C, D, E        |
   |   - Role: R, S              |
   |   - Individual: a, b        |
   +-----------------------------+
                |
                v
   +-----------------------------+
   | KNOWLEDGE BASE              |
   |   - TBox (schema)           |
   |   - ABox (data)             |
   |   - RBox (role hierarchy)   |
   +-----------------------------+
                |
                v
   +-----------------------------+
   | REASONER                    |
   |   - Pellet / HermiT / FaCT++|
   |   - Services:               |
   |       * Subsumption         |
   |       * Classification      |
   |       * Consistency         |
   |       * Realization         |
   +-----------------------------+
```

**Example [3 marks]:** Consider an ontology for a smart hospital CPS. We want to assert that *every patient in the ICU is monitored by at least one critical-care device*. This is encoded as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ICU\_Patient} &\sqsubseteq \text{Patient} \sqcap \exists \text{monitoredBy}.\text{CriticalCareDevice} \\
\text{CriticalCareDevice} &\sqsubseteq \text{MedicalDevice} \sqcap \exists \text{measures}.\text{VitalSign}
\end{aligned}
$$

The concept inclusion $C \sqsubseteq D$ means that the set-extension of $C$ is a subset of the set-extension of $D$ in every model $\mathcal{I}$:

$$C^{\mathcal{I}} \subseteq D^{\mathcal{I}}$$

**Reasoning Example [1 mark]:** Given the above TBox, a reasoner can deduce that every `ICU_Patient` is a `Patient` (via subsumption propagation) and is connected via the `monitoredBy` role to a `CriticalCareDevice`, which in turn is a `MedicalDevice`. Such entailments trigger alarm logic in the hospital CPS.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Compare semantic networks, frames, and conceptual dependency theory as knowledge representation structures. Illustrate each with one example.

**Model Solution:**

**Semantic Networks [2 marks]:** A semantic network represents knowledge as a directed graph where nodes denote concepts/entities and labeled edges denote binary relations.
- **Example:**
```
          [Mammal] --is-a--> [Animal]
              |
           hasPart
              v
          [Heart]
```
- **Key features:** intuitive, supports inheritance via `is-a` path traversal, lacks formal semantics, no default reasoning.

**Frames [2.5 marks]:** A frame is a structured data record representing a stereotyped situation or object, with named slots, facets (value, default, type), and attached procedures (daemons).
- **Example — *Bird* frame:**
  - `slot: canFly` → `default: yes` → `facet: type: Boolean`
  - `slot: habitat` → `facet: type: String`
  - `is-a: Animal`
  - `instance: Tweety`
  - `daemon: if-beak-color-changed then re-classify`
- **Key features:** modular, supports defaults and procedural attachment, well-suited for vision and scene understanding.

**Conceptual Dependency (CD) Theory [2.5 marks]:** Proposed by Schank, CD uses a small set of *primitive acts* (PTRANS, ATRANS, MTRANS, PTRANS, INGEST, EXPEL, etc.) to represent the meaning of any natural-language sentence, abstracting away from surface form.
- **Example — "John gave Mary a book":**
```
[John] --Atrans--> [Book] (from John to Mary)
   |
  ATRANS
   |
  [Mary]  <-- recipient
```
- **Key features:** canonical representation independent of surface form, supports inference via CD rules, ideal for story understanding and question answering.

**Comparative Summary [additional supporting points]:**
- Semantic networks emphasize *relational* structure; frames emphasize *object-centered* structure; CD emphasizes *event-centered* structure.
- All three predate modern DL-based ontologies and are largely subsumed by OWL 2 in contemporary systems, but remain conceptually important and appear in legacy CPS subsystems.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the role of semantic analysis in cognitive Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). With a block diagram, explain how the semantic layer integrates with the sensing, planning, and actuation layers.

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Semantic Analysis in CPS [2 marks]:**
A Cyber-Physical System is a tight integration of computation, communication, and physical processes. In *cognitive* CPS, the system must perceive, reason, and act in open, uncertain environments. Semantic analysis is the *interpretation layer* that:
1. Converts low-level sensor signals (camera frames, LIDAR point clouds, text logs) into symbolic, machine-readable meaning.
2. Aligns these symbols with a domain ontology and world model.
3. Enables *explainable* reasoning — every actuator command can be traced back to the linguistic/semantic evidence that triggered it.
4. Supports *human-machine collaboration* by natural-language interfaces grounded in the same knowledge representation.

**Block Diagram [3 marks]:**

```
+--------------------------------------------------------+
|                  COGNITIVE CPS STACK                   |
+--------------------------------------------------------+
|  ACTUATION LAYER  --> Motors, Brakes, Valves, Displays |
+--------------------------------------------------------+
|  PLANNING LAYER   --> Task scheduling, Path planning   |
+--------------------------------------------------------+
|  REASONING LAYER  --> DL reasoner, KG inference        |
+--------------------------------------------------------+
|  SEMANTIC LAYER   --> SRL, Compositionality, Grounding |
+--------------------------------------------------------+
|  PERCEPTION LAYER --> Sensors, CV, Speech recognition  |
+--------------------------------------------------------+
                ^                              |
                |  actuator commands           | sensor data
                |______________________________|
```

**Layer-by-Layer Integration [2 marks]:**
- **Perception → Semantic:** Raw signals are converted to typed symbols (e.g., *PedestrianAt(loc, t)*).
- **Semantic → Reasoning:** Symbols are matched against the ontology, entailments are derived, conflicts are detected.
- **Reasoning → Planning:** High-level goals are decomposed into actionable subtasks using the enriched KG.
- **Planning → Actuation:** Subtasks become actuator primitives (`brake(force = 0.8, duration = 2s)`).
- **Actuation → Physical World:** The system physically acts; new sensor data closes the loop.

**Example — Autonomous Vehicle:** A camera detects a stop sign (perception). SRL labels the agent as `sign`, action as `mandate`, patient as `vehicle`. The semantic layer produces the triple `(StopSign_42, mandates, Halt)` and links it to the traffic-law ontology. The reasoning layer checks the vehicle's current state and infers that braking is required. The planning layer computes a smooth deceleration profile. The actuation layer commands the brake-by-wire system.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the RDF and OWL stack of the Semantic Web. How does this stack support knowledge representation in CPS? Show how a simple ontology for an industrial robot is encoded in RDF/XML or Turtle syntax.

**Model Solution:**

**The Semantic Web Stack [3 marks]:**
The W3C Semantic Web stack is a layered architecture of interoperable standards:
- **URI / IRI** — global unique identifiers for resources.
- **XML / Turtle** — serialization syntaxes.
- **RDF** (Resource Description Framework) — a triple-based data model: $(s, p, o)$.
- **RDFS** (RDF Schema) — basic class and property hierarchies (`rdfs:subClassOf`, `rdfs:domain`, `rdfs:range`).
- **OWL** (Web Ontology Language) — richer axioms built on description logic: cardinality, transitivity, inverse, disjointness, etc.
- **SPARQL** — query language for RDF graphs.
- **RIF / SWRL** — rule languages for inference beyond DL.

**How the Stack Supports CPS [1.5 marks]:**
- **RDF** provides a flexible, schemaless data model ideal for fusing heterogeneous sensor streams.
- **RDFS** enables simple inheritance of device types and properties.
- **OWL 2** provides the formal semantics required for safety-critical inference (e.g., "no robot can occupy the same workspace as a human").
- **SPARQL** allows real-time queries over the live CPS knowledge graph (e.g., "list all robots whose last maintenance was > 90 days ago").
- **OWL 2 Profiles** ($\mathcal{EL}^{++}$, $\mathcal{QL}$, $\mathcal{RL}$) allow trading expressivity for tractability — essential for resource-constrained edge devices in CPS.

**Example Ontology in Turtle Syntax [2.5 marks]:**

```turtle
@prefix cps: <http://example.org/cps#> .
@prefix owl: <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#> .
@prefix rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#> .
@prefix rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> .
@prefix xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> .

cps:IndustrialRobot   rdf:type            owl:Class ;
                      rdfs:subClassOf     cps:Agent .

cps:WeldingRobot      rdf:type            owl:Class ;
                      rdfs:subClassOf     cps:IndustrialRobot .

cps:hasPayload        rdf:type            owl:ObjectProperty ;
                      rdfs:domain         cps:IndustrialRobot ;
                      rdfs:range          cps:Payload .

cps:hasWorkspace      rdf:type            owl:ObjectProperty ;
                      rdf:type            owl:TransitiveProperty ;
                      rdfs:domain         cps:IndustrialRobot ;
                      rdfs:range          cps:Workspace .

cps:RobotA            rdf:type            cps:WeldingRobot ;
                      cps:hasPayload      cps:WeldingTorch ;
                      cps:hasWorkspace    cps:CellA .

cps:RobotB            rdf:type            cps:WeldingRobot ;
                      cps:hasWorkspace    cps:CellA .
```

**Reasoning Output:** A reasoner can deduce that `CellA` is a shared workspace, and that the safety policy *"no two robots may share a workspace without coordination"* applies. This inference could not be drawn from raw RDF alone — it requires the OWL semantics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams for this topic:**
> 1. **Conflating "semantic analysis" with "syntactic parsing."** Parsing produces a *tree*; semantic analysis produces a *meaning representation*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if these are not clearly distinguished.
> 2. **Writing "$C \sqsubseteq D$" as "$C = D$."** Subsumption is set *inclusion*, not equality. Two classes can be co-extensional without being identical.
> 3. **Forgetting to ground the answer in CPS.** This is a *Cognitive Systems and AI in CPS* course, not a generic AI course. Pure generic NLP answers that do not mention sensors, actuators, IIoT, or CPS loop will lose at least 3 marks.
> 4. **Skipping examples.** A 7-mark question without a worked example typically caps at 4–5 marks. Always include at least one Turtle/OWL snippet or one worked logical-form derivation.
> 5. **Confusing RDF and OWL.** RDF is the *data model*; OWL is the *ontology language* built on top of it. OWL adds *axioms* (disjointness, transitivity, cardinality) that RDF cannot express.
> 6. **Omitting the expressivity–tractability trade-off.** A complete answer must mention that more expressive DLs (full SROIQ) have N2ExpTime worst-case complexity, motivating the use of OWL 2 profiles.
> 7. **Not labeling diagrams.** A Mermaid or hand-drawn diagram *without* node labels and arrows explained in the text receives partial credit only.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Semantic Analysis and Representation Structures**

- ☐ **Semantic Analysis** is the third stage of the NLP pipeline: morphological → syntactic → **semantic** → pragmatic. Its output is a formal meaning representation, not a parse tree.
- ☐ The four core subtasks of semantic analysis are: **lexical disambiguation, semantic role labeling, compositional interpretation, and knowledge grounding + inference**.
- ☐ **Compositionality (Frege–Montague):** The meaning of a complex expression is a *function* of the meanings of its parts and the way they are syntactically combined.
- ☐ **Lambda calculus** is the standard mathematical tool for compositional semantics; function application and $\beta$-reduction yield logical forms.
- ☐ **Semantic Role Labeling (SRL)** assigns roles like Agent, Patient, Instrument, Location, Time, Manner, Cause to constituents of a sentence.
- ☐ **Lexical disambiguation** (e.g., Lesk's algorithm) chooses the word sense that maximally overlaps with the surrounding context; modern systems use contextual embeddings (BERT).
- ☐ **Representation structures** include semantic networks, frames, conceptual dependency, FOL, description logics, conceptual graphs, RDF, OWL, and knowledge graphs.
- ☐ **Semantic networks** are graph-based; they support inheritance via `is-a` edges but lack formal semantics and default reasoning.
- ☐ **Frames** are object-oriented; they support slot defaults, faceted values, and procedural daemons — used heavily in vision and scene understanding.
- ☐ **Conceptual Dependency (Schank)** uses a small set of primitive acts (PTRANS, ATRANS, MTRANS, etc.) to represent any event canonically.
- ☐ **Description Logic (DL)** is the formal foundation of OWL; its constructors are $\sqcap, \sqcup, \neg, \exists R.C, \forall R.C$. Subsumption is the central inference.
- ☐ **OWL 2 profiles** trade expressivity for tractability: $\mathcal{EL}^{++}$ (PTime), $\mathcal{QL}$ (LogSpace query answering), $\mathcal{RL}$ (PTime rule-based).
- ☐ **RDF** is a triple model $(s, p, o)$ with formal semantics; **OWL** extends it with axioms; **SPARQL** queries it.
- ☐ **Knowledge Graphs** are large-scale RDF/OWL-based representations used in industry (Google KG, Wikidata, IIoT platforms).
- ☐ In **CPS**, semantic analysis fuses heterogeneous sensor data, supports explainable reasoning, and grounds actuator commands in a shared ontology.
- ☐ Key metrics: **MRR** for link prediction, **F1** for SRL/NER, **Cosine similarity** for embedding-based disambiguation.
- ☐ Always mention the **expressivity–tractability trade-off** when discussing DLs in exams.
- ☐ Always tie the answer back to a **CPS use case** (autonomous vehicle, smart factory, hospital, IIoT) to score full marks.
- ☐ Common pitfalls: confusing parsing with semantics, writing $=$ instead of $\sqsubseteq$, omitting CPS context, skipping examples.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Language Generation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Natural Language Generation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 — Natural Language Generation Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Natural Language Generation (NLG)** is the sub-field of Artificial Intelligence and Computational Linguistics concerned with the automated construction of coherent, meaningful, and contextually appropriate natural language text, either from structured machine-readable data (databases, knowledge graphs, sensor logs), from non-linguistic representations (numerical arrays, images, control signals), or from prior linguistic inputs.

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, NLG acts as the **linguistic output interface** of a cognitive control loop. It translates machine-internal states, sensor observations, and predictive analytics into human-readable narratives, instructions, or summaries that operators, engineers, or end-users can immediately interpret.

| KTU 2024 Term | Standard Symbol / Acronym | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Language Generation | NLG | Production of text from data |
| Natural Language Understanding | NLU | Extraction of meaning from text |
| Natural Language Processing | NLP | Umbrella discipline containing both |
| Cyber-Physical System | CPS | Tight coupling of computation, networking, and physical processes |
| Real-Time NLG | RT-NLG | Generation under strict timing constraints (< 100 ms) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PECPT754):** The course treats NLG as a *cognitive capability* of CPS — meaning the system not only *computes* a decision but also *explains* it in natural language. This bridges symbolic AI with human-machine teaming.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator Robot in a Factory"

Imagine a smart factory where a robot monitors 200 sensors. Internally, the robot's CPU sees only numbers: temperature = 87.3 °C, pressure = 4.2 bar, vibration = anomalous.

A human supervisor cannot read 200 numbers per second. So the robot includes a **"Translator Bot"** — its job is to listen to the numbers, decide *what is important*, decide *how to say it politely and clearly*, and then *speak a sentence* to the supervisor:

> "**Warning:** Bearing 7 in Pump A is overheating at **87.3 °C** and shows abnormal vibration. Recommend immediate shutdown to prevent seal damage."

That entire transformation — from raw numbers to a fluent, decision-oriented English sentence — is exactly what an **NLG system** does inside a CPS.

> [!TIP]
> **Cognitive Framing:** If NLU is the system's *ears* (turning speech into meaning), then NLG is its *mouth* (turning meaning into speech). Both are required for true bidirectional human-machine dialogue in CPS.

### 1.3 Key Distinctions the Examiner Loves

- **NLG ≠ Machine Translation (MT):** MT converts one *natural language* to another (e.g., English → Malayalam). NLG converts *non-linguistic data* to a natural language.
- **NLG ≠ Template Filling:** A template is a *single-shot substitution*; a true NLG system performs **content selection, lexicalisation, aggregation, and surface realisation** dynamically.
- **NLG ≠ Summarisation:** Summarisation takes *text* and condenses it. NLG can start from *zero text* (e.g., a SQL table) and produce text.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Classical NLG Pipeline (Reiter & Dale, 2000 Architecture)

A canonical NLG system is decomposed into **six sequential stages**. Modern neural systems may *fuse* these stages, but KTU examiners expect the classical decomposition.

#### Stage 1 — Content Determination (What to say?)

- **Goal:** Decide *which* pieces of information from the input data are relevant to the user's current goal.
- **Input:** Knowledge base, user model, discourse history, raw sensor stream.
- **Output:** A *message specification* — an abstract set of propositions to be communicated.
- **Engineering analogy:** A journalist choosing which facts from a press conference make it into tomorrow's headline.

#### Stage 2 — Document Structuring (In what order?)

- **Goal:** Organise the chosen propositions into a coherent linear (or hierarchical) rhetorical structure (e.g., *Introduction → Cause → Effect → Recommendation*).
- **Algorithms used:** **RST (Rhetorical Structure Theory)** trees, **Schemas**, **Text Plans**.
- **Engineering analogy:** Creating the *outline* of a report before writing the sentences.

#### Stage 3 — Lexicalisation (Which words?)

- **Goal:** For each proposition, pick the most appropriate *lexical items* (nouns, verbs, adjectives).
- **Example:** The proposition `OVERHEAT(bearing_7)` can be lexicalised as *overheating*, *too hot*, *exceeding thermal limits*. The choice depends on the user's expertise and the system's stylistic register.

#### Stage 4 — Aggregation (How to combine?)

- **Goal:** Merge multiple simple sentences into fewer, more readable complex/compound sentences.
- **Example:** *"Pump A failed. Pump B failed."* → *"Pumps A and B both failed."*

#### Stage 5 — Referring Expression Generation (REG) (Which noun phrase?)

- **Goal:** Generate noun phrases that allow the reader to uniquely identify an entity in the discourse context.
- **Algorithms:** **Dale & Reiter's incremental algorithm**, **Centering Theory**.
- **Example:** First mention → *"the temperature sensor TS-04"*; subsequent mention → *"it"* or *"the sensor"*.

#### Stage 6 — Surface Realisation (Final sentence)

- **Goal:** Apply morphological rules, grammatical rules, and orthographic conventions to produce well-formed text.
- **Tools/Models:** **FUF/SURGE**, **RealPro**, **OpenCCG**, **T5**, **GPT decoders**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expectation:** When asked *"Explain the architecture of an NLG system"*, the six-stage pipeline is the *expected* answer. Do **not** skip the distinction between *content selection* and *surface realisation* — these are the most commonly tested stages.

### 2.2 Modern Neural NLG (End-to-End)

Modern systems collapse the six stages into a single neural network — typically an **encoder-decoder Transformer**. The model receives a **structured meaning representation (MR)** and autoregressively decodes a sentence token by token.

**Conditional generation probability:**

$$
P(y \mid x) \;=\; \prod_{t=1}^{T} P\!\left(y_t \;\vert\; y_{<t},\, x\right)
$$

Where:
- $x$ = structured input (data record, table row, meaning representation)
- $y = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_T)$ = generated word sequence
- $y_{<t}$ = all previously generated tokens

The network parameters $\theta$ are trained by **minimising the negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy loss):**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) \;=\; -\sum_{(x, y) \in \mathcal{D}} \log P_\theta(y \mid x)
$$

### 2.3 NLG in the CPS Cognitive Loop

In PECPT754, NLG is positioned inside the **perception–cognition–action** triad of a CPS:

| CPS Layer | NLG Function | Example Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception** | Verbalise raw sensor readings | *"Ambient CO₂ is 850 ppm, 12% above baseline."* |
| **Cognition (Reasoning)** | Explain a diagnostic decision | *"Root cause analysis indicates a failing coolant valve."* |
| **Action (HMI / Logs)** | Generate operator instructions | *"Open Valve V-12 within 30 seconds."* |
| **Post-hoc Reporting** | Build end-of-shift narrative logs | *"At 14:32, three alerts were issued and resolved…"* |

### 2.4 Evaluation Metrics — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are *directly testable* in Part A or as sub-parts of Part B.

#### Metric 1 — BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy)

**Modified n-gram precision:**

$$
p_n \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{\text{candidate } C} \sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(\text{ngram})}{\displaystyle\sum_{\text{candidate } C} \sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{Count}(\text{ngram})}
$$

**Brevity penalty:**

$$
BP \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } c > r \\
e^{(1 - r/c)} & \text{if } c \le r
\end{cases}
$$

**Final BLEU score:**

$$
\text{BLEU} \;=\; BP \cdot \exp\!\left( \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n \right)
$$

Standard weights: $w_n = \frac{1}{N}$, with $N = 4$. Range: **0 to 1** (often reported ×100, i.e., 0–100).

#### Metric 2 — ROUGE-L (Longest Common Subsequence-based)

$$
R_{\text{lcs}} \;=\; \frac{\text{LCS}(X, Y)}{m}, \qquad
P_{\text{lcs}} \;=\; \frac{\text{LCS}(X, Y)}{n}
$$

$$
\text{ROUGE-L} \;=\; \frac{(1 + \beta^2)\, R_{\text{lcs}} \, P_{\text{lcs}}}{R_{\text{lcs}} + \beta^2 P_{\text{lcs}}}, \quad \beta \gg 1
$$

Where $m$ = length of reference, $n$ = length of candidate.

#### Metric 3 — METEOR

$$
\text{METEOR} \;=\; F_{\text{mean}} \cdot (1 - \text{Penalty})
$$

$$
F_{\text{mean}} \;=\; \frac{10 P R}{R + 9 P}, \qquad
\text{Penalty} \;=\; 0.5 \left(\frac{\text{chunks}}{\text{unigrams\_matched}}\right)^{\!\!3}
$$

#### Metric 4 — Perplexity (intrinsic, neural NLG)

$$
\text{PPL} \;=\; \exp\!\left( -\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(y_t \mid y_{<t}) \right)
$$

**Lower is better.** Perplexity measures how "surprised" the model is by the reference text.

### 2.5 High-Yield Comparison Table

| Dimension | Classical (Pipeline) NLG | Neural (End-to-End) NLG |
|---|---|---|
| **Modularity** | High — each stage inspectable | Low — black-box Transformer |
| **Data Hunger** | Modest (rule-based) to medium (statistical) | High (typically > 100 k parallel MR-text pairs) |
| **Fluency** | Often stilted | Human-like |
| **Faithfulness / Hallucination** | Controllable | Prone to hallucinated facts (critical in CPS) |
| **Latency** | Predictable, low | Variable, GPU-dependent |
| **Determinism in CPS** | High | Lower (use constrained decoding) |
| **Explainability** | Strong (rule traces) | Weak (attention is not explanation) |
| **Best Use in CPS** | Safety reports, alerts | Operator chatbots, dashboards |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** state that neural NLG is "always better". In safety-critical CPS (avionics, medical, nuclear), **deterministic pipeline NLG** is preferred because every lexical choice is auditable. This is a common KTU Part B follow-up.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation: BLEU Score on a CPS Alert-Generation Example

**Problem Setup.** A CPS diagnostic NLG system produces the *candidate* sentence $C$ to describe a pressure anomaly. The human-authored *reference* sentence is $R$.

- Candidate $C$: *"The pump pressure is high and the pump pressure is rising."*
- Reference $R$: *"Pump pressure is high and rising."*

We compute **unigram precision** and **bigram precision** and the final **BLEU-2** score (taking $N=2$ and uniform weights).

---

#### Step 1 — Tokenise the sentences

Lowercase and split by whitespace and punctuation.

$$
C = \{\, \text{the, pump, pressure, is, high, and, the, pump, pressure, is, rising} \,\}
$$

$$
R = \{\, \text{pump, pressure, is, high, and, rising} \,\}
$$

Number of candidate unigrams: $n_C = 11$. Number of reference unigrams: $n_R = 6$.

**Why this step?** BLEU operates on *token streams*, not character streams. The candidate is verbose, the reference is concise.

---

#### Step 2 — Compute clipped counts for each unigram in $C$

For every unigram in $C$, the *clipped count* is:

$$
\text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(w) \;=\; \min\!\left( \text{Count}_C(w),\; \text{Count}_R(w) \right)
$$

Building a table of unigram counts:

| Unigram $w$ | $\text{Count}_C(w)$ | $\text{Count}_R(w)$ | $\text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(w)$ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| the | 2 | 0 | 0 |
| pump | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| pressure | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| is | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| high | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| and | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| rising | 1 | 1 | 1 |

Total clipped count: $0 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 6$.

---

#### Step 3 — Unigram precision $p_1$

$$
p_1 \;=\; \frac{\sum_{w \in C} \text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(w)}{\sum_{w \in C} \text{Count}(w)} \;=\; \frac{6}{11}
$$

**Why divide by 11?** The denominator is the *raw* count of all unigrams in the candidate. This penalises the redundant repetition of *"the pump pressure is"* — a faithful design choice for safety-critical reporting.

$$
p_1 \;\approx\; 0.5454
$$

---

#### Step 4 — Bigram tokenisation

$$
C_{\text{bigrams}} = \{\, \text{``the pump''}, \text{``pump pressure''}, \text{``pressure is''}, \text{``is high''}, \text{``high and''}, \text{``and the''}, \text{``the pump''}, \text{``pump pressure''}, \text{``pressure is''}, \text{``is rising''} \,\}
$$

Total bigrams in $C$: $10$.

$$
R_{\text{bigrams}} = \{\, \text{``pump pressure''}, \text{``pressure is''}, \text{``is high''}, \text{``high and''}, \text{``and rising''} \,\}
$$

---

#### Step 5 — Clipped bigram counts

| Bigram $b$ | $\text{Count}_C(b)$ | $\text{Count}_R(b)$ | $\text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(b)$ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| the pump | 2 | 0 | 0 |
| pump pressure | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| pressure is | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| is high | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| high and | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| and the | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| is rising | 1 | 1 | 1 |

Clipped sum: $0 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 5$.

---

#### Step 6 — Bigram precision $p_2$

$$
p_2 \;=\; \frac{5}{10} \;=\; 0.5000
$$

**Why these bigrams matter in CPS:** The bigram *"is rising"* is semantically critical — it indicates a *trend*, not a static value. A CPS NLG that omits trends can mislead an operator.

---

#### Step 7 — Brevity penalty (BP)

The candidate length $c = 11$, the reference length $r = 6$. Since $c > r$:

$$
BP \;=\; 1
$$

**Interpretation:** No brevity penalty — the system was *verbose*, not *truncated*. Truncation is a more serious failure mode in CPS because it can drop safety-critical information.

---

#### Step 8 — Final BLEU-2

$$
\text{BLEU-2} \;=\; BP \cdot \exp\!\left( w_1 \log p_1 + w_2 \log p_2 \right)
$$

With $w_1 = w_2 = 0.5$:

$$
\text{BLEU-2} \;=\; 1 \cdot \exp\!\left( 0.5 \log(0.5454) + 0.5 \log(0.5000) \right)
$$

$$
= \exp\!\left( 0.5 \cdot (-0.6061) + 0.5 \cdot (-0.6931) \right)
$$

$$
= \exp\!\left( -0.30305 - 0.34655 \right) \;=\; \exp(-0.6496)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{BLEU-2} \;\approx\; 0.5222}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for PECPT754:** BLEU on CPS output is not just an academic number — a BLEU-2 of **0.52 with verbose redundancy** is a *signal* that the NLG system is failing the **Aggregation** stage of the classical pipeline. A board examiner will award marks for connecting the metric back to a *remediable pipeline stage*.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes of NLG in CPS

| Failure Mode | Pipeline Stage at Fault | Example | Real CPS Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hallucination** (model invents a sensor) | Surface Realisation (neural) | *"Coolant valve V-99 is open"* (V-99 does not exist) | Operator takes wrong action |
| **Omission** (drops critical fact) | Content Determination | Omit the *time-stamp* of an alarm | Forensic log useless after incident |
| **Repetition** (verbose) | Aggregation | *"Pressure is high. Pressure is rising."* | Cognitive overload on operator |
| **Ambiguous reference** (REG error) | Referring Expression | *"Open it"* (which valve?) | Wrong valve actuated |
| **Tense / aspect error** | Surface Realisation | *"Valve will open"* (should be *"is opening"*) | Misread urgency |
| **Unit confusion** | Lexicalisation | *"87.3"* with no unit (°C vs °F) | Catastrophic in nuclear/medical CPS |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Classical NLG Pipeline (Reiter–Dale Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Data Stream] --> B[Content Determination]
    B --> C[Document Structuring]
    C --> D[Lexicalisation]
    D --> E[Aggregation]
    E --> F[Referring Expression Generation]
    F --> G[Surface Realisation]
    G --> H[Output Natural Language Text]

    subgraph knowledgeBase[Knowledge Resources]
        K1[Domain Ontology]
        K2[User Model]
        K3[Discourse History]
    end

    K1 --- B
    K2 --- D
    K3 --- F

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style D fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style E fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style F fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style G fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style H fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style knowledgeBase fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Orange nodes are the *linguistic processing* stages; blue is *data ingress*; green is *delivered text*; purple is the *side knowledge* each stage queries. The same pipeline runs in **reverse** for an NLU system.

### 4.2 End-to-End Neural NLG Architecture (for CPS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph input[Structured Input]
        I1[Sensor Data Table]
        I2[Knowledge Graph Triples]
        I3[Operator Query]
    end

    input --> ENC[Transformer Encoder]
    ENC --> CTX[Contextual Embeddings]
    CTX --> DEC[Transformer Decoder with Cross Attention]
    DEC --> TOK1[Token 1]
    DEC --> TOK2[Token 2]
    DEC --> TOKN[Token N]
    TOK1 --> OUT[Generated Sentence]
    TOK2 --> OUT
    TOKN --> OUT

    OUT --> VAL[Validation Layer]
    VAL --> SAFE[Safety-Checked Output]

    style ENC fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style DEC fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style VAL fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    style SAFE fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style input fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Validation Layer is non-negotiable in CPS.** Pure neural decoders can hallucinate sensor IDs or unsafe actions. The validation layer enforces hard constraints (e.g., *"the valve number must exist in the asset registry"*) before the sentence is delivered to the operator.

### 4.3 NLG Inside the Cognitive CPS Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Physical Sensors] --> P[Perception]
    P --> R[Reasoning and Planning]
    R --> ACT[Actuator Commands]
    R --> NLG[NLG Module]
    NLG --> HMI[Operator HMI Display]
    HMI --> OP[Human Operator]
    OP --> DECIDE[Operator Decision]
    DECIDE --> ACT
    OP --> FB[Verbal Feedback]
    FB --> NLU[NLU Module]
    NLU --> R

    style S fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style NLG fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style NLU fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style HMI fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e,color:#000
    style OP fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed-loop interpretation:** The NLG and NLU modules together form a *linguistic channel* parallel to the physical control channel. This is the foundation of *explainable CPS* — every automated action can be justified in natural language and every operator utterance can be ingested as a new constraint.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Set A]**
*Define Natural Language Generation. How is it different from Natural Language Understanding?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Natural Language Generation (NLG) is the sub-field of NLP concerned with automatically producing coherent, meaningful natural language text from structured data, non-linguistic representations, or from prior linguistic input. In a CPS context, NLG converts machine states and sensor readings into human-readable narratives, alerts, and instructions.

**Difference from NLU (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | NLG (Output) | NLU (Input) |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Data → Text | Text → Meaning |
| Sub-tasks | Content selection, lexicalisation, surface realisation | Parsing, semantic role labelling, intent detection |
| CPS Analogy | System's "mouth" — it speaks | System's "ears" — it listens |
| Typical output | A sentence, paragraph, or report | A parse tree, intent class, or knowledge triple |

NLG and NLU are *complementary halves* of bidirectional human-machine communication, and modern CPS often deploy both to enable natural operator interaction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Set B]**
*List and briefly explain any three stages of the classical NLG pipeline.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per stage):**

1. **Content Determination (1 mark):** Selects which facts from the input knowledge base are relevant to the user's communicative goal. Example: from 200 sensor readings, choosing the 3 that crossed safety thresholds.

2. **Lexicalisation (1 mark):** Chooses the most appropriate words to express each chosen proposition. Example: the proposition `OVERHEAT(x)` may be realised as *"overheating"*, *"too hot"*, or *"exceeding thermal limits"* depending on the operator's expertise.

3. **Surface Realisation (1 mark):** Applies morphological, syntactic, and orthographic rules to produce a grammatically correct sentence. Example: applying subject-verb agreement → *"The valve **opens**"* (not *"open"*).

*(Acceptable alternative stages: Document Structuring, Aggregation, Referring Expression Generation — any three with correct definitions earn full marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, the student answers *one* of two alternatives. Each alternative carries sub-parts totalling 14 marks (typically 7 + 7).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper PECPT754, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the complete six-stage classical architecture of a Natural Language Generation system. For each stage, give one example from a *Cyber-Physical System* (e.g., smart grid, autonomous vehicle, industrial robot). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the comparative advantages and disadvantages of **classical pipeline NLG** versus **end-to-end neural NLG** when deployed in a *safety-critical* CPS such as an avionics flight-warning system. Conclude with a justified recommendation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a) [7 marks — 1.0 mark per stage + 1 mark for synthesis]**

| Stage | Function (≈ 0.7 mark) | CPS Example (≈ 0.3 mark) |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Content Determination | Decide *what* facts from the data to communicate | Choose 3 of 500 alerts that crossed the flight envelope limits |
| 2. Document Structuring | Organise the chosen facts into a rhetorical order | Order: *Trigger → Severity → Affected System → Pilot Action* |
| 3. Lexicalisation | Pick words for each proposition | Use *"Overspeed"* (aviation standard) instead of *"too fast"* |
| 4. Aggregation | Merge related propositions into concise sentences | *"Both hydraulic pumps A and B show low pressure"* (instead of two sentences) |
| 5. Referring Expression | Generate unambiguous noun phrases | First mention: *"the left hydraulic pump"*; later: *"it"* |
| 6. Surface Realisation | Apply grammar, morphology, orthography | Produces *"CAUTION: LEFT HYDRAULIC PRESSURE LOW. ACTION REQUIRED."* (aviation style) |

**Synthesis (1 mark):** The six stages are *sequential* and *modular* — an error in any one stage propagates downstream. In CPS, this modularity allows each stage to be *verified independently* against safety standards (e.g., DO-178C).

---

**Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]**

**Classical Pipeline NLG — Advantages (≈ 1.5 marks):**
- **Determinism:** Same input → same output, always. Critical for certification.
- **Auditability:** Every lexical choice is traceable to a rule or schema.
- **Low data requirements:** Can be hand-engineered with a domain expert.
- **Predictable latency:** No GPU dependency, suitable for embedded flight computers.

**Classical Pipeline NLG — Disadvantages (≈ 1 mark):**
- Brittle to unseen inputs; expensive to scale across domains.
- Output often sounds stilted; limited natural variation.

**End-to-End Neural NLG — Advantages (≈ 1.5 marks):**
- High *fluency*; produces natural, human-like sentences.
- *Generalises* across linguistic patterns; one model can serve many CPS subsystems.

**End-to-End Neural NLG — Disadvantages (≈ 1.5 marks):**
- **Hallucination** — may invent sensor names or actions (unacceptable in flight warning).
- **Non-determinism** at decoding time (sampling temperature).
- **Opaque** — no audit trail per lexical choice.
- **Compute-heavy** — GPUs not always available in certified avionics hardware.

**Recommendation (≈ 1 mark):** For the *avionics flight-warning system*, a **classical pipeline NLG with optional neural paraphrase post-processing** is recommended. The pipeline guarantees that *content* is correct and traceable; the neural layer only *rephrases* an already-validated message for fluency, never inventing new facts. This *hybrid* approach is consistent with DO-178C and DO-330 certification goals.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper PECPT754, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What is a **meaning representation (MR)** in the context of NLG? Construct a meaning representation for the following scenario and show how an end-to-end neural NLG system would convert it into a fluent English sentence for a CPS operator:

> *A temperature sensor TS-07 in furnace F-2 has recorded 945 °C, exceeding the safe limit of 900 °C. The coolant valve V-12 should be opened within 30 seconds.*

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **BLEU** evaluation metric in detail, with its full mathematical formulation. Using the formulas, **re-compute BLEU-1** for the following CPS scenario:

- *Reference (R):* "Oxygen level is critically low."
- *Candidate (C):* "The oxygen level is low and is very low."

Assume uniform weights. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]**

**Meaning Representation Defined (1 mark):** A Meaning Representation is a *structured, formal, machine-readable* encoding of the facts to be communicated. It may take the form of attribute–value pairs, RDF triples, a logical form, or a JSON object. The NLG system treats the MR as the *semantic content* it must verbalise — every fact in the MR must appear in the output, and no new fact should be added.

**Constructed MR (2 marks):** A common representation style for CPS alerts:

$$
\text{MR} \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\text{event} &= \text{overheat} \\
\text{sensor} &= \text{TS-07} \\
\text{component} &= \text{furnace F-2} \\
\text{value} &= 945 \text{ °C} \\
\text{limit} &= 900 \text{ °C} \\
\text{action} &= \text{open} \\
\text{actor} &= \text{valve V-12} \\
\text{deadline} &= 30 \text{ s} \\
\text{severity} &= \text{high} \\
\text{audience} &= \text{operator}
\end{cases}
$$

(Any equivalent formal representation — RDF triples, Prolog facts, Python dict — receives full credit.)

**End-to-End Neural Conversion (3 marks):**

1. **Tokenisation of MR** — Each field of the MR is serialised into a linear token sequence (e.g., `"event=overheat sensor=TS-07 component=furnace_F-2 value=945 limit=900 action=open actor=valve_V-12 deadline=30s severity=high audience=operator"`).
2. **Encoder Pass** — A Transformer encoder converts the serialised MR into a sequence of contextual embeddings $\mathbf{h}_1, \mathbf{h}_2, \dots, \mathbf{h}_k$.
3. **Autoregressive Decoding** — The decoder generates one word at a time, attending to the encoder's hidden states:

$$
P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \text{MR}) = \text{Softmax}\!\left( W_o \cdot \text{Decoder}(y_{<t}, \mathbf{h}) \right)
$$

4. **Generated Output (≈ 1 mark):**

> *"**Warning:** Furnace F-2's temperature sensor TS-07 has recorded **945 °C**, exceeding the **900 °C** safety limit. **Open coolant valve V-12 within 30 seconds** to prevent thermal damage."*

The model has performed **content selection** (all MR fields are present), **aggregation** (single coherent paragraph), **lexicalisation** (chose "exceeding" and "thermal damage"), and **surface realisation** (capitalisation, bold-equivalent emphasis) in a *single forward pass*.

---

**Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]**

**BLEU Concept (1 mark):** BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) measures the *n-gram overlap* between a machine-generated candidate and one or more human-written references, modified by a *brevity penalty* to discourage overly short outputs. Range: 0 to 1 (higher is better).

**Mathematical Formulation (2 marks):**

$$
\text{BLEU} \;=\; BP \cdot \exp\!\left( \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n \right)
$$

$$
p_n \;=\; \frac{\sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(\text{ngram})}{\sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{Count}(\text{ngram})}
$$

$$
BP \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } c > r \\
\exp(1 - r/c) & \text{if } c \le r
\end{cases}
$$

**Worked BLEU-1 Computation (4 marks):**

- Tokenise:

$$
C = \{\text{the, oxygen, level, is, low, and, is, very, low}\},\ n_C = 9
$$

$$
R = \{\text{oxygen, level, is, critically, low}\},\ n_R = 5
$$

- Clipped unigram counts:

| Unigram | $\text{Count}_C$ | $\text{Count}_R$ | $\text{Count}_{\text{clip}}$ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| the | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| oxygen | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| level | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| is | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| low | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| and | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| very | 1 | 0 | 0 |

Clipped sum: $0 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 4$.

- Unigram precision:

$$
p_1 \;=\; \frac{4}{9} \;\approx\; 0.4444
$$

- Brevity penalty: $c = 9 > r = 5$, so $BP = 1$.

- BLEU-1:

$$
\boxed{\text{BLEU-1} \;=\; \exp(\log(0.4444)) \;=\; 0.4444}
$$

**Critical Interpretation (in-built into the derivation):** The reference says *"critically low"* — a single, decisive phrase. The candidate dilutes this with *"low and is very low"*. BLEU-1 = 0.44 quantitatively exposes this dilution. In a CPS, dilution of a criticality word (*critical*) is a **safety-relevant linguistic failure** — an evaluator should flag this for the safety team.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on NLG questions (Module 3):**
>
> 1. **Confusing NLG with NLU or MT.** A question that says "explain how a CPS converts text to action" is *NLU*, not NLG. Read the direction of conversion carefully.
> 2. **Skipping the content-selection stage.** Many students jump straight to "surface realisation". Examiners explicitly test whether you know that NLG *first* decides *what to say* before deciding *how to say it*.
> 3. **Omitting units in CPS examples.** If your worked example says *"temperature is 87.3"* without °C, expect a 0.5-mark deduction. CPS is meaningless without units.
> 4. **Writing BLEU without the brevity penalty.** A BLEU formula missing the $BP$ term is incomplete. Examiners specifically check the $BP$ clause.
> 5. **Treating neural NLG as universally superior.** In safety-critical CPS, the *correct* answer is often *hybrid or classical* — saying "neural is always better" is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
> 6. **Forgetting the audience/user model.** NLG output must be *tailored* to the operator's expertise. A generic answer that ignores the user model loses marks on "explain the role of the user model in NLG".

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and carry it into the exam hall.**

- **NLG = Data → Text.** NLU = Text → Data. MT = Text A → Text B.
- **Six classical stages:** Content Determination → Document Structuring → Lexicalisation → Aggregation → Referring Expression Generation → Surface Realisation.
- **Modern neural NLG:** Encoder-decoder Transformer, autoregressive token generation, trained by minimising negative log-likelihood.
- **In CPS, NLG sits at the perception–cognition–action interface**, especially the HMI/output layer.
- **Three must-know metrics:** BLEU (precision-based, brevity-penalised), ROUGE-L (recall-based, LCS), METEOR (F-mean with fragmentation penalty). Perplexity is intrinsic.
- **BLEU-1 = clipped unigram precision × BP.** BP = 1 if $c > r$, else $\exp(1 - r/c)$.
- **Pipeline NLG = auditable, deterministic, low-data.** Neural NLG = fluent, generalisable, hallucination-prone.
- **For safety-critical CPS → prefer pipeline or hybrid NLG** (deterministic core + neural paraphrase).
- **Always include units, time-stamps, and unique entity IDs** in any CPS NLG example.
- **Validation/post-processing layer is mandatory** in deployed CPS NLG to catch hallucinated sensor IDs.
- **CPS-specific failure modes:** Hallucination, Omission, Repetition, Ambiguous Reference, Tense/Aspect Error, Unit Confusion — know the pipeline stage each maps to.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pattern Recognition Systems: Understanding Speech Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pattern Recognition Systems: Understanding Speech Recognition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pattern Recognition Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Pattern Recognition System** is a cognitive computational framework that automatically identifies, classifies, and interprets regularities (patterns) in input data by mapping observed signals into a predefined set of categories using statistical, structural, or learned representations.

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, pattern recognition acts as the *cognitive perception layer*, enabling physical agents (robots, autonomous vehicles, smart sensors) to perceive, infer, and respond meaningfully to unstructured real-world stimuli.

**Three Classical Paradigms of Pattern Recognition:**

| Paradigm | Core Idea | Mathematical Backbone |
|---|---|---|
| **Statistical** | Patterns as feature vectors in a metric space | Bayes' Decision Theory, HMM, GMM |
| **Structural / Syntactic** | Patterns as compositions of sub-patterns (grammars) | Formal Languages, Graph Matching |
| **Neural / Deep** | Patterns learned via hierarchical non-linear transformations | CNN, RNN, LSTM, Transformer |

## 1.2 Speech Recognition — The Topic of Focus

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)** is the process of converting an acoustic speech signal (a continuous waveform) into a sequence of linguistic symbols (phonemes, words, or sentences) by applying pattern recognition techniques over time-varying spectral representations.

**Sub-fields of Spoken Language Processing:**
- **ASR** — Speech → Text
- **TTS** (Text-to-Speech) — Text → Speech
- **Speaker Recognition** — Speech → Identity
- **Speech Synthesis / Coding** — Compression & regeneration

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Expert Stenographer"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition (Plain English):**
> Imagine hiring a highly trained stenographer sitting in a soundproof booth. They listen to a stream of words, mentally "chunk" them into syllables, consult their vast memory of language rules, and type out the words. Speech Recognition is essentially a **computational stenographer** that does exactly this — but it replaces the human brain with statistical models (HMMs) and deep neural networks, and the ear with **spectral feature extractors** (MFCC).

The stenographer performs three cognitive acts, which mirror the ASR pipeline:
1. **Feature extraction** (extracting meaningful "ear signatures" from the audio).
2. **Acoustic modeling** (matching sound patterns to phonemes).
3. **Language modeling** (choosing the most likely word sequence given grammar).

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Performance Metrics (Must be Memorized):**
> - **Word Error Rate (WER)** — *primary ASR accuracy metric*, expressed in **%**.
> - **Real-Time Factor (RTF)** — *speed metric*, where **RTF < 1.0** denotes real-time or faster decoding.
> - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — robustness indicator in **dB**.
> - **Frame Rate** — typically **10 ms** (100 frames/second) for narrowband ASR, **25 ms** with **10 ms** shift for wideband.
> - **Sampling Frequency** — **16 kHz** (narrowband) or **44.1 kHz** (wideband, CD quality).

---

## 1.5 Role of Speech Recognition in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

In a CPS architecture, speech recognition provides the **human-machine cognitive interface**:

- **Autonomous Vehicles** — voice command parsing for navigation.
- **Industrial IoT** — operator-instructed robotic arms.
- **Healthcare CPS** — hands-free surgical assistant logging.
- **Smart Home / Ambient Intelligence** — natural language actuation of physical devices.
- **Aerospace CPS** — cockpit voice-command avionics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pattern Recognition Pipeline (Universal Schema)

Every pattern recognition system, including ASR, follows a canonical operational flow:

1. **Sensing / Signal Acquisition** — Capture raw input (audio, image, telemetry).
2. **Pre-processing** — Noise suppression, normalization, segmentation.
3. **Feature Extraction** — Convert raw signal into discriminative numerical descriptors.
4. **Modeling** — Train a classifier (statistical / neural) on labeled patterns.
5. **Decision / Decoding** — Predict the class label of an unknown pattern.
6. **Post-processing** — Apply contextual or domain rules to refine output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this pipeline?**
> Raw signals (e.g., audio samples) are extremely high-dimensional and noisy. Mapping them into a lower-dimensional, discriminative **feature space** is what makes the pattern recognition problem *tractable* and *generalizable*.

## 2.2 The Speech Recognition Sub-Pipeline (ASR Architecture)

ASR is decomposed into three interlocking probabilistic models — this is the **fundamental KTU conceptual block**:

| Sub-Model | Formal Role | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Acoustic Model (AM)** | Maps audio features → phonemes | $P(O \mid W)$ |
| **Pronunciation Lexicon** | Maps words → phoneme sequences | Dictionary lookup |
| **Language Model (LM)** | Captures word-sequence likelihood | $P(W)$ |

The decoder searches for the **most probable word sequence** $\hat{W}$ given the observed audio $O$:

$$\hat{W} = \underset{W}{\arg\max}\, P(W \mid O)$$

Applying **Bayes' Theorem**, this is decomposed as:

$$\hat{W} = \underset{W}{\arg\max}\, \frac{P(O \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(O)}$$

Since $P(O)$ is independent of $W$, it is dropped:

$$\hat{W} = \underset{W}{\arg\max}\, P(O \mid W) \cdot P(W)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition (Bayes Decoding):**
> Think of the acoustic model as *"how well does the audio match this candidate sentence?"* and the language model as *"how likely is this sentence in English?"*. The decoder multiplies the two and picks the winner — exactly like a judge scoring a contestant on both technical accuracy (AM) and presentation (LM).

## 2.3 Feature Extraction — Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC)

MFCCs are the **de-facto standard acoustic features** for ASR. They mimic the human cochlea's non-linear frequency resolution.

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Pre-emphasis** — Boost high frequencies to compensate for the glottal source roll-off.
2. **Framing** — Slice signal into **25 ms** frames with **10 ms** shift (Hamming window).
3. **FFT** — Convert each frame into the frequency domain → magnitude spectrum.
4. **Mel Filterbank** — Apply triangular filters spaced on the **Mel scale**.
5. **Log Compression** — Take logarithm of filterbank energies.
6. **DCT (Discrete Cosine Transform)** — Decorrelate and compress to ~13 coefficients.
7. **Delta & Delta-Delta** — Append first and second-order time derivatives → 39-D vector.

The **Mel scale** is defined as:

$$\text{Mel}(f) = 1127 \cdot \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$$

## 2.4 Acoustic Modeling — Hidden Markov Model (HMM)

A speech signal is modeled as a piecewise-stationary process — the **HMM** captures this temporal structure elegantly.

**HMM Tuple:** $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $A$ | State transition matrix $P(s_j \mid s_i)$ |
| $B$ | Emission probability (GMM or DNN output) |
| $\pi$ | Initial state distribution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why HMMs dominated ASR for 30 years:**
> Speech is a **time-varying, doubly stochastic** process (the underlying phoneme sequence is hidden, and the observed audio is noisy). HMMs explicitly model this hidden-state assumption, while GMMs (or modern DNNs) handle the continuous emissions $B$.

## 2.5 Modern Deep Learning Acoustic Models — Hybrid HMM-DNN & End-to-End

| Approach | Description | Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **GMM-HMM** | Gaussian Mixture emissions | Classical baseline |
| **DNN-HMM (Hybrid)** | DNN replaces GMM for emissions | State-of-the-art pre-2015 |
| **CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification)** | End-to-end, no HMM, no alignment needed | Simpler pipeline |
| **Listen-Attend-Spell (LAS)** | Encoder-Decoder with attention | Sequence-to-sequence |
| **Whisper / wav2vec 2.0** | Self-supervised transformer-based | Robust, multilingual |

## 2.6 Language Modeling

The **N-gram Language Model** estimates the probability of a word sequence $W = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$:

$$P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}, w_{i-2}, \dots, w_{i-N+1})$$

For a **bigram (N=2)** model:

$$P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$$

Modern LMs are **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN-LM)** or **Transformer-based (BERT, GPT)**.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize Before the Exam**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Decoding Rule | $\hat{W} = \arg\max_W P(O \mid W) \cdot P(W)$ | — |
| 2 | Mel Scale | $\text{Mel}(f) = 1127 \cdot \ln(1 + f/700)$ | $f$ in Hz |
| 3 | Frame Energy (RMS) | $E = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} x_n^2}$ | Linear |
| 4 | HMM Joint Probability | $P(O, S) = \pi_{s_1} \prod_{t=2}^{T} a_{s_{t-1}, s_t} \prod_{t=1}^{T} b_{s_t}(o_t)$ | $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ |
| 5 | Forward Algorithm | $\alpha_t(j) = \left[\sum_{i} \alpha_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}\right] b_j(o_t)$ | Used in HMM training |
| 6 | Viterbi Decoding | $\delta_t(j) = \max_i \left[\delta_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}\right] b_j(o_t)$ | Best-state path |
| 7 | N-gram LM | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1}, w_i) / C(w_{i-1})$ | Bigram example |
| 8 | Word Error Rate | $\text{WER} = \frac{S + D + I}{N}$ | $S$=Subst, $D$=Del, $I$=Ins, $N$=Ref words |
| 9 | SNR | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{signal}} / P_{\text{noise}})$ | dB |
| 10 | Perplexity (LM) | $\text{PP} = P(w_1 \dots w_N)^{-1/N}$ | Lower is better |
| 11 | Cepstral Mean Normalization (CMN) | $\hat{c}_t = c_t - \mu$ where $\mu = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t} c_t$ | Channel robustness |
| 12 | Pre-emphasis Filter | $y[n] = x[n] - \alpha \cdot x[n-1]$ | $\alpha \in [0.95, 0.97]$ |
| 13 | Hamming Window | $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos(2\pi n / (N-1))$ | $0 \le n \le N-1$ |
| 14 | Real-Time Factor | $\text{RTF} = T_{\text{decode}} / T_{\text{audio}}$ | RTF $<$ 1.0 = real-time |
| 15 | Baum-Welch Re-estimation | $\hat{a}_{ij} = \frac{\sum_t \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_t \gamma_t(i)}$ | EM update of HMM |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap:** In WER, students often compute only *Substitutions*. The correct formula includes **Substitutions (S), Deletions (D), and Insertions (I)**. Forgetting I and D loses full marks.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CPS Voice Agents (e.g., Alexa, Siri)** — End-to-end ASR pipelines.
- **Medical CPS** — Voice-driven Electronic Health Record (EHR) systems.
- **Industrial CPS** — Voice-controlled PLCs and SCADA.
- **Automotive CPS** — In-cabin voice assistants (noise-robust ASR).
- **Defense CPS** — Cockpit voice-command avionics and battlefield CPS.
- **Accessibility CPS** — Speech-to-text for assistive devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayes' Decoding Rule from First Principles

We start with the foundational decision rule of statistical pattern recognition and derive the ASR decoding equation.

**Step 1 — Formal Problem Statement**

Given an observed acoustic feature sequence $O = (o_1, o_2, \dots, o_T)$, find the word sequence $W$ that maximizes the posterior probability:

$$W^* = \underset{W}{\arg\max}\, P(W \mid O)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Theorem**

$$P(W \mid O) = \frac{P(O \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(O)}$$

This step uses the identity $P(A \mid B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) / P(B)$, swapping the roles of $W$ and $O$.

**Step 3 — Recognize the Denominator is Constant**

Since the observation $O$ is fixed, $P(O)$ does not depend on $W$. The $\arg\max$ operation is therefore invariant to its scaling.

**Step 4 — Drop the Normalizer**

$$W^* = \underset{W}{\arg\max}\, P(O \mid W) \cdot P(W)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Logarithm (Numerical Stability)**

Products of small probabilities underflow in floating-point arithmetic. The logarithm preserves the $\arg\max$ (since $\log$ is monotonically increasing):

$$W^* = \underset{W}{\arg\max}\, \left[\log P(O \mid W) + \log P(W)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Final Form (Decoder Objective)**

The ASR decoder minimizes the **negative log-likelihood**:

$$W^* = \underset{W}{\arg\min}\, \left[-\log P(O \mid W) - \log P(W)\right]$$

This is the **exact objective** optimized by Viterbi decoding in HMM-based ASR and by beam-search in end-to-end models.

## 3.2 Derivation: Forward Algorithm for HMM Likelihood

The acoustic likelihood $P(O \mid \lambda)$ cannot be computed naively (it sums over exponentially many state sequences). The **Forward Algorithm** computes it in $O(N^2 T)$.

**Step 1 — Define the Forward Variable**

Let $\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \dots, o_t, s_t = j \mid \lambda)$ — the joint probability of the partial observation up to time $t$ and being in state $j$ at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Initialization (t = 1)**

$$\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1)$$

This is the probability of starting in state $j$ and emitting the first observation.

**Step 3 — Recursion (Inductive Step)**

To be in state $j$ at time $t$, we may have come from any state $i$ at time $t-1$:

$$\alpha_t(j) = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(o_t)$$

**Step 4 — Termination**

The total likelihood of the observation is the sum over all possible final states:

$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_T(j)$$

**Step 5 — Computational Complexity Analysis**

At each time step, the inner sum iterates over $N$ states. There are $T$ time steps.

$$\text{Complexity} = O(N^2 \cdot T)$$

This is the linear-in-$T$ polynomial complexity that makes HMM inference tractable.

## 3.3 Derivation: Word Error Rate (WER) Worked Example

**Reference Transcript:** `"the cat sat on the mat"`
**Hypothesized Output:** `"the cat sat a mat"`

Step-by-step alignment using the **Levenshtein (edit distance)** algorithm:

- `the` → `the` (correct)
- `cat` → `cat` (correct)
- `sat` → `sat` (correct)
- `on` → (deleted) → **1 Deletion**
- `the` → `a` (substituted) → **1 Substitution**
- `mat` → `mat` (correct)

**Counting:**

$$S = 1, \quad D = 1, \quad I = 0, \quad N = 6$$

**Step-by-Step Calculation:**

$$\text{WER} = \frac{S + D + I}{N} = \frac{1 + 1 + 0}{6} = \frac{2}{6} = 0.3333$$

Expressed as a percentage: **WER = 33.33%**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Pre-emphasis and Spectral Flattening

The discrete-time pre-emphasis filter is:

$$y[n] = x[n] - \alpha \cdot x[n-1]$$

**Z-transform derivation:**

$$Y(z) = X(z) - \alpha z^{-1} X(z) = X(z)\left(1 - \alpha z^{-1}\right)$$

The transfer function is:

$$H(z) = 1 - \alpha z^{-1} = \frac{z - \alpha}{z}$$

The frequency response is obtained by substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - \alpha e^{-j\omega}$$

**Magnitude Response:**

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = (1 - \alpha \cos\omega)^2 + (\alpha \sin\omega)^2 = 1 - 2\alpha \cos\omega + \alpha^2$$

**Key Behavior at $\omega = \pi$ (Nyquist, highest frequency):**

$$\vert H(e^{j\pi}) \vert = 1 + \alpha \approx 1.97 \quad (\text{when } \alpha = 0.97)$$

**Key Behavior at $\omega = 0$ (DC, lowest frequency):**

$$\vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = 1 - \alpha = 0.03$$

**Interpretation:** The filter amplifies high frequencies by ~20 dB while attenuating DC, **flattening the spectrum** of voiced speech whose natural roll-off is approximately $-12$ dB/octave. This improves subsequent FFT-based feature extraction.

## 3.5 Derivation: MFCC Cepstral Mean Normalization (CMN)

CMN removes convolutional channel distortion.

**Step 1 — Recognition that channel effects are convolutional**

$$x_{\text{obs}}[n] = x_{\text{clean}}[n] * h_{\text{channel}}[n]$$

**Step 2 — In the cepstral domain, convolution becomes addition**

$$c_{\text{obs}} = c_{\text{clean}} + c_{\text{channel}}$$

**Step 3 — Estimate the channel cepstrum as the time average**

$$\hat{c}_{\text{channel}} = \mu = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} c_{\text{obs}}(t)$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the estimate**

$$\hat{c}_{\text{clean}}(t) = c_{\text{obs}}(t) - \mu$$

This is a **first-order high-pass filter in the cepstral-time domain**, removing stationary channel bias while preserving dynamic speech content.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Pattern Recognition Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Raw Signal Input] --> B[Pre-Processing]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction]
    C --> D[Pattern Classifier]
    D --> E[Decision Output]
    D --> F[Trained Model Database]
    F --> D
    E --> G[Post-Processing]
    G --> H[Final Class Label]
```

## 4.2 Detailed ASR Architecture — Hybrid DNN-HMM

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Speech Waveform Input] --> S2[Pre-emphasis Filter]
    S2 --> S3[25ms Framing with 10ms Shift]
    S3 --> S4[Hamming Window]
    S4 --> S5[FFT Magnitude Spectrum]
    S5 --> S6[Mel Filterbank 40 channels]
    S6 --> S7[Log Compression]
    S7 --> S8[DCT to 13 MFCCs]
    S8 --> S9[Delta and Delta-Delta]
    S9 --> S10[39 D Feature Vector]
    S10 --> S11[DNN Acoustic Model]
    S11 --> S12[HMM State Emission Likelihood]
    S12 --> S13[Viterbi Decoder Beam Search]
    L1[N-gram or RNN Language Model] --> S13
    P1[Pronunciation Lexicon] --> S13
    S13 --> S14[Word Sequence Output]
```

## 4.3 End-to-End Deep Learning ASR (Whisper-Style)

```mermaid
graph TD
    W1[Raw Audio Waveform] --> W2[CNN Feature Encoder]
    W2 --> W3[Transformer Encoder]
    W3 --> W4[Cross Attention]
    W4 --> W5[Transformer Decoder]
    T1[Tokenized Text Prompt] --> W5
    W5 --> W6[Transcribed Text Output]
```

## 4.4 Bayes' Decoding as a Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1[Observed Audio Features O] --> Q2[Compute P of O given W for All Candidate Word Sequences]
    Q2 --> Q3[Multiply by P of W from Language Model]
    Q3 --> Q4[Argmax over All W]
    Q4 --> Q5[Best Word Sequence Output]
```

## 4.5 HMM Trellis — Viterbi Decoding Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1[Time Step 1] --> T2[Time Step 2]
    T2 --> T3[Time Step 3]
    T3 --> T4[Time Step 4]
    S1A[State S1] -.->|a11 b1| T1
    S1B[State S2] -.->|a21 b2| T1
    S1C[State S3] -.->|a31 b3| T1
    S2A[State S1] -.->|a11 b1| T2
    S2B[State S2] -.->|a22 b2| T2
    S2C[State S3] -.->|a33 b3| T2
```

## 4.6 Speech Recognition in a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) Context

```mermaid
graph TD
    U1[Human Operator] -->|Voice Command| M1[Microphone Sensor]
    M1 --> M2[Edge Pre-Processor Noise Suppression]
    M2 --> M3[On-Device ASR Engine]
    M3 --> M4[Intent Parser NLP]
    M4 --> M5[CPS Controller PLC MCU]
    M5 --> M6[Physical Actuator]
    M6 --> M7[Motor Valve Servo]
    M7 -->|Mechanical Action| P1[Physical Environment]
    P1 -->|Feedback| M1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation of the CPS-ASR Loop:**
> This is a **closed-loop cognitive CPS**: the microphone and ASR engine form the *sensory-cognitive layer*, the controller is the *decision layer*, and the actuator is the *physical interaction layer*. The feedback arrow closes the loop, enabling adaptive, voice-driven control of physical systems.
