- **Examiner Pitfalls to Avoid:** Sign errors in RL updates, missing expectations, missing discount factors, mixing $Q$ and $V$, omitting the $\arg\min$ / $\arg\max$ operator in the optimization statement.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute strategy:** If a 14-mark question asks "explain the General Learning Model," structure your answer in three blocks: (1) define and draw the 7-stage diagram, (2) state the ERM objective formally, (3) derive the gradient descent update in 4–5 steps. This single template will score you **at least 12/14** on any GLM question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Performance Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Measures** in Cognitive Systems and AI-driven Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) are a mathematically rigorous set of quantitative metrics used to evaluate, validate, and benchmark the correctness, robustness, efficiency, and real-time responsiveness of cognitive algorithms, learning models, and decision-making pipelines embedded within physical control loops.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, performance measures span three orthogonal evaluation axes:

1. **Statistical / Predictive Quality** — How *correct* are the cognitive outputs? (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC).
2. **Computational Efficiency** — How *fast* and *cheap* are the inferences inside the CPS loop? (Latency, Throughput, FLOPs, Memory footprint).
3. **System-Level Resilience** — How *reliably* does the cognitive CPS behave under disturbances, noise, and safety constraints? (MTTF, Safety Integrity Level, Convergence Time).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The School Examination System**
> Imagine a teacher who has prepared 100 MCQ answer sheets and wants to evaluate their newly hired automatic grading machine. The machine either says "Correct" or "Wrong" for each answer. To know if the machine is *good*, the teacher cannot just count "how many it got right" — she must ask:
> - Of all the *truly correct* answers, how many did the machine catch? (**Recall / Sensitivity**)
> - Of all the answers the machine *flagged correct*, how many were actually correct? (**Precision**)
> - Is the machine balanced between catching real answers and not being fooled by wrong ones? (**F1-Score**)
> - How long does the machine take to grade one paper? (**Latency**)
> - Does it crash when 1000 papers arrive at once? (**Throughput / Robustness**)
>
> These are exactly the *Performance Measures* used to evaluate a cognitive AI in a CPS — except the "papers" are sensor streams, the "answers" are actuator commands, and the "classroom" is a self-driving car, a smart grid, or a robotic arm.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Foundational Metrics Every KTU Student Must Memorize**
> - **Accuracy** — Fraction of total correct predictions.
> - **Precision** — Quality of positive predictions.
> - **Recall (Sensitivity / TPR)** — Coverage of actual positives.
> - **F1-Score** — Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
> - **AUC-ROC** — Threshold-independent discrimination ability.
>
> Plus the three CPS-critical metrics: **Latency**, **Throughput**, and **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix — The Heart of Classification Evaluation

A binary cognitive classifier produces four outcomes for every prediction, summarized in a **2×2 Confusion Matrix**:

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in CPS?**
> In a CPS like an autonomous drone's obstacle detector, a **False Negative** (missing an obstacle) can be *fatal*, whereas a **False Positive** (false alarm) is annoying but safe. This asymmetry is why **Recall** is often more critical than **Accuracy** in safety-critical CPS.

## 2.2 The 8 Core Classification Metrics — Operational Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Accuracy
- **Why**: Simplest measure of overall correctness.
- **How**: Ratio of correct predictions to total predictions.
- **Limitation**: Fails on imbalanced datasets (e.g., 99% "no-fault" samples in a power-grid CPS will yield 99% accuracy for a trivial "always say no-fault" classifier).

### 2.2.2 Error Rate
- **Why**: Complement of accuracy; often more intuitive for engineers.
- **How**: Fraction of misclassified samples.

### 2.2.3 Precision (Positive Predictive Value)
- **Why**: Measures reliability of positive class predictions.
- **How**: Of all items flagged as positive, how many truly are.

### 2.2.4 Recall (Sensitivity, True Positive Rate, Hit Rate)
- **Why**: Measures coverage of the positive class.
- **How**: Of all actual positives, how many were caught.

### 2.2.5 Specificity (True Negative Rate, Selectivity)
- **Why**: Mirror of recall, but for the negative class.
- **How**: Of all actual negatives, how many were correctly rejected.

### 2.2.6 F1-Score
- **Why**: Single scalar balancing Precision and Recall.
- **How**: Harmonic mean — penalizes extreme imbalance between P and R.

### 2.2.7 F-β Score (Generalization)
- **Why**: Allows asymmetric weighting — e.g., in medical CPS, recall may be 2× more important than precision.
- **How**: Weighted harmonic mean controlled by β.

### 2.2.8 AUC-ROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
- **Why**: Threshold-independent measure of separability; the *probability* that the classifier ranks a random positive higher than a random negative.
- **How**: Integrate TPR over FPR as the decision threshold sweeps [0, 1].

## 2.3 Regression Metrics — When the Cognitive Output is Continuous

In CPS applications like predictive maintenance (Remaining Useful Life estimation) or state estimation (Kalman-filtered sensor fusion), outputs are continuous scalars. Classification metrics no longer apply; we use **Regression Metrics**:

### 2.3.1 Mean Squared Error (MSE)
- **Why**: Penalizes large errors quadratically — sensitive to outliers.
- **How**: Average of squared residuals.

### 2.3.2 Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
- **Why**: Same units as the target variable (interpretable for engineers).
- **How**: Square root of MSE.

### 2.3.3 Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
- **Why**: Robust to outliers; linear penalty.
- **How**: Average of absolute residuals.

### 2.3.4 Coefficient of Determination (R²)
- **Why**: Fraction of variance explained by the model.
- **How**: 1 minus (residual variance / total variance); R² = 1 is perfect.

## 2.4 CPS-Specific Performance Measures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPS-Critical Metrics — Beyond Pure ML**
> In a real-time cognitive CPS (e.g., autonomous vehicle, smart manufacturing, surgical robot), the AI model is just one component. The *system-level* performance is governed by:

### 2.4.1 End-to-End Latency
- **Why**: Total delay from sensor input to actuator output; bounded by control-loop deadlines.
- **How**: Sum of sensing, preprocessing, inference, decision, and actuation delays.

### 2.4.2 Throughput
- **Why**: How many inference cycles per second the cognitive pipeline can sustain.
- **How**: Inverse of mean cycle time, often measured in FPS or Hz.

### 2.4.3 Jitter (Latency Variance)
- **Why**: Determinism — a CPS may tolerate 50 ms mean latency but require variance < 2 ms.
- **How**: Standard deviation of latency distribution.

### 2.4.4 Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) & Availability
- **Why**: For long-running CPS (satellite, nuclear plant), cognitive module reliability is a safety metric.
- **How**: Statistical expectation of operational lifetime before failure.

### 2.4.5 Safety Integrity Level (SIL) / ASIL
- **Why**: ISO 26262 / IEC 61508 standardized risk-reduction metric; the cognitive perception system must achieve a target ASIL.
- **How**: Probabilistic risk per hour of operation.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Range / Unit** | **Best Used When** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Balanced classes |
| Error Rate | $\frac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = 1 - Accuracy$ | $[0, 1]$ | Balanced classes |
| Precision (P) | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | FP cost is high |
| Recall / TPR (R) | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | FN cost is high (CPS safety) |
| Specificity / TNR | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Balanced evaluation |
| F1-Score | $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Imbalanced binary |
| F-β Score | $(1 + \beta^{2}) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^{2} P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Weighted P–R trade-off |
| AUC-ROC | $\int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR^{-1}(t))\, dt$ | $[0, 1]$ | Threshold-free ranking |
| MSE | $\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})^{2}$ | squared units | Outlier-sensitive regression |
| RMSE | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})^{2}}$ | original units | Interpretable regression |
| MAE | $\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i} \vert$ | original units | Outlier-robust regression |
| R² | $1 - \frac{\sum (y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})^{2}}{\sum (y_{i} - \bar{y})^{2}}$ | $(-\infty, 1]$ | Variance explained |
| Latency (L) | $L = t_{sense} + t_{infer} + t_{actuate}$ | seconds / ms | Real-time CPS |
| Throughput (T) | $T = \frac{N_{inferences}}{\Delta t}$ | Hz / FPS | Pipeline capacity |
| Jitter | $\sigma(L)$ or $IQR(L)$ | ms | Determinism check |
| MTTF | $\mathbb{E}[T_{failure}]$ | hours | Reliability |
| Availability (A) | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Uptime fraction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - In **autonomous driving (Tesla, Waymo)**: A perception model with 99% accuracy but 200 ms latency is *worthless* — control loops need ≤ 50 ms. Engineers thus optimize a **Pareto frontier** of (Accuracy, Latency).
> - In **medical CPS (e.g., IBM Watson for Oncology)**: Recall is mandated to be ≥ 0.99 because missing a tumor (FN) is catastrophic.
> - In **smart-grid CPS (predictive load forecasting)**: RMSE on MW predictions drives economic dispatch decisions — a 1% error can cost millions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why F1 is the Harmonic Mean (Not the Arithmetic Mean)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU 2024 ESE Module-4 derivations. Master it.

### Setup
We want a single scalar $F$ that combines Precision $P$ and Recall $R$ such that:
1. $F$ is symmetric in $P$ and $R$.
2. $F = 0$ if either $P = 0$ or $R = 0$ (harsh on bad classifiers).
3. $F \leq \min(P, R)$ (never exaggerates the worse component).

### Step 1 — Start with the General Mean
The generalized mean of order $k$ of two numbers $P$ and $R$ is:

$$
M_k(P, R) = \left( \frac{P^{k} + R^{k}}{2} \right)^{\frac{1}{k}}
$$

We want a mean that **penalizes the smaller value** heavily. As $k \to -\infty$, $M_k$ collapses to $\min(P, R)$. The harmonic mean corresponds to $k = -1$.

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Harmonic Mean
Substitute $k = -1$:

$$
M_{-1}(P, R) = \left( \frac{P^{-1} + R^{-1}}{2} \right)^{-1}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the Inner Expression
Common denominator of $P^{-1}$ and $R^{-1}$ is $PR$:

$$
P^{-1} + R^{-1} = \frac{R + P}{PR}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back

$$
M_{-1}(P, R) = \left( \frac{P + R}{2 PR} \right)^{-1} = \frac{2 PR}{P + R}
$$

### Step 5 — Define F1

$$
\boxed{F_{1} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}}
$$

### Step 6 — Verify the Properties
- If $P = 0$ or $R = 0$, numerator $= 0 \Rightarrow F_1 = 0$. ✓
- If $P = R = 1$, $F_1 = \frac{2}{2} = 1$. ✓
- If $P = 1, R = 0.5$, arithmetic mean $= 0.75$, but $F_1 = \frac{1}{1.5} \approx 0.67$ — penalizes the imbalance. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with Arithmetic Mean (AM)**
> - AM: $\frac{P + R}{2} = 0.75$ (over-optimistic for skewed classifiers).
> - HM: $F_1 = 0.67$ (honest assessment).
> The harmonic mean is the *correct* aggregation for *rates* (ratios). This is a direct consequence of the AM-GM-HM inequality chain:
>
> $$
> \min(P, R) \;\leq\; HM \;\leq\; GM \;\leq\; AM \;\leq\; \max(P, R)
> $$

## 3.2 Derivation: The F-β Generalization

### Goal
Introduce a parameter $\beta$ that controls how much more we value Recall over Precision (or vice versa).

### Definition

$$
F_{\beta} = (1 + \beta^{2}) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^{2} P + R}
$$

### Interpretation
- $\beta = 1$: $F_1$ (equal weight).
- $\beta > 1$: Recall weighted more heavily (e.g., $\beta = 2$ means Recall is 4× more important than Precision, since $(1 + \beta^2) = 5$ weights $R$).
- $\beta < 1$: Precision weighted more heavily.

### Step-by-step Algebraic Justification
Start from the requirement that $F_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2) PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$ is the *β-weighted harmonic mean*. To see this, observe:

$$
\frac{1}{F_{\beta}} = \frac{\beta^{2} P + R}{(1 + \beta^{2}) PR} = \frac{1}{1 + \beta^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{R} + \frac{\beta^{2}}{1 + \beta^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{P}
$$

This is a convex combination of $\frac{1}{P}$ and $\frac{1}{R}$ with weights $\frac{\beta^2}{1+\beta^2}$ and $\frac{1}{1+\beta^2}$. Hence $F_\beta$ is the weighted harmonic mean — the unique mean satisfying the three properties in §3.1 with an asymmetric penalty.

## 3.3 Derivation: ROC Curve and AUC

### Setup
A probabilistic classifier outputs a score $s(x) \in [0, 1]$ for each sample. To produce a hard label, we apply a threshold $\tau \in [0, 1]$:

$$
\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } s(x) \geq \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 1 — Define TPR and FPR as Functions of τ

$$
TPR(\tau) = \frac{TP(\tau)}{TP(\tau) + FN(\tau)} = \frac{\#\{x \in \mathcal{P} : s(x) \geq \tau\}}{\vert \mathcal{P} \vert}
$$

$$
FPR(\tau) = \frac{FP(\tau)}{FP(\tau) + TN(\tau)} = \frac{\#\{x \in \mathcal{N} : s(x) \geq \tau\}}{\vert \mathcal{N} \vert}
$$

### Step 2 — Parametric ROC Curve
As $\tau$ sweeps from $1 \to 0$:
- TPR increases from $0 \to 1$.
- FPR increases from $0 \to 1$.
- The locus of $(FPR(\tau), TPR(\tau))$ traces the **ROC curve**.

### Step 3 — AUC as Probability of Correct Ranking
The AUC-ROC has a beautiful probabilistic interpretation:

$$
\boxed{AUC = P\bigl(s(x_{+}) > s(x_{-})\bigr)}
$$

where $x_{+} \sim \mathcal{P}$ (positive) and $x_{-} \sim \mathcal{N}$ (negative) are drawn independently.

### Step 4 — Verification via Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney Statistic

$$
AUC = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathbf{1}[s(x_{i}^{+}) > s(x_{j}^{-})]}{m \cdot n}
$$

where $m = \vert \mathcal{P} \vert$, $n = \vert \mathcal{N} \vert$.

### Step 5 — Geometric Interpretation
- $AUC = 1.0$: Perfect classifier.
- $AUC = 0.5$: Random guessing (diagonal line).
- $AUC < 0.5$: Worse than random (predictions inverted).
- $AUC = 0$: Perfect anti-classifier.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Metric

| **Scenario** | **Class Imbalance** | **Cost of FP vs FN** | **Recommended Metric** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Email spam filter | Mild | FP cost > FN cost | **Precision** |
| Cancer / obstacle detection (CPS safety) | Severe | FN cost ≫ FP cost | **Recall** + F2-Score |
| General balanced classifier | None / mild | Symmetric | **Accuracy** + F1 |
| Ranking / retrieval systems | Often skewed | Threshold-free | **AUC-ROC / AUC-PR** |
| Regression (RUL prediction) | N/A | Outlier-sensitive | **RMSE** |
| Regression (sensor noise) | N/A | Outlier-robust | **MAE** |
| Real-time CPS (vehicle, robot) | N/A | Hard deadlines | **Latency + Jitter** |
| Long-running CPS (satellite, plant) | N/A | Safety | **MTTF + ASIL** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024**
> No single metric is universally "best." A KTU examiner expects students to **justify** their choice of metric based on (a) class balance, (b) asymmetric cost, and (c) deployment constraints (real-time, safety, interpretability).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Performance Evaluation Pipeline in a Cognitive CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical World Input] --> B[Sensor Layer]
    B --> C[Preprocessing Module]
    C --> D[Cognitive AI Model]
    D --> E[Decision / Actuation Layer]
    E --> F[Physical World Output]

    D --> G[Performance Evaluation Engine]
    H[Ground Truth Labels] --> G
    I[Latency Probes] --> G
    J[Reliability Monitor] --> G

    G --> K[Classification Metrics]
    G --> L[Regression Metrics]
    G --> M[System Metrics]

    K --> N[Confusion Matrix]
    K --> O[Precision Recall F1]
    K --> P[AUC ROC Curve]

    L --> Q[MSE RMSE MAE]
    L --> R[R Squared]

    M --> S[Latency Distribution]
    M --> T[Throughput Curve]
    M --> U[MTTF MTTR]

    N --> V[Final Performance Report]
    O --> V
    P --> V
    Q --> V
    R --> V
    S --> V
    T --> V
    U --> V
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map: The Three Pillars of CPS-AI Performance Measurement

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Performance Measures in Cognitive CPS] --> B[Statistical Quality]
    A --> C[Computational Efficiency]
    A --> D[System Resilience]

    B --> B1[Accuracy]
    B --> B2[Precision]
    B --> B3[Recall]
    B --> B4[F1 Score]
    B --> B5[AUC ROC]
    B --> B6[MSE RMSE MAE R2]

    C --> C1[Latency]
    C --> C2[Throughput]
    C --> C3[Jitter]
    C --> C4[FLOPs]
    C --> C5[Memory Footprint]

    D --> D1[MTTF]
    D --> D2[MTBF]
    D --> D3[ASIL SIL]
    D --> D4[Availability]
    D --> D5[Safety Violation Rate]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Confusion Matrix Generation and Metric Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Predictions Y Hat] --> C[Compare with Ground Truth Y]
    B[Ground Truth Y] --> C
    C --> D[Confusion Matrix Builder]
    D --> E[TP Count]
    D --> F[FP Count]
    D --> G[FN Count]
    D --> H[TN Count]

    E --> I[Metric Calculator]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I

    I --> J[Accuracy]
    I --> K[Precision]
    I --> L[Recall]
    I --> M[F1 Score]
    I --> N[Specificity]
    I --> O[AUC ROC]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: ROC Curve Construction (Threshold Sweep)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scored Predictions S of X] --> B[Initialize Threshold Tau to 0]
    B --> C[Apply Threshold Tau to Get Hard Labels]
    C --> D[Compute TPR and FPR at Tau]
    D --> E[Plot Point FPR Tau, TPR Tau]
    E --> F{Tau equals 1?}
    F -->|No| G[Increment Tau by Delta]
    G --> C
    F -->|Yes| H[Connect Points to Form ROC Curve]
    H --> I[Compute Area Under Curve]
    I --> J[AUC ROC Scalar Metric]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for the Diagrams**
> - Diagram 4.1 shows the *end-to-end* flow — physical world in, AI in the middle, performance evaluator in parallel.
> - Diagram 4.2 is the *taxonomy* — remember the three pillars for theory questions.
> - Diagrams 4.3 and 4.4 are *mechanism* diagrams — frequently drawn in KTU 14-mark questions to earn the full 7 marks for a "process" sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define the F1-score. Why is the harmonic mean preferred over the arithmetic mean for combining Precision and Recall?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

F1-score is the harmonic mean of Precision (P) and Recall (R):

$$
F_{1} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

**(1 mark)** for the formula.

The harmonic mean is preferred over the arithmetic mean because:
- It is **bounded above by the minimum** of P and R — hence cannot mask a poor value of either component.
- It yields $F_1 = 0$ if either $P = 0$ or $R = 0$, providing a *harsh* penalty for extreme imbalance.
- Example: For $P = 1, R = 0.1$, AM = 0.55 (misleading), but $F_1 = \frac{0.2}{1.1} \approx 0.18$ (honest). **(2 marks)** for justification with example.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Distinguish between Precision and Recall. In which CPS scenario is Recall more critical than Precision?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Precision | Recall |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ |
| Question Answered | "Of items flagged positive, how many are truly positive?" | "Of all actual positives, how many did we catch?" |
| Failure Mode Penalized | False Positives | False Negatives |

**(2 marks)** for the distinction with formulas.

**CPS Scenario (1 mark):** In a **collision-avoidance system of an autonomous vehicle**, missing an actual obstacle (FN) leads to a crash — therefore **Recall is more critical** than Precision. False alarms (FP) are inconvenient (hard braking) but safe.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 4 Pattern]`

**(a)** For a binary classifier used in a smart-grid fault detection CPS, the confusion matrix on a test set of 1000 samples is:

| | Predicted Fault | Predicted Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Fault** | 180 | 20 |
| **Actual Normal** | 30 | 770 |

Compute the Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, and F1-Score. Comment on which metric is most relevant given the safety-critical nature of the application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the ROC curve. Show with a stepwise derivation that the AUC-ROC equals the probability that a randomly chosen positive sample is scored higher than a randomly chosen negative sample. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1 — Extract the Counts from the Matrix** **(1 mark)**

- $TP = 180$
- $FN = 20$
- $FP = 30$
- $TN = 770$
- $Total = 1000$

**Step 2 — Compute Accuracy** **(1 mark)**

$$
Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{Total} = \frac{180 + 770}{1000} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.95
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Precision** **(1 mark)**

$$
Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} \approx 0.857
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Recall** **(1 mark)**

$$
Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Specificity** **(1 mark)**

$$
Specificity = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{770}{770 + 30} = \frac{770}{800} = 0.9625
$$

**Step 6 — Compute F1-Score** **(1 mark)**

$$
F_{1} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.857 \cdot 0.90}{0.857 + 0.90} = \frac{1.5426}{1.757} \approx 0.878
$$

**Step 7 — Safety-Critical Comment** **(1 mark)**

> [!NOTE]
> Although accuracy is 95%, in a safety-critical smart-grid CPS the 20 **False Negatives** (missed faults) are far more dangerous than the 30 False Positives (false alarms). A fault-detection system should be optimized for **Recall (0.90) → push to ≥ 0.99** using class weighting, threshold tuning, or cost-sensitive learning, even at the cost of slightly lower Precision.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — Define ROC** **(2 marks)**

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** plots the True Positive Rate $TPR(\tau)$ on the y-axis against the False Positive Rate $FPR(\tau)$ on the x-axis, as the decision threshold $\tau$ of a probabilistic classifier sweeps from $0$ to $1$.

**Step 2 — Define TPR and FPR** **(1 mark)**

$$
TPR(\tau) = \frac{TP(\tau)}{TP(\tau) + FN(\tau)}, \qquad FPR(\tau) = \frac{FP(\tau)}{FP(\tau) + TN(\tau)}
$$

**Step 3 — Express TPR and FPR as CDF Differences** **(1 mark)**

Let $F_{+}(s) = P(s(X) \leq s \mid Y = +1)$ and $F_{-}(s) = P(s(X) \leq s \mid Y = -1)$ be the CDFs of the score for positive and negative classes.

Then $TPR(\tau) = 1 - F_{+}(\tau)$ and $FPR(\tau) = 1 - F_{-}(\tau)$, so:

$$
TPR = 1 - F_{+}(\tau), \qquad FPR = 1 - F_{-}(\tau)
$$

**Step 4 — Set Up the AUC Integral** **(1 mark)**

By definition, AUC is the area under the ROC curve:

$$
AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(\tau)\, d(FPR(\tau)) = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(\tau) \cdot f_{-}(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

where $f_{-}(\tau) = \frac{d}{d\tau} FPR(\tau)$ is the score density of the negative class.

**Step 5 — Substitute the TPR Form** **(1 mark)**

$$
AUC = \int_{0}^{1} \bigl(1 - F_{+}(\tau)\bigr) f_{-}(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Integral as a Probability** **(1 mark)**

$$
AUC = \int_{0}^{1} P(s(X_{+}) > \tau \mid Y = +1) \cdot f_{-}(\tau)\, d\tau = P(s(X_{+}) > s(X_{-}))
$$

This is exactly the **Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney statistic**, completing the derivation. ∎

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 4 Pattern]`

**(a)** List and briefly explain any five classification performance metrics used to evaluate cognitive systems in CPS. For each metric, state one practical CPS use case where it is the most appropriate choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a regression-based Remaining Useful Life (RUL) prediction model for aircraft engines. The true and predicted RULs (in cycles) for 5 engines are: True = [100, 150, 200, 250, 300], Predicted = [110, 140, 210, 240, 295]. Compute MAE, MSE, RMSE, and R². Interpret which metric best reflects the engine's actual operational risk. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**(1 mark each × 5 = 5 marks for listing and explaining, 2 marks for use cases)**

1. **Accuracy** $\rightarrow \frac{TP+TN}{Total}$ — fraction of correct predictions.
   - **CPS use case**: Balanced quality-control classification on a manufacturing line (e.g., PCB defect detection with ~50–50 defect/non-defect mix).

2. **Precision** $\rightarrow \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — reliability of positive predictions.
   - **CPS use case**: Email/alert system for a SCADA control room — false alarms trigger costly operator interventions, so high Precision is needed.

3. **Recall** $\rightarrow \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — coverage of true positives.
   - **CPS use case**: Tumor / crack / obstacle detection in medical imaging or autonomous driving — missing a real defect is catastrophic.

4. **F1-Score** $\rightarrow \frac{2PR}{P+R}$ — balanced P–R aggregate.
   - **CPS use case**: Intrusion detection in an IoT network with imbalanced attack-vs-normal traffic (e.g., 1% attacks).

5. **AUC-ROC** $\rightarrow \int TPR\, dFPR$ — threshold-free ranking.
   - **CPS use case**: Ranking candidate fault hypotheses in a power-grid state estimator where the operating threshold may shift with grid loading.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 — Compute Residuals** **(1 mark)**

$$
e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i : \quad [-10,\; +10,\; -10,\; +10,\; +5]
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MAE** **(1 mark)**

$$
MAE = \frac{1}{n}\sum \vert e_i \vert = \frac{10 + 10 + 10 + 10 + 5}{5} = \frac{45}{5} = 9.0 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute MSE** **(1 mark)**

$$
MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum e_i^{2} = \frac{100 + 100 + 100 + 100 + 25}{5} = \frac{425}{5} = 85.0 \text{ cycles}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute RMSE** **(1 mark)**

$$
RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{85} \approx 9.220 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Mean of True Values** **(0.5 mark)**

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{100 + 150 + 200 + 250 + 300}{5} = \frac{1000}{5} = 200
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Total Sum of Squares** **(0.5 mark)**

$$
SS_{tot} = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^{2} = 100^{2} + 50^{2} + 0^{2} + 50^{2} + 100^{2} = 10000 + 2500 + 0 + 2500 + 10000 = 25000
$$

**Step 7 — Compute R²** **(1 mark)**

$$
R^{2} = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{425}{25000} = 1 - 0.017 = 0.983
$$

**Step 8 — Interpret for Operational Risk** **(1 mark)**

> [!NOTE]
> **MAE = 9 cycles** is the most *operationally honest* metric for an airline: it directly states that, on average, the model's RUL estimate deviates by 9 flight cycles from truth. This translates into a maintenance scheduling buffer. RMSE = 9.22 cycles is only slightly larger (errors are uniform), confirming no outlier-driven inflation. With $R^2 = 0.983$, the model explains 98.3% of variance — excellent fit. The maintenance engineer would plan 9-cycle early replacement to avoid in-flight failure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams**
> 1. **Confusing F1 with arithmetic mean** — Examiners allocate 2 marks *specifically* for stating the AM-GM-HM inequality chain and giving a numerical example.
> 2. **Reporting Accuracy on imbalanced data without caveat** — Always mention "Accuracy is misleading for skewed class distributions; F1/AUC-PR should be preferred."
> 3. **Forgetting units in regression metrics** — MSE is in *cycles²*; RMSE/MAE are in *cycles*. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark for unit omission.
> 4. **AUC-ROC derivation jumps** — Do NOT skip the step expressing TPR as $1 - F_{+}(\tau)$. This is the most-skipped step and worth 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing Latency with Throughput** — Latency is *per-inference* time; Throughput is *inferences-per-second*. They are reciprocals only when the system is pipelined perfectly.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention CPS-specific metrics** — A 14-mark question on "Performance Measures in Cognitive CPS" *must* mention at least one CPS-specific metric (Latency, Jitter, ASIL, MTTF) to score full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Performance Measures in Cognitive CPS**
>
> ✅ **Confusion Matrix (TP, FP, FN, TN)** is the foundation — every classification metric derives from it.
>
> ✅ **Accuracy** = $\frac{TP+TN}{Total}$ — use only for balanced classes.
>
> ✅ **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — minimizes False Alarms.
>
> ✅ **Recall** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — minimizes Misses (critical for CPS safety).
>
> ✅ **F1-Score** = $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$ — harmonic mean; harsh on imbalance.
>
> ✅ **F-β** = $(1+\beta^2)\frac{PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$ — β > 1 weighs Recall more (CPS use case).
>
> ✅ **AUC-ROC** ∈ [0, 1] = $P(s(x_+) > s(x_-))$ — threshold-free, ranking-based.
>
> ✅ **MSE penalizes outliers quadratically**; **MAE is robust**; **RMSE has same units as target**; **R² ∈ (-∞, 1]**.
>
> ✅ **CPS-specific trio**: Latency, Throughput, Jitter — beyond pure ML accuracy.
>
> ✅ **Safety metrics**: MTTF, MTBF, Availability, ASIL/SIL for ISO-compliant CPS.
>
> ✅ **Always justify your metric choice** with the triple: (i) class balance, (ii) FP-FN cost asymmetry, (iii) deployment constraints.
>
> ✅ **Real-world CPS rule of thumb**: Optimize a *Pareto frontier* of (Statistical Quality, Latency, Safety) — never a single metric.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Learning Automata and Genetic Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Learning Automata and Genetic Algorithms ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Learning Automata (LA) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** A *Learning Automaton* is a finite-state adaptive system that interacts with a stochastic environment by selecting actions from a finite set, observing a random reinforcement (penalty/reward) response from the environment, and using a learning algorithm to evolve its action-selection probability distribution toward the optimal action that minimizes expected penalty. **The Quintuple Mathematical Definition:** A Learning Automaton is formally defined as a 5-tuple: $$LA = \langle Q, \alpha, \beta, F, G \rangle$$ Where: - $Q$ = Finite set of internal states - $\alpha = \{\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \dots, \alpha_r\}$ = Finite set of $r$ actions - $\beta = \{0, 1, \dots, N\}$ = Set of environment reinforcement responses - $F: Q \times \beta \rightarrow Q$ = State transition function - $G: Q \rightarrow \alpha$ = Output function (next action selector) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Key Distinction:** A Learning Automaton is **NOT** a standard reinforcement learning agent in the modern deep-RL sense. It is a *probabilistic adaptive decision-maker* that operates without an explicit value function, instead evolving the **action probability vector** $P(n) = [p_1(n), p_2(n), \dots, p_r(n)]$ directly. --- ### 1.2 The Environment in LA The **environment** $E = \langle \alpha, \beta, c \rangle$ is characterized by the penalty probabilities: $$c_i = \Pr[\beta(n) = 1 \mid \alpha(n) = \alpha_i], \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, r$$ Where $c_i$ is the probability that action $\alpha_i$ will be penalized by the environment. **Environment Classification (P, Q, S models):** | Model | Response Set $\beta$ | Nature | |-------|----------------------|--------| | **P-Model** | $\beta \in \{0, 1\}$ | Binary (penalize / not penalize) | | **Q-Model** | $\beta \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, N\}$ | Finite multi-valued | | **S-Model** | $\beta \in [0, 1]$ | Continuous interval | > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Property:** If $c_i < c_j$, then action $\alpha_i$ is considered *better* than $\alpha_j$. The goal of the LA is to converge to the action with the **lowest penalty probability**. --- ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Slot Machine Gambler 🎰 Imagine a gambler at a row of **5 slot machines** (one-armed bandits) in a casino. Each machine has a *different unknown* payout probability, and the gambler does not know which is best. - The gambler pulls one lever at a time → this is the **action** $\alpha_i$. - The machine either pays or doesn't → this is the **reinforcement** $\beta$. - Over many pulls, the gambler mentally keeps track of *"Machine 3 seems to pay more often"* → this is the **action probability vector** $P(n)$. - Eventually, the gambler always pulls the best lever → the automaton has **converged**. The *Learning Automaton* is precisely this gambler, formalized mathematically. The slot machines represent the **stochastic environment**, and the gambler's strategy-update rule represents the **learning algorithm** $G$. --- ### 1.4 Genetic Algorithms (GA) — Definition **Formal Definition:** A *Genetic Algorithm* is a population-based metaheuristic optimization algorithm inspired by the mechanics of natural selection and biological evolution. It maintains a population of candidate solutions (called *chromosomes* or *individuals*), evaluates them using a *fitness function*, and iteratively applies stochastic operators — **selection**, **crossover**, and **mutation** — to evolve increasingly fit populations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag:** GAs belong to the broader class of *Evolutionary Computation (EC)* algorithms and are classified as **global search** metaheuristics (as opposed to local search methods like Hill Climbing). --- ### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — The Survival of the Fittest Species 🦎 Consider an island populated by **100 lizards** of varying sizes and colors. A predator (hawk) selectively catches the slowest, most visible ones. - **Population of 100 lizards** = GA population of candidate solutions - **Each lizard's traits** (size, color) = chromosome encoding - **Survival chance** = fitness score (slower visible lizards have low fitness) - **Predator** = selection pressure - **Offspring inheriting parent traits** = crossover (recombination) - **Random color change in offspring** = mutation After many generations, only fast, well-camouflaged lizards remain → the GA has **optimized** the population. The hawk represents the **fitness function**. > [!NOTE] > **Terminology Mapping for KTU Exams:** > - Lizard = **Individual / Chromosome** > - Trait gene = **Gene / Allele** > - Full DNA strand = **Genotype** > - Observable lizard = **Phenotype** > - Survival = **Fitness** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Architecture of a Learning Automaton System The closed-loop interaction between the **Automaton** and **Environment** proceeds as: 1. At time instant $n$, the automaton selects action $\alpha(n) = \alpha_i$ based on the probability distribution $P(n)$. 2. The environment receives $\alpha_i$ and emits reinforcement $\beta(n) \in \{0, 1\}$ (for P-model). 3. The automaton's learning algorithm $G$ updates $P(n) \rightarrow P(n+1)$. 4. Steps 1–3 repeat for $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ until convergence. --- ### 2.2 Classification of Learning Automata #### 2.2.1 Fixed Structure Stochastic Automata (FSSA) The transition graph structure is **fixed**; only probabilities of state transitions change over time. | Automaton Type | Action Set Size $r$ | Key Property | |----------------|--------------------:|--------------| | **Tsetlin** | 2 | States per action = 2N; symmetric, optimal | | **Krinsky** | 2 | Variant of Tsetlin; similar properties | | **Krylov** | $r$ | Multi-action generalization; more states | > [!NOTE] > FSSAs are **ε-optimal** (converge to optimal action with probability arbitrarily close to 1) but the state space grows rapidly with $r$, making them impractical for large action sets. #### 2.2.2 Variable Structure Stochastic Automata (VSSA) ⭐ (KTU High-Yield) The structure itself adapts. VSSAs are defined by the **action probability update rule**. **General Update Rule (Linear Reward-Penalty family):** If $\alpha(n) = \alpha_i$ and $\beta(n) = 0$ (reward): $$p_j(n+1) = p_j(n) - L \cdot p_j(n), \quad j \neq i$$ $$p_i(n+1) = p_i(n) + L \cdot \sum_{j \neq i} p_j(n)$$ If $\alpha(n) = \alpha_i$ and $\beta(n) = 1$ (penalty): $$p_j(n+1) = \frac{p_j(n)}{1 - a \cdot p_i(n)} \cdot \begin{cases} 1 - a, & j = i \\ 1, & j \neq i \end{cases}$$ Where $L, a \in [0,1]$ are learning parameters. **The Linear Reward-Inaction ($L_{RI}$) Scheme** — most commonly examined: If $\alpha(n) = \alpha_i$ **and** $\beta(n) = 0$: $$p_i(n+1) = p_i(n) + a \cdot [1 - p_i(n)]$$ $$p_j(n+1) = (1 - a) \cdot p_j(n), \quad j \neq i$$ If $\beta(n) = 1$: **NO update** (inaction on penalty). > [!IMPORTANT] > **$L_{RI}$ Convergence:** Guaranteed **ε-optimal** convergence; the penalty probability $c_i$ of each action can be estimated, and the algorithm converges to the action with the minimum $c_i$. **The Linear Reward-Penalty ($L_{RP}$) Scheme:** If $\beta(n) = 0$ (reward): same as $L_{RI}$ reward update. If $\beta(n) = 1$ (penalty): $$p_i(n+1) = (1 - b) \cdot p_i(n)$$ $$p_j(n+1) = \frac{b}{r-1} + (1 - b) \cdot p_j(n), \quad j \neq i$$ > [!WARNING] > $L_{RP}$ updates on **both** reward and penalty, making it more responsive but with slower convergence in stationary environments. $L_{RI}$ is preferred for stationary problems. --- ### 2.3 The Pursuit Algorithm (VSSA) ⭐⭐ (KTU Favourite) The **Pursuit Algorithm** is an **estimator-based** VSSA. It maintains: - **Action probability vector:** $P(n)$ - **Estimated penalty probabilities:** $\hat{D}(n) = [\hat{d}_1(n), \hat{d}_2(n), \dots, \hat{d}_r(n)]$ **Update Rule:** Let $i^* = \arg\min_j \hat{d}_j(n)$ (the currently estimated best action). For all $j \neq i^*$: $$p_j(n+1) = (1 - \lambda) \cdot p_j(n)$$ $$p_{i^*}(n+1) = 1 - \sum_{j \neq i^*} p_j(n+1)$$ And the estimator update: $$\hat{d}_i(n+1) = \hat{d}_i(n) + \frac{\beta(n) - \hat{d}_i(n)}{n_i(n)}$$ Where $n_i(n)$ is the count of times action $\alpha_i$ was selected. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Pursuit"?** The probability vector *pursues* (chases) the action that the current estimator deems best — hence the name. --- ### 2.4 Genetic Algorithm — Deep Theoretical Structure #### 2.4.1 Chromosome Encoding Schemes | Encoding | Use Case | Example | |----------|----------|---------| | **Binary** | Classical GA | `10110010` | | **Real-Valued** | Continuous optimization | `[3.14, 2.71, 1.62]$` | | **Permutation** | Ordering problems (TSP) | `[5, 2, 8, 1, 7]` | | **Tree-based (GP)** | Symbolic regression | `(+ (* x y) z)` | #### 2.4.2 The Canonical GA Algorithm (5 Phases) **Phase 1 — Initialization:** Generate $N$ random chromosomes (population size $N$). **Phase 2 — Fitness Evaluation:** For each chromosome $x_k$, compute $f(x_k)$. **Phase 3 — Selection:** Probabilistically choose parents based on fitness. Common methods: - **Roulette Wheel Selection:** $P[\text{select } x_k] = \frac{f(x_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f(x_j)}$ - **Tournament Selection:** Pick $k$ individuals, select the fittest. - **Rank-Based Selection:** Sort by fitness, select based on rank. **Phase 4 — Crossover (Recombination):** With probability $p_c$, combine two parents to produce offspring. Types: - **Single-Point Crossover** - **Two-Point Crossover** - **Uniform Crossover** **Phase 5 — Mutation:** With probability $p_m$ (small, typically $1/L$ where $L$ = chromosome length), randomly flip bits. **Replacement:** Form the next generation by elitism or generational replacement. --- ### 2.5 Holland's Schema Theorem ⭐⭐ (KTU Critical) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Schema Theorem (1975):** The central theorem explaining *why* GAs work. A **schema** $H$ is a template over the alphabet $\{0, 1, *\}$ where $*$ is a "don't care" symbol. For example, $H = 1**0$ matches both $1010$ and $1110$. **Schema Properties:** - **Order $o(H)$** = number of fixed (non-$*$) positions - **Defining length $\delta(H)$** = distance between the first and last fixed positions **The Schema Theorem Equation:** $$E[m(H, n+1)] \geq m(H, n) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$$ Where: - $m(H, n)$ = number of schema instances at generation $n$ - $f(H)$ = average fitness of schema instances - $\bar{f}$ = average fitness of population - $p_c$ = crossover probability - $p_m$ = mutation probability - $L$ = chromosome length **Interpretation:** Short, low-order, above-average fitness schemata (called **building blocks**) grow exponentially across generations. --- ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU 2024 EXAM TABLE** | # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---------|---------|----------------| | 1 | LA Action Probability Constraint | $\sum_{i=1}^{r} p_i(n) = 1$ | All probabilities sum to 1 | | 2 | Environment Penalty Probability | $c_i = \Pr[\beta=1 \mid \alpha=\alpha_i]$ | $c_i \in [0, 1]$ | | 3 | $L_{RI}$ Reward Update | $p_i(n+1) = p_i(n) + a[1 - p_i(n)]$ | $a \in (0, 1)$ = learning step | | 4 | $L_{RP}$ Penalty Update | $p_i(n+1) = (1-b) p_i(n)$ | $b \in (0, 1)$ = penalty step | | 5 | Pursuit Estimator Update | $\hat{d}_i(n+1) = \hat{d}_i(n) + \frac{\beta - \hat{d}_i}{n_i}$ | $n_i$ = selection count of $\alpha_i$ | | 6 | Pursuit Probability Update | $p_j(n+1) = (1-\lambda) p_j(n), \; j \neq i^*$ | $\lambda$ = learning rate | | 7 | GA Roulette Wheel Probability | $P_k = \frac{f(x_k)}{\sum_j f(x_j)}$ | Unitless probability | | 8 | Schema Theorem | $E[m(H,n+1)] \geq m(H,n) \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} [1 - p_c \frac{\delta}{L-1} - p_m o]$ | Bounded below | | 9 | Schema Order | $o(H) = $ count of fixed bits | $o(H) \in [0, L]$ | | 10 | Schema Defining Length | $\delta(H) = $ last fixed $-$ first fixed | $\delta(H) \in [0, L-1]$ | | 11 | Fitness Ratio | $E[m(H,n+1)] / m(H,n) \geq f(H)/\bar{f}$ | Selection pressure term | | 12 | Convergence Condition $L_{RI}$ | $\lim_{n \to \infty} p_m(n) = 1$ | $m$ = min-penalty action | --- ### 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where Learning Automata and GAs are deployed in real engineering systems:** - **LA Applications:** Adaptive routing in communication networks, cognitive radio spectrum sensing, intrusion detection systems, dynamic resource allocation in CPS, adaptive control of unknown plants, optimization in non-stationary environments. - **GA Applications:** Antenna design optimization, VLSI circuit layout, neural network weight training (neuroevolution), job-shop scheduling, vehicle routing, structural engineering optimization, hyperparameter tuning of ML models, CPS scheduling. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: $L_{RI}$ Update Rule Preserves Probability Axiom **Claim:** The Linear Reward-Inaction update rule for VSSA preserves the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{r} p_i(n) = 1$. **Setup:** At time $n$, let the automaton select $\alpha(n) = \alpha_i$ and receive $\beta(n) = 0$ (reward). The update rule is: $$p_i(n+1) = p_i(n) + a \cdot [1 - p_i(n)]$$ $$p_j(n+1) = (1 - a) \cdot p_j(n), \quad \forall j \neq i$$ **Proof:** We must show: $\sum_{k=1}^{r} p_k(n+1) = 1$. Separate the sum into the rewarded action $i$ and the rest: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{k=1}^{r} p_k(n+1) &= p_i(n+1) + \sum_{j \neq i} p_j(n+1) \\ &= \left\{ p_i(n) + a[1 - p_i(n)] \right\} + \sum_{j \neq i} (1 - a) p_j(n) \\ &= p_i(n) + a - a \cdot p_i(n) + (1 - a) \sum_{j \neq i} p_j(n) \\ &= p_i(n)(1 - a) + a + (1 - a) \sum_{j \neq i} p_j(n) \\ &= a + (1 - a) \left[ p_i(n) + \sum_{j \neq i} p_j(n) \right] \\ &= a + (1 - a) \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{r} p_k(n) \\ &= a + (1 - a) \cdot 1 \quad \text{(since probabilities sum to 1 at time } n \text{)} \\ &= a + 1 - a = 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** $\sum_{k=1}^{r} p_k(n+1) = 1$ holds at all time steps. The probability axiom is preserved. ∎ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Expected Change in $p_i$ for $L_{RI}$ Algorithm **Goal:** Compute $E[\Delta p_i(n)]$ where $\Delta p_i(n) = p_i(n+1) - p_i(n)$. **Case 1 — Action $\alpha_i$ is selected and rewarded ($\beta = 0$):** The action $\alpha_i$ is chosen with probability $p_i(n)$, and the probability of reward is $(1 - c_i)$. In this case: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta p_i(n) &= p_i(n+1) - p_i(n) \\ &= \left\{ p_i(n) + a[1 - p_i(n)] \right\} - p_i(n) \\ &= a[1 - p_i(n)] \end{aligned} $$ **Case 2 — Some other action $\alpha_j$ ($j \neq i$) is selected and rewarded:** Action $\alpha_j$ is chosen with probability $p_j(n)$, rewarded with probability $(1 - c_j)$. The change in $p_i$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta p_i(n) &= p_i(n+1) - p_i(n) \\ &= (1 - a) p_i(n) - p_i(n) \\ &= -a \cdot p_i(n) \end{aligned} $$ **Case 3 — Penalty ($\beta = 1$):** By definition of $L_{RI}$, *no update* occurs. So $\Delta p_i(n) = 0$. **Total Expected Change:** $$ \begin{aligned} E[\Delta p_i(n)] &= p_i(n)(1 - c_i) \cdot a[1 - p_i(n)] + \sum_{j \neq i} p_j(n)(1 - c_j) \cdot [-a p_i(n)] + 0 \\ &= a(1 - c_i) p_i(n)[1 - p_i(n)] - a p_i(n) \sum_{j \neq i} p_j(n)(1 - c_j) \\ &= a p_i(n) \left\{ (1 - c_i)[1 - p_i(n)] - \sum_{j \neq i} p_j(n)(1 - c_j) \right\} \end{aligned} $$ **Key Insight:** The expected change is positive when the term in braces is positive, which occurs when action $\alpha_i$'s reward probability exceeds the weighted average of the others — exactly the intuitive condition for "this action is better than average." --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — $L_{RI}$ Algorithm (P-model) **Problem:** An LA has $r = 3$ actions with true penalty probabilities $c_1 = 0.7$, $c_2 = 0.4$, $c_3 = 0.1$. Initial $P(0) = [0.5, 0.3, 0.2]$. Learning parameter $a = 0.1$. Simulate **5 steps** of $L_{RI}$ and observe probability evolution. **Step 1 (n=0 → n=1):** - Selected $\alpha_1$ (prob 0.5), environment returns $\beta = 1$ (penalty). - $L_{RI}$ on penalty → **no update**. $P(1) = [0.5, 0.3, 0.2]$. **Step 2 (n=1 → n=2):** - Selected $\alpha_2$ (prob 0.3), environment returns $\beta = 0$ (reward). - Update: $p_2(2) = 0.3 + 0.1(1 - 0.3) = 0.37$ - Update: $p_1(2) = 0.9 \times 0.5 = 0.45$, $p_3(2) = 0.9 \times 0.2 = 0.18$ - Verify: $0.45 + 0.37 + 0.18 = 1.0$ ✓ - $P(2) = [0.45, 0.37, 0.18]$. **Step 3 (n=2 → n=3):** - Selected $\alpha_3$ (prob 0.18), environment returns $\beta = 0$ (reward). - Update: $p_3(3) = 0.18 + 0.1(1 - 0.18) = 0.252$ - Update: $p_1(3) = 0.9 \times 0.45 = 0.405$, $p_2(3) = 0.9 \times 0.37 = 0.333$ - $P(3) = [0.405, 0.333, 0.252]$. **Step 4 (n=3 → n=4):** - Selected $\alpha_1$ (prob 0.405), environment returns $\beta = 1$ (penalty). - **No update** under $L_{RI}$. $P(4) = [0.405, 0.333, 0.252]$. **Step 5 (n=4 → n=5):** - Selected $\alpha_3$ (prob 0.252), environment returns $\beta = 0$ (reward). - Update: $p_3(5) = 0.252 + 0.1(1 - 0.252) = 0.3252$ - Update: $p_1(5) = 0.9 \times 0.405 = 0.3645$, $p_2(5) = 0.9 \times 0.333 = 0.2997$ - $P(5) = [0.3645, 0.2997, 0.3252]$. **Observed Trend:** The probability of $\alpha_3$ (best action, $c_3 = 0.1$) is monotonically increasing: $0.2 \to 0.18 \to 0.252 \to 0.252 \to 0.3252$. The algorithm is correctly identifying the best action. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of VSSA Variants | Criterion | $L_{RI}$ | $L_{RP}$ | Pursuit | |-----------|----------|----------|---------| | Updates on reward? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (estimate + prob) | | Updates on penalty? | ❌ No (inaction) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (estimate only) | | Convergence Speed (Stationary) | Fast | Slower | Fast | | Convergence in Non-Stationary | Slow (lags) | Better (adapts) | Best | | Memory Requirement | Stores $P(n)$ | Stores $P(n)$ | Stores $P(n)$ + $\hat{D}(n)$ | | Parameter Sensitivity | Low | Medium | Medium | | ε-Optimality | ✅ Proven | ✅ Proven | ✅ Proven | | KTU Exam Frequency | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | --- ### 3.5 Schema Theorem — Term-by-Term Interpretation The Schema Theorem: $$E[m(H, n+1)] \geq m(H, n) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$$ **Term 1 — Selection Multiplier $\frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}$:** - If $f(H) > \bar{f}$ (above-average fitness) → multiplier $> 1$ → schema **grows**. - If $f(H) < \bar{f}$ → multiplier $< 1$ → schema **decays**. **Term 2 — Crossover Survival $1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1}$:** - Long defining length $\delta(H)$ → high disruption probability under single-point crossover. - Short schemata survive crossover better. **Term 3 — Mutation Survival $1 - p_m \cdot o(H)$:** - Higher order $o(H)$ → more fixed positions → more likely to be flipped by mutation. - Low-order schemata are mutation-robust. **Net Effect:** GA implicitly **exponentially samples** schemata with *short defining length*, *low order*, and *above-average fitness* — these are the **building blocks** of good solutions. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Learning Automaton — Closed-Loop Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["AUTOMATON
Action Selector +
Probability Vector Pn"] -->|"Selects action alpha i"| B["ENVIRONMENT
Stochastic
System"] B -->|"Reinforcement beta n
= 0 or 1"| C["REINFORCEMENT
SIGNAL
PROCESSOR"] C -->|"Updates P n to P n+1"| A B -->|"Penalty Probability ci"| D["HIDDEN
STATE
(unknown to LA)"] D -.-> B A -->|"Epsilon Optimal
Convergence"| E["OPTIMAL
ACTION
alpha m"] ``` ### 4.2 Genetic Algorithm — Complete Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD P0["Initialize Population P0
N random chromosomes"] --> FE["Fitness Evaluation
f xk for each individual"] FE --> SEL{"Selection Operator
Roulette or Tournament"} SEL --> XOV["Crossover Operator
Pc probability
Single point or Uniform"] XOV --> MUT["Mutation Operator
Pm probability
Bit flip"] MUT --> NEW["New Generation P n+1"] NEW -->|"Termination
Check"| T{"Max Generations
OR Convergence?"} T -->|No| FE T -->|Yes| SOL["Optimal or Near Optimal
Solution"] ``` ### 4.3 Hierarchy of Learning Automata Types ```mermaid flowchart TD LA["Learning Automata LA"] --> FSSA["Fixed Structure
Stochastic Automata FSSA"] LA --> VSSA["Variable Structure
Stochastic Automata VSSA"] FSSA --> T1["Tsetlin
r = 2"] FSSA --> T2["Krinsky
r = 2"] FSSA --> T3["Krylov
r greater than 2"] VSSA --> V1["LRI
Linear Reward Inaction"] VSSA --> V2["LRP
Linear Reward Penalty"] VSSA --> V3["Pursuit Algorithm
Estimator Based"] VSSA --> V4["Discretized LA
DLELA"] VSSA --> V5["Continuous LA
GELEA"] ``` ### 4.4 GA Operator Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PARENTS["Parent Pool"] PA["Parent 1
10110010"] PB["Parent 2
01011101"] end PA --> X["Single Point Crossover
Split at position 4"] PB --> X X --> CA["Offspring 1
10110101"] X --> CB["Offspring 2
01011010"] CA --> MU1["Mutation
Flip bit 2"] CB --> MU2["Mutation
Flip bit 6"] MU1 --> OFF1["Final Offspring 1
11110101"] MU2 --> OFF2["Final Offspring 2
01011000"] ``` ### 4.5 Schema Theorem — Visual Building Block Concept ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Schema H1 = 1 * * * 0
Order 2 Delta 4
High Fitness"] --> BB["BUILDING BLOCKS
Short Delta and
Low Order and
Above Average Fitness"] S2["Schema H2 = * * 1 0
Order 2 Delta 2
High Fitness"] --> BB S3["Schema H3 = 0 0 * * *
Order 2 Delta 1
Medium Fitness"] --> BB BB --> EXP["Exponentially Growing
across generations"] S4["Long High Order
Low Fitness Schema"] --> DEC["Decaying
disrupted by crossover and mutation"] EXP --> SOL["Optimal Solution Emerges"] ``` ### 4.6 Closed-Loop CPS Perspective ```mermaid flowchart LR CPS["Cognitive Physical System
CPS"] --> SENS["Sensors"] SENS --> LA["Learning Automaton
Decision Module"] LA --> ACT["Actuators"] ACT --> PHYS["Physical Process"] PHYS --> SENS LA -.->|"GA optimizes
LA parameters"| GA["Genetic Algorithm
Tuner"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1: Define Learning Automaton. Explain the P-model and Q-model environments.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **Learning Automaton (LA)** is a finite-state machine that interacts with a stochastic environment and learns the optimal action through a reinforcement learning mechanism. It is formally a 5-tuple $\langle Q, \alpha, \beta, F, G \rangle$. - **P-model environment:** The reinforcement response $\beta \in \{0, 1\}$ is binary. Output 0 indicates reward (no penalty); output 1 indicates penalty. It is the simplest model used in classical LA theory. - **Q-model environment:** The reinforcement response $\beta \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, N\}$ takes one of $N+1$ discrete values, allowing finer-grained feedback (e.g., graded penalties). The P-model is sufficient for stationary binary-reward problems; the Q-model is used when the environment can convey multi-level feedback. --- #### **Question 2: What is a schema in Genetic Algorithms? Define its order and defining length with an example.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** A **schema** is a similarity template defined over the ternary alphabet $\{0, 1, *\}$, where $*$ is a "don't care" symbol matching either 0 or 1. A schema represents a *hyperplane* in the solution space containing all chromosomes that match its fixed positions. - **Order $o(H)$:** Number of fixed (non-$*$) positions. Example: For $H = 1*0**$, $o(H) = 2$. - **Defining length $\delta(H)$:** Distance between the leftmost and rightmost fixed positions. For $H = 1*0**$, the fixed positions are 1 and 3, so $\delta(H) = 3 - 1 = 2$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A: Learning Automata** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a Learning Automaton with a neat block diagram. Define VSSA and derive the update equations of the $L_{RI}$ scheme for the P-model. **Model Solution:** **Architecture (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 diagram. The system consists of an **Automaton** (holding the action probability vector $P(n)$) and an **Environment** (which provides reinforcement $\beta(n)$ based on hidden penalty probabilities $c_i$). The cycle is: action selection → environment response → probability update. **VSSA Definition (1 mark):** A Variable Structure Stochastic Automaton is one in which the state transition probabilities and the action selection probabilities are functions of the reinforcement signal. The structure itself changes as learning progresses. **$L_{RI}$ Update Derivation (3 marks):** Let the automaton have $r$ actions with current probability vector $P(n) = [p_1, p_2, \dots, p_r]$. Suppose action $\alpha_i$ is selected and receives $\beta(n) = 0$ (reward). To **reward** $\alpha_i$, increase its probability; to **decrease** the others proportionally: $$ \begin{aligned} p_i(n+1) &= p_i(n) + a \cdot [1 - p_i(n)] \\ p_j(n+1) &= (1 - a) \cdot p_j(n), \quad \forall j \neq i \end{aligned} $$ Where $a \in (0, 1)$ is the learning step size. If $\beta(n) = 1$ (penalty), the algorithm takes **no action** (hence "Inaction"). The proof that $\sum p_i = 1$ is preserved was shown in Section 3.1. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Pursuit Algorithm** in detail. How does it differ from $L_{RI}$? Show that it is ε-optimal. **Model Solution:** **Pursuit Algorithm Description (4 marks):** The Pursuit Algorithm maintains two structures: - $P(n) = [p_1(n), \dots, p_r(n)]$ — action probability vector - $\hat{D}(n) = [\hat{d}_1(n), \dots, \hat{d}_r(n)]$ — empirical estimator of penalty probabilities **Steps:** 1. Select action $\alpha(n) = \alpha_i$ according to $P(n)$. 2. Observe $\beta(n)$ and update the estimator: $\hat{d}_i(n+1) = \hat{d}_i(n) + \frac{\beta(n) - \hat{d}_i(n)}{n_i(n)}$. 3. Identify the *currently estimated best action*: $i^*(n) = \arg\min_j \hat{d}_j(n+1)$. 4. "Pursue" this best action by updating $P(n+1)$: $$ \begin{aligned} p_j(n+1) &= (1 - \lambda) p_j(n), \quad j \neq i^* \\ p_{i^*}(n+1) &= 1 - \sum_{j \neq i^*} p_j(n+1) \end{aligned} $$ **Differences from $L_{RI}$ (2 marks):** | Aspect | $L_{RI}$ | Pursuit | |--------|----------|---------| | Estimator | None (direct update) | Maintains $\hat{D}(n)$ | | Update on Penalty | No update | Updates estimator only | | Adaptability to Non-Stationary | Slow (no learning from penalties) | Fast (estimator tracks changes) | | Memory | $r$ values | $2r$ values | **ε-Optimality (1 mark):** Thathachar and Sastry (1986) proved that the Pursuit algorithm is ε-optimal: by choosing a sufficiently small learning parameter $\lambda$, the algorithm can be made to converge to the optimal action with probability arbitrarily close to 1. --- #### **Question B: Genetic Algorithms** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the fundamental operators of a Genetic Algorithm: Selection, Crossover, and Mutation. Compare Roulette Wheel and Tournament selection. **Model Solution:** **Selection (2.5 marks):** Selection mimics "survival of the fittest." Higher-fitness individuals have a higher probability of being chosen as parents. **Roulette Wheel** assigns probability proportional to fitness: $P_k = f(x_k) / \sum_j f(x_j)$. **Tournament Selection** picks $k$ individuals at random and selects the fittest among them; this is repeated until the parent pool is filled. **Crossover (2.5 marks):** Crossover combines two parent chromosomes to produce offspring. In **single-point crossover**, a cut point is chosen, and bits beyond the cut are swapped. Example: parents `101|1001` and `011|0110` produce offspring `1010110` and `0111001`. **Two-point crossover** uses two cut points. **Uniform crossover** swaps each bit independently with probability 0.5. **Mutation (1 mark):** Mutation flips each bit with a small probability $p_m$ (typically $1/L$ where $L$ is chromosome length). Mutation maintains **genetic diversity** and prevents premature convergence to local optima. **Comparison (1 mark):** Roulette Wheel is simple but suffers from *fitness scaling* issues (a few super-fit individuals dominate); Tournament is more robust to scaling and is preferred in modern GAs. **(b) [7 Marks]** State and explain Holland's Schema Theorem. Discuss its significance and limitations. **Model Solution:** **Statement (2 marks):** The Schema Theorem states that the expected number of instances of a schema $H$ in generation $n+1$ is bounded below by: $$E[m(H, n+1)] \geq m(H, n) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$$ **Explanation (3 marks):** - The **selection term** $f(H)/\bar{f}$ shows that above-average-fitness schemata grow exponentially. - The **crossover term** $[1 - p_c \delta(H)/(L-1)]$ shows that short-defining-length schemata are less likely to be disrupted. - The **mutation term** $[1 - p_m o(H)]$ shows that low-order schemata are robust to bit flips. - Combined: GAs **implicitly parallel-process** an enormous number of schemata (the "**implicit parallelism**" — approximately $N^3$ schemata per generation, where $N$ is population size). **Significance (1 mark):** The Schema Theorem provides the **theoretical foundation** for why GAs work. It formalizes the intuition that GAs discover good solutions by combining *building blocks* — short, low-order, high-fitness schemata. **Limitations (1 mark):** - Applies to **proportionate selection** only; not directly applicable to tournament selection. - Holds for **infinite populations**; finite populations introduce sampling noise. - Assumes **non-overlapping** schemata and **no epistasis** (gene-gene interactions). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Learn From Past Mistakes:** > > 1. **Confusing $L_{RI}$ with $L_{RP}$:** Many students write the penalty update under $L_{RI}$ — but by definition, $L_{RI}$ does *no* update on penalty. Mixing them loses **2–3 marks** instantly. > > 2. **Forgetting the probability normalization:** When presenting the $L_{RI}$ update, students often forget to verify that the probabilities sum to 1 after the update. The derivation in Section 3.1 is what examiners expect. Missing this loses **1–2 marks**. > > 3. **Misinterpreting "Pursuit":** Students often think "Pursuit" means the algorithm chases the highest-reward action via the probability vector *only*. They forget the **estimator $\hat{D}(n)$** is the actual driver. Always mention the estimator. > > 4. **Schema Theorem — citing the formula without explaining terms:** Writing $E[m(H, n+1)] \geq \dots$ without explaining $f(H), \bar{f}, \delta(H), o(H)$ is treated as **incomplete** and loses **up to 3 marks**. > > 5. **Confusing schema order with defining length:** $o(H)$ is the *count* of fixed bits; $\delta(H)$ is the *distance* between first and last fixed bits. Easy to swap. > > 6. **Omitting ε-optimality:** When asked about VSSA convergence, students often say "it converges" without stating that it is **ε-optimal** (converges to the optimal action with probability $> 1 - \varepsilon$). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋 - A **Learning Automaton** is a tuple $\langle Q, \alpha, \beta, F, G \rangle$; it learns the optimal action by evolving $P(n)$. - **P-model** uses binary $\beta \in \{0, 1\}$; **Q-model** uses multi-valued; **S-model** uses continuous. - **FSSA** has fixed transition graph (Tsetlin, Krinsky, Krylov); **VSSA** adapts its structure. - **$L_{RI}$** updates only on reward; **$L_{RP}$** updates on both reward and penalty. - **Pursuit Algorithm** uses an explicit estimator $\hat{D}(n)$ to track the best action and updates $P(n)$ to chase it. - All classical VSSAs are **ε-optimal** — they converge to the optimal action with probability $\geq 1 - \varepsilon$ for arbitrarily small $\varepsilon > 0$. - A **Genetic Algorithm** is a population-based metaheuristic using **selection, crossover, mutation** to evolve solutions. - **Encoding types:** binary, real-valued, permutation, tree-based. - **Selection methods:** Roulette Wheel (proportional), Tournament (k-way), Rank-based. - **Crossover types:** single-point, two-point, uniform. - **Schema Theorem:** Above-average, short, low-order schemata (building blocks) grow exponentially. - **Implicit parallelism:** GAs process $\approx N^3$ schemata per generation (Holland). - **Fitness function** drives selection pressure; **mutation** maintains diversity and prevents premature convergence. - Both LA and GA are **model-free, gradient-free** optimization methods, ideal for CPS applications where system models are uncertain or non-stationary. > [!NOTE] > **One-Line Memory Aid for the Exam Hall:** > *"**LA** = *learns* actions by *pursuit* of low penalty; **GA** = *evolves* populations by *building* high-fitness schemas."*

topics.title: Learning by Induction: LEX
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Learning by Induction: LEX

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LEX (Learn from EXamples / Learned Experience)** is a classical **symbolic inductive learning system** developed by **Tom M. Mitchell (Stanford University, ~1983)** that automatically acquires **heuristic search-control rules** for the problem domain of **symbolic integration in calculus**, by observing its own problem-solving traces and generalizing them into generally-applicable rules.

Formally stated within the KTU 2024 paradigm of Cognitive Systems and AI in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS):

> **LEX ≡ ⟨PG, PS, C, G, O⟩**
> where **PG** = Problem Generator, **PS** = Problem Solver (the *APPLY* engine), **C** = Critic, **G** = Generalizer (Variant Space learner), and **O** = Operationality Criterion.

It is the prototypical example of a system that performs **knowledge-intensive inductive learning** — using background knowledge to constrain and guide generalization, rather than learning from raw sensory data.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Calculus Tutor Bot" 🍎

Imagine a high school student learning integration:

- They are given a textbook of integration problems, but the chapter on *techniques* (substitution, parts, partial fractions) is torn out.
- A teacher (the **Critic**) watches them work through problems and says: *"Good — using substitution worked here. Bad — using parts on this one led to nowhere."*
- After each attempt, the student notes down the **pattern** of the problem: *"Ah, whenever I see a function nested inside another, with the derivative sitting nearby, I should try u-substitution."*
- Over time, the student **distils these one-off experiences into reusable heuristics** stored in a personal rule-book.

LEX is precisely that student — except the rule-book is a *version space* (a set of all plausible rules) maintained by a *candidate-elimination* algorithm, and the "textbook" is generated synthetically.

## 1.3 Why LEX Matters in Cognitive CPS

| Property | Significance for CPS |
|---|---|
| Inductive generalization | Mirrors how an embedded agent **learns control policies** from limited trajectories in physical environments. |
| Version-space reasoning | Equivalent to **symbolic world-model refinement** under uncertainty in CPS decision modules. |
| Operationality bias | Analogous to deploying only **executable, low-latency rules** onto a real-time CPS controller. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag:** This topic falls under **Module 4 – Learning Approaches in Cognitive Systems**, and directly maps to **CO4**: *Apply inductive and analytical learning techniques to model decision-making in CPS.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Problem-Space Formulation of Symbolic Integration

LEX defines the integration task as a **state-space search problem**:

- **State Space $\mathcal{S}$:** Set of all integration expressions (e.g., $\int x \cos(x^2)\,dx$).
- **Initial State $s_0$:** The given integral.
- **Goal State $s_g$:** A *primitive* integral that is recognizable as a standard form.
- **Operators $\mathcal{O}$:** Algebraic transformations, substitution $u = g(x)$, integration by parts, partial fractions, trigonometric identities.
- **Path Cost:** Number of operator applications.

The classical integration program **APPLY** (a forward-chaining LISP interpreter) searches this space using **existing (human-supplied) heuristics** $h_1, h_2, \dots, h_n$.

## 2.2 The Four Architectural Components of LEX

### 2.2.1 Problem Generator (PG)
Randomly synthesizes a new integration problem of the form
$$\int f(x)\,dx$$
where $f(x)$ belongs to a pre-specified **grammar of integrands** (polynomials, trig functions, exponentials, products, compositions).

### 2.2.2 Problem Solver (PS) — The *APPLY* Engine
- Uses a depth-first, ordered search over the operator set $\mathcal{O}$.
- At each state, the **operationalization** of a heuristic $h_i$ is attempted.
- Produces a **search trace** $\tau = (s_0 \xrightarrow{h_i} s_1 \xrightarrow{h_j} \dots \xrightarrow{h_k} s_g)$.

### 2.2.3 Critic (C) — The Oracle
Given the trace $\tau$ and the known solution, the Critic identifies the **operator sequence** that actually advanced the solution. It labels:
- **Positive Traces:** States in which operator $h_i$ was *correctly* applied.
- **Negative Traces:** States in which $h_i$ was *incorrectly* applied (or could have been applied but failed).

### 2.2.4 Generalizer (G) — The Inductive Core
- Takes the positive/negative examples of $h_i$'s applicability.
- Maintains a **version space** $\mathcal{V}_i \subseteq \mathcal{H}$ of all hypotheses about *when* $h_i$ should be triggered.
- Uses the **Candidate-Elimination Algorithm** to refine $\mathcal{V}_i$ after each new example.
- Persists a **most-general** and a **most-specific** boundary: $\mathcal{V}_i = [S, G]$, where $S \preceq G$ in the partial order of hypothesis generality.

## 2.3 The Operationality Criterion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operationality** — A learned concept is *operational* iff it is **efficiently recognizable** in the working memory of the problem solver AND **directly usable** to choose the next operator.

In LEX, the *operationality criterion* prevents the system from learning abstract features (e.g., "mathematical beauty") that cannot be syntactically matched against a candidate integral.

Mathematically, for a hypothesis $h$, the criterion demands:
$$\text{cost-match}(h) \leq \epsilon \quad \wedge \quad \text{coverage}(h) \geq \delta$$
where $\epsilon$ and $\delta$ are configurable thresholds.

## 2.4 The Inductive Bias of LEX

> [!NOTE]
> **LEX's inductive bias:** *"The desired heuristic is a conjunction of pre-defined operational feature tests."*

This is a **representation bias** (a restriction of the hypothesis language to conjunctive concepts) and a **preference bias** (favoring the *most general* hypothesis consistent with the data). This bias is what makes generalization tractable given very few examples.

## 2.5 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Operational Note** |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{H}$ | Hypothesis space of all candidate heuristics | Conjunctions of feature tests |
| $\mathcal{V}_i = [S, G]$ | Version space for heuristic $h_i$ | Bounded by specific $S$ and general $G$ sets |
| $S$ (Specific boundary) | Most specific consistent hypotheses | Generalized only on positive examples |
| $G$ (General boundary) | Most general consistent hypotheses | Specialized only on negative examples |
| $\succeq$ | "More general than" partial order | $g \succeq s$ iff $g$ covers $s$ |
| $\text{covers}(h, e)$ | True iff hypothesis $h$ is satisfied by example $e$ | Used in consistency check |
| Operationality | Learnable concept must be efficiently matchable | Limits hypothesis language |
| Inductive Bias | Conjunctive-form restriction of $\mathcal{H}$ | Permits tractable learning from $\leq 5$ examples |
| Single-Example Generalization | Each positive trace updates $S$ immediately | Distinguishes LEX from batch learners |
| Background Knowledge $B$ | Set of pre-defined operational features | Acts as the *language* of $h$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walk-Throughs

## 3.1 The Candidate-Elimination Algorithm: Formal Steps

Given hypothesis space $\mathcal{H}$ partially ordered by $\succeq$ ("more general than"), and a stream of training examples $E$:

**Initialize:**
$$\begin{aligned}
S_0 &\leftarrow \{\text{most specific hypothesis in } \mathcal{H}\} \\
G_0 &\leftarrow \{\text{most general hypothesis in } \mathcal{H}\}
\end{aligned}$$

**For each training example $e \in E$:**

**Case 1 — $e$ is a POSITIVE example:**
$$\begin{aligned}
G &\leftarrow \{g \in G \mid \text{covers}(g, e)\} \quad &\text{(remove inconsistent generals)} \\
S &\leftarrow \text{min\_generalizations}(S \cup \{e\}) \quad &\text{w.r.t. } G
\end{aligned}$$

**Case 2 — $e$ is a NEGATIVE example:**
$$\begin{aligned}
S &\leftarrow \{s \in S \mid \neg \text{covers}(s, e)\} \quad &\text{(remove inconsistent specifics)} \\
G &\leftarrow \text{min\_specializations}(G \cup \{e\}) \quad &\text{w.r.t. } S
\end{aligned}$$

**Termination:** When $S = G$ (single hypothesis found) or $\mathcal{V} = \emptyset$ (inconsistent data) or no new examples remain.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Learning the *u*-Substitution Heuristic in LEX

This is the canonical worked example from Mitchell (1983). LEX is learning the heuristic:
> *"Try the substitution $u = g(x)$ when the integrand has the form $F(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$."*

### 3.2.1 Defining the Hypothesis Space

The operational features recognized by LEX for this heuristic are:

| **Feature** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|
| $f_1$ | The integrand contains a function $F$ of some inner expression $g(x)$ |
| $f_2$ | The integrand contains a multiplicative factor resembling $g'(x)$ |
| $f_3$ | The outer function $F$ is a trigonometric function ($\sin$ or $\cos$) |
| $f_4$ | The integrand is a polynomial times a non-polynomial |

Each hypothesis is a 4-tuple $(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4)$ where $x_i \in \{\text{True}, \text{False}, \emptyset\}$ and $\emptyset$ denotes "feature absent / irrelevant."

### 3.2.2 Positive and Negative Traces from APPLY

**Positive Example 1:** $E^+_1 = \int x \cos(x^2)\,dx$
Features observed: $f_1 = T$, $f_2 = T$, $f_3 = T$, $f_4 = T$.

**Negative Example 1:** $E^-_1 = \int x^2 + 5x + 7\,dx$
Features observed: $f_1 = F$, $f_2 = F$, $f_3 = F$, $f_4 = F$.

**Positive Example 2:** $E^+_2 = \int 2x \sin(x^2)\,dx$
Features observed: $f_1 = T$, $f_2 = T$, $f_3 = T$, $f_4 = T$.

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Generalization

**Step 1 — Initialize Version Space:**
$$\begin{aligned}
S_0 &= (F, F, F, F) \\
G_0 &= (T, T, T, T)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Process $E^+_1 = (T, T, T, T)$:**

Since $E^+_1$ is positive, $S$ must be generalized to cover it, but no more than $G$ allows.

$$\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \text{min\_generalizations of } (F, F, F, F) \text{ that cover } (T, T, T, T) \\
    &= (T, T, T, T)
\end{aligned}$$

$G$ is unchanged (still covers $E^+_1$):
$$G_1 = (T, T, T, T)$$

At this point, the version space contains only the all-True hypothesis, which is **too general** (it would also suggest substitution for $\int x \sin(x)\,dx$ where it might not apply).

**Step 3 — Process $E^-_1 = (F, F, F, F)$:**

Since $E^-_1$ is negative, $G$ must be specialized to exclude it, but no more than $S$ allows.

Identify the minimum specializations of $(T, T, T, T)$ that:
- Do **not** cover $(F, F, F, F)$, and
- Are **more specific** than $S_1 = (T, T, T, T)$ (automatically true).

The minimum specialization is to flip each True to False (in any combination) — but to retain coverage of $E^+_1 = (T, T, T, T)$, we must **keep at least one** feature True. The minimal hypotheses that exclude the negative example and cover the positive example are:

$$\begin{aligned}
G_2 = \{\, &(F, T, T, T), \\
&(T, F, T, T), \\
&(T, T, F, T), \\
&(T, T, T, F) \,\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Process $E^+_2 = (T, T, T, T)$:**

The positive example must be covered by every hypothesis in $G$. We specialize $G$ minimally to those hypotheses that also cover $E^+_2$:

$$G_3 = (T, T, T, T)$$

Re-checking: every member of $G_2$ covers $E^+_2 = (T, T, T, T)$ since each has at least one True in the positions where $E^+_2$ is True. So $G$ remains as the 4-element set, while:

$$S_1 = (T, T, T, T)$$

**Step 5 — Final Version Space:**

$$\mathcal{V} = [S_1, G_2]$$

This represents **4 candidate rules**. The system's *preference bias* selects the most general among them — for instance $(T, T, F, T)$ — yielding the learned heuristic:

> *"If the integrand contains an outer function with a derivative-like factor AND is a polynomial times non-polynomial, try $u = g(x)$."*

This is exactly the kind of *operational, conjunctive* rule the operationality criterion demands.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: General-to-Specific Ordering

For the boolean feature tuple $(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4)$ with $x_i \in \{T, F\}$, the generality relation is defined as:

$$h_j \succeq h_k \iff \forall i: \; h_j[i] = T \;\Rightarrow\; h_k[i] = T$$

That is, $h_j$ is **at least as general** as $h_k$ if every feature that $h_j$ requires to be True, $h_k$ also requires to be True. The partial order forms a **Boolean lattice**.

The *minimum generalization* operation, given a hypothesis $h$ that does not cover a positive example $e^+$, is:
$$\text{min\_gen}(h, e^+) = \big\{ h' \in \mathcal{H} \;\big|\; h' \succeq h, \; \text{covers}(h', e^+), \; \nexists h'' : h \prec h'' \prec h' \big\}$$

The dual *minimum specialization* operation is defined symmetrically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A common derivation error is to forget that **background knowledge $B$** (the operational features) is the *only thing* that determines the lattice structure. Change $B$ and you change the version space — even with identical training data.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LEX System Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PG[Problem Generator]
    PS[Problem Solver APPLY Engine]
    C[Critic Oracle]
    G[Generalizer Candidate Elimination]
    KB[(Operational Feature Library)]
    VS[(Version Space Vi for Heuristic hi)]
    TR[(Search Trace Tau)]
    HR[(Heuristic Rule Book)]

    PG -->|synthetic integral| PS
    PS -->|uses| KB
    PS -->|emits trace| TR
    TR --> C
    C -->|positive and negative examples| G
    G -->|consults| KB
    G -->|refines| VS
    VS -->|committed rule| HR
    HR -->|updated heuristic set| PS

    classDef agent fill:#1f3a68,stroke:#0a1f3d,color:#ffffff
    classDef store fill:#4a7a3a,stroke:#1f3a1f,color:#ffffff
    classDef flow fill:#a36a1a,stroke:#5a3a0a,color:#ffffff

    class PG,PS,C,G agent
    class KB,VS,TR,HR store
```

## 4.2 Learning Loop Flow — Data Movement Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Version Space V0 = S0 G0] --> B[Problem Generator PG proposes new integral]
    B --> C[APPLY engine attempts solution using current heuristics]
    C --> D{Solution found?}
    D -->|No| E[Re-pose problem with different heuristic ordering]
    E --> C
    D -->|Yes| F[Critic compares APPLY trace to canonical solution]
    F --> G[Label operator applications as positive or negative]
    G --> H[Generalizer G applies Candidate Elimination step]
    H --> I{Version space collapsed or converged?}
    I -->|No| B
    I -->|Yes| J[Commit most general consistent rule to Rule Book]
    J --> K[Restart loop for next heuristic hi+1]

    classDef init fill:#2a4a7a,stroke:#0a1f3d,color:#ffffff
    classDef process fill:#7a2a4a,stroke:#3a0a1f,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#a37a2a,stroke:#5a3a0a,color:#ffffff
    classDef terminal fill:#3a7a2a,stroke:#1f3a0a,color:#ffffff

    class A init
    class B,C,E,F,G,H process
    class D,I decision
    class J,K terminal
```

## 4.3 Version-Space Convergence Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph init[Initial State]
        S0[S0 specific point]
        G0[G0 general point]
    end
    subgraph step1[After E positive 1]
        S1[S1 covers E positive 1]
        G1[G1 covers E positive 1]
    end
    subgraph step2[After E negative 1]
        S2[S2 unchanged]
        G2[G2 specialized set of 4 candidates]
    end
    subgraph final[Converged Rule]
        Sfinal[S final specific]
        Gfinal[G final general]
    end

    S0 --> S1 --> S2 --> Sfinal
    G0 --> G1 --> G2 --> Gfinal
    Sfinal -->|S equals G selects| Rule((Learned Heuristic))
    Gfinal --> Rule

    classDef state fill:#1f4a7a,stroke:#0a1f3d,color:#ffffff
    classDef result fill:#a31f4a,stroke:#5a0a1f,color:#ffffff
    class S0,G0,S1,G1,S2,G2,Sfinal,Gfinal state
    class Rule result
```

## 4.4 Hypothesis Lattice for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP((T T T T)) --> A1((F T T T))
    TOP --> A2((T F T T))
    TOP --> A3((T T F T))
    TOP --> A4((T T T F))
    A1 --> B1((F F T T))
    A1 --> B2((F T F T))
    A1 --> B3((F T T F))
    A2 --> B4((T F F T))
    A2 --> B5((T F T F))
    A3 --> B6((T T F F))
    A4 --> B7((F T T F))
    A4 --> B8((T F T F))
    A3 --> B9((T F T F))
```
