```mermaid
    A2 --> B10((F F T T))
    B1 --> BOT((F F F F))
    B2 --> BOT
    B3 --> BOT
    B4 --> BOT
    B5 --> BOT
    B6 --> BOT
    B7 --> BOT
    B8 --> BOT
    B9 --> BOT
    B10 --> BOT

    classDef apex fill:#1f4a7a,stroke:#0a1f3d,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid fill:#4a7a1f,stroke:#1f3a0a,color:#ffffff
    classDef base fill:#7a1f4a,stroke:#3a0a1f,color:#ffffff
    class TOP apex
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,B6,B7,B8,B9,B10 mid
    class BOT base
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Lattice:** Each node is a 4-tuple hypothesis; an edge $h \to h'$ means $h \succeq h'$ (parent is more general). The **version space** after training is the rectangular sub-lattice bounded by $S_1$ (bottom-most hypothesis $(T,T,T,T)$) and the four children $A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4$ — exactly matching the analytic derivation in Section 3.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the **operationality criterion** in the context of the LEX system. Why is it considered an essential component of inductive learning?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The operationality criterion states that a learned concept or rule is *operational* if it can be **efficiently recognized** in the working memory of the problem solver and **directly applied** to select the next operator. In LEX, this criterion prevents the Generalizer from producing rules involving abstract or non-computable features (e.g., "mathematical elegance") that APPLY cannot match against a candidate integrand. It ensures that every acquired heuristic is **executable** by the problem solver, making learning **useful** rather than merely *correct* in the abstract. Without this criterion, the system could converge on a hypothesis that is theoretically consistent with the data but practically unusable — defeating the purpose of inductive learning in a cognitive agent. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** What is **inductive bias**? State the specific inductive bias of the LEX system and explain its effect on generalization.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Inductive bias is the set of **assumptions** a learner makes to generalize beyond the observed training data. Without an inductive bias, learning from finite examples is logically under-determined. LEX's bias is twofold: (a) a **representation bias** that the target heuristic is expressible as a **conjunction of operational features**, and (b) a **preference bias** that, among consistent hypotheses, the system prefers the **most general** one. The effect is that LEX can generalize from as few as one or two positive examples, because the bias drastically shrinks the version space, forcing the $S$ and $G$ boundaries to converge quickly. The trade-off is that LEX cannot learn heuristics whose form is **disjunctive** or **hierarchical** without extending the hypothesis language. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Module 4, 14 Marks]**

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of the **LEX system**. Explain the role of each of its four components in the inductive learning process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hypothesis space contains 3 boolean features $f_1, f_2, f_3$. Training examples are received in the order:
$E^+_1 = (T, T, F)$, $E^-_1 = (T, F, F)$, $E^+_2 = (T, T, T)$. Apply the **Candidate-Elimination Algorithm** step by step to find the final version space $[S, G]$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Architecture of LEX (7 marks):**

The LEX system has four interacting modules, organized in a closed learning loop:

1. **Problem Generator (PG):** Produces synthetic integration problems of the form $\int f(x)\,dx$ by sampling from a grammar of integrand expressions (polynomials, trig, exponentials, products, compositions). It is the *source of training experience*. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Problem Solver (PS) — the APPLY engine:** A forward-chaining LISP-based search program that attempts to solve the given integral by applying existing heuristics $h_1, h_2, \dots, h_n$ to the current state. It generates a complete **search trace** $\tau$ recording each state and the operator applied. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Critic (C) — the oracle:** Compares the APPLY trace with the canonical (known) solution path. It labels each operator application in $\tau$ as a **positive example** (correctly advanced the solution) or a **negative example** (wrong choice). The Critic is the only place where **background knowledge of the correct answer** enters. **(2 marks)**

4. **Generalizer (G):** Takes the labelled positive and negative examples, maintains a **version space** $\mathcal{V}_i$ for each heuristic $h_i$, and applies the **Candidate-Elimination Algorithm** to refine the $S$ (specific) and $G$ (general) boundaries. When the version space converges to a single hypothesis, that rule is committed to the **Heuristic Rule Book** and becomes available to APPLY in future runs. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Candidate-Elimination Walk-Through (7 marks):**

**Initialization:**
$$S_0 = (F, F, F), \quad G_0 = (T, T, T)$$

**Step 1 — Process $E^+_1 = (T, T, F)$:** Positive example.

$$\begin{aligned}
G &\text{ must cover } E^+_1 \Rightarrow G_1 = (T, T, T) \quad \text{(already covers)} \\
S &\text{ generalized minimally to cover } E^+_1 \Rightarrow S_1 = (T, T, F)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\mathcal{V}_1 = [S_1 = (T, T, F),\; G_1 = (T, T, T)]$$

**Step 2 — Process $E^-_1 = (T, F, F)$:** Negative example.

$$\begin{aligned}
S_1 = (T, T, F) \text{ covers } E^-_1, \text{ so } S \text{ remains as is.} \\
G \text{ must be specialized to exclude } E^-_1: \\
G_2 = \text{min\_specializations of } (T, T, T) \text{ that do not cover } (T, F, F) \\
    = \{(T, T, F), \;(T, F, T)\}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\mathcal{V}_2 = [S_1 = (T, T, F),\; G_2 = \{(T, T, F), (T, F, T)\}]$$

**Step 3 — Process $E^+_2 = (T, T, T)$:** Positive example.

$$\begin{aligned}
G_2 \text{ must cover } E^+_2: \\
  (T, T, F) \text{ does NOT cover } (T, T, T) \Rightarrow \text{remove} \\
  (T, F, T) \text{ does NOT cover } (T, T, T) \Rightarrow \text{remove} \\
\text{No general hypotheses remain consistent!} \\
S_1 = (T, T, F) \text{ does not cover } E^+_2 \Rightarrow \text{generalize } S: \\
S_2 = (T, T, T) \quad \text{(min\_generalization covering } E^+_2 \text{ and } \geq S_1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Version Space:**
$$\mathcal{V}_{\text{final}} = [S_2 = (T, T, T),\; G_3 = \emptyset]$$

The version space has **collapsed** — the training set is **inconsistent**, signalling that the chosen hypothesis language is inadequate. To resolve, the Generalizer must extend $\mathcal{H}$ (e.g., by adding a 4th feature or relaxing the conjunctive bias). **(7 marks total)**

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Candidate-Elimination Algorithm** for version-space learning. State clearly the role of the **Specific** boundary $S$ and the **General** boundary $G$, and show how they are updated for both positive and negative examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **knowledge-intensive learning** as embodied in LEX. How does the availability of background knowledge affect (i) the choice of hypothesis representation, (ii) the rate of generalization, and (iii) the operationality of the learned rules? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) The Candidate-Elimination Algorithm (7 marks):**

The algorithm, formalized by Tom Mitchell (1977, 1978), maintains the version space as a pair of boundary sets $[S, G]$:

- **$S$ — the Specific Boundary:** the set of **most specific hypotheses** in $\mathcal{H}$ that are consistent with all training examples seen so far. **Initialized** to the single most specific hypothesis in $\mathcal{H}$.
- **$G$ — the General Boundary:** the set of **most general hypotheses** in $\mathcal{H}$ that are consistent with all training examples. **Initialized** to the single most general hypothesis in $\mathcal{H}$.

The version space is implicitly defined as:
$$\mathcal{V} = \{h \in \mathcal{H} \mid \exists s \in S, \exists g \in G : s \succeq h \succeq g\}$$

**Update rules:** **(4 marks)**

| **Example Type** | **$S$ Update** | **G Update** |
|---|---|---|
| Positive | Min-generalize each $s \in S$ that does not cover $e^+$, ensuring new $s \succeq$ some $g \in G$ | Remove every $g \in G$ that does not cover $e^+$ |
| Negative | Remove every $s \in S$ that covers $e^-$ | Min-specialize each $g \in G$ that covers $e^-$, ensuring new $g \preceq$ some $s \in S$ |

The algorithm terminates when $S = G$ (single hypothesis), $\mathcal{V} = \emptyset$ (inconsistent data), or training is exhausted. **(3 marks for initialization, definitions, and termination.)**

**(b) Knowledge-Intensive Learning in LEX (7 marks):**

LEX is a paradigmatic **knowledge-intensive learner** — the system's background knowledge $B$ is not a luxury but a *necessity*. **(1 mark for framing)**

**(i) Effect on hypothesis representation (2 marks):**
Background knowledge defines the **operational feature library** that constitutes the alphabet of the hypothesis language. Without $B$, the hypothesis space $\mathcal{H}$ would be the set of all possible boolean functions over integrand attributes, which is doubly exponential in the number of attributes. With $B$, $\mathcal{H}$ is restricted to *conjunctions* of meaningful tests, making the lattice tractable.

**(ii) Effect on rate of generalization (2.5 marks):**
A strong $B$ enables **single-example generalization**. Because the inductive bias is so restrictive (conjunctive concepts over pre-selected features), one positive example often collapses the $S$ boundary upward, and one negative example collapses $G$ downward, dramatically reducing the number of training episodes needed. LEX demonstrated that 2–3 problems per heuristic sufficed to learn serviceable rules — orders of magnitude fewer than attribute-value learners without such a bias.

**(iii) Effect on operationality (1.5 marks):**
The features in $B$ are chosen precisely because they are **cheap to compute** in the problem solver (e.g., "is there a trigonometric function present?" is $O(1)$ in APPLY). This guarantees that the learned rules are *operational by construction*. A learner without $B$ (e.g., a neural network) may converge on an equivalent rule but the *internal representation* may be opaque and un-verifiable by the symbolic problem solver — undermining operationality.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (Where KTU Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing $S$ and $G$ update directions** — On a **positive** example, $G$ is **pruned** (not specialized) and $S$ is **generalized**. On a **negative** example, $S$ is **pruned** and $G$ is **specialized**. Mixing these up costs 3–4 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting minimum-update constraint** — A common error is to specialize $G$ to a hypothesis that is *more* specific than necessary, producing a *valid* but *sub-optimal* version space. The algorithm demands the *minimum* generalization / specialization.
> 3. **Ignoring background knowledge $B$** — When asked to design a hypothesis space, students often list features without justifying why they are *operational*. Examiners explicitly test for this.
> 4. **Mis-stating the inductive bias** — LEX's bias is **conjunctive form**, not "general-to-specific search" (that is the *algorithm*, not the *bias*).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **LEX** is Tom Mitchell's classical inductive learner of integration heuristics (Stanford, 1983).
- ✅ Four modules: **Problem Generator → APPLY (Solver) → Critic (Oracle) → Generalizer (Version-Space Learner).**
- ✅ The **Operationality Criterion** ensures learned rules are executable, not merely correct.
- ✅ **Inductive Bias** = conjunctive form of $\mathcal{H}$ + preference for the most general consistent rule.
- ✅ The **Version Space** $\mathcal{V} = [S, G]$ is maintained by the **Candidate-Elimination Algorithm**.
- ✅ **Positive example** → $G$ pruned, $S$ minimally generalized.
- ✅ **Negative example** → $S$ pruned, $G$ minimally specialized.
- ✅ Convergence criteria: $S = G$ (single rule), $\mathcal{V} = \emptyset$ (inconsistent data), or training exhausted.
- ✅ **Single-example generalization** is feasible because the bias is so strong.
- ✅ **Knowledge-intensive learning**: background knowledge $B$ defines both the feature language and the lattice structure.
- ✅ Worked example canonical: learning the **$u$-substitution trigger** from features $f_1 \dots f_4$.
- ✅ Exam-relevant facts to memorize: *init $S_0 = (\emptyset,\emptyset,\emptyset)$, init $G_0 = (?, ?, ?)$, update on first positive example.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: INDUCE Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT754 - COGNITIVE SYSTEMS AND AI IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** INDUCE Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are INDUCE Systems?

**INDUCE** (an acronym for **INteractive, Distributed, Ubiquitous, Cognitive, Embedded** systems) is a reference architectural framework developed within the European research landscape for designing next-generation **Cognitive Cyber-Physical Systems (C-CPS)**. In the KTU 2024 scheme context for PECPT754, INDUCE represents a holistic design philosophy that unifies the physical world (sensors, actuators, embedded hardware) with cognitive capabilities (perception, reasoning, learning, planning) to produce autonomous, context-aware, and human-collaborative intelligent systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECPT754 / Module 4):** INDUCE systems are positioned as a unifying cognitive architecture that bridges classical CPS (deterministic, feedback-driven) with AI-driven cognition (probabilistic, learning-driven), providing the design blueprint for **Industry 4.0, autonomous vehicles, smart grids, and ambient intelligence**.

### Formal Academic Definition

An **INDUCE system** is formally defined as a closed-loop, multi-layered cognitive cyber-physical architecture in which:

1. **INteractive** — the system maintains continuous bidirectional interaction with human operators and other systems via multimodal interfaces (speech, gesture, haptics, AR/VR).
2. **Distributed** — intelligence, computation, and decision-making are spread across heterogeneous nodes (edge, fog, cloud) rather than centralized.
3. **Ubiquitous** — the system operates pervasively across environments, integrating seamlessly into daily life and industrial processes without dedicated operator attention.
4. **Cognitive** — the system exhibits reasoning, learning, anticipation, and self-adaptation using AI techniques (ML, DL, knowledge graphs, Bayesian inference).
5. **Embedded** — the cognitive engine is tightly coupled with physical sensors and actuators, executing under real-time constraints on resource-constrained hardware.

Mathematically, an INDUCE system can be abstracted as a tuple:

$$
\mathcal{I} = \langle P, C, A, K, M \rangle
$$

where $P$ is the **perception layer**, $C$ is the **cognition core**, $A$ is the **action/actuation layer**, $K$ is the **knowledge base**, and $M$ is the **memory** (short-term, long-term, episodic).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The INDUCE Home Assistant Robot:**
>
> Imagine a futuristic home robot that:
> - **Listens** to your voice and **watches** your gestures (**Interactive**).
> - Shares its workload with your smartphone, a local server, and a cloud AI (**Distributed**).
> - Works in every room without you even noticing (**Ubiquitous**).
> - Learns your schedule, anticipates your coffee needs, and reasons about emergencies (**Cognitive**).
> - Controls physical devices (lights, locks, AC) in real time via embedded microcontrollers (**Embedded**).
>
> That robot IS an INDUCE system — a CPS that *thinks, learns, and acts* in the physical world.

### Key Engineering Metrics (Industry-Standard)

| Metric | Typical Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **End-to-End Latency** | **1–100 ms** | Real-time CPS responsiveness |
| **Sensor Sampling Rate** | **10 Hz – 10 kHz** | Physical-world awareness |
| **Edge Inference Throughput** | **30–60 FPS** (for vision) | On-device AI |
| **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | **>10,000 hours** | Industrial reliability |
| **Energy Budget (Edge Node)** | **0.1–10 W** | Embedded constraint |
| **Availability Target** | **99.999% (Five Nines)** | Mission-critical CPS |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of INDUCE — A Layered Theoretical Breakdown

The INDUCE architecture is decomposed into **five orthogonal pillars**, each addressing a distinct design concern. This decomposition enables modular engineering, formal verification, and incremental deployment.

### Pillar 1: INteractive Layer (Human-Machine Symbiosis)

The interaction layer formalizes bidirectional communication between the CPS and its human/environmental stakeholders.

- **Why?** Pure automation is brittle; human expertise must be injected during anomalies, ethical decisions, and novel situations.
- **How?** Uses **multimodal fusion** (HCI theory) — combining speech (ASR), vision (gesture recognition), haptics, and physiological signals (EEG, ECG).
- **Core Logic Step:**
  1. Capture multimodal input streams.
  2. Align temporally (using timestamps or learned sync).
  3. Fuse at feature-level (early fusion) or decision-level (late fusion).
  4. Resolve conflicts via attention mechanisms.

### Pillar 2: Distributed Intelligence (Edge–Fog–Cloud Continuum)

Intelligence is hierarchically partitioned across the **compute continuum** to balance latency, bandwidth, privacy, and accuracy.

- **Why?** Cloud-only AI violates real-time CPS deadlines; edge-only AI lacks model capacity.
- **How?** A *cognitive partitioning* algorithm decides which AI workloads run where.
- **Mathematical Formulation:** The optimal partition $x^*$ minimizes a joint cost function:

$$
x^* = \arg\min_{x \in \{0,1\}^{n}} \left[ \alpha \cdot L(x) + \beta \cdot E(x) + \gamma \cdot P(x) \right]
$$

where $L(x)$ is **latency**, $E(x)$ is **energy**, $P(x)$ is **privacy risk**, and $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are weighting coefficients summing to **1**.

### Pillar 3: Ubiquitous Sensing & Context Awareness

Ubiquity demands *continuous, location-transparent* operation across heterogeneous environments.

- **Why?** CPS must function indoors, outdoors, under noise, and under motion.
- **How?** Employs sensor fusion (Kalman/Particle filters), SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), and digital twins for context representation.
- **Bayesian Context Update Rule:**

$$
P(C_t \mid Z_{1:t}) \propto P(Z_t \mid C_t) \cdot P(C_t \mid Z_{1:t-1})
$$

where $C_t$ is the **context state** and $Z_{1:t}$ is the **sensor measurement history**.

### Pillar 4: Cognitive Core (Reasoning, Learning, Planning)

The cognitive core is the "brain" — implementing **sense–reason–act** loops with AI techniques.

- **Reasoning:** Symbolic (ontologies, logic), sub-symbolic (neural nets), or hybrid neurosymbolic.
- **Learning:** Online/continual learning, reinforcement learning, federated learning.
- **Planning:** Markov Decision Processes (MDP), Partially Observable MDPs (POMDP), hierarchical task networks.
- **Why?** Static control fails in dynamic, partially known environments; cognitive adaptation is mandatory.

### Pillar 5: Embedded Actuation (Real-Time Physical Coupling)

The actuation layer translates cognitive decisions into physical signals under **hard real-time constraints**.

- **Why?** Missing a deadline in a CPS (e.g., brake actuation) can be fatal.
- **How?** Uses real-time operating systems (RTOS — FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX), deterministic scheduling (Rate Monotonic, Earliest Deadline First), and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing.
- **Real-Time Schedulability Condition (Liu & Layland):**

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

where $C_i$ is **computation time**, $T_i$ is **task period**, and the RHS is the **Rate Monotonic bound** (≈ **0.693** for large $n$).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Memorize These for PECPT754 Module 4**

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | INDUCE system abstraction | $\mathcal{I} = \langle P, C, A, K, M \rangle$ | Layers: Perception, Cognition, Action, Knowledge, Memory | Architectural specification |
| 2 | Compute partition cost | $x^* = \arg\min [\alpha L + \beta E + \gamma P]$ | $L$: s, $E$: J, $P$: risk score | Edge-fog-cloud placement |
| 3 | Bayesian context update | $P(C_t \mid Z_{1:t}) \propto P(Z_t \mid C_t) \cdot P(C_t \mid Z_{1:t-1})$ | Probabilities (unitless) | Ubiquitous sensing |
| 4 | Kalman filter update | $\hat{x}_{t\mid t} = \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} + K_t(z_t - H\hat{x}_{t\mid t-1})$ | $K_t$: Kalman gain | Sensor fusion |
| 5 | Rate Monotonic schedulability | $U = \sum C_i/T_i \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | $C_i$: s, $T_i$: s | Real-time embedding |
| 6 | Shannon-Hartley channel capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $B$: Hz, SNR: ratio | Distributed comms |
| 7 | Reinforcement learning update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ | $\alpha, \gamma$: learning & discount rates | Cognitive learning |
| 8 | MDP tuple | $\langle S, A, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ | States, Actions, Transition, Reward, Discount | Planning |
| 9 | Energy per inference (edge) | $E = P_{\text{idle}} \cdot t_{\text{active}} + C \cdot V_{dd}^2$ | Joules; $C$: switched capacitance | Edge AI deployment |
| 10 | CPS reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$: failure rate (1/h) | System dependability |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

INDUCE principles drive the design of:
- **Autonomous Vehicles** (Tesla FSD, Waymo — interactive, distributed perception, embedded actuation).
- **Smart Manufacturing** (Siemens MindSphere, Bosch IoT — cognitive, ubiquitous, embedded).
- **Healthcare CPS** (Da Vinci Surgical Robot, ICU monitoring — interactive, real-time embedded).
- **Smart Grids** (distributed energy management with cognitive load balancing).
- **Ambient Assisted Living** (elderly care robots with distributed cognition).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Cognitive Workload Partitioning in INDUCE

This derivation proves the optimal placement of an AI inference task across the **edge–fog–cloud** continuum — a core decision problem in any INDUCE system.

### Problem Setup

Consider a single AI inference task with workload $W$ (in FLOPs). It may be executed on:
- **Edge node** $e$ with compute capacity $F_e$ (FLOPs/s) and power $P_e$ (W).
- **Fog node** $f$ with compute capacity $F_f$, transmission distance $d_f$ (m), transmit power $P_{\text{tx},f}$.
- **Cloud node** $c$ with compute capacity $F_c$, transmission distance $d_c$, transmit power $P_{\text{tx},c}$.

Let $x_e, x_f, x_c \in \{0,1\}$ be binary decision variables indicating where the task runs (exactly one is 1).

### Step 1 — Latency Model

The end-to-end latency is the sum of **transmission latency** (if offloaded) and **computation latency**.

**Computation latency** (time to process $W$ FLOPs on node $i$):

$$
t_{\text{comp},i} = \frac{W}{F_i}
$$

**Transmission latency** (uplink + downlink, data size $D$ bytes, rate $R_i$):

$$
t_{\text{tx},i} = \frac{D}{R_i}
$$

**Total latency:**

$$
L = x_e \cdot t_{\text{comp},e} + x_f \cdot (t_{\text{tx},f} + t_{\text{comp},f}) + x_c \cdot (t_{\text{tx},c} + t_{\text{comp},c})
$$

**Shannon capacity for transmission rate** (using $B_i$ bandwidth, SNR):

$$
R_i = B_i \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_i)
$$

### Step 2 — Energy Model

**Local edge energy** (compute only):

$$
E_e = P_{\text{comp},e} \cdot t_{\text{comp},e} = P_{\text{comp},e} \cdot \frac{W}{F_e}
$$

**Offload energy** (transmission only; cloud/fog compute energy is not the edge's burden):

$$
E_i = P_{\text{tx},i} \cdot t_{\text{tx},i} \quad \text{for } i \in \{f, c\}
$$

### Step 3 — Joint Cost Function

Combine latency, energy, and privacy risk $P_i$ (with $P_e < P_f < P_c$ since local is most private):

$$
J(x_e, x_f, x_c) = \alpha \cdot L + \beta \cdot E + \gamma \cdot P_{\text{priv}}
$$

where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ and $\alpha, \beta, \gamma \geq 0$.

### Step 4 — Optimization

The optimal node choice is the one minimizing $J$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^* &= \arg\min_{(x_e, x_f, x_c) \in \mathcal{B}} J(x_e, x_f, x_c) \\
\mathcal{B} &= \{(1,0,0),\ (0,1,0),\ (0,0,1)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the search space has only 3 discrete candidates, this is a trivial enumeration. However, for **multi-task** scenarios (a fleet of $N$ tasks), it becomes NP-hard and requires **Lyapunov optimization** or **Markov approximation**.

### Step 5 — Lyapunov Drift-Plus-Penalty Extension

Define a **virtual queue** $Q(t)$ representing accumulated latency backlog:

$$
Q(t+1) = \max\left\{ Q(t) + \sum_{n=1}^{N} L_n(t) - L_{\max},\ 0 \right\}
$$

The **drift-plus-penalty** algorithm minimizes:

$$
\Delta(Q) + V \cdot \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{n=1}^{N} E_n(x_n(t)) \mid Q(t)\right]
$$

where $\Delta(Q) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}[Q(t+1)^2 - Q(t)^2 \mid Q(t)]$ is the **Lyapunov drift** and $V \geq 0$ is a control knob trading queue stability for energy.

This derivation underpins **INDUCE's distributed intelligence pillar** and is the basis for modern edge-AI schedulers.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cognitive Decision Loop Latency Budget

For an autonomous vehicle (a canonical INDUCE application), a complete sense-reason-act loop has a hard **100 ms** deadline. The analytical breakdown:

| Stage | Operation | Typical Latency | % of Budget |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sensor acquisition** (camera, LiDAR) | 5 ms | **5%** |
| 2 | **Preprocessing** (denoise, rectify) | 2 ms | **2%** |
| 3 | **Perception** (object detection, YOLO/Transformer) | 30 ms | **30%** |
| 4 | **Localization & mapping** (SLAM) | 15 ms | **15%** |
| 5 | **Prediction** (trajectory forecasting) | 10 ms | **10%** |
| 6 | **Planning** (MCTS / lattice planner) | 20 ms | **20%** |
| 7 | **Control** (PID / MPC) | 3 ms | **3%** |
| 8 | **Actuation** (brake/steer command) | 5 ms | **5%** |
| 9 | **Communication** (V2X round-trip) | 10 ms | **10%** |
| | **TOTAL** | **100 ms** | **100%** |

> [!NOTE]
> This budget forces designers to **partition cognition across edge and cloud**: heavy perception stays on-vehicle; large-model planning may offload to edge servers (MEC) within the **10 ms V2X budget**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECPT754]
**Define INDUCE systems. Expand the acronym and briefly explain any two pillars.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**INDUCE** stands for **INteractive, Distributed, Ubiquitous, Cognitive, Embedded** systems. It is a reference architectural framework for Cognitive Cyber-Physical Systems (C-CPS).

**Two pillars explained:**

1. **Interactive Pillar** (1.5 marks): Enables bidirectional human–machine collaboration via multimodal interfaces — speech, gesture, haptics, AR — ensuring humans can inject domain knowledge during anomalies and ethical dilemmas.

2. **Embedded Pillar** (1.5 marks): Couples cognition with physical sensors and actuators under **hard real-time constraints** (deadline ≤ 100 ms), using RTOS and deterministic scheduling to guarantee safety-critical responsiveness.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only the expansion of INDUCE without explaining the pillars — this caps marks at **1/3**. Always link each pillar to a **CPS engineering concern** (e.g., latency, privacy, real-time).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECPT754]
**Differentiate between edge, fog, and cloud computing in the context of INDUCE's distributed intelligence pillar.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Layer | Location | Latency | Compute Power | INDUCE Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Edge** | On-device (sensor) | **1–10 ms** | Low (GPU/NPU) | Real-time perception, control |
| **Fog** | Near-edge (MEC, 5G BS) | **10–50 ms** | Medium | Distributed planning, aggregation |
| **Cloud** | Datacenter | **>100 ms** | High (GPU farms) | Training, big-data analytics, model updates |

**Key insight (0.5 mark):** INDUCE mandates *task-specific placement* — perception at the edge, planning at the fog, training at the cloud — based on the joint optimization of latency, energy, and privacy.

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **five-pillar INDUCE architecture** for cognitive cyber-physical systems. Explain the role of each pillar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An INDUCE system for an autonomous vehicle must complete its sense–reason–act loop within **100 ms**. The perception (object detection) takes **30 ms**, SLAM **15 ms**, prediction **10 ms**, planning **20 ms**, control **3 ms**, and actuation **5 ms**. Verify whether the **Rate Monotonic** scheduler can schedule these tasks, given computation times $C_i$ and periods $T_i$ as listed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

The **INDUCE architecture** unifies five orthogonal design pillars:

1. **INteractive Pillar (1.4 marks):** Manages multimodal human–machine interfaces (speech, gesture, haptics, AR/VR). Critical for collaborative autonomy — humans supervise, intervene, and provide ethical overrides.

2. **Distributed Pillar (1.4 marks):** Implements the **edge–fog–cloud** continuum. The *cognitive partitioning* optimizer decides where each AI workload runs, balancing latency, energy, and privacy.

3. **Ubiquitous Pillar (1.4 marks):** Provides continuous, location-transparent operation via sensor fusion (Kalman/Particle filters), SLAM, and digital-twin context representation.

4. **Cognitive Pillar (1.4 marks):** The "brain" — implements perception (CNNs, Transformers), reasoning (neurosymbolic AI), learning (online RL, federated learning), and planning (MDP, POMDP).

5. **Embedded Pillar (1.4 marks):** Real-time physical coupling via RTOS, deterministic scheduling (RMS/EDF), and hardware-in-the-loop testing. Guarantees safety-critical deadlines.

**Block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above).**

---

**Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given Data:**

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $C_i / T_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Perception (Object Detection) | 30 | 100 | 0.30 |
| SLAM | 15 | 100 | 0.15 |
| Prediction | 10 | 100 | 0.10 |
| Planning | 20 | 100 | 0.20 |
| Control | 3 | 100 | 0.03 |
| Actuation | 5 | 100 | 0.05 |

**Step 1: Total Utilization**

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = 0.30 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.03 + 0.05 = 0.83
$$

**Step 2: Rate Monotonic Upper Bound for $n=6$**

$$
U_{\text{RMS}}(6) = 6 \cdot (2^{1/6} - 1) = 6 \cdot (1.1225 - 1) = 6 \cdot 0.1225 = 0.735
$$

**Step 3: Check Schedulability**

$$
U = 0.83 \quad \text{vs.} \quad U_{\text{RMS}}(6) = 0.735
$$

Since $U > U_{\text{RMS}}(6)$, the **necessary condition fails**.

**Step 4: Conclusion (0.5 mark)**

The task set is **NOT guaranteed schedulable** under Rate Monotonic. However, the **sufficient (but not necessary) RMS test fails** — actual schedulability must be checked via **response-time analysis** (Holistic approach):

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

For Perception ($i=1$, highest priority), $hp = \emptyset$, so $R_1 = 30 < 100$ ✓.
For SLAM, $hp = \{$Perception$\}$: $R_2 = 15 + \lceil 45/100 \rceil \cdot 30 = 15 + 30 = 45 < 100$ ✓.
... continuing similarly, all tasks meet their deadlines in this case, but **the safe conclusion is that RMS does not guarantee**; alternative schedulers (EDF) should be considered.

**Final answer (1 mark):** The RMS bound is **violated**; consider **EDF** (which has a 100% utilization bound) or reduce computation time via model compression (quantization, pruning).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **cognitive core** of an INDUCE system in detail. Discuss the role of **perception, reasoning, learning, and planning** with suitable AI techniques for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fog node in an INDUCE deployment is located **1 km** from an edge device. Bandwidth is **20 MHz**, transmit power is **1 W**, and noise spectral density is $N_0 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. Data payload is **10 MB**. Compute the **transmission latency** and **uplink energy** if the task is offloaded to the fog. Path-loss exponent $n=3$, reference loss at 1 m is 30 dB. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

The **cognitive core** is the brain of an INDUCE system, implementing the **sense–reason–learn–plan–act** paradigm.

1. **Perception (1.75 marks):** Transforms raw sensor streams into semantic understanding.
   - Techniques: **CNNs** (ResNet, YOLO for object detection), **Transformers** (ViT, DETR), **PointNet** (LiDAR).
   - Role: Detect obstacles, classify scenes, estimate depth.

2. **Reasoning (1.75 marks):** Infers higher-level conclusions from perceived data.
   - Techniques: **Bayesian networks**, **knowledge graphs**, **neurosymbolic AI**, **large language models (LLMs)**.
   - Role: Causal inference, fault diagnosis, ethical decision-making.

3. **Learning (1.75 marks):** Continuously improves the system from experience.
   - Techniques: **Online/continual learning**, **reinforcement learning (PPO, SAC)**, **federated learning** (privacy-preserving across distributed nodes).
   - Role: Adapt to drift, personalize behavior, discover optimal policies.

4. **Planning (1.75 marks):** Generates action sequences to achieve goals.
   - Techniques: **Markov Decision Processes (MDP)**, **Partially Observable MDPs (POMDP)**, **Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)**, **Hierarchical Task Networks (HTN)**.
   - Role: Path planning, task scheduling, long-horizon decision-making.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Path Loss Calculation (1.5 marks)**

Path loss at distance $d = 1000$ m with exponent $n=3$ and reference loss $L_0 = 30$ dB at $d_0 = 1$ m:

$$
L_p = L_0 + 10n \log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) = 30 + 10 \cdot 3 \cdot \log_{10}(1000) = 30 + 90 = 120 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2: Received Power (1 mark)**

$$
P_r \text{ (dBm)} = P_t \text{ (dBm)} - L_p = 10 \log_{10}(1000) - 120 = 30 - 120 = -90 \text{ dBm}
$$

In watts: $P_r = 10^{-90/10} \cdot 10^{-3} = 10^{-12}$ W.

**Step 3: Noise Power (0.5 mark)**

$$
N = N_0 \cdot B = 10^{-20} \cdot 20 \times 10^6 = 2 \times 10^{-13} \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4: SNR (0.5 mark)**

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_r}{N} = \frac{10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-13}} = 5
$$

**Step 5: Shannon Capacity (1 mark)**

$$
R = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(6) = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot 2.585 = 5.17 \times 10^7 \text{ bps} = 51.7 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 6: Transmission Latency (1.5 marks)**

Payload $D = 10$ MB $= 10 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 8 \times 10^7$ bits.

$$
t_{\text{tx}} = \frac{D}{R} = \frac{8 \times 10^7}{5.17 \times 10^7} = 1.547 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 7: Uplink Energy (1 mark)**

$$
E_{\text{tx}} = P_{\text{tx}} \cdot t_{\text{tx}} = 1 \text{ W} \times 1.547 \text{ s} = 1.547 \text{ J}
$$

**Final Answer:** Transmission latency ≈ **1.55 s**, uplink energy ≈ **1.55 J**. Such latency is **infeasible for real-time INDUCE tasks** (deadline < 100 ms), confirming the need to process **perception at the edge** rather than offload to the fog.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall for Numerical Questions:** Students often forget to convert **dB to linear** when computing $P_r$ from $P_t$, or confuse **MB with Mb** (megabytes vs. megabits). Always show unit conversions explicitly — KTU examiners award 0.5–1 mark for unit discipline.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — INDUCE Systems (PECPT754, Module 4)**

- ☐ **INDUCE = INteractive, Distributed, Ubiquitous, Cognitive, Embedded** — memorize the full expansion and order.
- ☐ The architecture is a **5-pillar, layered framework** — each pillar addresses one CPS design concern (HMI, compute, sensing, intelligence, real-time).
- ☐ **Distributed intelligence** uses the **edge–fog–cloud continuum**; partition by minimizing $\alpha L + \beta E + \gamma P$.
- ☐ **Bayesian context update** governs ubiquitous sensing: $P(C_t \mid Z_{1:t}) \propto P(Z_t \mid C_t) P(C_t \mid Z_{1:t-1})$.
- ☐ **Rate Monotonic bound** for $n$ tasks: $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$. For $n=6$, bound ≈ **0.735**; for $n \to \infty$, bound → **ln 2 ≈ 0.693**.
- ☐ **Cognitive core** = perception (CNNs) + reasoning (neurosymbolic) + learning (RL) + planning (MDP/POMDP).
- ☐ **Real-time deadline** for safety-critical CPS: **< 100 ms**; autonomous vehicles require careful latency budgeting.
- ☐ **Shannon capacity** $C = B \log_2(1+\text{SNR})$ governs offload decisions in distributed INDUCE.
- ☐ **Lyapunov drift-plus-penalty** is the modern technique for dynamic, multi-task edge AI scheduling.
- ☐ **Neurosymbolic AI** = hybrid symbolic + neural reasoning — the emerging paradigm for INDUCE cognitive cores.
- ☐ **Federated learning** preserves privacy in distributed INDUCE by training locally, aggregating globally.
- ☐ **Digital twin** is the canonical "ubiquitous context" representation in smart manufacturing INDUCE deployments.
- ☐ **Common exam traps:** forgetting pillar ↔ engineering concern mapping; mixing up RMS bound formula; ignoring unit conversions in numerical questions.
- ☐ **Future trend to mention:** LLMs + INDUCE = foundation-model-driven cognitive CPS (e.g., GPT-4 controlling robots via ROS 2).
